# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Model Note:** KTU Part A questions test **CO1 (Remembering/Understanding)** with crisp definitions and one supporting numerical example.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECHT867 Model Paper]
**Differentiate between the 5Rs Hierarchy of Waste Management. Briefly explain how Process Systems Engineering approaches waste as a secondary feedstock.**

**Model Answer:**
The 5Rs Hierarchy (Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover) is a **priority-ordered decision framework** where *Refuse* eliminates need for waste generation, *Reduce* minimizes generation, *Reuse* extends product lifecycle, *Recycle* reprocesses into same/similar products, and *Recover* extracts energy.

PSE treats waste as a **secondary feedstock** because:
1. Mass-energy balances apply identically to waste and virgin raw materials.
2. Multi-objective optimization (cost, energy, emissions) selects the best valorization pathway.
3. Process integration tools (pinch analysis, MILP) identify industrial symbiosis opportunities.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1.5 for 5Rs definition + 1.5 for PSE perspective]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]
**With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an Anaerobic Digester. State two key operating parameters.**

**Model Answer:**
An Anaerobic Digester (AD) is a sealed, oxygen-free reactor where **hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, and methanogenic microorganisms** decompose organic matter into **biogas (60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂)** and **nutrient-rich digestate**.

Working: Feed slurry (8–12% TS) is fed → heated to **mesophilic (35–40 °C)** or **thermophilic (50–55 °C)** zone → residence time **15–40 days** → biogas rises to gas dome, digestate exits bottom.

**Two key operating parameters:**
- **Temperature**: Mesophilic (35–40 °C) preferred for stability.
- **pH**: Maintained at **6.8–7.5**; lower pH inhibits methanogens.

**[3 marks breakdown: 1.5 for working + 1 for parameters + 0.5 for sketch/values]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **two sub-parts** (typically 7 marks each). The Internal Choice option means the student picks **one of two alternatives**.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe with a flow diagram the **working of a Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) Incineration plant with energy recovery**. Discuss the air pollution control system used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An MSW has the following ultimate analysis on dry basis: C = 42%, H = 5%, O = 35%, S = 0.5%, N = 0.8%, Ash = 16.7%. Moisture content (as-received) = 30%. Calculate: **(i)** HHV on dry basis, **(ii)** LHV on dry basis, **(iii)** HHV as-received, **(iv)** Stoichiometric air required per kg of as-received MSW. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

```
Waste Receipt → Storage Pit (3-5 day capacity)
        ↓
Crane Feeding → Combustion Grate (850-1100 °C)
        ↓
Boiler (Steam at 40 bar, 400 °C) → Steam Turbine
        ↓                              ↓
Ash Discharge                    Generator → Electricity to Grid
        ↓
Air Pollution Control (APC):
   ├─ Cyclone (Particulate)
   ├─ Scrubber (SO₂, HCl) using lime slurry
   ├─ ESP/Baghouse (PM removal to <30 mg/Nm³)
   ├─ SCR/SNCR (NOx reduction by NH₃ injection)
   └─ Dioxin/Furan control via activated carbon
```

**Key Design Parameters:**
- **Grate furnace technology**: Martin grate, Von Roll grate (common in India).
- **Combustion efficiency**: >99.99% (4-T rule: Temperature >850°C, Time >2s, Turbulence, O₂ excess).
- **Energy recovery**: 1 tonne MSW → ~400 kWh electricity (gross), ~150 kWh (net after plant consumption).

**APC System Compliance (Indian CPCB norms 2018):**
- PM < 30 mg/Nm³, SO₂ < 200 mg/Nm³, NOx < 250 mg/Nm³, HCl < 50 mg/Nm³, Hg < 0.05 mg/Nm³.
- Dioxins < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.

**[Marks: 2 diagram + 2 working + 1 APC description + 1 numerical example + 1 norms]**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**(i) HHV on dry basis (Dulong's formula):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV_{dry} &= 0.338(42) + 1.428\left(5 - \dfrac{35}{8}\right) + 0.095(0.5) \\
&= 14.196 + 1.428(5 - 4.375) + 0.0475 \\
&= 14.196 + 1.428(0.625) + 0.0475 \\
&= 14.196 + 0.8925 + 0.0475 \\
&= 15.14 \;\text{MJ/kg (dry)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) LHV on dry basis:**
$$LHV_{dry} = 15.14 - 0.2198 \times 5 = 15.14 - 1.099 = 14.04 \;\text{MJ/kg (dry)}$$

**(iii) HHV as-received:**

Two methods — use the formula directly on as-received composition (which requires converting to dry ash-free basis) or apply moisture dilution:

Method: HHV as-received = $HHV_{dry} \times (1-M) - 2.442 \times M$ (where M = moisture fraction)
$$HHV_{ar} = 15.14 \times 0.70 - 2.442 \times 0.30 = 10.60 - 0.733 = 9.86 \;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**(iv) Stoichiometric air per kg as-received:**

Using $A_s = \dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ on dry basis:
$$A_{s,\,dry} = \dfrac{8}{3}(42) + 8(5) + 0.5 - 35 = 112 + 40 + 0.5 - 35 = 117.5 \;\text{kg O}_2/100\text{ kg fuel (dry)}$$
$$= 1.175 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg dry fuel}$$
$$A_s^{air} = \dfrac{1.175}{0.232} = 5.06 \;\text{kg air/kg dry fuel}$$

As-received: $5.06 \times 0.70 = 3.54$ kg air/kg as-received MSW.

**[Marks: 2 HHV + 1.5 LHV + 1.5 HHV wet + 2 air calc]**

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, 14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss the **global scenario of municipal solid waste generation and management**. Compare the Indian context with developed nations in terms of per-capita waste generation, recycling rates, and disposal methods. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Buswell equation** for predicting biogas yield from organic substrates. A fruit-processing industry generates **50 tonnes/day of organic waste** with VS = 85%, VS destruction = 70%, and methane yield coefficient = 0.40 Nm³ CH₄/kg VS destroyed. Calculate daily CH₄ generation and its electricity equivalent at 35% conversion efficiency. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Global Scenario (2022–2024 data, World Bank):**

| Region | MSW (kg/cap/day) | Recycled (%) | Landfilled (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-income (USA, EU) | 2.0–2.5 | 30–45 | 40–50 |
| Upper-middle (China, Brazil) | 1.0–1.5 | 15–25 | 50–60 |
| Lower-middle (India, Indonesia) | 0.4–0.7 | 5–15 | 70–85 |
| Low-income (Sub-Saharan Africa) | 0.2–0.4 | <5 | >90 |

**Key Comparisons:**
- **Per-capita**: India = **0.62 kg/day**, USA = **2.1 kg/day**, EU = **1.5 kg/day**.
- **Recycling**: Germany = **~67%** (highest globally), India = **~13.5%**, USA = **~32%**.
- **Open dumping**: India = **~40%**, USA = **<1%**, EU = **<1%**.
- **Energy recovery**: EU = **~25%**, China = **~15%**, India = **<1%**.

**Indian Specifics:**
- 62 million tonnes/year (2021 CPCB).
- **CPCB 2024 projections**: 165 million tonnes/year by 2030.
- **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0** targets 100% scientific processing by 2026.
- **SWM Rules 2016** mandate Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), segregation at source.

**[Marks: 2 global data + 2 comparison table + 1 Indian stats + 1 policy + 1 SWOT remark]**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Buswell Equation — Theoretical Background:**

For a generic organic substrate $C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d$, complete anaerobic digestion yields:

$$C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d + \left(\dfrac{2n + a/2 - b/2 + 3c/2 - d}{2}\right) H_2O \to$$
$$\left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{c}{8} + \dfrac{d}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4}\right) CO_2 + c NH_3 + d H_2S$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**(Step 1) Volatile Solids fed:**
$$VS_{feed} = 50{,}000 \times 0.85 = 42{,}500 \;\text{kg VS/day}$$

**(Step 2) VS destroyed (70%):**
$$VS_{destroyed} = 42{,}500 \times 0.70 = 29{,}750 \;\text{kg VS/day}$$

**(Step 3) CH₄ generated:**
$$V_{CH_4} = 29{,}750 \times 0.40 = 11{,}900 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

**(Step 4) Biogas composition (typical CH₄ = 60%):**
$$V_{biogas} = \dfrac{11{,}900}{0.60} = 19{,}833 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

**(Step 5) Energy content:**
$$E_{th} = 11{,}900 \times 35.88 = 426{,}972 \;\text{MJ/day}$$

**(Step 6) Electrical energy (η = 0.35):**
$$E_{el} = 426{,}972 \times 0.35 = 149{,}440 \;\text{MJ/day}$$
$$E_{el} = \dfrac{149{,}440}{3.6} = 41{,}511 \;\text{kWh/day} = 41.5 \;\text{MWh/day}$$

**(Step 7) Bonus — Annual revenue (₹6/kWh feed-in tariff):**
$$\text{Revenue} = 41{,}511 \times 6 \times 365 = \text{₹} \;90.9\;\text{million/year} \;(\text{~₹ 9 crore/year})$$

**[Marks: 1 Buswell explanation + 1 daily load + 1 VS destroyed + 1.5 CH₄ volume + 1.5 electrical energy + 1 revenue interpretation]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Waste Management Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion (3 marks lost in MSE frequently)**: Dulong's formula needs **weight percentages**, not fractions. If you write C = 0.42 instead of 42%, your HHV will be off by **factor 100**.
>
> 2. **As-received vs dry basis**: When a problem states "Moisture = 30%", you **MUST** convert all elemental analyses to dry basis first, or apply the moisture dilution at the end. Skipping this costs **3–4 marks**.
>
> 3. **Buswell equation overkill**: For numerical questions, you don't need to write the full generalized Buswell formula. Use the **yield coefficient** directly when given. Only write the full stoichiometric equation if specifically asked.
>
> 4. **Neglecting CO₂ in biogas**: When asked "biogas volume", use **60% CH₄ content** to back-calculate; when asked "methane volume", do **NOT** divide.
>
> 5. **Pinch of units**: CH₄ yield is **Nm³/kg VS destroyed**, not Nm³/kg waste. Always chain through VS and destruction efficiency.
>
> 6. **Missing diagrams in Part B**: A **mandatory flow diagram** (even a simple block diagram) carries **2 marks minimum** for questions involving any process description.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Waste Management & Energy Recovery**

### Key Concepts (Must-Know)
- ☐ 5Rs Hierarchy in correct priority order.
- ☐ Global MSW statistics: **2.01 Bt/year (2022)**, projected **3.4 Bt by 2050**.
- ☐ Indian per-capita MSW: **~0.62 kg/day**; ~75% collected, ~25% processed.
- ☐ Dulong's formula: $HHV = 0.338C + 1.428(H-O/8) + 0.095S$ (MJ/kg).
- ☐ Stoichiometric air: $A_s = (8C/3 + 8H + S - O)/100$ (per kg fuel).
- ☐ Anaerobic Digestion: mesophilic 35–40°C, retention 15–40 days, biogas 60% CH₄ + 40% CO₂.
- ☐ Biogas HHV: **21.5 MJ/Nm³**; pure CH₄ HHV: **35.88 MJ/Nm³**.
- ☐ Incineration: 850–1100°C, mass reduction ~90%.
- ☐ Gasification: 700–900°C, syngas CV 4–18 MJ/Nm³.
- ☐ Pyrolysis: 300–800°C, three products (char, oil, gas).
- ☐ Methane GWP = **28–36** (IPCC AR6 100-year basis).

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- ☐ **1 tonne MSW → ~400 kWh electricity** (gross incineration).
- ☐ **1 Nm³ CH₄ → 35.88 MJ thermal → 12.56 MJ electrical @ 35%**.
- ☐ **CHP engine efficiency**: 35–42% electrical + 40–50% thermal = 80–90% total.
- ☐ **Self-sustaining combustion threshold**: CV > 11 MJ/kg wet basis.

### Indian Policy & Regulatory Snapshot
- ☐ **SWM Rules 2016** (MoEFCC).
- ☐ **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility)** for plastic packaging.
- ☐ **CPCB emission norms** for WTE: PM < 30 mg/Nm³, dioxin < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.
- ☐ **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0** targets (2026).
- ☐ **Biogas-based CNG**: SATAT scheme (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation).

### Comparison Themes for Comparative Essays
- ☐ **Incineration vs Anaerobic Digestion** (most asked).
- ☐ **Gasification vs Pyrolysis** (oxygen presence).
- ☐ **Material recycling vs Energy recovery** (hierarchy debate).
- ☐ **Developed vs developing country waste management**.

### Technology Selection Decision Path
- ☐ Wet organic → **AD**.
- ☐ High CV dry (>11 MJ/kg) → **Incineration / RDF**.
- ☐ Plastics, tyres → **Pyrolysis / Gasification**.
- ☐ Large volume landfill legacy → **LFG recovery**.

### Process Systems Engineering Connection
- ☐ Waste = **secondary feedstock** (mass-energy balance identical to virgin).
- ☐ Use **pinch analysis** for minimum external heat demand.
- ☐ Apply **MILP/MINLP** for technology network synthesis.
- ☐ **LCA** for environmental burden comparison.
- ☐ **Exergy analysis** for maximum theoretical work recovery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types and Sources of Waste: Classification of Waste (Municipal, Industrial, Agricultural)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types and Sources of Waste: Classification of Waste (Municipal, Industrial, Agricultural)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Systems Engineering (PSE)** and sustainable design, **waste** is defined as any unwanted or unusable material, substance, or by-product generated from human activities (domestic, industrial, or agricultural) that is discarded because it has lost its economic, functional, or nutritional value to the original owner. According to the **Basel Convention (1989)** and the **Indian Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000 (and its 2016 amendments)**, waste is formally classified based on its **physical state**, **source of generation**, and **intrinsic chemical/biological hazard potential**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The classification of waste into **Municipal**, **Industrial**, and **Agricultural** streams is the foundational step in designing any process flow-sheet for waste-to-energy, material recovery, or pollution control. Without proper classification, mass and energy balances cannot be closed.

### 1.2 The Three Primary Waste Classes (KTU 2024 Mandate)

The KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PECHT867**) explicitly identifies the following three primary classifications based on the **source of generation**:

1. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — Solid waste collected by or on behalf of the municipal authority, primarily from households, commercial establishments, and street sweepings. Also known as **trash, refuse, or garbage**.
2. **Industrial Waste** — Waste generated from manufacturing, processing, and industrial operations. It can be **solid (sludge, slag, ash)**, **liquid (effluents)**, or **gaseous (flue gases)**.
3. **Agricultural Waste** — Residues and by-products generated from crop cultivation, livestock operations, and agro-processing industries. Often termed **biomass waste**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Drainage" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a large apartment complex (representing a city or industrial zone). The complex has three distinct plumbing systems:
> - **The Kitchen Drain (Municipal Waste):** Receives food scraps, packaging, paper — mixed, dirty, and comes from individual flats. The complex's garbage chute funnels this to a central bin (the municipal landfill).
> - **The Factory Outlet (Industrial Waste):** Receives concentrated, often toxic chemicals from the basement workshops. This pipe cannot mix with the kitchen drain (segregation principle).
> - **The Garden Compost Pit (Agricultural Waste):** Receives leaves, grass cuttings, and vegetable peels — biodegradable and naturally decomposable.
> - **The Process Engineer's Job:** Is to design a master "treatment plant" that handles each pipe separately because mixing them would create a "super-toxin" or unprocessable sludge. This is why **classification precedes processing**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard physical and regulatory parameters are universally applied in waste classification:

- **Moisture Content (MC):** Measured as a percentage (%) of wet weight. Critical for determining biological treatability.
- **Calorific Value (CV):** Measured in **kJ/kg** or **kcal/kg**. Determines the energy recovery potential.
- **Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N):** A dimensionless number; ideal for composting is **25:1 to 30:1**.
- **Bulk Density:** Expressed in **kg/m³**; for uncompacted MSW, typically **100 to 200 kg/m³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Class 1: Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) — Deep Analysis

Municipal Solid Waste is the most heterogeneous stream. The KTU syllabus expects students to understand its sub-classification by **physical composition** and **biodegradability**.

#### 2.1.1 Sub-Classification of MSW (By Biodegradability)

- **Biodegradable Waste (Organic Fraction):** Food waste, yard waste, paper, textiles (natural fibers). Typical composition: **40%–60%** of total MSW in India.
- **Non-Biodegradable Waste (Inorganic Fraction):** Plastics, glass, metals, rubber, leather.
- **Hazardous Domestic Waste (Domestic Hazardous Waste — DHW):** Batteries, paints, pesticides, e-waste — requires special handling under **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, 2016**.

#### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind MSW Segregation

The engineering rationale is rooted in **Process Integration**. Different waste fractions require different treatment trains:
- **Organic** → Anaerobic digestion / Composting / Biomethanation
- **Recyclables** (paper, plastic, metal) → Material Recovery Facility (MRF)
- **Inerts** (construction debris) → Sanitary landfilling
- **Hazardous** → Specialized Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility (TSDF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The design of a process flow-sheet for an Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM) system depends entirely on the **per-capita waste generation rate**, which in Indian cities is approximately **0.5 kg/person/day** (as per **CPHEEO Manual, 2016**).

### 2.2 Class 2: Industrial Waste — Deep Analysis

Industrial waste is the most diverse and high-impact stream. It is sub-classified by **physical state** and **hazard potential**.

#### 2.2.1 Sub-Classification of Industrial Waste (By Physical State)

- **Solid Industrial Waste:** Fly ash, slag, red mud, kiln dust, sludge from ETP (Effluent Treatment Plant).
- **Liquid Industrial Waste (Effluents):** Process wastewater, spent solvents, acid/alkali liquors.
- **Gaseous Industrial Waste (Emissions):** SO₂, NOₓ, CO, Particulate Matter (PM), VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds).

#### 2.2.2 Sub-Classification by Hazard (The 17-Class System)

The **EPA / Indian Hazardous Waste Rules (1989, amended 2016)** categorize industrial waste into:
- **Class 1:** Ignitable
- **Class 2:** Corrosive (pH < 2 or > 12.5)
- **Class 3:** Reactive
- **Class 4:** Toxic (TCLP-leachable heavy metals)
- **Class 5–17:** Specific industrial categories (petroleum, pesticides, dyes, etc.)

### 2.3 Class 3: Agricultural Waste — Deep Analysis

Agricultural waste is the **largest by volume** globally (estimated at **~5 billion tonnes/year**), but the most under-utilized.

#### 2.3.1 Sub-Classification of Agricultural Waste

- **Crop Residues:** Straw (rice, wheat), stalks, husks, bagasse, cotton stalks.
- **Agro-Industrial By-products:** Molasses, oil cakes, fruit/vegetable pomace.
- **Livestock Waste:** Manure (dung), slurry, poultry litter.
- **Aquaculture & Fisheries Waste:** Fish processing offal.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the **high-yield formulas, metrics, and classification thresholds** required for solving KTU 2024 examination problems on this topic.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula or Definition** | **Standard Unit** | **Typical Range / Threshold** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Moisture Content (MC) | $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | % (wet basis) | MSW: 40–60%, Agri: 8–15% |
| Calorific Value (CV) | $CV = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{m_{waste}}$ | kJ/kg or kcal/kg | MSW: 1000–1500 kcal/kg, Agri: 3500–4500 kcal/kg |
| Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio | $C/N = \dfrac{\%C_{organic}}{\%N_{total}}$ | Dimensionless | Composting ideal: 25:1 to 30:1 |
| Bulk Density (BD) | $BD = \dfrac{m_{waste}}{V_{container}}$ | kg/m³ | Uncompacted MSW: 100–200 |
| Per Capita Waste Generation | $P = \dfrac{M_{total}}{N_{population} \times t}$ | kg/person/day | India: 0.5 kg, USA: 2.0 kg |
| Density Reduction Factor | $\rho_{reduced} = \rho_{loose} \times (1 - R_{compaction})$ | kg/m³ | Used for landfill volume design |
| pH Hazard Threshold | — | — | Corrosive if $pH \lt 2$ or $pH \gt 12.5$ |
| Ignitability Threshold | — | °C | Flash point $\lt 60$ °C (Class 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the **backbone of process design**. The Calorific Value equation directly determines whether a waste is suitable for **incineration** (CV > 1500 kcal/kg) or requires **co-firing with auxiliary fuel**. The C/N ratio dictates whether a feedstock can sustain aerobic composting or requires **co-composting** with nitrogen-rich amendments (e.g., poultry manure).

### 2.5 The 'How' — Connecting Classification to Process Engineering

In Process Systems Engineering, classification is the input to the **mass balance equation**:
$$M_{in} = M_{out} + M_{accumulated} + M_{reacted}$$

For a waste processing plant, $M_{in}$ is the sum of all classified streams:
$$M_{in} = M_{Municipal} + M_{Industrial} + M_{Agricultural}$$

Without accurate classification (knowing the **chemical composition** and **phase** of each stream), the **stoichiometry** and **reactor sizing** downstream will be erroneous.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Per Capita Waste Generation Rate

The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently tests the student's ability to **derive and apply** the per-capita waste generation equation, which is foundational for designing municipal collection systems.

#### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let:
- $M_{total}$ = Total mass of waste generated in a city in a given period
- $N_{population}$ = Total population of the city (served by the waste management system)
- $t$ = Time period of generation
- $P$ = Per capita waste generation rate (the desired quantity)

#### Step 2: State the Proportionality

By definition, the total mass of waste is **directly proportional** to both the population and the time of generation:
$$M_{total} \propto N_{population} \cdot t$$

#### Step 3: Introduce the Proportionality Constant

The constant of proportionality is the per-capita rate ($P$):
$$M_{total} = P \cdot N_{population} \cdot t$$

#### Step 4: Rearrange to Solve for $P$

$$P = \dfrac{M_{total}}{N_{population} \cdot t}$$

#### Step 5: Substitute Units for Verification

- $M_{total}$ in **kg**
- $N_{population}$ in **persons**
- $t$ in **days**

Therefore, $P$ carries the units:
$$[P] = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{person} \cdot \text{day}}$$

This is the standard **kg/capita/day** metric used by **CPHEEO** and the **World Bank**.

#### Step 6: Worked Numerical Evaluation

> **Problem:** A city with a population of **5,00,000** generates **250 tonnes** of MSW per day. Calculate the per-capita waste generation rate.

**Solution:**

$$M_{total} = 250 \text{ tonnes/day} = 250 \times 10^3 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$N_{population} = 5,00,000 \text{ persons}$$

$$t = 1 \text{ day}$$

Substituting into the derived formula:
$$P = \dfrac{250 \times 10^3 \text{ kg}}{5,00,000 \text{ persons} \times 1 \text{ day}}$$

$$P = \dfrac{2,50,000}{5,00,000} \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{person} \cdot \text{day}}$$

$$P = 0.5 \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{person} \cdot \text{day}}$$

This matches the standard Indian benchmark — confirming the derivation is dimensionally and numerically correct.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Moisture Content on Wet Basis

The Moisture Content formula is often incorrectly applied. The KTU board expects the rigorous derivation:

#### Step 1: Define Wet and Dry Masses

- $W_{wet}$ = Mass of waste as-collected (containing moisture)
- $W_{dry}$ = Mass of waste after oven-drying at **103 °C to 105 °C** (until constant mass, per **ASTM D2216**)

#### Step 2: Define the Mass of Moisture

The moisture mass is the difference:
$$M_{moisture} = W_{wet} - W_{dry}$$

#### Step 3: Express as a Percentage of Wet Mass

By definition, **moisture content on a wet basis** is:
$$MC = \dfrac{M_{moisture}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$$

#### Step 4: Final Algebraic Form

Substituting $M_{moisture}$:
$$MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$$

#### Step 5: Numerical Application

> **Problem:** A sample of food waste weighs **500 g** when fresh. After oven drying, it weighs **125 g**. Calculate the moisture content.

**Solution:**

$$W_{wet} = 500 \text{ g}$$

$$W_{dry} = 125 \text{ g}$$

$$MC = \dfrac{500 - 125}{500} \times 100$$

$$MC = \dfrac{375}{500} \times 100$$

$$MC = 75\%$$

This high moisture content (>55%) is typical of the **organic fraction of MSW** and explains why direct incineration of fresh food waste is **thermodynamically inefficient** — pre-drying or co-firing is required.

### 3.3 The Waste Classification Analytical Matrix

The following matrix is the **single most important analytical tool** for KTU 2024 exam answers on this topic. It is expected to be reproduced (from memory) in Part B answers.

| **Attribute** | **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | **Industrial Waste** | **Agricultural Waste** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Source** | Households, commercial, institutional | Manufacturing, processing, refining | Farms, livestock, agro-processing |
| **Typical Quantity (India)** | ~62 million tonnes/year (2016 data) | ~7.5 million tonnes hazardous/year | ~500 million tonnes crop residue/year |
| **Dominant Composition** | Organic (40–60%), Paper, Plastic | Heavy metals, VOCs, Acids, Sludges | Cellulose, Lignin, Hemicellulose |
| **Physical State** | Mostly solid; some liquid | Solid, liquid, gas | Mostly solid; some slurry |
| **Moisture Content (%)** | 40–60 (high) | Variable (5–95) | 8–15 (low, except manure) |
| **Calorific Value (kcal/kg)** | 1000–1500 (low) | Highly variable | 3500–4500 (high) |
| **C/N Ratio** | 30:1 – 80:1 (variable) | N/A (often toxic) | 60:1 – 100:1 (very high — needs N) |
| **Biodegradability** | Mixed (50% biodegradable) | Often non-biodegradable | Highly biodegradable |
| **Primary Treatment** | Landfill, composting, incineration | Physico-chemical, TSDF, incineration | Composting, biogas, pellets, biochar |
| **Key Environmental Concern** | Leachate, methane, plastic | Toxicity, heavy metals, BOD/COD | Open burning → air pollution |
| **Relevant Indian Rule** | MSW Rules, 2016 | Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016 | — (state-level) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

#### Question 1: Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List any four major components of MSW generated in an Indian city.

**Model Answer:**

Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) is defined as the solid waste material collected by the municipal authority from residential, commercial, institutional, and street-cleaning operations within a city or town. It is a heterogeneous mixture of biodegradable and non-biodegradable materials.

The four major components are:
1. **Organic/Biodegradable fraction** — food waste, yard waste (40–60%).
2. **Paper and cardboard** — newspapers, packaging (~8–12%).
3. **Plastics** — packaging, carry bags, PET bottles (~5–10%).
4. **Glass and metals** — bottles, cans, scrap (~5–8%).

> *(Word count: ~70 words — within the 3-mark answer length of 60–80 words.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### Question 2: Differentiate between Industrial Waste and Agricultural Waste based on (i) source, (ii) biodegradability, and (iii) typical treatment method.

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **Industrial Waste** | **Agricultural Waste** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Source | Manufacturing, refining, chemical processing | Farms, livestock, agro-processing |
| Biodegradability | Mostly non-biodegradable; often toxic | Highly biodegradable (cellulose/lignin) |
| Typical Treatment | Physico-chemical, TSDF, incineration | Composting, biogas, pelletization |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### Question A: Discuss in detail the various sources and types of Municipal Solid Waste. Explain its physical composition with typical Indian values. Describe any two primary treatment methods for MSW with their process principles.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sources and Types of MSW (7 Marks):**

Municipal Solid Waste originates from diverse sources within a municipal jurisdiction. The major sources are:
- **Residential/ Households:** Kitchen waste, garden waste, packaging, paper, e-waste, discarded textiles.
- **Commercial Establishments:** Offices, shops, restaurants, hotels — predominantly paper, food, and plastic.
- **Institutional Sources:** Schools, hospitals, government buildings.
- **Street Sweepings:** Dust, leaves, roadside litter.
- **Construction and Demolition (C&D) Waste:** Concrete, bricks, wood (handled separately under C&D Rules, 2016).
- **Biomedical Waste:** From hospitals and clinics (handled under BMW Rules, 2016).

**Types of MSW (by physical composition):**
- **Organic/Biodegradable:** Food, yard waste, paper, natural textiles.
- **Recyclable Inorganic:** Plastics, glass, metals, paper.
- **Inert Waste:** Construction debris, rubble.
- **Hazardous Domestic Waste:** Batteries, paints, pesticides, e-waste.

**Typical Indian Composition (CPHEEO, 2016):**
- Organic: 40–60%
- Recyclables: 15–25%
- Inerts: 15–20%
- Moisture: 40–55%

**(b) Primary Treatment Methods for MSW (7 Marks):**

**Method 1: Anaerobic Digestion (Biomethanation)**
- **Principle:** In the absence of oxygen, microorganisms (methanogens) decompose the organic fraction of MSW to produce **biogas** (≈ 60% CH₄, 40% CO₂) and **digestate** (a nutrient-rich slurry).
- **Process Steps:** Segregation → Shredding → Mixing with water (slurry preparation) → Digestion in sealed reactor (mesophilic: 35 °C / thermophilic: 55 °C; retention 20–40 days) → Gas collection → Sludge management.
- **Engineering Advantage:** Simultaneous waste stabilization, energy recovery, and volume reduction (up to 60%).

**Method 2: Composting (Aerobic Treatment)**
- **Principle:** Under aerobic conditions, microbes oxidize organic carbon to CO₂ and water, converting waste into **humus-like stable product (compost)**.
- **Process Steps:** Source segregation → Windrow formation (trapezoidal heaps, 2–3 m wide × 1.5 m high) → Periodic turning (every 3–7 days for aeration) → Moisture maintenance (50–60%) → C/N monitoring (target 25:1 – 30:1) → Maturation (4–8 weeks) → Sieving and packaging.
- **Engineering Advantage:** Produces a marketable soil conditioner; simple, low-capital technology suitable for Indian municipalities.

---

#### Question B: Classify Industrial Waste in detail. Explain the criteria for hazardous waste identification. Describe the process flow-sheet for the treatment of a typical heavy-metal-bearing industrial effluent.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Industrial Waste (7 Marks):**

Industrial waste is classified along three primary axes:

**1. By Physical State:**
- **Solid:** Slags, fly ash, kiln dust, ETP sludge, scrap metal, red mud (alumina industry).
- **Liquid (Effluents):** Spent process liquors, wash waters, acid/alkali streams, spent solvents.
- **Gaseous:** SO₂ from sulfuric acid plants; NOₓ from furnaces; CO from steel plants; VOCs from petrochemicals; Particulate Matter from cement kilns.

**2. By Source Industry:**
- **Chemical & Petrochemical:** Solvents, tars, spent catalysts.
- **Metallurgical:** Slag, sludge, pickling liquor.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Spent solvents, API residues.
- **Textile/Dye:** Colored effluents, high BOD/COD.

**3. By Hazard Category (EPA 17-Class System):**
- Ignitable, Corrosive, Reactive, Toxic, and 13 specific industrial categories.

**Criteria for Hazardous Waste Identification:**
- **Flammability:** Flash point < 60 °C.
- **Corrosivity:** pH < 2 or > 12.5.
- **Reactivity:** Unstable, reacts violently with water.
- **Toxicity (TCLP test):** Leachate contains heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr⁶⁺) above regulatory limits.
- **Listed Wastes:** Specific industrial process wastes (e.g., petroleum, pesticides) listed in Schedules.

**(b) Treatment Process for Heavy-Metal Industrial Effluent (7 Marks):**

**Process Flow-Sheet:**

1. **Equalization & Neutralization:** Effluent is collected in a holding tank; pH is adjusted to **8.0–9.0** using **Ca(OH)₂ or NaOH** to precipitate heavy metals as hydroxides.
2. **Coagulation-Flocculation:** Addition of **ferrous sulfate (FeSO₄)** or **alum** along with a polyelectrolyte causes fine precipitates to agglomerate into settleable flocs.
3. **Sedimentation/Clarification:** In a clarifier (residence time 2–4 hours), the metal hydroxide sludge settles; clarified supernatant is taken to tertiary treatment.
4. **Filtration:** Sand/anthracite filters remove residual suspended solids.
5. **Ion Exchange / Adsorption (Tertiary Polishing):** The polished stream is passed through **cation-exchange resins** or **activated carbon columns** to remove dissolved heavy metal ions to < 1 ppm.
6. **Sludge Dewatering & Stabilization:** The metal-rich sludge is dewatered in a **filter press** and stabilized (e.g., by cementation or vitrification) before secure disposal in a **TSDF**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss:**
> 1. **Skipping the formula or units:** In any question involving moisture content, calorific value, or per-capita generation, students often write the formula but forget the **units** (e.g., kg/person/day). KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly carries **1 mark for correct units** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing Hazardous Waste categories:** Writing "Class 1 = Toxic" instead of "Class 1 = Ignitable" is a common error. Memorize the **EPA 4-characteristic system** (Ignitable, Corrosive, Reactive, Toxic) before the exam.
> 3. **Omitting source segregation:** When discussing MSW treatment, students often describe the reactor but **forget pre-treatment** (segregation/shredding). Process flow-sheets must start from source.
> 4. **Using "waste" and "by-product" interchangeably:** Agricultural waste (e.g., bagasse) is often a **by-product** with economic value — KTU answers that call it "useless waste" lose marks. Use the term **residue** or **biomass resource** when appropriate.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**

- ✅ Waste classification is based on **three primary criteria**: Physical State, Source of Generation, and Hazard Potential.
- ✅ The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on three sources: **Municipal, Industrial, Agricultural**.
- ✅ **MSW** = heterogeneous; **Industrial** = often hazardous & concentrated; **Agricultural** = largest by volume, highly biodegradable.
- ✅ Indian per-capita MSW generation = **0.5 kg/person/day** (CPHEEO 2016).
- ✅ **Moisture content** is reported on a **wet basis** — remember the denominator is **W_wet**, not W_dry.
- ✅ **Calorific value** of MSW (~1000–1500 kcal/kg) is low; agricultural residues (~3500–4500 kcal/kg) are suitable for energy recovery.
- ✅ **C/N ratio** for ideal composting is **25:1 to 30:1**; agricultural residues are high-C and require nitrogen amendment.
- ✅ **Corrosive waste** threshold: **pH < 2 or > 12.5**.
- ✅ **Ignitable waste** threshold: **Flash point < 60 °C**.
- ✅ The 4 EPA hazard characteristics: **Ignitable, Corrosive, Reactive, Toxic (ICRT)**.
- ✅ Indian regulations to memorize: **MSW Rules 2016, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, BMW Rules 2016, C&D Waste Rules 2016**.
- ✅ **Process Engineering Link:** Classification → Characterization → Mass Balance → Treatment Train Selection.
- ✅ Industrial effluent treatment typical flow-sheet: **Equalization → Neutralization → Coagulation-Flocculation → Sedimentation → Filtration → Tertiary Polishing (Ion Exchange/Activated Carbon) → Sludge Stabilization**.
- ✅ MSW treatment routes: **Organic → Biomethanation/Composting; Recyclables → MRF; Inerts → Landfill; Hazardous → TSDF**.
- ✅ Agricultural waste options: **Composting, Biogas (digestion), Pelletization, Biochar, Second-generation bioethanol**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources and Characteristics of Waste Suitable for Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Sources and Characteristics of Waste Suitable for Energy Conversion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** refers to the process of generating energy—typically in the form of **electricity**, **heat**, or **transportation fuels (gaseous/liquid biofuels)**—from various forms of waste materials that would otherwise be discarded into landfills or the environment. In the context of Process Systems Engineering (PSE), this involves identifying, characterizing, and mathematically modeling waste streams as **feedstocks** for thermochemical, biochemical, or physico-chemical conversion processes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT867 – Module 1):** > *"Waste suitable for energy conversion refers to organic, industrial, agricultural, and municipal solid waste streams that possess sufficient calorific value, manageable moisture content, and a balanced carbon-to-nitrogen (C/N) ratio to undergo controlled conversion into useful forms of energy without causing severe environmental degradation."* The waste streams are broadly classified into the following categories (as per the KTU 2024 PECHT867 syllabus framework): | S.No. | Waste Category | Typical Examples | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Household refuse, food waste, paper, plastics | | 2 | **Industrial Waste** | Chemical sludges, refinery residues, bagasse | | 3 | **Agricultural Waste** | Rice straw, wheat husk, coconut shells, bagasse | | 4 | **Bio-medical / Hazardous Waste** | Pathological waste, pharmaceutical residues | | 5 | **Electronic Waste (E-Waste)** | Circuit boards, plastic housings (limited thermal value) | | 6 | **Sewage Sludge / Wastewater** | Activated sludge, biogas digestate | | 7 | **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste** | Wood, insulation, drywall | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Bank Account of Carbon":** > Imagine a large municipal landfill as a **bank account** that has been passively collecting **carbon deposits (organic matter)** for decades. The waste dumped daily is essentially **deposited money** (chemical potential energy locked in C–H and C–C bonds). Waste-to-energy technology acts like a **financial advisor** that withdraws this energy safely and converts it into **usable currency**—electricity, heat, or biofuel—instead of letting it rot, emit methane, or pollute groundwater. > > For this "withdrawal" to be profitable (i.e., energetically and economically viable), the **waste must have certain properties**—just like a cheque must have a valid signature, a date, and sufficient funds. These "properties" are what we call the **characteristics of waste**: moisture, calorific value, ash content, composition, and reactivity. ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Process Systems Engineering Process engineers do not simply "burn waste." They must: 1. **Identify** which waste streams are *thermodynamically* and *kinetically* suitable. 2. **Quantify** the energy potential using rigorous analytical methods (proximate/ultimate analysis). 3. **Design** reactors, heat exchangers, gasifiers, digesters, and burners that handle the *specific* characteristics of that waste. 4. **Optimize** the system to maximize energy recovery while minimizing pollutant emissions (NOx, SOx, dioxins, fly ash). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "5-C Rule" of Waste Characterization (Exam-Favorite Framework):** > Every waste stream suitable for energy conversion must be analyzed along **five dimensions**: > **C1 – Composition**, **C2 – Calorific Value**, **C3 – Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio (C/N)**, **C4 – Consistency (Moisture & Ash)**, **C5 – Contaminants (Chlorine, Sulfur, Heavy Metals)**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown The characterization of waste for energy conversion is structured into **three primary analytical frameworks** and **one thermodynamic feasibility check**. ### Step 1: Physical Characterization This quantifies the *physical nature* of the waste, which dictates the type of conversion technology. - **Moisture Content (M, %wb)**: Mass of water per unit mass of wet waste. Governs drying energy requirement. - **Bulk Density (ρ_b, kg/m³)**: Mass per unit volume as collected. Dictates reactor/feeder sizing. - **Particle Size Distribution**: Affects combustion kinetics and gas–solid contacting. - **Ash Content (A, %db)**: Non-combustible inorganic residue. Reduces calorific value and causes slagging. ### Step 2: Chemical Characterization (Proximate & Ultimate Analysis) This is the **core of waste characterization** and is a high-frequency exam topic. **Proximate Analysis** (ASTM D7582 / IS 1350): - **Moisture (M)** - **Volatile Matter (VM)** – drives combustion/gasification reactivity. - **Ash Content (A)** - **Fixed Carbon (FC)** – solid combustible residue after devolatilization. Constraint: $M + VM + A + FC = 100\%$ **Ultimate Analysis** (CHNS-O): - **Carbon (C)** - **Hydrogen (H)** - **Nitrogen (N)** - **Sulfur (S)** - **Oxygen (O)** (by difference) - **Heating Value (HHV/LHV)** in **MJ/kg** or **kcal/kg**. ### Step 3: Biological Characterization Relevant for **anaerobic digestion** and **bioethanol fermentation**. - **C/N Ratio (Carbon to Nitrogen mass ratio)**: The most critical biochemical indicator. - **Biodegradability Index (BI)**: Ratio of biodegradable to total organic carbon. - **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)**: Indicates biochemical energy potential. ### Step 4: Thermodynamic Feasibility Check For combustion, the **adiabatic flame temperature (AFT)** must exceed **~1000 °C** for self-sustaining combustion; otherwise, auxiliary fuel is required. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table – Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Moisture Content (wet basis)** | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet}} \times 100$ | % | Energy lost to evaporation | | 2 | **Moisture Content (dry basis)** | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Used in energy balance | | 3 | **Proximate Sum Check** | $M + VM + A + FC = 100$ | % | Mass balance closure | | 4 | **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** | $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ | MJ/kg | Dulong-type equation | | 5 | **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** | $LHV = HHV - h_{fg} \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100}\right)$ | MJ/kg | Actual usable energy | | 6 | **C/N Ratio** | $(C/N) = \dfrac{\text{mass \% of C}}{\text{mass \% of N}}$ | dimensionless | Optimum range: **20–30** for AD | | 7 | **Stoichiometric Air (combustion)** | $A_{st} = \dfrac{8C + 8H + S - O}{0.232 \cdot 100}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Theoretical O₂ demand | | 8 | **Excess Air Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{A_{actual}}{A_{st}}$ | dimensionless | Typically **1.2 – 1.5** for WtE | | 9 | **Energy Density (Volumetric)** | $E_v = HHV \times \rho_b$ | MJ/m³ | Reactor sizing metric | | 10 | **Methane Yield (Buswell Eq.)** | $C_nH_aO_bN_c \rightarrow$ CH₄ + CO₂ + NH₃ + H₂O | mol/mol | Anaerobic digestion | > **Standard Thermodynamic Constants (Use Boldly in KTU Answers):** > - Latent heat of vaporization of water: **h_fg = 2.442 MJ/kg** (at 25 °C) > - Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of C: **2.667 kg O₂/kg C** > - Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of H₂: **8.0 kg O₂/kg H₂** > - Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of S: **1.0 kg O₂/kg S** ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of This System | Sector | Application | | :--- | :--- | | **Power Generation** | Municipal incinerators, RDF-fired boilers, biomass IPPs | | **Transportation Fuels** | Biogas (CBG), biodiesel from waste cooking oil, ethanol from MSW | | **Industrial Process Heat** | Bagasse-fired boilers in sugar mills, black liquor recovery | | **District Heating** | Combined Heat & Power (CHP) from WtE plants (Copenhagen model) | | **Hydrogen Production** | Biomass gasification + water-gas shift, dark fermentation | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Ultimate Analysis to Heating Value (Dulong Equation) We begin with the **Dulong-Berthelot equation**, a cornerstone of solid-fuel thermochemistry. The derivation proceeds by summing the heat released from the complete combustion of each combustible element in 1 kg of dry waste. ### Step 1: Set Up the Combustion Reactions (per kg of dry waste) For each element $i$ in the fuel, complete combustion proceeds as: $$ C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H_{C} = -32.79 \text{ MJ/kg C} $$ $$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H_{H} = -141.80 \text{ MJ/kg H} $$ $$ S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Delta H_{S} = -9.30 \text{ MJ/kg S} $$ ### Step 2: Express Each Term in Mass Percent Let $C, H, S, O, N, A$ denote the **mass percentages** of carbon, hydrogen, sulfur, oxygen, nitrogen, and ash on a **dry basis** (so $C + H + S + O + N + A = 100$). For 1 kg of dry fuel, the mass of carbon = $C/100$ kg. The heat released by carbon alone is therefore: $$ q_C = 32.79 \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} \text{ MJ/kg of dry fuel} $$ Similarly: $$ q_H = 141.80 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} \text{ MJ/kg of dry fuel} $$ $$ q_S = 9.30 \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} \text{ MJ/kg of dry fuel} $$ ### Step 3: Subtract Energy Required to Vaporize Inherent Moisture and Bound Hydrogen Water When hydrogen burns, it forms water. To condense this water to liquid (the HHV convention), all of it must be released as latent heat. The total water produced is: - From the **hydrogen** content: $\dfrac{9H}{100}$ kg H₂O per kg fuel (since 1 kg H → 9 kg H₂O). - From the **inherent moisture** $M$ in the fuel: $\dfrac{M}{100}$ kg H₂O per kg fuel. Total water to condense: $\dfrac{9H + M}{100}$ kg per kg fuel. Latent heat consumed: $$ q_{vap} = 2.442 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) \text{ MJ/kg of dry fuel} $$ ### Step 4: Subtract the Energy to Liberate the Bound Oxygen A fraction of the oxygen in the fuel is already chemically bound to carbon and hydrogen. The heat credit for this "free oxygen" is approximated as: $$ q_{O} = 0.1034 \cdot \dfrac{O}{100} \text{ MJ/kg of dry fuel} $$ Similarly, the energy associated with the formation of nitrogen oxides is small but conventionally subtracted: $$ q_{N} = 0.0151 \cdot \dfrac{N}{100} \text{ MJ/kg of dry fuel} $$ ### Step 5: Assemble the HHV Equation $$ \begin{aligned} HHV &= 32.79 \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} + 141.80 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} + 9.30 \cdot \dfrac{S}{100} \\ &\quad - 2.442 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) - 0.1034 \cdot \dfrac{O}{100} - 0.0151 \cdot \dfrac{N}{100} \end{aligned} $$ Multiplying out and grouping the coefficients into the standard form used in KTU exam questions: $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} HHV \; [MJ/kg] =\; & 0.3279\,C + 1.418\,H + 0.093\,S \\ & - 0.0244\,(9H + M) - 0.001034\,O - 0.000151\,N \end{aligned} } $$ > [!NOTE] > **Simplified Dulong Form (Most Common in KTU 2024 Question Papers):** > $$HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$$ > This version uses lumped empirical coefficients and is what you should write in the exam unless specifically asked to derive the full thermodynamic form. ### Step 6: Conversion to LHV Once HHV is known, the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** is obtained by removing the latent heat of the water vapor leaving the combustion chamber: $$ LHV = HHV - 2.442 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9H + M}{100}\right) \text{ MJ/kg} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Waste Streams vs. Suitable Energy Conversion Technology | Waste Stream | Moisture (%) | HHV (MJ/kg) | C/N Ratio | Ash (%) | Best-Fit Technology | Reason | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **Food Waste** | 60 – 80 | 3 – 5 | 15 – 20 | 5 – 10 | Anaerobic Digestion | High moisture, low C/N, biodegradable | | **Rice Straw** | 8 – 15 | 13 – 15 | 60 – 80 | 15 – 20 | Combustion / Gasification | High VM, low moisture | | **Bagasse** | 45 – 55 | 8 – 10 | 80 – 100 | 2 – 5 | Co-firing in Sugar Mill Boilers | Bulk availability, moderate HHV | | **MSW (Mixed)** | 30 – 50 | 7 – 12 | 30 – 60 | 20 – 40 | Incineration + RDF | Heterogeneous composition | | **Sewage Sludge** | 70 – 85 | 1 – 3 | 8 – 12 | 25 – 40 | Anaerobic Digestion + Drying | Very high moisture | | **Used Tyres** | 1 – 3 | 30 – 35 | 50 – 70 | 5 – 10 | Pyrolysis / Cement Kiln Co-firing | High HHV, low moisture | | **Wood Waste** | 10 – 25 | 14 – 18 | 400 – 600 | 1 – 3 | Direct Combustion | High C/N, low ash | | **Plastic (Mixed)** | < 1 | 35 – 45 | ∞ (no N) | 5 – 10 | Co-processing in Cement Kilns | Very high HHV, no biogenic C | ## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (Typical 7-Mark Question Style):** > A sample of dried **rice straw** has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): > C = 41.0, H = 5.2, N = 0.8, S = 0.1, O = 38.9, Ash = 14.0, Moisture (incoming air-dry sample) = 10.0 > > **Calculate (a) HHV and (b) LHV of the sample as received.** **Solution:** **Part (a) – HHV (dry basis):** $$ \begin{aligned} HHV_{db} &= 0.3491(41.0) + 1.1783(5.2) + 0.1005(0.1) \\ &\quad - 0.1034(38.9) - 0.0151(0.8) - 0.0211(14.0) \\ &= 14.31 + 6.13 + 0.01 - 4.02 - 0.012 - 0.30 \\ &\approx 16.12 \text{ MJ/kg (dry basis)} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (b) – LHV (as-received basis):** First, convert HHV to wet basis: $$ HHV_{ar} = HHV_{db} \cdot (1 - 0.10) = 16.12 \times 0.90 = 14.51 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ Now compute the LHV: $$ LHV_{ar} = HHV_{ar} - 2.442 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9 \cdot 5.2 + 10.0}{100}\right) \cdot (1 - 0.10) $$ Wait — careful. The hydrogen and moisture values here are on the **dry basis for H** and the **as-received basis for M**. The correct approach: use the *as-received* values of H and M, both equal to $H_{db} \cdot (1-M_{ar})$ and $M_{ar}$ respectively. With $M_{ar} = 10\%$: $$ LHV_{ar} = 14.51 - 2.442 \cdot \left(\dfrac{9(5.2)(0.90) + 10.0}{100}\right) = 14.51 - 0.327 \approx 14.18 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ **Final Answer:** HHV = **16.12 MJ/kg (db)**, LHV = **14.18 MJ/kg (ar)**. ✓ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification of Waste Streams for Energy Conversion ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Waste Sources for Energy Conversion] --> B[Biogenic Wastes] A --> C[Non-Biogenic Wastes] A --> D[Industrial Wastes] B --> B1[Agricultural Residues] B --> B2[Forestry and Wood Waste] B --> B3[Municipal Solid Waste MSW] B --> B4[Food and Kitchen Waste] B --> B5[Sewage and Animal Slurry] C --> C1[Plastic Waste] C --> C2[Used Tyres and Rubber] C --> C3[Electronic Waste E-Waste] C --> C4[Construction and Demolition Debris] D --> D1[Chemical Industry Sludge] D --> D2[Petroleum Refinery Residues] D --> D3[Pulp and Paper Mill Liquor] D --> D4[Pharmaceutical and Hazardous Waste] B1 --> R1[Conversion Route Selection] B3 --> R1 D1 --> R1 C1 --> R1 R1 --> R2[Thermochemical Combustion Gasification Pyrolysis] R1 --> R3[Biochemical Anaerobic Digestion Fermentation] R1 --> R4[Physicochemical Transesterification Pelletization] R2 --> O[Energy Output: Electricity Heat Biofuels] R3 --> O R4 --> O ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow: From Waste Characterization to Energy Conversion Selection ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Step1: Waste Sampling and Collection] --> T[Step2: Physical Characterization
Moisture Bulk Density Particle Size] T --> U[Step3: Proximate Analysis
M VM Ash FC] U --> V[Step4: Ultimate Analysis
C H N S O HHV] V --> W[Step5: Biological Tests
C/N Ratio BI TOC] W --> X[Step6: Decision Matrix
Match Waste Profile to Technology] X --> Y[Step7: Energy Conversion Process] Y --> Z[Step8: Energy Output and Emission Control] style S fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style T fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style U fill:#ffc988,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style V fill:#ffb656,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style W fill:#ffa033,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style X fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Y fill:#e67e22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style Z fill:#d35400,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Decision Logic for WtE Technology ```mermaid flowchart TD I[Incoming Waste Stream] --> P1{Is Moisture Content above 60 percent?} P1 -- YES --> P2[Route: Biochemical Pathway] P1 -- NO --> P3{Is Ash Content below 20 percent and HHV above 12 MJ/kg?} P3 -- YES --> P4[Route: Direct Thermochemical Combustion] P3 -- NO --> P5{Is Volatile Matter above 70 percent?} P5 -- YES --> P6[Route: Gasification or Pyrolysis] P5 -- NO --> P7[Route: Co-processing in Cement Kiln or Industrial Boiler] P2 --> A1[Anaerobic Digester or Fermenter] P4 --> A2[Fluidized Bed Combustor or Grate Furnace] P6 --> A3[Downdraft Gasifier or Pyrolysis Reactor] P7 --> A4[Rotary Kiln Cement Co-firing] A1 --> OUT[Energy Vector: Biogas or Bioethanol] A2 --> OUT A3 --> OUT A4 --> OUT style I fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px style P1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style P3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style OUT fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Waste Characterization → Reactor Type Mapping ```mermaid graph LR W[Waste Stream] --> A[Characteristic A: Moisture] W --> B[Characteristic B: Volatile Matter] W --> C[Characteristic C: HHV] W --> D[Characteristic D: C over N Ratio] W --> E[Characteristic E: Ash Content] A --> M{Threshold Evaluation} B --> M C --> M D --> M E --> M M --> R1[Reactor Type 1: Grate Furnace] M --> R2[Reactor Type 2: Fluidized Bed Gasifier] M --> R3[Reactor Type 3: Anaerobic Digester] M --> R4[Reactor Type 4: Pyrolysis Reactor] M --> R5[Reactor Type 5: Biodiesel Transesterification Unit] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define proximate and ultimate analyses of solid waste. List any four parameters determined in each.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Proximate Analysis** determines the **physical and thermal fractions** of a solid waste sample on a percentage basis. It includes: > 1. **Moisture Content (M)** – mass loss on heating at 105 °C. > 2. **Volatile Matter (VM)** – mass loss on heating at 950 °C in absence of air. > 3. **Ash Content (A)** – non-combustible residue after complete combustion. > 4. **Fixed Carbon (FC)** – combustible residue remaining after removal of M, VM, and A. > > **Ultimate Analysis** determines the **elemental chemical composition** of the waste: > 1. **Carbon (C)** and **Hydrogen (H)** – by CHN analyzer. > 2. **Nitrogen (N)** – by Kjeldahl or Dumas method. > 3. **Sulfur (S)** – by Eschka method. > 4. **Oxygen (O)** – by difference ($100 - C - H - N - S - Ash$). > > **Significance:** Proximate analysis gives **combustion behavior**; ultimate analysis gives **stoichiometric air** and **calorific value** via Dulong's equation. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the significance of the C/N ratio in selecting an anaerobic digestion feedstock.** > [!NOTE] > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > The **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) ratio** is the mass ratio of total carbon to total nitrogen in the organic feedstock. It is a **critical design parameter** for anaerobic digestion (AD) because: > > 1. **Microbial nutrition balance:** Methanogenic archaea require carbon for energy and nitrogen for cell synthesis. The **optimum C/N range is 20–30** for stable AD. > 2. **C/N too high (> 35):** Nitrogen becomes limiting → microbial growth is slow → volatile fatty acid (VFA) accumulation → digester "souring." > 3. **C/N too low (< 15):** Excess nitrogen → ammonia toxicity → inhibition of methanogens → reduced CH₄ yield. > 4. **Practical use:** Waste streams with low C/N (e.g., food waste ≈ 15) are **co-digested** with high C/N wastes (e.g., rice straw ≈ 70) to achieve balanced digestion. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** Always state which value is being calculated. HHV assumes water condenses to liquid; LHV assumes water leaves as vapor (this is the **actual boiler condition**). > 2. **Forgetting dry-basis conversion:** If moisture content is given, convert carefully between dry basis (db) and as-received (ar) before plugging into Dulong's equation. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. > 3. **Unit mismatch:** Dulong's equation requires **mass percentages**, not mass fractions. A 1% error in conversion (e.g., 0.41 instead of 41) will lead to a **wrong answer by a factor of 100**. > 4. **Proximate analysis constraint:** Students often forget the closure equation $M + VM + A + FC = 100$ and leave FC unaccounted. > 5. **Ignoring ash contribution to slagging:** Always mention that high ash content (especially with K, Na, Cl) causes slagging and fouling in boilers — a 2-mark "engineering insight" question that examiners love. --- ### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **various sources of waste** suitable for energy conversion. Classify them into **biogenic**, **non-biogenic**, and **industrial** categories with at least **two examples** in each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **biomass sample** of sawdust has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis): C = 47.5, H = 6.2, O = 40.0, N = 0.3, S = 0.1, Ash = 5.9. The as-received moisture content is **8%**. **Calculate**: - (i) The **HHV** of the sample on a dry basis using the simplified Dulong equation. - (ii) The **LHV** of the sample on an as-received basis. - (iii) The **stoichiometric air required** per kg of fuel on a dry basis. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) – Sources of Waste (7 marks):** Waste suitable for energy conversion is broadly classified into: 1. **Biogenic Wastes** (carbon of recent biological origin): - *Agricultural residues:* Rice straw, wheat husk, maize stover, sugarcane bagasse. - *Forestry and wood waste:* Sawdust, bark, wood chips, logging residues. - *Municipal organic fraction:* Food waste, garden waste, paper, cardboard. - *Animal and human waste:* Cow dung, poultry litter, sewage sludge. 2. **Non-Biogenic Wastes** (fossil-based or synthetic): - *Plastics:* Polyethylene, polypropylene, PET, mixed plastic fraction of MSW. - *Rubber products:* Used tyres, conveyor belts, gaskets. - *Electronic waste (e-waste):* PCB laminates, plastic housings, capacitor films. - *Construction and demolition (C&D) waste:* Wood, insulation foam, vinyl flooring. 3. **Industrial Wastes** (process-specific by-products): - *Chemical industry sludge, *Petroleum refinery residues (e.g., pet-coke, asphalt), *Pulp and paper mill black liquor, *Pharmaceutical off-spec products. [A neat **flow diagram** showing the three categories with sub-branches should be drawn — refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the reference structure. **Marks: 2 (diagram) + 3 (classification) + 2 (examples)**.] **Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) HHV (dry basis):** Using the simplified Dulong equation: $HHV = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ $$ \begin{aligned} HHV_{db} &= 0.3491(47.5) + 1.1783(6.2) + 0.1005(0.1) \\ &\quad - 0.1034(40.0) - 0.0151(0.3) - 0.0211(5.9) \\ &= 16.582 + 7.305 + 0.010 - 4.136 - 0.005 - 0.124 \\ &= 19.63 \text{ MJ/kg (dry basis)} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) LHV (as-received basis):** Step 1: Convert HHV to as-received basis: $$ HHV_{ar} = HHV_{db} \times (1 - M_{ar}) = 19.63 \times (1 - 0.08) = 19.63 \times 0.92 = 18.06 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ Step 2: Subtract the latent heat of water vapor (from H and M on as-received basis): The hydrogen on as-received basis is $H_{ar} = 6.2 \times 0.92 = 5.704\%$. The moisture is $M_{ar} = 8.0\%$. $$ LHV_{ar} = HHV_{ar} - 2.442 \times \left(\dfrac{9 \times 5.704 + 8.0}{100}\right) = 18.06 - 2.442 \times (0.5934) = 18.06 - 1.45 = 16.61 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ **(iii) Stoichiometric Air (dry basis):** For complete combustion, the oxygen demand per kg of dry fuel is: $$ O_{2,st} = \dfrac{\tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O}{100} \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} O_{2,st} &= \dfrac{\tfrac{8}{3}(47.5) + 8(6.2) + 0.1 - 40.0}{100} \\ &= \dfrac{126.67 + 49.6 + 0.1 - 40.0}{100} = \dfrac{136.37}{100} = 1.364 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ Since air contains 23.2% O₂ by mass: $$ A_{st} = \dfrac{1.364}{0.232} = 5.88 \text{ kg air/kg fuel (dry basis)} $$ **Final Answer:** - (i) **HHV = 19.63 MJ/kg (db)** - (ii) **LHV = 16.61 MJ/kg (ar)** - (iii) **Stoichiometric air = 5.88 kg/kg of dry fuel** --- ### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Discuss the **physical and chemical characteristics** of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) that determine its suitability for **incineration-based energy recovery**. What are the **typical threshold values** of moisture, calorific value, and ash content for **self-sustaining combustion** in a municipal incinerator? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define the term **Calorific Value**. With the help of a stepwise derivation, **derive the simplified Dulong's equation** for estimating the Higher Heating Value (HHV) of a solid waste from its ultimate analysis. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) – MSW Characteristics for Incineration (7 marks):** The suitability of MSW for incineration is determined by the following key characteristics: | Parameter | Typical MSW Value | Threshold for Self-Sustaining Combustion | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | **Moisture (M, %wb)** | 30 – 55% | < **50%** (preferably < 40%) | | **HHV (MJ/kg, as-received)** | 7 – 12 MJ/kg | > **7 MJ/kg** (~ 1700 kcal/kg) | | **Ash Content (%db)** | 20 – 40% | < **40%** (preferably < 25%) | | **Volatile Matter (%db)** | 50 – 70% | High VM = easy ignition | | **Fixed Carbon (%db)** | 5 – 15% | Provides char burnout | | **C/N Ratio** | 30 – 60 | Less critical for combustion | | **Chlorine (%, db)** | 0.5 – 1.5% | < 1% preferred (dioxin control) | | **Bulk Density (kg/m³)** | 150 – 300 | Dictates grate area | **Self-Sustaining Combustion Criteria:** 1. **HHV > 7 MJ/kg** on as-received basis. 2. **Moisture < 50%** (so latent heat loss < 50% of HHV). 3. **Three-Times Rule (T rule):** $\dfrac{HHV \times 100}{M + 9H + A} \geq 3$ for grate furnaces (Ryu, 2007). 4. The **adiabatic flame temperature** must exceed 1000 °C. 5. The waste must ignite without auxiliary fuel (after initial start-up). **If HHV < 7 MJ/kg:** **RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel)** pellets must be manufactured by removing inert and wet fractions, then auxiliary fuel (e.g., coal) is co-fired. **Part (b) – Derivation of Simplified Dulong's Equation (7 marks):** *Refer to the full stepwise derivation provided in Section 3.1 of these notes.* **Assumptions:** 1. Complete combustion of C → CO₂, H → H₂O(l), S → SO₂. 2. The calorific value contributions are additive and independent (no interaction terms). 3. The energy required to liberate bound oxygen in the fuel is approximated as $0.1034 \times O/100$ MJ/kg. 4. Nitrogen contributes a small endothermic correction (NOx formation) of $0.0151 \times N/100$ MJ/kg. 5. Ash acts as a thermal sink (latent + sensible heat loss) approximated as $0.0211 \times A/100$ MJ/kg. 6. Moisture in the fuel is evaporated and the water leaves as vapor (so its latent heat reduces LHV; for HHV, all water is condensed). **Final Dulong's Equation:** $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} HHV \;[MJ/kg] = \; & 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S \\ & - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A \end{aligned} } $$ **Validity:** This equation is accurate within ±5% for most solid fuels with HHV in the range 5 – 30 MJ/kg. For high-ash or high-moisture wastes (e.g., MSW, sludge), accuracy drops, and the **Channiwala–Parikh correlation** or direct bomb calorimetry is preferred. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE – Module 1** - [ ] **Three primary waste categories** for WtE: **Biogenic, Non-Biogenic, Industrial** — know 2 examples in each. - [ ] **Proximate analysis** = M + VM + A + FC = 100% (mass balance closure). - [ ] **Ultimate analysis** = C + H + N + S + O + Ash = 100% (with O by difference). - [ ] **HHV vs LHV:** HHV assumes H₂O condenses (latent recovered); LHV assumes H₂O leaves as vapor (industrial reality). - [ ] **Dulong's equation coefficients to memorize:** 0.3491(C), 1.1783(H), 0.1005(S), −0.1034(O), −0.0151(N), −0.0211(A). - [ ] **Latent heat of vaporization of water** at 25 °C = **2.442 MJ/kg** — used for HHV↔LHV conversion. - [ ] **C/N ratio for AD:** Optimum **20–30**; > 35 → slow digestion; < 15 → ammonia toxicity. - [ ] **MSW self-sustaining combustion threshold:** HHV > **7 MJ/kg** (as-received), Moisture < **50%**. - [ ] **Three-Times Rule (Ryu, 2007):** $100 \times HHV / (M + 9H + A) \geq 3$ for grate furnace auto-thermal operation. - [ ] **Stoichiometric O₂ demand:** $\tfrac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ (in kg O₂ per 100 kg fuel); air = O₂/0.232. - [ ] **Typical HHV ranges (remember these!):** - Wood/biomass: 14 – 18 MJ/kg - MSW: 7 – 12 MJ/kg - Sewage sludge: 1 – 3 MJ/kg (hence dewatering is essential) - Plastics: 35 – 45 MJ/kg - Used tyres: 30 – 35 MJ/kg - Bagasse: 8 – 10 MJ/kg - [ ] **Excess air ratio (λ) for WtE plants:** Typically **1.2 – 1.5** (low values to maintain temperature, but high enough to control CO). - [ ] **Selection rule of thumb:** High moisture + biodegradable → AD; Low moisture + high HHV → Combustion; High VM + low ash → Gasification; High lipid content → Biodiesel; Plastic/rubber → Co-processing in cement kilns. - [ ] **Environmental constraints** (often asked in 1-mark questions): Chlorine > 1% → dioxin/furan risk; Sulfur > 0.5% → SO₂ scrubber needed; Heavy metals → fly ash classified as hazardous. - [ ] **Circular Economy Linkage:** WtE is the **"R3 – Energy Recovery"** step in the waste hierarchy: **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Landfill (last resort)**. - [ ] **KTU 2024 Hot-Button Topic:** Conversion of **Sewage Sludge to Biogas** via anaerobic digestion (Palakkad Smart City project reference, if asked). - [ ] **Don't confuse "energy content" with "energy recovered"** — efficiency of conversion (η) typically: Incineration 20–30%, Gasification 60–75%, AD 30–40%, Pyrolysis 70–80% (mass-to-energy). - [ ] **Formula to remember on fingertips:** $A_{st} = \dfrac{8C + 8H + S - O}{23.2}$ in kg air per kg fuel (the **23.2** is mass % of O₂ in air, NOT 21 — that is the volumetric %).

topics.title: Waste Collection, Segregation, and Preprocessing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Waste Collection, Segregation, and Preprocessing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Waste Collection, Segregation, and Preprocessing Methods* collectively refer to the **front-end unit operations of any integrated Solid Waste Management (SWM) or Industrial Effluent Management System**. They constitute the engineered sequence by which heterogeneous waste streams are gathered from point-of-generation, classified into homogeneous fractions (organic, recyclable, inert, hazardous), and conditioned through physical/mechanical unit operations to yield a feed of consistent composition, moisture, calorific value, and particle size suitable for downstream treatment, energy recovery, or final disposal.

In **Process Systems Engineering (PSE)** terms, these steps form the **feed preparation train** of the overall waste-to-resource process flowsheet. They directly govern the mass balance closure, energy integration opportunities, and economic viability of subsequent processing units (e.g., incinerators, composting tunnels, anaerobic digesters, pyrolysis reactors, or Material Recovery Facilities – MRFs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen to Plate" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a chef preparing a complex multi-dish meal. Before cooking begins, the chef must:
> 1. **Collect** ingredients from the refrigerator/pantry (raw waste aggregation).
> 2. **Segregate** them — vegetables in one bowl, spices in another, dairy separately (source separation).
> 3. **Preprocess** — wash, peel, chop, grind, sieve (size reduction & classification).
>
> Skipping or doing this poorly produces a bad dish. Similarly, sloppy waste preprocessing produces **non-uniform feed**, leading to **incomplete combustion, reactor fouling, poor biogas yield, leachate issues**, and ultimately **process failure**. The "recipe" (downstream process) is only as good as the "mise-en-place" (preprocessing).

## 1.3 Classification of Waste Streams Relevant to PSE

| S.No. | Waste Category | Typical Examples | KTU-Relevant Processing Consideration |
|:-----:|----------------|------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Kitchen waste, paper, plastics, glass | High moisture (40–60%), low calorific value (~1000 kcal/kg) as collected |
| 2 | **Industrial (Process) Waste** | Sludges, spent catalysts, off-spec products | Often hazardous, requires MSDS-based handling |
| 3 | **Hazardous Waste** | Heavy metals, PCBs, halogenated organics | Subject to Hazardous Waste Management Rules |
| 4 | **Biomedical Waste (BMW)** | Pathological, sharps, infectious | Mandatory autoclaving/incineration pre-processing |
| 5 | **E-Waste** | PCBs, batteries, screens | Manual dismantling + hydrometallurgical processing |
| 6 | **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** | Concrete, wood, metals | High inert fraction, low biodegradability |

## 1.4 The PSE Block-Flow Perspective

From a **process flowsheet viewpoint**, waste preprocessing constitutes a **multi-input / multi-output (MIMO) separation network** with the following generic structure:

$$\text{Heterogeneous Waste (Feed)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Collection}}\; \text{Aggregated Stream} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Segregation}}\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Homogeneous Fraction}_i \;\xrightarrow{\text{Preprocessing}}\; \text{Process-Ready Feed}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **Moisture Content (MC):** Typically **20–60%** for fresh MSW
> - **Bulk Density:** **200–500 kg/m³** (uncompacted MSW)
> - **Calorific Value (CV):** **800–2000 kcal/kg** for Indian MSW
> - **C/N Ratio:** **25–40** for raw MSW (ideal for composting: **25–30**)
> - **Collection Efficiency (η_c):** **70–90%** for door-to-door systems
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Waste Collection — Sub-Systems and Engineering Analysis

Waste collection is the **logistically intensive, capex-heavy** part of any SWM system. In PSE design, it is modeled as a **vehicle routing problem (VRP)** with stochastic demand.

### 2.1.1 Modes of Collection

1. **Door-to-Door / Curbside Collection**
   - Highest segregation efficiency (**80–95%**).
   - Requires dedicated workforce and small vehicles (e.g., auto-tipper, dumper placer).
   - Bin size: typically **50–120 L** HDPE bins per household.

2. **Community Bin / Stationary Container System**
   - Bins of **1.1 m³ (1100 L)** placed at fixed locations.
   - Collection frequency: **daily to twice-weekly** depending on waste generation rate.

3. **Transfer Station (TS) Based System**
   - Used in **metropolitan/mega-city** contexts.
   - Small collection vehicles → Transfer Station → Large bulk carriers (10–25 tonne capacity).
   - Reduces transportation cost by **30–45%**.

4. **Bring Centers / Drop-off Centers**
   - For recyclables (paper, glass, e-waste).
   - Public participation dependent.

### 2.1.2 Mass Balance for Collection System

$$\dot{m}_{\text{collected}} = \dot{m}_{\text{generated}} \times \eta_c \times (1 - f_{\text{lost}})$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{\text{collected}}$ = mass flow rate of waste actually reaching the processing plant (kg/day)
- $\dot{m}_{\text{generated}}$ = total waste generated (kg/day)
- $\eta_c$ = **collection efficiency** (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $f_{\text{lost}}$ = fraction lost to scavenging, open dumping, or unaccounted flow

## 2.2 Waste Segregation — Principle of Separation

Segregation leverages **differences in physical, chemical, or surface properties** among waste components.

### 2.2.1 Levels of Segregation

| Level | Location | Tools Used | Purity Achieved |
|:-----:|----------|------------|:---------------:|
| **Primary** | Source (household/industry) | Two/three-bin system (wet/dry/hazardous) | **85–95%** |
| **Secondary** | Community/Transfer Station | Manual picking lines | **75–85%** |
| **Tertiary** | MRF (Material Recovery Facility) | Trommel, air classifier, magnetic separator, eddy current | **90–99%** |

### 2.2.2 The Property-Driven Separation Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Each unit operation in preprocessing exploits a **specific property contrast** between waste fractions. Exam questions frequently test this mapping.

| Property Exploited | Unit Operation | Separates |
|-------------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Particle Size** | Trommel screen, vibrating screen | Oversize (paper, plastic) vs. undersize (organics, fines) |
| **Density** | Air classifier, sink-float tank | Heavy (glass, metal) vs. light (plastic film, paper) |
| **Magnetic Susceptibility** | Overhead magnetic drum | Ferrous metals from non-ferrous stream |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Eddy current separator | Aluminum, copper from non-metallics |
| **Optical Reflectance** | NIR optical sorter | Polymer type (PET vs. HDPE vs. PP) |
| **Shape / Aspect Ratio** | Disc screen, ballistic separator | 2D (flat paper/film) vs. 3D (bottles, cans) |

## 2.3 Preprocessing Methods — Engineered Unit Operations

### 2.3.1 Size Reduction

**Purpose:** Increase surface area, liberate recoverable materials, improve homogenization.

- **Shredders** (slow-speed, high torque): 50–200 mm output
- **Hammer mills** (high-speed, impact): 10–50 mm output
- **Granulators** (shear): 1–10 mm output
- **Pulpers** (for paper/plastic wet processing)

**Bond's Work Index relation** for comminution energy:
$$E = K_B \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_F}} \right)$$

Where:
- $E$ = specific energy consumption (kWh/tonne)
- $K_B$ = Bond's work index (kWh/tonne) — **typical for MSW: 50–100**
- $d_P$ = product size (mm)
- $d_F$ = feed size (mm)

### 2.3.2 Screening & Classification

The **Trommel screen** is the workhorse of MSW preprocessing.

$$\eta_{\text{screen}} = \frac{\text{mass of undersize passing through}}{\text{mass of undersize in feed}} \times 100\%$$

Trommel design uses the empirical **critical rotational speed**:
$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \cdot \sqrt{\sin\theta - \phi \cos\theta}$$

Where:
- $N_c$ = critical speed (rpm)
- $D$ = drum diameter (m)
- $\theta$ = drum inclination angle
- $\phi$ = angle of repose of waste (**typically 35–45°** for MSW)

### 2.3.3 Drying & Dewatering

Reduces moisture to enable thermal processing or to make feedstock suitable for refuse-derived fuel (RDF) manufacturing.

**Moisture removal equation:**
$$m_{\text{water}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \left( X_{\text{in}} - X_{\text{out}} \right)$$

Where $X$ is moisture content on a wet basis (kg water / kg wet waste).

### 2.3.4 Baling & Densification

- **Baling pressure:** typically **20–50 bar** for MSW.
- **Bale density:** **600–900 kg/m³** (vs. 200–400 kg/m³ loose).
- Reduces transportation volume by **factor 2.5–4**.

### 2.3.5 Composting / Bio-Stabilization (Biological Preprocessing)

For the organic fraction:
$$\text{C}_a \text{H}_b \text{O}_c \text{N}_d + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{Heat} + \text{Humus}$$

**C/N Ratio Criterion:**
$$(C/N)_{\text{ideal}} = 25\text{–}30$$

If $(C/N)_{\text{feed}} > 35$, nitrogen supplementation is required.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Table

| S.No. | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Typical Range | Application |
|:-----:|-------------------|---------------|-----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Collection mass balance | $\dot{m}_{c} = \dot{m}_{g} \cdot \eta_{c} \cdot (1-f_{\text{lost}})$ | kg/day | System sizing |
| 2 | Per capita waste generation | $w_{pc} = \dot{m}_{g,\text{city}} / N_{\text{pop}}$ | **0.3–0.6 kg/cap/day** (India) | Capacity design |
| 3 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $X = m_{w}/(m_{w}+m_{d})$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Feed characterization |
| 4 | Bulk density | $\rho_b = m/V_{\text{loose}}$ | **200–500 kg/m³** | Storage, transport |
| 5 | Calorific value (Dulong) | $CV = 8080 C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240 S$ | kcal/kg | Combustion design |
| 6 | Bond's comminution energy | $E = K_B (1/\sqrt{d_P} - 1/\sqrt{d_F})$ | kWh/tonne | Shredder power |
| 7 | Trommel critical speed | $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D} \cdot \sqrt{\sin\theta - \phi\cos\theta}$ | rpm | Trommel design |
| 8 | Screen efficiency | $\eta = m_{\text{undersize,out}} / m_{\text{undersize,in}}$ | % | Separation train design |
| 9 | C/N ratio | $C/N = m_{\text{carbon}}/m_{\text{nitrogen}}$ | **25–30 ideal** | Composting |
| 10 | Aerobic respiration stoichiometry | $\text{CH}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + NH_3$ | — | Biodegradation kinetics |
| 11 | Bale densification ratio | $DR = \rho_{\text{bale}}/\rho_{\text{loose}}$ | **2.5–4** | Transport economics |
| 12 | RDF specification (Indian) | $\text{CV} \geq 1500$ kcal/kg, $X \leq 25\%$ | — | Compliance |
