# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per Capita Waste Generation and Plant Capacity Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A KTU model town has a population of **2,50,000**. Per capita waste generation rate is **0.45 kg/cap/day**. The collection efficiency is **82%**, and 8% of collected waste is lost to scavenging before reaching the Material Recovery Facility. The MRF operates **350 days/year**, **2 shifts/day, 8 hours/shift**. Determine:
1. Total waste generated per day (kg/day and TPD).
2. Actual feed to MRF (TPD).
3. Annual feed to MRF (tonnes/year).
4. Average hourly throughput (kg/hr) for MRF equipment sizing.

### Step 1 — Total Daily Waste Generation

By definition:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{generated}} = w_{pc} \times N_{\text{pop}}$$

Substituting values:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{generated}} = 0.45 \;\text{kg/cap/day} \times 2{,}50{,}000 \;\text{cap}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{generated}} = 1{,}12{,}500 \;\text{kg/day}$$

Converting to **Tonnes Per Day (TPD)**:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{generated}} = 112.5 \;\text{TPD}$$

### Step 2 — Actual Feed to MRF (Accounting for Collection Efficiency & Losses)

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MRF}} = \dot{m}_{\text{generated}} \times \eta_c \times (1 - f_{\text{lost}})$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MRF}} = 112.5 \times 0.82 \times (1 - 0.08)$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MRF}} = 112.5 \times 0.82 \times 0.92$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MRF}} = 112.5 \times 0.7544$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MRF}} = 84.87 \;\text{TPD}$$

### Step 3 — Annual Feed to MRF

$$\dot{m}_{\text{annual}} = \dot{m}_{\text{MRF}} \times N_{\text{operating days}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{annual}} = 84.87 \times 350 = 29{,}704.5 \;\text{tonnes/year}$$

### Step 4 — Average Hourly Throughput for Equipment Sizing

Total operating hours per year:
$$H_{\text{annual}} = N_{\text{days}} \times N_{\text{shifts/day}} \times T_{\text{shift}}$$

$$H_{\text{annual}} = 350 \times 2 \times 8 = 5{,}600 \;\text{hrs/year}$$

Hourly throughput:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{hourly}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{annual}}}{H_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{29{,}704.5 \times 1000}{5{,}600}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{hourly}} = 5{,}304.4 \;\text{kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{hourly}} \approx 5.30 \;\text{tonnes/hour}}$$

This is the **design capacity** the shredder, trommel, and conveyors must handle (with a safety factor of **1.25–1.5** applied).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Trommel Screen Critical Speed

**Problem Statement:** A trommel screen has drum diameter $D = 2.5$ m, inclination angle $\theta = 4°$, and the waste angle of repose is $\phi = 40°$. Determine the rotational speed required to cascade the material efficiently (i.e., the critical speed).

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation

The cascade condition for a trommel requires centripetal component equal to gravitational component along the surface:

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \cdot \sqrt{\sin\theta - \phi\cos\theta}$$

Note: The angle $\phi$ here is treated in radians when used inside the trig functions of the empirical formula, OR expressed as a numeric factor (sin/cos of angle). The standard form uses $\phi$ as the *coefficient* $\tan^{-1}$ of friction OR the literal angle — depending on textbook convention. We adopt the **literal angle form** per standard chemical engineering references.

### Step 2 — Convert Angles

$$\theta = 4° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin 4° = 0.0698,\;\; \cos 4° = 0.9976$$

$$\phi = 40° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin 40° = 0.6428,\;\; \cos 40° = 0.7660$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Argument of the Square Root

$$A = \sin\theta - \phi_{\text{coeff}}\cos\theta$$

Where $\phi_{\text{coeff}} = \tan(40°) = 0.8391$.

$$A = 0.0698 - (0.8391 \times 0.9976)$$

$$A = 0.0698 - 0.8371 = -0.7673$$

Since this gives a **negative value**, the trommel will not cascade under the literal-angle form. This indicates we must use the **friction-coefficient form**:

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \cdot \sqrt{\sin\theta + \mu\cos\theta}$$

with $\mu = \tan\phi = 0.8391$ but the **sign convention differs**:

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{2.5}} \cdot \sqrt{\sin(4°) + \mu\cos(4°)}$$

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{1.5811} \cdot \sqrt{0.0698 + 0.8391 \times 0.9976}$$

$$N_c = 26.755 \cdot \sqrt{0.0698 + 0.8371}$$

$$N_c = 26.755 \cdot \sqrt{0.9069}$$

$$N_c = 26.755 \times 0.9523$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx 25.5 \;\text{rpm}}$$

Operational practice uses **0.5 to 0.75 of $N_c$**, so **12–19 rpm** is recommended.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bond's Work Index for Shredder Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A hammer mill must reduce shredded MSW from a feed size of **150 mm** to a product size of **20 mm**. Bond's Work Index for MSW is **80 kWh/tonne**. Determine the energy required (in kWh/tonne and kJ/kg) and the motor power if the throughput is **5 tonnes/hour**.

### Step 1 — Apply Bond's Equation

$$E = K_B \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_F}} \right)$$

$$E = 80 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{20}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{150}} \right)$$

$$\sqrt{20} = 4.472, \quad \sqrt{150} = 12.247$$

$$E = 80 \cdot (0.2236 - 0.0817)$$

$$E = 80 \times 0.1419$$

$$\boxed{E = 11.35 \;\text{kWh/tonne}}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to kJ/kg

$$E = 11.35 \times 3.6 = 40.86 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

### Step 3 — Motor Power for Given Throughput

$$P = E \times \dot{m} = 11.35 \;\text{kWh/tonne} \times 5 \;\text{tonne/hr}$$

$$\boxed{P = 56.75 \;\text{kW}}$$

Applying a safety factor of **1.3** for starting loads:
$$P_{\text{motor,design}} = 56.75 \times 1.3 \approx 73.8 \;\text{kW} \approx 75 \;\text{kW}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Waste Composition vs. Preprocessing Decision

| Waste Fraction | wt% (Indian MSW avg) | Critical Property | Preprocessing Required | Downstream Route |
|:---:|:---:|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Organic (food, garden) | **40–60%** | Moisture, C/N | Shredding → Dewatering → Composting/Digestion | Compost, biogas |
| Paper / Cardboard | **8–15%** | Fiber strength | Pulping or baling | Recycling, RDF |
| Plastics (mixed) | **8–12%** | Polymer type, density | NIR sort → wash → pelletize | Pyrolysis, RDF |
| Glass | **4–6%** | Density, brittleness | Crushing → air classify | Cullet → new glass |
| Metals (ferrous) | **1–3%** | Magnetic | Magnetic drum | Re-melting |
| Metals (non-ferrous) | **0.5–1.5%** | Conductivity | Eddy current | Re-melting |
| Inerts (stones, soil) | **15–25%** | Size, density | Trommel undersize | Landfill cover |
| Hazardous (battery, medical) | **0.5–2%** | Toxicity | Specialized handling | Secure disposal |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary segregation of municipal solid waste. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Level | Location | Tools/Method | Typical Purity |
|:-----:|----------|--------------|:--------------:|
| **Primary** | Source (households, industries) | Two-bin / Three-bin system, color-coded bins (green for wet, blue for dry) | **85–95%** |
| **Secondary** | Transfer station / Community bin | Manual picking line, light/heavy separation | **75–85%** |
| **Tertiary** | Material Recovery Facility (MRF) | Trommel, air classifier, magnetic drum, eddy current, NIR sorter | **90–99%** |

**Example:** Primary — Household waste segregation into wet (organic) and dry (recyclable). Secondary — Pickers at transfer station removing visible plastic. Tertiary — Eddy current separator recovering aluminum cans.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **segregation** with **collection**. Segregation is the act of *separating heterogeneous waste into homogeneous fractions*, whereas collection is the act of *gathering and transporting* waste. Marks lost if these are interchanged.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]`

**Q: Define Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF). List four characteristic specifications that make waste suitable for RDF production.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** RDF is a **densified, calorific-enriched fuel** produced from the **non-biodegradable, high-calorific-value fraction of MSW** (paper, plastics, textiles, wood) after removing inert and organic fractions through preprocessing (shredding, drying, screening).

**Four Specifications for RDF Suitability:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Typical Specification |
|:-----:|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Calorific Value** | $\geq 1500$ kcal/kg (some standards require $\geq 3500$) |
| 2 | **Moisture Content** | $\leq 20\text{–}25\%$ (wet basis) |
| 3 | **Chlorine Content** | $\leq 1\%$ (to prevent HCl emission / corrosion) |
| 4 | **Ash Content** | $\leq 20\%$ |
| 5 | **Bulk Density (after baling)** | $\geq 600$ kg/m³ |
| 6 | **Particle Size** | $\leq 50$ mm (uniform, for fluidized bed combustion) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** RDF ≠ just shredded MSW. RDF must be **processed** (dried, screened, de-stoned, ferrous removed) to meet quality specifications. Simply calling shredded waste "RDF" will attract 1-mark penalty.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the **door-to-door collection system** for municipal solid waste with its advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A town has a population of **1,50,000**. The per capita waste generation is **0.5 kg/cap/day**. If the collection efficiency is **78%** and **10%** of collected waste is lost during handling, determine:
1. The actual waste reaching the processing plant (TPD).
2. The annual feed to the plant if it operates **300 days/year**.
3. The size of a primary shredder if the design safety factor is **1.3** and the Bond Work Index for MSW is **85 kWh/tonne**, feed size = **200 mm**, product size = **25 mm**, throughput = **5 TPH**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Door-to-Door Collection System:**

In this system, **collection workers physically visit each household, shop, or establishment** along a defined route at predetermined timings (often between **6:00 AM – 10:00 AM** to avoid public inconvenience). They use **small motorized vehicles** such as:
- Auto-tippers (3-wheeled, capacity 1–1.5 m³)
- Dumper placers (capacity 3–5 m³)
- Tricycles / e-rickshaws (for narrow lanes)

Waste is collected in **two or three segregated bins** per household:
- **Green bin** — Wet/Biodegradable waste
- **Blue bin** — Dry/Recyclable waste
- **Red/Black bin** (optional) — Domestic hazardous waste

**Advantages:**
1. **High source-segregation efficiency** (up to 90%) — waste never gets mixed during transport.
2. **Reduced street litter** and improved urban hygiene.
3. **Better recyclables recovery** as contamination is minimal.
4. **Lower downstream processing cost** due to homogeneous feed.
5. **Employment generation** for informal sector workers if integrated with NGOs/ULBs.

**Limitations:**
1. **High labor and vehicle cost** (operating expense).
2. **Time-intensive** — only 2–3 hours of productive collection per day per worker.
3. **Requires behavioral change** among citizens — non-compliance is common.
4. **Not feasible in high-rise apartments** without dedicated chute systems.
5. **Inefficient in narrow by-lanes** — manual head-loading sometimes required.

**Diagram (textual):** House → Bins → Auto-tipper → Transfer Station → MRF → Landfill/Compost Plant

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Total Daily Waste Generation**

$$\dot{m}_{g} = w_{pc} \times N_{pop} = 0.5 \times 1{,}50{,}000 = 75{,}000 \;\text{kg/day} = 75 \;\text{TPD}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Feed to Processing Plant**

$$\dot{m}_{p} = \dot{m}_{g} \times \eta_c \times (1 - f_{\text{lost}})$$

$$\dot{m}_{p} = 75 \times 0.78 \times (1 - 0.10) = 75 \times 0.78 \times 0.90$$

$$\dot{m}_{p} = 52.65 \;\text{TPD}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Feed to the Plant**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{annual}} = 52.65 \times 300 = 15{,}795 \;\text{tonnes/year}$$

**Step 4 — Shredder Power Requirement (Bond's Equation)**

$$E = K_B \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{d_F}} \right)$$

$$E = 85 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{25}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{200}} \right)$$

$$\sqrt{25} = 5.000, \quad \sqrt{200} = 14.142$$

$$E = 85 \times (0.2000 - 0.0707) = 85 \times 0.1293$$

$$E = 10.99 \;\text{kWh/tonne}$$

**Power for 5 TPH throughput:**

$$P = E \times \dot{m} = 10.99 \times 5 = 54.95 \;\text{kW}$$

**With safety factor 1.3:**

$$P_{\text{design}} = 54.95 \times 1.3 = 71.4 \;\text{kW} \approx 75 \;\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{design}} \approx 75 \;\text{kW}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **sequence of preprocessing operations** in a typical Material Recovery Facility (MRF) for MSW. Identify at least **five unit operations** and the **property exploited** by each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A composting plant receives **organic waste** with the following composition:
- Carbon content = **45%**
- Nitrogen content = **1.2%**
- Moisture content = **55%**

Determine:
1. The **C/N ratio** and comment on its suitability for aerobic composting.
2. If the C/N ratio is too high, calculate the **mass of urea (CO(NH₂)₂, 46% N)** to be added per tonne of waste to bring the C/N ratio to **30**.
3. The **theoretical oxygen demand** for complete aerobic stabilization of **1000 kg** of this waste (assume waste formula: C₆H₁₀O₅N, molecular weight = 200 g/mol). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**MRF Block Flow (textual):**

$$ \text{Incoming MSW} \to \text{Weighbridge} \to \text{Tipping Floor} \to \text{Manual Sorting} \to \text{Primary Shredder} $$
$$\to \text{Trommel Screen} \to \text{Air Classifier} \to \text{Magnetic Separator} \to \text{Eddy Current} \to \text{NIR Sorter} \to \text{Baler} \to \text{Output Streams}$$

**Five Unit Operations and Properties Exploited:**

| S.No. | Unit Operation | Property Exploited | Separation Achieved |
|:-----:|----------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Trommel Screen** | Particle size | Oversize (>50 mm): paper, plastic, textiles; Undersize: organics, fines |
| 2 | **Air Classifier** | Density (aerodynamic) | Light fraction (paper, film) vs. heavy fraction (glass, stone, metal) |
| 3 | **Magnetic Drum** | Magnetic susceptibility | Ferrous metals (Fe, steel) separated from non-ferrous stream |
| 4 | **Eddy Current Separator** | Electrical conductivity | Aluminum, copper ejected from non-metallic stream |
| 5 | **NIR Optical Sorter** | Spectral reflectance | Polymer-type sorting (PET, HDPE, PP) |
| 6 | **Ballistic Separator** | Shape / aspect ratio | 2D flat items (paper, film) from 3D items (bottles, cans) |

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — C/N Ratio Calculation**

$$C/N = \frac{\% \text{Carbon}}{\% \text{Nitrogen}} = \frac{45}{1.2} = 37.5$$

**Comment:** The value **37.5 is higher than the ideal range of 25–30**. Aerobic composting will proceed slowly due to **nitrogen limitation** — microbes cannot multiply rapidly without sufficient N. Therefore, **nitrogen supplementation is required**.

**Step 2 — Mass of Urea Required**

Target $C/N = 30$. Initial carbon is 45% of 1 tonne = **450 kg C**.
Required nitrogen for C/N = 30:
$$N_{\text{required}} = \frac{450}{30} = 15 \;\text{kg N per tonne}$$

Initial nitrogen in waste:
$$N_{\text{initial}} = 1.2\% \times 1000 = 12 \;\text{kg N per tonne}$$

Additional nitrogen needed:
$$\Delta N = 15 - 12 = 3 \;\text{kg N per tonne}$$

Since urea contains **46% N by mass**:
$$m_{\text{urea}} = \frac{3}{0.46} = 6.52 \;\text{kg urea per tonne of waste}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{urea}} \approx 6.52 \;\text{kg/tonne}}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Oxygen Demand**

The aerobic stabilization reaction per mole of C₆H₁₀O₅N (MW = 200 g/mol):

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5\text{N} + a\,\text{O}_2 \to b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + d\,\text{NH}_3$$

**Balancing:**

- C balance: $b = 6$
- H balance: $2c + 3d = 10$
- N balance: $d = 1 \Rightarrow c = 3.5$
- O balance: $5 + 2a = 2b + c = 12 + 3.5 = 15.5 \Rightarrow a = 5.25$

So per mole of substrate:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_5\text{N} + 5.25\,\text{O}_2 \to 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 3.5\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3$$

**Moles of substrate in 1000 kg:**
$$n = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{200} = 5000 \;\text{moles}$$

**Moles of O₂ required:**
$$n_{O_2} = 5000 \times 5.25 = 26{,}250 \;\text{moles}$$

**Mass of O₂ (MW = 32 g/mol):**
$$m_{O_2} = 26{,}250 \times 32 = 8{,}40{,}000 \;\text{g} = 840 \;\text{kg}$$

**Volume of O₂ at STP (22.4 L/mol):**
$$V_{O_2} = 26{,}250 \times 22.4 = 5{,}88{,}000 \;\text{L} = 588 \;\text{m}^3 \;\text{O}_2$$

**Equivalent air (air contains 21% O₂ by volume):**
$$V_{\text{air}} = \frac{588}{0.21} = 2800 \;\text{m}^3 \;\text{air}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Theoretical O}_2 \text{ Demand} = 840 \;\text{kg} \;(\approx 2800 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ of air})}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- [ ] **Collection vs. Segregation vs. Preprocessing** — clearly three distinct stages; do NOT conflate.
- [ ] **Door-to-door** gives **85–95%** segregation purity; **MRF** gives **90–99%**.
- [ ] **Per capita waste in India:** **0.3–0.6 kg/cap/day** (memorize **0.45** as default).
- [ ] **Composition of Indian MSW (memorize %):** Organic **40–60%**, Recyclables **25–35%**, Inerts **15–25%**, Hazardous **0.5–2%**.
- [ ] **Bulk density of MSW:** **200–500 kg/m³** loose; **600–900 kg/m³** baled.
- [ ] **Ideal C/N ratio for composting:** **25–30**; if higher, add nitrogen (urea, sewage sludge, poultry manure).
- [ ] **RDF specifications:** $CV \geq 1500$ kcal/kg, $X \leq 25\%$, $Cl \leq 1\%$.
- [ ] **Trommel screen** operates at **0.5–0.75 × critical speed**.
- [ ] **Bond's Work Index for MSW:** ~**80 kWh/tonne** (for quick estimation).
- [ ] **Aerobic stabilization stoichiometry** of carbohydrate-like organics: substrate formula C₆H₁₀O₅N → 5.25 O₂ (moles).
- [ ] **Safety factor for shredder design:** typically **1.25–1.5**.
- [ ] **Five key properties exploited in preprocessing:** size, density, magnetism, conductivity, optical reflectance.
- [ ] **Collection efficiency η_c** is dimensionless; always report range **70–90%** for Indian cities.
- [ ] **MRF outputs:** organic → composting; recyclables → market; inerts → landfill cover; reject → sanitary landfill.
- [ ] **Hazwaste Routes:** Never mix with MSW; route to **TSDF (Treatment, Storage and Disposal Facility)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Final Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Always include **units** in every numerical answer — 1 mark deduction per missing unit.
> 2. **Always show the formula before substituting values** — partial marking requires this.
> 3. **Free-body diagrams and block diagrams** must be drawn even if rough — they carry **1–2 marks** independently of text.
> 4. For **C/N ratio problems**, clearly state whether **wet or dry basis** is being used.
> 5. **Trommel speed** problems: students often forget the **0.5–0.75 multiplier** — operational speed, not critical speed, is the design answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incineration: Process, Advantages, and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Incineration: Process, Advantages, and Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incineration** is a controlled, high-temperature thermal oxidation process (typically **850 °C – 1200 °C**) in which combustible components of waste materials are converted into non-combustible residues (ash), flue gases (CO₂, H₂O, N₂), and recoverable heat energy. It is a unit operation classified under **Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** processes within the broader domain of Process Systems Engineering.

In Process Systems Engineering, incineration is modeled as a **steady-state reacting flow system** governed by conservation of mass and energy, with the **general reaction** for a generic waste component represented as:

$$ \text{C}_x\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c\text{S}_d + \left(x + \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} + d\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{a}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \frac{c}{2}\,\text{N}_2 + d\,\text{SO}_2 $$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Controlled Campfire"**
>
> Imagine a regular campfire burning garbage in your backyard. Smoke, odor, unburnt pieces, and toxic gases escape freely. **Incineration is the engineered, scientific version of that campfire.** It is designed inside a sealed, insulated chamber (the *furnace*) with:
> - **Precisely controlled temperature** (like adjusting the knob on a gas stove),
> - **Optimized oxygen supply** (enough air to burn everything, but not too much to waste energy),
> - **Sufficient residence time** (holding the waste long enough to ensure complete burn),
> - **A pollution-control tail section** (the *APCD* — Air Pollution Control Devices, like a filter system attached to the exhaust).
>
> Instead of ash, smoke, and complaints, you get a small quantity of inert ash, clean flue gas, and hot water/steam you can actually use to run a turbine.

### 1.3 Why Incineration is Studied in Process Systems Engineering

| Perspective | Role in PSE |
|---|---|
| Mass Balance | Tracks C, H, O, N, S atoms from feed to flue gas + ash |
| Energy Balance | Computes recoverable heat for combined heat & power (CHP) |
| Reaction Engineering | Analyzes pyrolysis, gasification, oxidation sub-steps |
| Control Engineering | Maintains bed temperature, stoichiometry, residence time |
| Sustainability | Quantifies net CO₂, dioxin, and ash disposal footprint |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (used in KTU problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Residence Time** in combustion zone: **≥ 2 seconds** (regulatory minimum for municipal solid waste)
> - **Turbulence Factor (Reynolds Number in furnace):** typically **Re > 10,000**
> - **Excess Air:** **20 % – 100 %** above stoichiometric
> - **Volume Reduction Ratio:** **~ 90 %** (typical for MSW)
> - **Mass Reduction Ratio:** **~ 70 %**
> - **Energy Recovery Efficiency:** **20 % – 30 %** electrical, **60 %–80 %** combined (CHP)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Sequential Sub-Processes of Incineration

Incineration is *not* a single reaction — it is a **sequence of four coupled physico-chemical stages** in the furnace. KTU questions frequently test this hierarchy.

1. **Drying Stage (100 °C – 200 °C)**
   - Physical process: latent heat of vaporization removes moisture.
   - **Energy required:** $Q_{\text{dry}} = m_w \cdot h_{fg}$ where $h_{fg} \approx 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 100 °C.
   - No chemical reaction occurs.

2. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization (200 °C – 600 °C)**
   - Thermal decomposition in oxygen-starved zones.
   - Complex organic matter → volatile hydrocarbons (CH₄, C₂H₆, tars) + char (solid carbon).
   - Endothermic nature demands sustained heat input.

3. **Combustion / Oxidation (600 °C – 1200 °C)**
   - Volatiles + char + excess O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O.
   - **Primary reaction:** $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$  (ΔH = −393.5 kJ/mol)
   - **Secondary reaction:** $\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}$ (incomplete)
   - **Water-gas shift:** $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$

4. **Residence & Quench (residence time ≥ 2 s)**
   - Ensures complete oxidation of CO, dioxin precursors, and VOCs.
   - Rapid post-combustion quench to **≤ 200 °C** suppresses dioxin re-formation.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — Engineering Reasoning

- **Drying efficiency** determines start-up time and auxiliary fuel demand.
- **Pyrolysis rate** controls the volatile load reaching the over-bed flame.
- **Combustion temperature & turbulence** are the *primary levers* to minimize CO, soot, and unburnt hydrocarbon (UHC) emissions.
- **Residence time** is regulated by **3-T rule**: **Temperature, Turbulence, Time**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every high-yield equation you may need in Part A (3-mark) or Part B (14-mark) questions on incineration. **Memorize the units, not just the symbols.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand | $O_{2,\text{st}} = x + \tfrac{a}{4} - \tfrac{b}{2} + d$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | For waste $\text{C}_x\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c\text{S}_d$ |
| 2 | Theoretical Air | $A_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{O_{2,\text{st}}}{0.21}$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | Air contains 21 % O₂ by volume |
| 3 | Actual Air | $A_{\text{act}} = \lambda \cdot A_{\text{th}}$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | $\lambda$ = excess air ratio (1.2 – 2.0) |
| 4 | Higher Heating Value (HHV) | $\text{HHV} = 338\,C + 1442(H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 94\,S$ | kJ/kg | **Dulong's formula** — standard for waste |
| 5 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2.442 \cdot \dfrac{9H}{100} \cdot 8.936$ | kJ/kg | Subtracts latent heat of water vapor |
| 6 | Flue Gas Mass | $m_{\text{fg}} = m_{\text{waste}} + m_{\text{air}} - m_{\text{ash}}$ | kg/kg waste | Mass balance closure |
| 7 | Volume Reduction Ratio | $\text{VRR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{ash}}}{V_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical 85 – 95 % |
| 8 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{recovered}}}{Q_{\text{waste}} + Q_{\text{aux}}}$ | dimensionless | CHP plants achieve 70 – 80 % |
| 9 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | $\text{AFR}_{\text{st}} = \dfrac{A_{\text{th}} \cdot 28.97}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | Used in combustion controls |
| 10 | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_{\text{ref}} + \dfrac{\sum \Delta H_{\text{comb}}}{\sum n_i c_{p,i}}$ | K | Verifies if combustion is self-sustaining |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention for KTU:** When writing absolute value or "given" in exam scripts, **never use** the symbol `|` inside the table — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Fields

- **Chemical Engineering:** Reactor design of rotary kilns, fluidized beds.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Boiler design, superheater tube-bank layout.
- **Civil/Environmental Engineering:** MSW management, ash monofill design.
- **Instrumentation:** Furnace temperature profiling, O₂ trim control loops.
- **Power Engineering:** WtE steam Rankine cycle integration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand from Ultimate Analysis

> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** A solid waste sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: **C = 45 %, H = 6 %, O = 30 %, N = 2 %, S = 1 %, Ash = 16 %**. Determine: (i) Stoichiometric O₂ demand per kg of waste, (ii) Theoretical air required per kg of waste, (iii) Actual air supplied if excess air = **50 %**.

#### Step 1 — Convert Mass Fractions to Moles

Basis: **1 kg of dry waste**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{C}} &= \frac{0.45 \times 1000}{12} = 37.5 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{H}} &= \frac{0.06 \times 1000}{1} = 60.0 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{O,waste}} &= \frac{0.30 \times 1000}{32} = 9.375 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{N}} &= \frac{0.02 \times 1000}{28} = 0.714\;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{S}} &= \frac{0.01 \times 1000}{32} = 0.3125\;\text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Ultimate analysis gives mass fractions; dividing each by the atomic weight converts them into molar quantities, which is essential because **chemical reactions obey mole ratios, not mass ratios.**

#### Step 2 — Write Sub-Reactions for Each Element

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \\
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{SO}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Net O₂ Required

The oxygen already present in the waste *partially* fulfills the demand. Hence we subtract the bound oxygen atoms converted into usable O₂:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\text{needed}} &= n_{\text{C}} + \tfrac{n_{\text{H}}}{4} + n_{\text{S}} - \tfrac{n_{\text{O,waste}}}{2} \\
&= 37.5 + \tfrac{60}{4} + 0.3125 - \tfrac{9.375}{2} \\
&= 37.5 + 15.0 + 0.3125 - 4.6875 \\
&= 48.125 \;\text{mol O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to mass:

$$
m_{O_2} = 48.125 \times 32 = 1540 \;\text{g} = 1.540 \;\text{kg O}_2 \;\text{per kg waste}
$$

#### Step 4 — Theoretical Air Requirement

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{air,th}} &= \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} \\
&= \frac{1.540}{0.232} = 6.638 \;\text{kg air per kg waste}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Air is 23.2 % O₂ by *mass* (vs 21 % by volume), so the denominator is 0.232, not 0.21. **This is a classic KTU trap — students often use 0.21 incorrectly.**

#### Step 5 — Actual Air with 50 % Excess

$$
m_{\text{air,act}} = 1.5 \times 6.638 = 9.957 \;\text{kg air per kg waste}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Higher Heating Value via Dulong's Formula

Using the same composition (C=45, H=6, O=30, S=1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV} &= 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 94\,S \\
&= 338(45) + 1442\!\left(6 - \tfrac{30}{8}\right) + 94(1) \\
&= 15210 + 1442(6 - 3.75) + 94 \\
&= 15210 + 1442(2.25) + 94 \\
&= 15210 + 3244.5 + 94 \\
&= 18548.5 \;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Volume Reduction Ratio Demonstration

> A MSW incinerator receives **1000 m³/day** of waste (bulk density 250 kg/m³) and produces **75 m³/day** of bottom ash (bulk density 900 kg/m³). Compute (a) VRR, (b) mass reduction ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{VRR} &= \frac{1000 - 75}{1000} \times 100 = 92.5\% \\
m_{\text{in}} &= 1000 \times 250 = 250{,}000 \;\text{kg/day} \\
m_{\text{ash}} &= 75 \times 900 = 67{,}500 \;\text{kg/day} \\
\text{MRR} &= \frac{250000 - 67500}{250000} \times 100 = 73.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Incineration Sub-Process Mapping

| Sub-process | Temperature Window | Dominant Physics | Governing Equation | Engineering Control Knob |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Drying** | 100 – 200 °C | Heat & mass transfer (evaporation) | $Q = m_w h_{fg}$ | Pre-heating of combustion air |
| **Pyrolysis** | 200 – 600 °C | Thermal decomposition (endothermic) | Arrhenius: $k = A e^{-E/RT}$ | Solid residence time, particle size |
| **Combustion** | 600 – 1200 °C | Oxidation kinetics (exothermic) | $\text{C}_x\text{H}_a + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Excess air ratio $\lambda$ |
| **Quench / Scrub** | 1200 → 200 °C | Gas cooling, gas-liquid absorption | Energy balance on spray tower | Quench water flow rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix is KTU-relevant:** Module-2 incineration questions are 60 % qualitative (asking you to identify which stage is endothermic, which stage produces CO, etc.) and 40 % quantitative (calculating air or HHV). The matrix above prepares you for both.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Incineration Plant

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional flow** of a typical Mass-Burn Incineration Plant with Energy Recovery — from waste reception to stack emission. Each block corresponds to a unit operation studied in Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WASTE RECEIPT AND SHREDDING"] --> B["WEIGHBRIDGE AND QUALITY CHECK"]
    B --> C["WASTE STORAGE BUNKER (3 TO 5 DAY RESERVE)"]
    C --> D["FEED HOPPER WITH HYDRAULIC RAM FEEDER"]
    D --> E["GRATE FURNACE PRIMARY COMBUSTION CHAMBER 850 TO 1200 C"]
    E --> F["SECONDARY COMBUSTION CHAMBER RESIDENCE TIME GE 2 S"]
    F --> G["BOILER HEAT EXCHANGER WASTE HEAT RECOVERY"]
    G --> H["ENERGY RECOVERY STEAM TURBINE AND GENERATOR"]
    G --> I["FLUE GAS COOLING ECONOMIZER"]
    I --> J["DRY LIME SCRUBBER SO2 REMOVAL"]
    J --> K["FABRIC FILTER BAGHOUSE PARTICULATE REMOVAL"]
    K --> L["SCR SYSTEM NOX REDUCTION"]
    L --> M["INDUCED DRAFT FAN AND STACK EMISSION MONITOR CEMS"]
    M --> N["ATMOSPHERIC DISCHARGE CLEAN FLUE GAS"]
    E --> O["BOTTOM ASH QUENCH AND DISCHARGE"]
    O --> P["ASH TREATMENT AND LANDFILL OR RECYCLING"]
    F --> Q["FLY ASH COLLECTION AND STABILIZATION"]
    Q --> R["HAZARDOUS WASTE LANDFILL"]
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style explanation):**

- **Nodes D → E → F** represent the **three-T rule zone** (Temperature, Turbulence, Time). Any deficiency here causes dioxin or CO formation.
- **Node G** is the energy-recovery pinch point; this is where the steam cycle integrates with the combustion process.
- **Nodes J → K → L** form the *APCD train* — typical KTU question: *"Arrange the APCDs in proper sequence and justify."*
- **Side streams (O, Q)** highlight that incineration is **not 100 % emission-free**; bottom ash + fly ash still need disposal — a key disadvantage.

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Sub-Process Timeline Within Furnace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0["WASTE ENTERS GRATE"] --> A1["STAGE 1 DRYING 100 TO 200 C"]
    A1 --> A2["STAGE 2 DEVOLATILIZATION 200 TO 600 C"]
    A2 --> A3["STAGE 3 COMBUSTION OF CHAR 600 TO 1200 C"]
    A3 --> A4["STAGE 4 COMPLETE OXIDATION RES GE 2 S"]
    A4 --> A5["ASH DISCHARGE"]
    A1 -. latent heat demand .-> A1
    A2 -. endothermic .-> A2
    A3 -. exothermic .-> A3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define incineration. State the four sequential sub-processes that occur inside an incinerator.*

**Model Answer:**
Incineration is a controlled thermal oxidation process in which combustible waste is combusted at **850 °C – 1200 °C** to produce inert ash, flue gas, and recoverable heat. The four sequential sub-processes are:
1. **Drying** (100 – 200 °C): moisture evaporation.
2. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization** (200 – 600 °C): breakdown into volatiles and char.
3. **Combustion** (600 – 1200 °C): oxidation of volatiles and char.
4. **Residence & Quench** (≥ 2 s at high T; then rapid cooling to suppress dioxins).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four stages with temperature windows.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any **three** advantages and **three** disadvantages of incineration as a waste-management technique.*

**Model Answer:**
**Advantages:**
1. Volume reduction up to **90 %** — minimizes landfill burden.
2. Energy recovery as electricity/heat (WtE concept).
3. Destruction of pathogens and toxic organic matter at high temperature.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High capital and operating cost.
2. Air pollutants — NOx, SOx, dioxins, furans, heavy metals.
3. Public opposition (NIMBY); CO₂ contributes to climate change.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for advantages, 1.5 for disadvantages.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — ESE Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **any one** of the two full questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Sub-parts are **compulsory** within the chosen question.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** A solid waste has the following composition by mass: **C = 50 %, H = 5 %, O = 30 %, N = 3 %, S = 2 %, Ash = 10 %**. Calculate the:
(i) stoichiometric oxygen required per kg of waste,
(ii) theoretical air required per kg of waste,
(iii) actual air supplied if excess air is **80 %**.

**(b)** With a neat block-flow diagram, describe the **four sequential sub-processes** in an incinerator. State the temperature range and dominant physical/chemical phenomenon for each stage.

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Basis: 1 kg of dry waste.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{C}} &= \frac{0.50 \times 1000}{12} = 41.667 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{H}} &= \frac{0.05 \times 1000}{1} = 50.0 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{O,waste}} &= \frac{0.30 \times 1000}{32} = 9.375 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{N}} &= \frac{0.03 \times 1000}{28} = 1.071 \;\text{mol} \\
n_{\text{S}} &= \frac{0.02 \times 1000}{32} = 0.625 \;\text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

(i) Stoichiometric O₂:

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_{2,\text{st}} &= n_{\text{C}} + \tfrac{n_{\text{H}}}{4} + n_{\text{S}} - \tfrac{n_{\text{O,waste}}}{2} \\
&= 41.667 + 12.5 + 0.625 - 4.6875 \\
&= 50.105 \;\text{mol O}_2 \;\Rightarrow\; 50.105 \times 32 = 1603.4 \;\text{g} \\
&\approx \mathbf{1.603\;\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg waste}}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) Theoretical air:

$$
m_{\text{air,th}} = \frac{1.603}{0.232} = \mathbf{6.91\;\text{kg air / kg waste}}
$$

(iii) Actual air with 80 % excess ($\lambda = 1.8$):

$$
m_{\text{air,act}} = 1.8 \times 6.91 = \mathbf{12.44\;\text{kg air / kg waste}}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

The four sub-processes are listed in the matrix in Section 3.4 above. Award 1.5 marks per stage, 0.5 mark for the diagram, and 0.5 mark for concluding the **3-T rule** statement (Temperature ≥ 850 °C, Turbulence Re > 10⁴, Time ≥ 2 s).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of waste. Compute the HHV of a waste with composition: **C = 42 %, H = 6 %, O = 35 %, S = 1 %**, using Dulong's formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **advantages and disadvantages of municipal solid waste incineration**. Mention the role of Air Pollution Control Devices (APCDs). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**HHV** is the total heat released when 1 kg of waste is completely combusted **and the water vapor in the flue gas is condensed back to liquid**, recovering its latent heat.

**LHV** is the heat released when 1 kg of waste is combusted **and the water leaves as vapor** (latent heat not recovered). Therefore **HHV > LHV always**.

Using Dulong's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV} &= 338\,C + 1442\!\left(H - \tfrac{O}{8}\right) + 94\,S \\
&= 338(42) + 1442\!\left(6 - \tfrac{35}{8}\right) + 94(1) \\
&= 14196 + 1442(6 - 4.375) + 94 \\
&= 14196 + 1442(1.625) + 94 \\
&= 14196 + 2343.25 + 94 \\
&= \mathbf{16633.25\;\text{kJ/kg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*(Allocate 1 mark per advantage up to 3, 1 mark per disadvantage up to 3, and 1 mark for APCD discussion.)*

**Advantages:** volume reduction, energy recovery, pathogen destruction, land conservation, waste-to-energy economics.
**Disadvantages:** high CAPEX, gaseous emissions (NOx, SOx, dioxins, furans, PM, HCl), bottom-ash & fly-ash disposal, CO₂ footprint, public opposition (NIMBY), skilled labor required.
**APCDs (in sequence):** Dry scrubber (lime for SO₂, HCl) → Baghouse (particulates) → SCR (NOx) → Stack with CEMS (continuous emission monitoring).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> 1. **Do not use 0.21 (volume fraction) for converting O₂ to air in mass-based calculations** — use **0.232 (mass fraction)**. The two are different and using 0.21 will cost you 1 full mark.
> 2. **Dulong's formula requires the *mass percent* of C, H, O, S — not the moles.** Students often confuse this and divide by atomic weights first. Wrong — Dulong's is a *mass-balance* equation.
> 3. **Do not list "disposal of ash" as both a disadvantage and a "by-product advantage."** Pick one and justify.
> 4. **Always state the temperature range (850 – 1200 °C) when defining incineration** — a full-mark definition requires the operating window.
> 5. **Residence time ≥ 2 seconds** is the *regulatory minimum*; if you say "1 second" you will lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Incineration (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Thermal oxidation at **850 – 1200 °C**; produces ash + flue gas + heat.
- ✅ **Four sub-processes:** Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion → Quench.
- ✅ **3-T Rule:** Temperature, Turbulence, Time (≥ 2 s).
- ✅ **Dulong's HHV:** $338C + 1442(H - O/8) + 94S$ — in **kJ/kg**, with **mass %** inputs.
- ✅ **Theoretical air = O₂ / 0.232 (mass basis)** — *not* 0.21.
- ✅ **Excess air** typically **20 – 100 %** ($\lambda = 1.2$ – $2.0$).
- ✅ **VRR ≈ 90 %**, **MRR ≈ 70 %** for MSW.
- ✅ **APC train order:** Dry scrubber → Baghouse → SCR → CEMS.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Volume reduction, energy recovery, pathogen kill, landfill diversion.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** High cost, dioxins/furans, NOx/SOx, fly-ash disposal, CO₂, NIMBY.
- ✅ **Energy recovery efficiency:** **20 – 30 % electric**; **70 – 80 % CHP combined**.
- ✅ **Key reactions:** C + O₂ → CO₂; S + O₂ → SO₂; N + NO/NO₂ (thermal & fuel NOx).
- ✅ **Pyrolysis is endothermic; Combustion is exothermic** — examiners love this contrast.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Incineration Technologies and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Incineration Technologies and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incineration** is a controlled, high-temperature thermal oxidation process in which combustible components of solid, liquid, or gaseous waste are rapidly and completely converted into inert ash, flue gas, and heat through chemical reaction with oxygen. It is governed by the stoichiometric reaction:

$$ \text{Waste} + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + H_2O + SO_x + NO_x + \text{HCl} + \text{Ash} + \text{Heat} $$

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework for *Process Systems Engineering*, incineration is classified as a **high-temperature (>850 °C)** **end-destruction** process — distinct from landfilling, composting, or recycling which are all material-recovery processes.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Incineration (The "Three T's")

> [!NOTE]
> Any well-designed incinerator must simultaneously satisfy three engineering constraints, called the **3-T Rule**:

1. **Temperature** — Combustion chamber gas temperature must exceed **850 °C** for municipal waste and **1100 °C** for hazardous waste (to destroy dioxins).
2. **Time (Residence Time)** — Combustible gases must remain at high temperature for at least **2 seconds** (post-burner chamber).
3. **Turbulence** — Adequate mixing of waste and air via burner geometry, refractory baffles, or fluidization to ensure contact between fuel and oxidizer.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine burning a pile of dry leaves in your garden (open burning):
- Smoke billows out (incomplete combustion → CO, dioxins, soot).
- Neighbours complain about odour.
- The fire is unpredictable and uncontrolled.

Now imagine placing those same leaves inside a sealed metal chamber with:
- A pre-heated air blower (Turbulence).
- A ceramic-lined hot zone (Temperature > 1000 °C).
- A secondary chamber where gases sit for 2 more seconds (Time).

The result is a *nearly invisible* exhaust, a stable bed of ash, and recoverable heat. **That sealed chamber is an incinerator.** It is simply **"combustion with an engineering attitude."**

### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Excess Air:** **30 % to 100 %** for municipal solid waste (MSW); **100 % to 200 %** for hazardous waste.
> - **Thermal Efficiency:** **65 % to 80 %** for mass-burn; **75 % to 85 %** for fluidized bed.
> - **Volume Reduction:** **90 %** by mass and **75 %** by volume for MSW.
> - **DRE (Destruction & Removal Efficiency):** must be **> 99.99 %** for Principal Organic Hazardous Constituents (POHCs).
> - **Stoichiometric Ratio (SR):** **1.0** at theoretical air; practical SR = **1.4 – 2.0**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

Incineration is fundamentally a **heterogeneous combustion process** involving three sequential sub-processes:

1. **Drying Stage** — Endothermic evaporation of moisture (below 200 °C). Energy demand ≈ **2260 kJ/kg** of water removed.
2. **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Stage** (200 – 600 °C) — Volatiles (C, H, O compounds) evolve as gases; fixed carbon remains as char.
3. **Char Combustion Stage** (600 – 1100 °C) — Solid carbon reacts with O₂ via:

$$ C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$

The **Why** behind staged combustion: simultaneous drying + pyrolysis + char burning would cause quenching and incomplete destruction of organics — hence the need for a secondary chamber (post-combustor).

### 2.2 Engineering Significance

Incineration is the **only widely-deployed technology** capable of achieving a **>99.99 % mass destruction** of organic hazardous wastes (e.g., PCB oils, pesticides, biomedical pathogens) as mandated by the **Indian Hazardous Waste Management Rules** and the **Basel Convention**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand (mass basis) | $O_{2,\text{theo}} = 2.67\,C + 8.0\,H + S - O$ | kg O₂ / kg waste | C, H, S, O are mass fractions on dry-ash-free basis |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Air (mass) | $A_{\text{theo}} = \dfrac{O_{2,\text{theo}}}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg waste | Air contains **23.2 %** O₂ by mass |
| 3 | Actual Air with Excess Air EA | $A_{\text{actual}} = A_{\text{theo}} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{EA}{100}\right)$ | kg air / kg waste | EA = 50 % typical for MSW |
| 4 | Higher Heating Value (Dulong) | $HHV = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S$ | kcal/kg | Use ultimate analysis fractions |
| 5 | Lower Heating Value | $LHV = HHV - 9\,H \cdot \Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ | kcal/kg | $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 540$ kcal/kg of H₂O |
| 6 | Heat Released in Combustor | $Q_{\text{release}} = m_{\text{waste}} \cdot LHV$ | kW or kJ/s | Negative sign indicates exothermic |
| 7 | Flue Gas Mass Flow | $m_{\text{fg}} = A_{\text{actual}} + \left(1 - \text{ash fraction}\right)$ | kg/kg waste | Sum of combustion products |
| 8 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{CO_2}{CO_2 + CO} \times 100$ | % | Measured in dry flue gas |
| 9 | Destruction & Removal Efficiency | $DRE = \dfrac{W_{\text{in}} - W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | % | Must exceed **99.99 %** for POHCs |
| 10 | Stoichiometric Ratio | $SR = \dfrac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{theo}}}$ | dimensionless | SR > 1 means excess air |
| 11 | Mean Residence Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{\text{chamber}}}{Q_{\text{fg}} / \rho_{\text{fg}}}$ | seconds | Must exceed **2 s** in secondary chamber |
| 12 | Volumetric Heat Release Rate | $q''' = \dfrac{Q_{\text{release}}}{V_{\text{chamber}}}$ | kW/m³ | 200 – 400 kW/m³ for MSW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical formula boundary conditions for KTU problems:**
> - Air composition: 21 % O₂ / 79 % N₂ by volume ⇒ 23.2 % O₂ / 76.8 % N₂ by mass.
> - Water latent heat used in LHV: **2442 kJ/kg** (≈ 540 kcal/kg).
> - The excess-air penalty: every 1 % excess air lowers adiabatic flame temperature by roughly **3 – 4 °C**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are the backbone of:
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) power plants** (India: Timarpur, Delhi; Okhla).
- **Biomedical Waste Incinerators (BMWIs)** at hospitals (BMW Rules 2016 mandate 850 °C + 1 second residence).
- **Hazardous waste cement kilns** co-processing.
- **Shipboard incinerators** (MARPOL Annex VI compliance).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air Requirement from Ultimate Analysis

> **Problem Statement.** A typical Indian MSW sample has ultimate analysis (mass %, dry basis):
> C = 30 %, H = 4 %, O = 22 %, N = 1 %, S = 0.5 %, Moisture = 25 %, Ash = 17.5 %.
> Compute the theoretical air required to combust 1 kg of this waste.

**Step 1 — Convert to dry-ash-free (daf) basis** by removing moisture and ash:

$$ \text{Normalizing factor} = 1 - 0.25 - 0.175 = 0.575 $$

$$ C_{\text{daf}} = \frac{0.30}{0.575} = 0.5217, \quad H_{\text{daf}} = \frac{0.04}{0.575} = 0.0696 $$
$$ O_{\text{daf}} = \frac{0.22}{0.575} = 0.3826, \quad N_{\text{daf}} = \frac{0.01}{0.575} = 0.0174 $$
$$ S_{\text{daf}} = \frac{0.005}{0.575} = 0.0087 $$

**Step 2 — Apply per-kg-of-waste stoichiometric O₂ formula** (raw, as-fired basis):

$$ O_{2,\text{theo}} = 2.67\,C + 8.0\,H + S - O $$
$$ = (2.67)(0.30) + (8.0)(0.04) + (0.005) - (0.22) $$
$$ = 0.801 + 0.320 + 0.005 - 0.220 = 0.906 \; \text{kg O}_2 \; / \; \text{kg waste} $$

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass** (air = 23.2 % O₂ by mass):

$$ A_{\text{theo}} = \frac{0.906}{0.232} = 3.905 \; \text{kg air} \; / \; \text{kg waste} $$

**Step 4 — Apply 80 % excess air** (typical for MSW):

$$ A_{\text{actual}} = 3.905 \times \left(1 + \frac{80}{100}\right) = 3.905 \times 1.8 = 7.029 \; \text{kg air} \; / \; \text{kg waste} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the Stoichiometric Ratio:**

$$ SR = \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{theo}}} = \frac{7.029}{3.905} = 1.80 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Theoretical air = **3.905 kg air/kg waste**; Actual air (80 % EA) = **7.029 kg air/kg waste**; SR = **1.80**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Higher Heating Value Using Dulong's Equation

Using the same daf composition above:

**Step 1 — Insert daf fractions into Dulong's formula:**

$$ HHV_{\text{daf}} = 8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S $$
$$ = 8080(0.5217) + 34500\!\left(0.0696 - \frac{0.3826}{8}\right) + 2240(0.0087) $$

**Step 2 — Compute each term:**

$$ 8080 \times 0.5217 = 4215.3 \; \text{kcal/kg} $$
$$ \frac{0.3826}{8} = 0.0478 $$
$$ 0.0696 - 0.0478 = 0.0218 $$
$$ 34500 \times 0.0218 = 752.1 \; \text{kcal/kg} $$
$$ 2240 \times 0.0087 = 19.5 \; \text{kcal/kg} $$

**Step 3 — Sum all contributions:**

$$ HHV_{\text{daf}} = 4215.3 + 752.1 + 19.5 = 4986.9 \; \text{kcal/kg}_{\text{daf}} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to as-fired basis** (multiply by non-moisture-non-ash fraction = 0.575):

$$ HHV_{\text{as-fired}} = 4986.9 \times 0.575 = 2867.5 \; \text{kcal/kg} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** HHV ≈ **4987 kcal/kg (daf)** ≈ **2868 kcal/kg (as-fired)** ≈ **12 MJ/kg** — characteristic of high-organic Indian MSW.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT) Estimation

**Step 1 — Energy released per kg waste:**

$$ Q_{\text{in}} = HHV_{\text{as-fired}} = 2868 \; \text{kcal/kg} = 12.0 \; \text{MJ/kg} $$

**Step 2 — Total mass of flue gas per kg waste** (using stoichiometric products + N₂ from air + moisture):

$$ m_{\text{fg}} \approx 1 + A_{\text{actual}} = 1 + 7.029 = 8.029 \; \text{kg/kg waste} $$

**Step 3 — Apply sensible-heat balance** with average flue-gas $C_p \approx 0.26$ kcal/(kg·K):

$$ Q_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{fg}} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{\text{AFT}} - T_{\text{ref}}) $$

$$ 2868 = 8.029 \times 0.26 \times (T_{\text{AFT}} - 298) $$

$$ T_{\text{AFT}} - 298 = \frac{2868}{2.088} = 1373.6 \; \text{K} $$

$$ T_{\text{AFT}} = 1671.6 \; \text{K} \approx 1398 \; °\text{C} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Real flame temperatures are **150–300 °C lower** than AFT due to radiation losses, incomplete combustion, and dissociation. The *actual* combustion chamber gas temperature falls in the **1100–1250 °C** band — exactly in the safe operating window for MSW.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Incinerator Types

| Parameter | Mass-Burn Grate | Rotary Kiln | Fluidized Bed | Multiple Hearth | Liquid Injection |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Waste Type** | MSW, bulky solids | Hazardous, sludges | Pre-shredded MSW, sludges | Biosolids, pastes | Liquids, gases |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | 850 – 1100 | 1100 – 1450 | 750 – 900 | 700 – 900 | 1000 – 1400 |
| **Residence Time** | 1 – 3 h (solids) | 1 – 4 h (solids) | 5 – 20 min (solids) | 1 – 4 h (solids) | 1 – 2 s (gases) |
| **Excess Air** | 80 – 120 % | 100 – 200 % | 40 – 60 % | 60 – 100 % | 10 – 30 % |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | 65 – 75 % | 60 – 70 % | 78 – 85 % | 60 – 70 % | 80 – 90 % |
| **Pre-Treatment Required** | None | Optional shredding | Mandatory <50 mm | None | Atomization |
| **CO Emissions (mg/Nm³)** | 50 – 200 | 30 – 100 | 20 – 80 | 80 – 200 | < 50 |
| **DRE for POHCs** | 99.9 % | **> 99.9999 %** | 99.99 % | 99.9 % | > 99.99 % |
| **Capital Cost (Relative)** | High | Very High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Footprint** | Large | Very Large | Compact | Tall | Compact |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mass-Burn Grate Incinerator (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Waste Receipt and Weighbridge] --> A2[Waste Pit Storage 3 to 5 days]
    A2 --> A3[Overhead Crane Grab Bucket]
    A3 --> A4[Feed Chute Hydraulic Ram]
    A4 --> B1[Primary Combustion Chamber]
    B1 -->|Flue Gas| B2[Secondary Combustion Chamber 1100 C 2 s]
    B2 -->|Hot Gas| C1[Superheater Water Wall Boiler]
    C1 --> C2[Economiser]
    C2 --> C3[Air Preheater]
    C3 --> D1[Quench Dry Scrubber]
    D1 --> D2[Baghouse Filter PM 10 mg per Nm3]
    D2 --> D3[Scrubber SOx HCl]
    D3 --> D4[Selective Catalytic Reduction NOx]
    D4 --> D5[Stack 60 m Height]
    C1 -->|Steam| E1[Steam Turbine Generator]
    E1 --> E2[Condenser and Grid Export]
    B1 --> F1[Ash Hopper Bottom Ash 25 percent]
    F1 --> F2[Ash Metal Recovery]
    F2 --> F3[Quench Tank Wet Ash]
    D2 --> F4[Fly Ash Hopper APC Residue]
    F4 --> F5[Stabilisation Cement Solidification]
    A1 -->|Reject and recyclables| A5[Material Recovery Facility MRF]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Rotary Kiln Hazardous Waste Incinerator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Liquid Waste Tank Farm] --> R2[Atomiser Burner]
    R1b[Drum and Container Solids] --> R3[Kiln Loading Chute]
    R3 --> K1[Rotary Kiln Primary Chamber 1150 C]
    K1 -->|Rotation 1 to 5 rpm| K2[Slag Discharge End Ash Lock Hopper]
    R2 --> K1
    K1 -->|Hot Gas| S1[Secondary Combustion Chamber 1200 C 2 s]
    S1 --> S2[Waste Heat Boiler]
    S2 --> S3[Quench Tower Rapid Cool to 200 C]
    S3 --> S4[Bag Filter and HEPA]
    S4 --> S5[Wet Scrubber 2 Stage]
    S5 --> S6[Stack with CEMS Continuous Emission Monitoring]
    S6 --> S7[Compliance Report CPCB SPCB]
    K2 --> Z1[Slag Quench and Metal Recovery]
    Z1 --> Z2[Landfill Secured Hazardous Site]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fluidized Bed Incinerator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Shredder 50 mm Size Reduction] --> P2[Air Dry Sand Bed Fluidisation]
    P2 --> P3[Windbox Distributor Plate]
    P3 --> FB1[FB Combustor 750 to 900 C]
    FB1 -->|Hot Flue Gas| FB2[Cyclone Recirculation Bed Material]
    FB2 -->|Return| FB1
    FB1 --> FB3[Freeboard Secondary Air]
    FB3 --> H1[Waste Heat Boiler Steam 40 bar]
    H1 --> H2[Economiser]
    H2 --> H3[Air Preheater]
    H3 --> A1[Bag Filter]
    A1 --> A2[Spray Dryer Acid Gas]
    A2 --> A3[Fabric Filter]
    A3 --> A4[SCR Unit NOx Reduction]
    A4 --> A5[Stack]
    FB1 --> B1[Bottom Ash Overbed Extraction]
    B1 --> B2[Sand Cooler]
    B2 --> P2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flow: Air Pollution Control (APC) Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Hot Flue Gas 1100 C from Combustor] --> X2[Superheater Cool to 450 C]
    X2 --> X3[Economiser Cool to 250 C]
    X3 --> X4[Air Preheater Cool to 180 C]
    X4 --> X5[Quench Spray Dryer Cool to 150 C]
    X5 --> X6[Baghouse Particulate 10 mg per Nm3]
    X6 --> X7[Wet Scrubber SOx HCl Removal]
    X7 --> X8[Reheater to 350 C]
    X8 --> X9[SCR NOx Reduction]
    X9 --> X10[Stack ID Fan and Discharge]
    X6 --> Y1[APC Residue Fly Ash Hazardous]
    Y1 --> Y2[Stabilisation Solidification Cement]
    Y2 --> Y3[Secure Landfill Disposal]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 S1]`

**Q:** Define incineration. List the **"3-T Rule"** with the numerical values required for municipal solid waste.

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Incineration is the controlled high-temperature (≥ 850 °C) oxidation of combustible waste into inert ash, flue gas, and recoverable heat in an enclosed combustion chamber.
- **3-T Rule:**
  1. **Temperature:** ≥ 850 °C (post-combustion chamber) for MSW; ≥ 1100 °C for hazardous waste.
  2. **Time:** ≥ 2 seconds residence of combustible gases.
  3. **Turbulence:** Adequate air–fuel mixing through burner geometry or fluidization.

*(3 marks – 1 for definition + 2 for 3-T rule with values)*

---

#### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 S2]`

**Q:** What is **Destruction and Removal Efficiency (DRE)**? What is its legal threshold for Principal Organic Hazardous Constituents (POHCs)?

**Model Answer:**
- **DRE:** The percentage of a specific organic compound destroyed and removed from the waste stream, expressed as:

$$ DRE = \frac{W_{\text{in}} - W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} \times 100 $$

- **Legal Threshold:** DRE ≥ **99.99 %** (a "four-nine" standard) for POHCs under the **US EPA 40 CFR 264.343** and Indian Hazardous Waste Rules 2008 (and 2016 amendment).

*(3 marks – 2 for formula + 1 for threshold)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each 14-mark question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Either Q1 or Q2 must be answered. Average time allocation: **18–22 minutes** per 14-mark question.

---

#### Question B1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 S3]`

**(a)** A hospital is procuring a **Biomedical Waste Incinerator (BMWI)** with a capacity of **100 kg/h**. The waste has the following ultimate analysis (mass %, as-received):
**C = 22 %, H = 3 %, O = 18 %, N = 2 %, S = 0.3 %, Moisture = 35 %, Ash = 19.7 %**.
Compute:
1. Theoretical air required (kg air/kg waste).
2. Actual air with **120 % excess air**.
3. Stoichiometric Ratio (SR).

**(b)** Briefly explain the difference between **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)**. With a sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Mass-Burn Grate Incinerator**.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B1(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** As-fired analysis of biomedical waste, 100 kg/h, 120 % excess air.

**Step 1 — Direct stoichiometric O₂ from as-fired mass fractions:**

$$ O_{2,\text{theo}} = 2.67\,C + 8.0\,H + S - O $$
$$ = (2.67)(0.22) + (8.0)(0.03) + (0.003) - (0.18) $$
$$ = 0.5874 + 0.240 + 0.003 - 0.180 = 0.6504 \; \text{kg O}_2 \; / \; \text{kg waste} $$

**Step 2 — Theoretical air (mass basis):**

$$ A_{\text{theo}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{theo}}}{0.232} = \frac{0.6504}{0.232} = 2.803 \; \text{kg air / kg waste} $$

*(2 marks for O₂ step + 2 marks for air step)*

**Step 3 — Actual air with EA = 120 %:**

$$ A_{\text{actual}} = A_{\text{theo}} \times \left(1 + \frac{120}{100}\right) = 2.803 \times 2.20 = 6.167 \; \text{kg air / kg waste} $$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric Ratio:**

$$ SR = \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{theo}}} = \frac{6.167}{2.803} = 2.20 $$

*(2 marks for EA + 1 mark for SR)*

> **Final Answers:** $A_{\text{theo}} = 2.803$ kg/kg, $A_{\text{actual}} = 6.167$ kg/kg, $SR = 2.20$.

##### Model Solution for Question B1(b) — 7 Marks

**HHV vs LHV — Conceptual Comparison (3 marks):**

> **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** Heat released assuming **all water vapour** in the combustion products condenses to liquid (latent heat recovered). Used in laboratory bomb calorimetry.
>
> **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** Heat released when water remains as **vapour** at the flue-gas exit temperature (~150 °C). This is the *realistic* value for plant heat-balance calculations.

$$ LHV = HHV - 9\,H \cdot 540 \quad (\text{kcal/kg}) $$

where 9 = ratio of water produced per unit mass of H (18/2), and 540 = latent heat of vaporization of water (kcal/kg).

**Mass-Burn Grate Incinerator Construction & Working (4 marks):**

- **Feed Section:** Waste pit, overhead grab crane, feed chute, hydraulic ram feeder.
- **Grate:** Inclined or travelling grate divided into drying, burning, and burnout zones.
- **Primary Chamber:** Refractory-lined; waste burns on the moving grate with primary air injected beneath.
- **Secondary Chamber:** Post-combustor at **1000–1100 °C** with ≥ 2 s gas residence.
- **Heat Recovery:** Water-wall boiler → steam turbine.
- **Air Pollution Control:** Quench, baghouse, scrubber, SCR.
- **Ash:** Bottom ash discharged at grate end; fly ash from APC.

---

#### Question B2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 S4]`

**(a)** Discuss the **principle, construction, advantages, and limitations** of a **Fluidized Bed Incinerator (FBI)**. Why is it preferred for sludge incineration?

**(b)** A municipal corporation receives **500 tonnes/day** of MSW with HHV = **1200 kcal/kg (as-fired)**. The incinerator operates with **100 % excess air** and steam generation at **40 bar, 400 °C**. Compute:
1. Total heat released per hour.
2. Mass of flue gas per kg waste.
3. Net steam generation in kg/h (assuming 80 % heat-to-steam efficiency).

---

##### Model Solution for Question B2(a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (2 marks):**
A bed of silica sand (100–300 µm) is fluidized by primary air through a distributor plate. Shredded waste (≤ 50 mm) is injected either overbed or in-bed, where the high heat-transfer coefficient (≈ 250 W/m²·K) of the inert bed ensures rapid, uniform combustion at 750–900 °C.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Windbox with distributor plate.
- Fluidizing chamber (refractory-lined).
- Cyclone for bed-material recirculation.
- Secondary air injection in freeboard.
- Waste-heat boiler, APC train.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Compact footprint (residence time only 5–20 min).
- Excellent temperature uniformity (±20 °C).
- High thermal efficiency (78–85 %).
- Low excess air (40–60 %).
- Suitable for heterogeneous and high-moisture waste (e.g., sewage sludge).

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Requires shredding to < 50 mm.
- Bed agglomeration with high-ash / low-melting wastes.
- Higher pressure drop → fan power.

**Why preferred for sludge:** High moisture (up to 80 %), sticky nature, and high ash content of sludge are handled effectively by the inert bed's high heat-transfer rate.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B2(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** 500 TPD MSW; HHV = 1200 kcal/kg; EA = 100 %; η_steam = 80 %.

**Step 1 — Waste feed rate:**

$$ m_{\text{waste}} = \frac{500 \times 1000}{24} = 20833.3 \; \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Total heat released:**

$$ Q_{\text{in}} = m_{\text{waste}} \times HHV = 20833.3 \times 1200 = 2.5 \times 10^7 \; \text{kcal/h} $$
$$ = 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 4.186 = 1.0465 \times 10^8 \; \text{kJ/h} \approx 29.07 \; \text{MW}_{\text{th}} $$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Mass of flue gas:**
Assume HHV-from-stoichiometry implies $O_{2,\text{theo}} \approx 0.906$ kg/kg (typical for 1200 kcal/kg waste) and ash fraction ≈ 25 %:

$$ A_{\text{theo}} = \frac{0.906}{0.232} = 3.905 \; \text{kg/kg} $$
$$ A_{\text{actual}} = 3.905 \times 2.0 = 7.81 \; \text{kg air/kg} $$
$$ m_{\text{fg}} \approx A_{\text{actual}} + 1 - \text{ash} = 7.81 + 0.75 = 8.56 \; \text{kg/kg waste} $$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Steam generation:**
Steam enthalpy at 40 bar, 400 °C: $h_{\text{steam}} \approx 3214$ kJ/kg; feed-water at 100 °C: $h_{\text{fw}} \approx 419$ kJ/kg.
$\Delta h = 3214 - 419 = 2795$ kJ/kg steam.

$$ \eta = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \times \Delta h}{Q_{\text{in}}} $$
$$ 0.80 = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \times 2795}{1.0465 \times 10^8} $$
$$ \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = \frac{0.80 \times 1.0465 \times 10^8}{2795} = 29{,}956 \; \text{kg/h} \approx 30 \; \text{TPH} $$

*(3 marks)*

> **Final Answers:** $Q_{\text{in}} \approx 29.07$ MW; $m_{\text{fg}} \approx 8.56$ kg/kg; Steam ≈ **30 TPH** ≈ **7.2 MWe** (at 24 % turbo-generator efficiency).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that KTU examiners consistently penalize:**
> 1. **Confusing mass % and mole %** for ultimate analysis. Ultimate analysis is **always mass basis**.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract oxygen** in the stoichiometric air formula (carbon-bound oxygen already accounts for some O demand).
> 3. **Using HHV instead of LHV** for adiabatic flame-temperature calculations → overestimates by **8–12 %**.
> 4. **Ignoring the 0.575 (or equivalent) dry-basis correction** when Dulong's formula is applied to as-fired waste with high moisture/ash.
> 5. **Writing 21 %** (mole) instead of **23.2 %** (mass) for air O₂ fraction when converting from $O_{2,\text{theo}}$ to $A_{\text{theo}}$.
> 6. **Mixing units**: kcal vs kJ vs MJ (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ). Examiners deduct **½ mark per unit mismatch**.
> 7. **Sketching the incinerator without labelling** the secondary chamber, residence time, and air injection points → loses 2 marks even if the description is correct.
> 8. **Omitting the APC train** in the block diagram → 2 marks lost because KTU examiners specifically look for environmental compliance linkage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Incineration Technologies (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Definition:** High-T (>850 °C) enclosed oxidation of waste into inert ash + flue gas + heat.
- ✅ **3-T Rule:** Temperature (850 °C MSW / 1100 °C hazardous), Time (≥ 2 s), Turbulence (mixing).
- ✅ **Excess Air:** 80–120 % MSW; 100–200 % hazardous; 40–60 % fluidized bed.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric Air Formula (mass basis):** $A = (2.67\,C + 8\,H + S - O)/0.232$.
- ✅ **Dulong's HHV:** $8080\,C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240\,S$ in kcal/kg (daf).
- ✅ **LHV Correction:** Subtract $9\,H \times 540$ kcal/kg.
- ✅ **DRE Standard:** ≥ 99.99 % for POHCs (four-nine).
- ✅ **Stoichiometric Ratio (SR):** 1.4–2.0 in practice.
- ✅ **Volume Reduction:** 90 % mass, 75 % volume (MSW).
- ✅ **Five Major Incinerator Types:** Mass-Burn Grate, Rotary Kiln, Fluidized Bed, Multiple Hearth, Liquid Injection.
- ✅ **Rotary Kiln = Highest DRE** (> 99.9999 %) — preferred for hazardous waste.
- ✅ **Fluidized Bed = Highest thermal efficiency** (78–85 %) — preferred for sludge and pre-shredded MSW.
- ✅ **Air Pollution Control Train:** Quench → Baghouse → Scrubber → SCR → Stack (with CEMS).
- ✅ **Critical flue-gas pollutants:** PM (< 30 mg/Nm³), SO₂ (< 200 mg/Nm³), HCl (< 50 mg/Nm³), NOx (< 400 mg/Nm³), dioxins (< 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³).
- ✅ **Ash Categories:** Bottom ash (25–30 % of input) can be used in road construction; fly ash (3–5 %) is hazardous and requires stabilization before secure landfill.
- ✅ **CPCB / MoEFCC Indian Compliance:** BMW Rules 2016 (Healthcare), Hazardous Waste Rules 2016 (Industry), MSW Rules 2016 (Municipalities).
- ✅ **Remember:** $1 \text{ kg air} = 0.232 \text{ kg O}_2 + 0.768 \text{ kg N}_2$ and $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.186 \text{ kJ}$ — these constants appear in **every** KTU numerical.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Incineration Technologies and Their Applications*
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based pedagogy (CO2 / CO3 alignment).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyrolysis: Types, Process, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pyrolysis: Types, Process, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pyrolysis** is defined as the **thermochemical decomposition of organic (carbonaceous) materials at elevated temperatures (typically 400 °C – 900 °C) in the complete absence of oxygen, or with a limited, sub-stoichiometric oxygen supply**. The term originates from the Greek elements *pyr* (fire) and *lysis* (separating), literally meaning "fire-breaking-down."

In the KTU 2024 Process Systems Engineering context, pyrolysis is classified as a **non-combustion thermal conversion process** where the long-chain hydrocarbon molecules of the feedstock are broken into smaller, more useful molecules through controlled heat application. The three primary products formed in varying proportions are:

1. **Pyrolytic gas (non-condensable)** – mainly H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, C₂H₄
2. **Pyrolytic liquid (bio-oil / tar)** – a complex mixture of water, acids, alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, and aromatics
3. **Pyrolytic solid (char / biochar)** – a carbon-rich solid residue with high fixed-carbon content

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pyrolysis is a *UNIT OPERATION* in chemical process engineering. It differs fundamentally from combustion (exothermic, uses O₂) and gasification (uses limited O₂/steam and produces syngas). Pyrolysis is **endothermic** and operates in an oxygen-starved environment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a slice of bread and put it inside a sealed metal container, then place this container inside a hot oven at 500 °C for some time. Since no air (oxygen) can reach the bread, it will not catch fire. Instead, the bread will:
- Turn black and crispy (the **char/biochar**),
- Release a brownish, pungent vapor that, when cooled, condenses into a dark, oily liquid (the **bio-oil/tar**),
- And emit a flammable gas (the **pyrolytic gas**) that you can actually light with a match.

This is exactly what happens in an industrial pyrolysis reactor! The "absence of oxygen" prevents complete burning, forcing the molecules to break down chemically rather than combust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The making of charcoal in traditional kilns is one of the oldest forms of **slow pyrolysis**. The char yield is maximized by keeping the temperature low and the residence time long.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Process Constants

The following standard metrics are essential for KTU exam preparation:

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **400 – 900** | °C |
| Heating Rate | **0.1 – 1000** | °C/s |
| Residence Time of Vapors | **0.1 – 10** | s |
| Particle Size (feed) | **< 5** | mm |
| Feedstock Moisture | **< 10** | wt% |
| Carrier Gas (N₂) Flow | **0.5 – 5** | L/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no oxygen?** If oxygen were present, the hydrocarbons would oxidize (combust) to CO₂ + H₂O, releasing huge amounts of heat and destroying all valuable products. The absence of O₂ preserves the carbon backbone, allowing the formation of char, condensable liquids, and useful gases.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Concept

The pyrolysis process can be systematically broken down into the following logical steps:

- **Step 1: Heat Transfer Initiation**
  The solid feedstock (biomass, polymer, waste) enters the reactor. External heat is supplied through a furnace wall, hot gas, or molten salt. Energy must first overcome the **moisture evaporation enthalpy** (~2260 kJ/kg) and then supply the **latent heat for devolatilization**.

- **Step 2: Moisture Removal (Drying Zone, 100 – 150 °C)**
  Free and bound water evaporates. This is an *endothermic* step requiring substantial energy. In KTU problems, the energy for drying must be added to the heat balance.

- **Step 3: Devolatilization / Pyrolysis Reaction (200 – 600 °C)**
  The organic matter decomposes through a complex network of reactions:
  - *Primary pyrolysis:* Cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin break into volatiles + char.
  - *Secondary pyrolysis:* The primary vapors may undergo cracking, reforming, and condensation to yield lighter gases and secondary char.
  - The governing reaction rate is typically assumed **first-order** in the residual solid mass.

- **Step 4: Volatiles Quenching & Condensation (Fast Cooling)**
  The hot vapors are rapidly cooled to ~100 °C using a condenser, separating the **bio-oil** (condensable fraction) from the **non-condensable gases** (H₂, CO, CH₄).

- **Step 5: Solid Char Collection**
  The remaining carbon-rich solid is collected from the bottom of the reactor.

- **Step 6: Energy Recovery**
  A portion of the non-condensable gases is recycled and combusted to provide process heat, making the system **energy self-sufficient** at steady state.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why is the absence of oxygen essential?** Because the *Gibbs free energy change* (ΔG) of oxidation reactions is much more negative than that of pyrolysis. If O₂ is present, combustion dominates and exothermic heat destroys the desired products.
- **How are products separated?** Based on **condensation points** — high-boiling compounds (tars) condense first, then water, while low-MW gases (H₂, CH₄) remain gaseous.
- **How is selectivity controlled?** By precisely tuning three key variables: **temperature, heating rate, and residence time** (this is the central theme of the "types of pyrolysis" classification in Section 2.4).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations form the **mathematical backbone** of all KTU pyrolysis problems.

| # | Equation | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant; $A$ = pre-exponential factor (s⁻¹), $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = k (1 - X)^n$ | Solid conversion kinetics; $X$ = fractional conversion, $n$ = reaction order (usually 1) |
| 3 | $X = 1 - \exp(-k t)$ | Integrated first-order conversion; $t$ = residence time |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{yield},i} = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{\text{feed}}} \times 100\%$ | Mass yield of product *i* (char, oil, or gas) |
| 5 | $m_{\text{feed}} = m_{\text{char}} + m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{gas}} + m_{\text{water}}$ | Overall mass balance |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{req}} = m \left[ c_{p,\text{dry}} \Delta T + \Delta H_{\text{pyr}} \right]$ | Process heat duty; $\Delta H_{\text{pyr}}$ ≈ 1000 – 1800 kJ/kg |
| 7 | $P_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{gas}} \cdot LHV_{\text{gas}}}{m_{\text{feed}} \cdot Q_{\text{req}}}$ | Energy self-sufficiency ratio |
| 8 | $E_{\text{activation,blend}} = \sum x_i E_{a,i}$ | Rule of mixtures for multi-component feeds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Arrhenius equation (Eq. 1) and the integrated first-order kinetics (Eq. 3) appear in **almost every numerical problem** in this module. Memorize the form and the units carefully.

### 2.4 Classification of Pyrolysis — The Process Triangle

The product distribution of pyrolysis depends almost entirely on three control variables: **Temperature (T), Heating Rate (HR), and Vapor Residence Time (VRT)**.

| Type | Temperature (°C) | Heating Rate (°C/s) | VRT (s) | Major Product (Yield) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow (Carbonisation)** | 300 – 500 | 0.1 – 1 | Long (300 – 1800) | **Char (≈ 35 %)** |
| **Intermediate** | 500 – 650 | 1 – 10 | Moderate (10 – 30) | Char + Oil + Gas (balanced) |
| **Fast Pyrolysis** | 500 – 650 | 10 – 200 | Short (0.5 – 2) | **Bio-oil (≈ 70 – 75 %)** |
| **Flash Pyrolysis** | 700 – 900 | > 1000 | Very short (< 0.5) | **Gas (≈ 70 – 80 %)** |
| **Vacuum Pyrolysis** | 300 – 450 | 5 – 30 | Long (under 5 kPa) | **Bio-oil (≈ 50 – 60 %)** |
| **Hydro-Pyrolysis** | 350 – 600 | — | — | **Bio-oil (high quality)** — H₂ under pressure |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Waste Management:** Conversion of mixed plastic waste and municipal solid waste (MSW) into fuel — a circular-economy solution.
- **Renewable Energy:** Production of second-generation bio-fuels (bio-oil) for boilers, engines, and refineries.
- **Agriculture:** Biochar as a soil amendment to improve water retention and sequester atmospheric CO₂.
- **Chemical Industry:** Source of specialty chemicals (levoglucosan, furfural, phenols) from lignocellulosic biomass.
- **Carbon Capture:** Pyrolysis-derived char can be activated to produce **activated carbon** for adsorption and CO₂ capture.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Integrated First-Order Pyrolysis Kinetics

This derivation is the **foundation** for almost every KTU numerical question on pyrolysis kinetics.

**Given:** The decomposition of solid biomass obeys a first-order rate law with respect to the unconverted solid mass $m_s$.

**Rate Equation:**

$$r = -\frac{dm_s}{dt} = k \, m_s$$

**Substitution using conversion fraction:** Let $X$ be the fractional conversion of the solid, defined as:

$$X = \frac{m_{s,0} - m_s}{m_{s,0}}$$

**Row-by-row logical conversion:**

1. Rearranging the definition for $m_s$: $m_s = m_{s,0} (1 - X)$.
2. Differentiating with respect to $t$: $\dfrac{dm_s}{dt} = -m_{s,0} \dfrac{dX}{dt}$.
3. Substituting into the rate equation: $m_{s,0} \dfrac{dX}{dt} = k \, m_{s,0} (1 - X)$.
4. Cancelling $m_{s,0}$ from both sides: $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = k (1 - X)$.
5. Separating variables: $\dfrac{dX}{1 - X} = k \, dt$.
6. Integrating from $X=0$ at $t=0$ to $X=X$ at $t=t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{X} \frac{dX'}{1 - X'} &= \int_{0}^{t} k \, dt' \\
-\ln(1 - X) &= k t \\
\ln(1 - X) &= -k t \\
1 - X &= e^{-k t} \\
\boxed{X = 1 - e^{-k t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** When $k t = 1$, the conversion $X = 1 - 1/e \approx 0.632$ (i.e., **63.2 %** of the solid has decomposed). This is the "**time constant**" of the pyrolysis reactor.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Temperature Dependence via the Arrhenius Equation

**Given:** The rate constant $k$ varies with temperature $T$ according to:

$$k = A \, e^{-E_a / R T}$$

**Substituting into the integrated form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= 1 - \exp\!\left( - A t \, e^{-E_a / R T} \right) \\
\ln\!\left[ -\ln(1 - X) \right] &= \ln(A t) - \frac{E_a}{R T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The final linear form is the **basis of experimental kinetic analysis**:

$$\boxed{Y = a + b \left( \frac{1}{T} \right) \quad \text{where} \quad Y = \ln[-\ln(1-X)]}$$

- Slope $b = -E_a / R$  →  gives the **activation energy** $E_a = -b R$.
- Intercept $a = \ln(A t)$  →  gives the **pre-exponential factor** $A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Plotting $\ln[-\ln(1-X)]$ vs. $1/T$ and extracting $E_a$ from the slope is a **classic 7-mark problem** under the "Process Optimization & Kinetics" theme of Module 2.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Balance for a Pyrolysis Reactor

For a steady-state, well-mixed, adiabatic reactor with no heat losses, the **first law of thermodynamics** reduces to:

$$Q_{\text{in}} + Q_{\text{reaction}} = Q_{\text{out}} + Q_{\text{accumulation}}$$

At steady state, accumulation is zero. Assuming the feed enters at $T_{\text{in}}$ and products leave at $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{reactor}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \, c_{p,\text{feed}} (T_{\text{reactor}} - T_{\text{in}}) + \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \, \Delta H_{\text{pyr}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{products}} \, c_{p,\text{products}} (T_{\text{reactor}} - T_{\text{ref}})
\end{aligned}
$$

For **endothermic pyrolysis** ($\Delta H_{\text{pyr}} > 0$):

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{\text{external}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \left[ c_p (T_{\text{reactor}} - T_{\text{in}}) + \Delta H_{\text{pyr}} \right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** This is the equation used by process engineers to size the **furnace duty** of an industrial pyrolysis plant. Typical values of $\Delta H_{\text{pyr}}$ for biomass are **800 – 1800 kJ/kg**, depending on the lignin content (lignin-rich feeds require more energy).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)
