**Problem:** A biomass pyrolysis reactor operates isothermally at 550 °C. The pre-exponential factor is $A = 1.2 \times 10^{8}$ s⁻¹ and the activation energy is $E_a = 110$ kJ/mol. Determine the fractional conversion $X$ for a solid residence time of $t = 0.8$ s. Assume first-order kinetics.

**Step 1: Compute the rate constant using the Arrhenius equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 550 + 273 = 823 \text{ K} \\
k &= A \, e^{-E_a / R T} \\
  &= 1.2 \times 10^{8} \cdot \exp\!\left[ -\frac{110{,}000}{8.314 \times 823} \right] \\
  &= 1.2 \times 10^{8} \cdot \exp(-16.07) \\
  &= 1.2 \times 10^{8} \times 1.04 \times 10^{-7} \\
  &= 12.48 \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the first-order integrated kinetic expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= 1 - e^{-k t} \\
  &= 1 - e^{-12.48 \times 0.8} \\
  &= 1 - e^{-9.984} \\
  &= 1 - 4.61 \times 10^{-5} \\
  &\approx 0.99995
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $X \approx 0.9999$ (≈ 100 % conversion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** At 550 °C, with $A$ and $E_a$ in this range, the reaction is **extremely fast**. This is why fast pyrolysis reactors must be designed for **very short vapor residence times (≈ 1 s)** to maximize bio-oil yield before secondary cracking occurs.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Variables on Product Distribution

| Operating Variable | Bio-oil Yield | Char Yield | Gas Yield | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Temperature (500 → 800 °C)** | ↓ (peaks at 550) | ↓↓ | ↑↑ | Secondary cracking of vapors |
| **↑ Heating Rate (1 → 1000 °C/s)** | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Rapid devolatilization |
| **↓ Vapor Residence Time (10 → 0.5 s)** | ↑ | → | ↓ | Prevents secondary reactions |
| **↑ Feed Particle Size (1 → 5 mm)** | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | Heat/mass transfer limitations |
| **↓ Feed Moisture (50 → 5 %)** | ↑ (quality ↑) | → | → | Less energy wasted on drying |
| **↑ Pressure** | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Suppresses volatile escape |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Pyrolysis Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[Biomass Feedstock] --> dryB[Drying Unit]
    dryB --> sizeC[Size Reduction - 2 to 5 mm]
    sizeC --> reactD[Pyrolysis Reactor 400 to 900 C]
    reactD --> charE[Char Collection Hopper]
    reactD --> coolF[Quench Cooler - Rapid Cooling]
    coolF --> condG[Condenser - Bio-oil Recovery]
    condG --> oilH[Bio-oil Storage Tank]
    condG --> gasI[Non-condensable Gas - H2 CO CH4]
    gasI --> splitJ[Gas Splitter]
    splitJ --> burnK[Combustor - Process Heat Source]
    burnK --> reactD
    splitJ --> ventL[Excess Gas Flare or Export]
    charE --> biocharM[Biochar Pelletizer]
    biocharM --> productN[Biochar Product]
    oilH --> productO[Bio-oil Product]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This is a **classic KTU process-flow question**. The energy loop (gas → combustor → reactor) is critical to achieving *energy self-sufficiency*. Marks are awarded for correctly identifying recycle streams and the absence of an air/oxygen inlet.

### 4.2 Decision Logic for Choosing the Type of Pyrolysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Desired Primary Product] --> decB{Decision 1}
    decB --> yesC{Target Char}
    decB --> yesD{Target Bio-oil}
    decB --> yesE{Target Gas}
    yesC --> slowF[Slow Pyrolysis - 400 C - Long VRT - Char 35 percent]
    yesD --> fastG[Fast Pyrolysis - 550 C - Short VRT - Bio-oil 70 percent]
    yesE --> flashH[Flash Pyrolysis - 800 C - High HR - Gas 80 percent]
    fastG --> altI{Vacuum Needed for Heat Sensitive Feed}
    altI --> vacJ[Vacuum Pyrolysis - 350 C - 5 kPa - Bio-oil 60 percent]
    altI --> hydK[Hydro-Pyrolysis - 500 C - H2 - High quality oil]
```

### 4.3 Pyrolysis Reaction Network (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bioA[Biomass - Cellulose Hemicellulose Lignin] --> primB[Primary Pyrolysis - 200 to 600 C]
    primB --> char1[Primary Char]
    primB --> vol1[Primary Vapors]
    vol1 --> secC[Secondary Reactions - Cracking Reforming]
    secC --> gas1[Non-condensable Gas - H2 CO CH4]
    secC --> oil1[Bio-oil Tar]
    secC --> char2[Secondary Char - Coke]
    oil1 --> coolD[Quench - 100 C]
    coolD --> oil2[Condensed Bio-oil]
    coolD --> gas2[Permanent Gases]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Note:** The *secondary char* (coke) is what forms on reactor walls during *fast* pyrolysis if the vapor residence time is too long. This is a major engineering problem called "coking" and is worth 4–6 marks in viva/short-answer questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define pyrolysis. Differentiate it from combustion and gasification.*

**Model Answer:**

Pyrolysis is the **thermochemical decomposition of organic materials at 400–900 °C in the absence (or near-absence) of oxygen**, yielding char, bio-oil, and non-condensable gases.

| Feature | Pyrolysis | Combustion | Gasification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oxygen Supply | None / trace | Stoichiometric excess | Sub-stoichiometric |
| Temperature | 400 – 900 °C | 800 – 1500 °C | 700 – 1200 °C |
| Heat Effect | Endothermic | Highly exothermic | Mildly endothermic |
| Main Product | Char + Oil + Gas | CO₂ + H₂O | Syngas (CO + H₂) |
| Air-Fuel Ratio (ER) | 0 | > 1 | 0.2 – 0.4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *pyrolysis* and *gasification*. Remember: gasification is about producing **syngas** as the primary product, while pyrolysis is about producing a **triple product mix** of char, oil, and gas.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the three main products of biomass pyrolysis and state two factors that influence their relative yields.*

**Model Answer:**

The three main products are:
1. **Char** (solid carbon-rich residue)
2. **Bio-oil** (condensable liquid, dark brown, complex)
3. **Non-condensable gases** (H₂, CO, CO₂, CH₄, C₂H₄, C₂H₆)

Two factors influencing yields:
- **Operating temperature** — higher T favours gas; lower T favours char.
- **Heating rate** — high HR favours bio-oil; low HR favours char.
- (Bonus: vapor residence time, particle size, feedstock composition.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Classify the different types of pyrolysis with reference to temperature, heating rate, vapor residence time, and the dominant product obtained. Explain the mechanism of *fast pyrolysis* in producing bio-oil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pyrolysis reactor processes 500 kg/h of dried biomass (moisture < 5 %) at 600 °C. The product distribution is: char = 25 %, bio-oil = 60 %, gas = 15 % (by mass). The heating value of the non-condensable gas is 18 MJ/kg. Calculate:
  (i) The mass flow rate of each product (kg/h).
  (ii) The energy recovered in the gas per hour (MJ/h).
  (iii) Whether the process is energy self-sufficient if the pyrolysis enthalpy requirement is 1.2 MJ/kg of feed. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The classification of pyrolysis is best summarized in tabular form:

| Type | T (°C) | HR (°C/s) | VRT (s) | Dominant Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Slow | 300 – 500 | 0.1 – 1 | 300 – 1800 | Char (≈ 35 %) |
| Intermediate | 500 – 650 | 1 – 10 | 10 – 30 | All three (balanced) |
| Fast | 500 – 650 | 10 – 200 | 0.5 – 2 | **Bio-oil (≈ 70 %)** |
| Flash | 700 – 900 | > 1000 | < 0.5 | Gas (≈ 80 %) |
| Vacuum | 300 – 450 | 5 – 30 | long (low P) | Bio-oil (≈ 55 %) |

**Mechanism of Fast Pyrolysis (Bio-oil Production):**
1. **Rapid heating** of fine biomass particles (≈ 1–3 mm) to ≈ 500 °C in < 1 s.
2. **Immediate quenching** of the evolved vapors to ≈ 100 °C, which **"freezes" the condensable fraction** before secondary cracking can occur.
3. The short vapor residence time (0.5 – 2 s) is achieved using an **inert carrier gas (N₂)** flowing through a fluidized-bed reactor at high velocity.
4. Result: maximum liquid yield (≈ 75 wt%) with char + gas as byproducts.

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**(i) Mass flow rates of each product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{char}} &= 500 \times 0.25 = \mathbf{125 \text{ kg/h}} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}  &= 500 \times 0.60 = \mathbf{300 \text{ kg/h}} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{gas}}  &= 500 \times 0.15 = \mathbf{75 \text{ kg/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Energy recovered in the gas per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{gas}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \times LHV_{\text{gas}} \\
               &= 75 \times 18 \\
               &= \mathbf{1350 \text{ MJ/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy self-sufficiency check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{req}} &= \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \times \Delta H_{\text{pyr}} \\
               &= 500 \times 1.2 = 600 \text{ MJ/h} \\
\text{Energy Ratio } P_{\text{energy}} &= \frac{E_{\text{gas}}}{E_{\text{req}}} = \frac{1350}{600} = 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $P_{\text{energy}} = 2.25 > 1$, the process is **energy self-sufficient**, and the excess 750 MJ/h can be exported for combined heat and power (CHP) generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. In part (a), do not write vague definitions — **always present the comparative table**, which carries 4 of the 7 marks.
> 2. In part (b), students often forget to convert percentages correctly; re-state the assumed basis (here 500 kg/h feed) clearly.
> 3. The phrase "energy self-sufficient" means the gas-LHV must exceed the **total** energy input, not just the pyrolysis enthalpy.

---

#### **Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the integrated first-order rate expression for the fractional conversion $X$ of a solid biomass particle undergoing pyrolysis, starting from the Arrhenius equation. Define all terms clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a kinetic study, biomass conversion data are obtained at two temperatures:
  - At $T_1 = 500$ °C, $X_1 = 0.40$ at $t = 0.50$ s.
  - At $T_2 = 600$ °C, $X_2 = 0.80$ at $t = 0.50$ s.
  Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate:
  (i) The rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$ at the two temperatures.
  (ii) The activation energy $E_a$ in kJ/mol.
  (iii) The pre-exponential factor $A$ using $T_1$. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The detailed derivation is presented in Section 3.1. The student should reproduce:

1. The differential form: $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = k(1 - X)$.
2. Integration: $-\ln(1 - X) = k t$.
3. Final result: $X = 1 - e^{-k t}$.
4. Substitution of Arrhenius: $X = 1 - e^{-A t e^{-E_a/RT}}$.
5. The linearized form for plotting: $\ln[-\ln(1 - X)] = \ln(A t) - \dfrac{E_a}{R T}$.

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**(i) Rate constants:**

Using $X = 1 - e^{-k t}$ → $k = -\dfrac{\ln(1 - X)}{t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.40)}{0.50} = -\frac{\ln(0.60)}{0.50} = -\frac{(-0.5108)}{0.50} = \mathbf{1.022 \text{ s}^{-1}} \\
k_2 &= -\frac{\ln(1 - 0.80)}{0.50} = -\frac{\ln(0.20)}{0.50} = -\frac{(-1.6094)}{0.50} = \mathbf{3.219 \text{ s}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Activation energy:**

Using the two-temperature form of the Arrhenius equation:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\frac{E_a}{R}\left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)
$$

Temperature conversions: $T_1 = 773$ K, $T_2 = 873$ K.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{3.219}{1.022}\right) &= -\frac{E_a}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{873} - \frac{1}{773} \right) \\
\ln(3.150) &= -\frac{E_a}{8.314} \times (-1.486 \times 10^{-4}) \\
1.1473 &= \frac{E_a \times 1.486 \times 10^{-4}}{8.314} \\
E_a &= \frac{1.1473 \times 8.314}{1.486 \times 10^{-4}} \\
\boxed{E_a \approx 64{,}200 \text{ J/mol} = 64.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Pre-exponential factor using $T_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_1 &= A \, e^{-E_a / R T_1} \\
A &= k_1 \cdot e^{E_a / R T_1} \\
  &= 1.022 \cdot \exp\!\left( \frac{64{,}200}{8.314 \times 773} \right) \\
  &= 1.022 \cdot \exp(9.989) \\
  &= 1.022 \times 21{,}930 \\
\boxed{A \approx 2.24 \times 10^{4} \text{ s}^{-1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors in Kinetic Problems:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert °C → K (subtracting 273.15). This single mistake loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. Using $R = 8.314$ kJ/mol·K — **incorrect** for this equation. Use **J/mol·K** so that $E_a$ comes out in J/mol.
> 3. Sign errors in $\ln(k_2/k_1)$ — recheck carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Pyrolysis = **thermal decomposition in the absence of oxygen** at **400 – 900 °C**.
- ✅ Three products: **char, bio-oil, non-condensable gases**; yields depend on **T, heating rate, VRT**.
- ✅ **Slow pyrolysis** → max char; **Fast pyrolysis** → max bio-oil; **Flash pyrolysis** → max gas.
- ✅ **First-order kinetics** is the standard assumption: $X = 1 - e^{-k t}$.
- ✅ The **Arrhenius equation** governs the temperature dependence: $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$.
- ✅ The energy for pyrolysis is **endothermic**; biomass needs **800 – 1800 kJ/kg**.
- ✅ **Energy self-sufficiency** is achieved by recycling a portion of the pyrolytic gas as fuel for the reactor.
- ✅ **Biochar** has agricultural and environmental applications (soil amendment, carbon sequestration).
- ✅ **Bio-oil** is acidic, viscous, oxygenated — needs upgrading (hydrodeoxygenation) for use as transport fuel.
- ✅ **Vacuum pyrolysis** lowers the boiling points of the volatiles, useful for heat-sensitive feedstocks.
- ✅ **Hydro-pyrolysis** uses pressurized H₂ to produce high-quality, low-oxygen bio-oil.
- ✅ The **linearized Arrhenius plot** $\ln[-\ln(1-X)]$ vs $1/T$ is the standard way to extract $E_a$ and $A$ from TGA data.
- ✅ **Vapor quenching** at ≈ 100 °C is critical to "freeze" bio-oil and prevent secondary cracking to gas.
- ✅ Industrial reactors: **fluidized bed, rotating cone, auger, ablative, and entrained flow** — each suited to a specific pyrolysis type.
- ✅ Reactor **coking** (secondary char) is a major design challenge — minimized by short VRT and rapid quenching.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For this topic, memorize (i) the comparative table of pyrolysis types, (ii) the derivation of first-order kinetics, and (iii) the worked numerical pattern for $E_a$ extraction. Together, these account for **> 80 %** of the marks typically asked in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gasification: Principles, Types, and Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gasification: Principles, Types, and Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gasification** is a thermo-chemical conversion process in which a carbonaceous feedstock (coal, biomass, pet-coke, municipal solid waste, or any solid/liquid hydrocarbon) is partially oxidized at sub-stoichiometric air ratios (typically **0.25 – 0.5** of the theoretical oxygen required for complete combustion) and elevated temperatures (**800 °C – 1500 °C**) to produce a combustible gaseous mixture known as **Producer Gas** or **Synthesis Gas (Syngas)**.

The product gas is composed primarily of **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Hydrogen (H₂)**, **Methane (CH₄)**, **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Water Vapor (H₂O)**, and small quantities of **Nitrogen (N₂)**, **Hydrogen Sulfide (H₂S)**, **Ammonia (NH₃)**, and **Tar**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the single most critical operating parameter in gasification:
>
> $$\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Actual Air-to-Fuel Ratio}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air-to-Fuel Ratio}}$$
>
> - **ER < 1** → Gasification (fuel-rich, partial oxidation)
> - **ER = 1** → Perfect Combustion (theoretical)
> - **ER > 1** → Combustion (excess air)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Campfire Analogy" — How Gasification Differs from Combustion**
>
> Imagine you are burning a log in a fireplace. If you **flood the log with excess oxygen** (open the damper fully), you get a hot, bright yellow flame — this is **combustion**. The carbon in the wood becomes **CO₂**, the hydrogen becomes **H₂O**, and almost all chemical energy is released instantly as heat.
>
> Now, suppose you **suffocate the log by restricting the air supply** (close the damper to ~30 %). The wood cannot fully burn. Instead, it smoulders, releases pungent gases, and produces a flame that is cooler and bluish. The carbon atoms are only **partially oxidized** — instead of forming **CO₂**, they stop at **CO**. Some hydrogen escapes as **H₂** rather than **H₂O**.
>
> **That "smouldering" process is gasification.** The trapped gases (CO + H₂) carry roughly **70 – 85 %** of the original fuel energy, and you can pipe them away to drive a gas turbine, an internal combustion engine, or a Fischer-Tropsch reactor. Gasification essentially converts a dirty solid fuel into a clean, versatile, gaseous fuel.

---

## 1.3 Why Gasification? — The Strategic Position in Process Engineering

Gasification sits at the crossroads of **three industrial paradigms**:

| Industrial Paradigm | Role of Gasification |
|---|---|
| **Power Generation** | Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle (IGCC) plants achieve **45 – 50 %** thermal efficiency |
| **Chemical Synthesis** | Syngas (H₂ + CO) is the building block for **methanol, ammonia, synthetic diesel (Fischer-Tropsch)** |
| **Environmental Remediation** | Converts hazardous wastes, MSW, and sewage sludge into clean energy, removing **>99 %** of dioxins |

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** | **70 – 85 %** | Fraction of feed chemical energy retained in product gas |
| **Carbon Conversion (CC)** | **>95 %** (high T) | Fraction of feed carbon converted to gaseous products |
| **Calorific Value of Producer Gas** | **4 – 18 MJ/Nm³** | Determines downstream application |
| **Air-to-Fuel Ratio (mass)** | **1.5 – 2.5** | Controls ER and hence product distribution |
| **Steam-to-Fuel Ratio** | **0 – 1.0** | Critical in steam-blown gasifiers for H₂ enrichment |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Reaction Zones

A gasifier is not a homogeneous reactor. Internally, it contains **four overlapping reaction zones** through which the fuel descends (in moving-bed) or circulates (in fluidized/entrained systems).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this **DD-VR-CR-HR** mnemonic — it appears in nearly every KTU question paper:
>
> **D**rying → **V**olatilization (Pyrolysis) → **C**ombustion (Oxidation) → **R**eduction

### Zone 1 — Drying Zone (Temperature: 100 °C – 200 °C)
- The wet feedstock (moisture **10 – 60 %**) is dried by hot up-coming gases.
- **Endothermic** moisture evaporation consumes roughly **2.26 MJ/kg of water**.

$$W_{\text{latent}} = m_{H_2O} \times h_{fg}, \quad h_{fg} \approx 2260 \; \text{kJ/kg at 100 °C}$$

### Zone 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone (Temperature: 200 °C – 700 °C)
- Volatile matter (VMs) in the feedstock is released as **tars, light hydrocarbons, CO, CO₂, H₂, H₂O**.
- Residual **char** (fixed carbon + ash) is left behind.
- General stoichiometry for cellulose (biomass proxy):

$$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n \;\longrightarrow\; n \cdot C \;+\; n \cdot H_2O \;+\; \text{tars} \;+\; \text{gases}$$

### Zone 3 — Combustion (Oxidation) Zone (Temperature: 900 °C – 1500 °C)
- Char reacts with **O₂** in a highly exothermic manner, releasing the heat needed to drive the entire gasification process.
- **Three primary reactions**:

$$C + O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

$$C + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO \quad \Delta H_{298} = -111.4 \; \text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; H_2O \quad \Delta H_{298} = -241.8 \; \text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

### Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (Temperature: 700 °C – 1100 °C)
This is the **heart of gasification**, where the producer gas composition is finalized.

$$C + CO_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,CO \quad \text{(Boudouard Reaction)} \quad \Delta H_{298} = +172.5 \; \text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

$$C + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; CO + H_2 \quad \text{(Water-Gas Reaction)} \quad \Delta H_{298} = +131.4 \; \text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

$$C + 2\,H_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CH_4 \quad \text{(Methanation)} \quad \Delta H_{298} = -74.8 \; \text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

$$CO + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2 + H_2 \quad \text{(Water-Gas Shift)} \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41.2 \; \text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for gasification problems in KTU ESE. Every formula is exam-grade and has appeared in previous papers.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equivalence Ratio** | $ER = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | — | Controls partial vs. complete oxidation |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric Air** | $A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{8\over 3 C + 8 H - O}{0.232} \times \frac{1}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Theoretical minimum air |
| 3 | **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** | $CGE = \frac{\dot{m}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f} \times 100$ | % | Measure of energy capture |
| 4 | **Carbon Conversion (CC)** | $CC = \frac{C_{\text{in fuel}} - C_{\text{in char}}}{C_{\text{in fuel}}} \times 100$ | % | Measure of gasification completeness |
| 5 | **Air-to-Fuel Ratio (mass)** | $AFR = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg/kg | Direct operating control |
| 6 | **Producer Gas Yield** | $Y_g = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel,dry}}}$ | Nm³/kg | Volume of gas per kg dry feed |
| 7 | **Heating Value of Producer Gas** | $LHV_g = \Sigma \, y_i \cdot LHV_i$ | MJ/Nm³ | Energy content calculation |
| 8 | **Steam Decomposition Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{steam}} = \frac{H_2 \text{ from steam}}{H_2 \text{ total}} \times 100$ | % | Steam-blown systems |
| 9 | **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\dot{m}_g (h_g - h_{\text{ref}})}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f}$ | % | Heat balance verification |
| 10 | **Boudouard Equilibrium Constant** | $\ln K_p = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{RT}$ | — | CO/CO₂ split at given T |
| 11 | **Le Chatelier Shift Direction** | $\frac{d \ln K}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$ | — | Effect of temperature on equilibria |
| 12 | **Gas Residence Time** | $\tau = \frac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{gas,exit}}}$ | s | Reactor sizing |

---

## 2.3 The Effect of Operating Parameters on Product Composition

> [!TIP]
> **The 4 Golden Levers of Gasification** — drawn directly from KTU 2024 Module 2 outcomes:

| Lever | Effect on **H₂ + CO** (syngas yield) | Effect on **CH₄** | Effect on **CO₂** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | **Strongly increases** | Decreases | Decreases |
| ↑ Pressure | Decreases H₂ / CO ratio | **Increases CH₄** | — |
| ↑ Steam/Fuel Ratio | Increases H₂ / decreases CO | Slightly decreases | Increases |
| ↓ Equivalence Ratio | Increases syngas yield | Increases | Decreases |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IGCC Power Plants**: Texaco, Shell, and Siemens entrained-flow gasifiers in Texas, Netherlands, and China.
- **Synthetic Natural Gas (SNG)**: Great Plains Synfuels Plant, North Dakota (uses Lurgi fixed-bed gasifiers).
- **Waste-to-Energy**: SVZ Schwarze Pumpe (Germany) gasifies MSW to produce methanol and electricity.
- **Biomass to Liquid (BTL)**: CHOREN Industries (Germany) — biomass → Carbo-V® gasifier → Fischer-Tropsch → diesel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement from Ultimate Analysis

> Given the ultimate analysis of a fuel on a **mass basis**:
> - Carbon = **C** %, Hydrogen = **H** %, Oxygen = **O** %, Sulfur = **S** %, Nitrogen = **N** %, Ash = **A** %, Moisture = **M** %
>
> Derive the theoretical (stoichiometric) oxygen and air required for **complete combustion**, and relate it to the actual air used in gasification.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the combustion reactions for each combustible element.**

For 1 kg of dry fuel:
- Carbon combustion: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$
- Hydrogen combustion: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$
- Sulfur combustion: $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$

**Step 2 — Calculate oxygen consumed by each element (in kg O₂ per kg fuel).**

For carbon: 1 mole of C (12 kg) needs 1 mole of O₂ (32 kg).
$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for C} = \frac{32}{12} \cdot \frac{C}{100} = \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{C}{100} \quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}]$$

For hydrogen: 1 mole of H₂ (2 kg) needs 0.5 mole of O₂ (16 kg).
$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for H}_2 = \frac{16}{2} \cdot \frac{H}{100} = 8 \cdot \frac{H}{100} \quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}]$$

For sulfur: 1 mole of S (32 kg) needs 1 mole of O₂ (32 kg).
$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for S} = \frac{32}{32} \cdot \frac{S}{100} = \frac{S}{100} \quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}]$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the oxygen already present in the fuel.**

The oxygen bound in the fuel molecule is **already available** for combustion, so it must be subtracted.

$$\text{Net O}_2 = \frac{8}{3} \cdot \frac{C}{100} + 8 \cdot \frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100} \quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}]$$

**Step 4 — Convert from oxygen to air.**

Dry air contains **23.2 % oxygen by mass**.

$$\boxed{\,A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{\frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O}{23.2} \times 10^{-1} \quad [\text{kg air/kg fuel}]\,}$$

Or equivalently (with mass percentages as decimals, e.g., 0.65 for 65 %):

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{8 C + 24 H + 3 S - 3 O}{69.6} \quad [\text{kg/kg}]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Equivalence Ratio.**

In gasification, we use only **25 % – 50 %** of the stoichiometric air:

$$A_{\text{actual}} = ER \times A_{\text{stoich}}, \quad \text{with } ER = 0.25 \text{ to } 0.50$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the energy content of the feedstock.**

The chemical energy entering per unit time is:

$$\dot{E}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f \quad [\text{kW or MJ/h}]$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = mass flow rate of dry fuel (kg/h)
- $LHV_f$ = lower heating value of fuel (MJ/kg)

**Step 2 — Define the energy content of the producer gas.**

The chemical energy leaving as product gas per unit time is:

$$\dot{E}_{\text{out,gas}} = \dot{m}_g \cdot LHV_g \quad [\text{kW or MJ/h}]$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_g$ = mass flow rate of dry producer gas (kg/h or Nm³/h)
- $LHV_g$ = lower heating value of producer gas (MJ/Nm³)

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.**

$$\boxed{\,CGE = \frac{\dot{m}_g \cdot LHV_g}{\dot{m}_f \cdot LHV_f} \times 100 \; [\%]\,}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute using gas yield.**

Define the **gas yield** $Y_g = \dot{V}_g / \dot{m}_f$ (Nm³/kg). Then:

$$CGE = \frac{Y_g \cdot LHV_g}{LHV_f} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style):** A biomass gasifier processes **100 kg/h** of rice husk ($LHV_f = 14 \; MJ/kg$) and produces **220 Nm³/h** of producer gas with $LHV_g = 5.2 \; MJ/Nm^3$. Calculate the CGE.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$CGE = \frac{220 \times 5.2}{100 \times 14} \times 100 = \frac{1144}{1400} \times 100 = 81.7 \;\%$$
>
> This falls within the **70 – 85 %** typical range — an excellent gasifier design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equilibrium Composition via Water-Gas Shift Reaction

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant equilibrium at gasification temperatures.**

Above **800 °C**, the **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) reaction** dictates the H₂/CO ratio:

$$CO + H_2O \;\rightleftharpoons\; CO_2 + H_2$$

**Step 2 — Write the equilibrium constant expression.**

$$K_p(T) = \frac{P_{CO_2} \cdot P_{H_2}}{P_{CO} \cdot P_{H_2O}}$$

At total pressure **P**, let the mole fractions be $y_{CO}, y_{H_2O}, y_{CO_2}, y_{H_2}$. Then:

$$K_p = \frac{y_{CO_2} \cdot y_{H_2}}{y_{CO} \cdot y_{H_2O}}$$

(Pressure dependence cancels because $\Delta n_{\text{gas}} = 0$.)

**Step 3 — Apply van't Hoff equation to obtain K as a function of T.**

$$\frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

For WGS, $\Delta H^\circ_{298} = -41.2 \; kJ/mol$. Integrating (assuming $\Delta H^\circ$ constant):

$$\ln \frac{K_p(T)}{K_p(298)} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \left( \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{T} \right)$$

With $K_p(298) \approx 1.0 \times 10^5$ (strongly product-favoured at low T):

$$\ln K_p(T) = -4959 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{298} \right) + 11.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute K at 1000 °C (1273 K).**

$$\ln K_p(1273) = -4959 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{1273} - \frac{1}{298} \right) + 11.5$$

$$\ln K_p(1273) = -4959 \cdot (0.000786 - 0.003356) + 11.5$$

$$\ln K_p(1273) = -4959 \cdot (-0.002570) + 11.5 = 12.74 + 11.5 = 24.24$$

Wait — this requires re-evaluation. The published WGS equilibrium constant at **1273 K** is approximately **K_p ≈ 1.0 – 1.4**, indicating near-unity. The integration above uses approximate enthalpy and reference values; for KTU exam purposes, the correct engineering answer is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **T > 900 °C**, the Water-Gas Shift **approaches unity** ($K_p \approx 1$), giving a product gas H₂/CO ratio close to **1:1**, ideal for **Fischer-Tropsch synthesis**.

**Step 5 — Engineering implication.**

To obtain a **2:1 H₂/CO ratio** (required for methanol synthesis), gasifiers typically operate at **lower temperatures (~750 °C)** or use a downstream **WGS reactor** with excess steam.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parametric Sensitivity Table

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **most-tested analytical tool** in KTU Module 2 viva voce and Part B questions.

| Operating Parameter | Syngas Yield | LHV of Gas | Carbon Conversion | Tar Production |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **↑ Temperature** | ↑ Strongly | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↓↓ |
| **↑ Pressure** | ↓ slightly | — | ↑ | ↓ |
| **↑ ER** | ↓↓ | ↓↓↓ | ↑↑ | ↓ |
| **↑ Steam/Fuel** | ↑ H₂ | ↓ (dilution) | ↑ slightly | ↓↓ |
| **↑ Particle Size** | ↓ | — | ↓↓ | ↑↑ |
| **↑ Moisture in Feed** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio for Gasification

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use a typical coal ultimate analysis.**

Take a coal with **C = 70 %, H = 5 %, O = 10 %, S = 1 %, N = 1.5 %, Ash = 12.5 %** (on dry basis).

**Step 2 — Calculate stoichiometric oxygen.**

$$O_{2,\text{stoich}} = \frac{8}{3}(0.70) + 8(0.05) + (0.01) - (0.10) \quad [\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}]$$

$$O_{2,\text{stoich}} = 1.867 + 0.400 + 0.010 - 0.100 = 2.177 \; \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to stoichiometric air.**

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{2.177}{0.232} = 9.38 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — Apply ER = 0.30 for gasification.**

$$A_{\text{actual}} = 0.30 \times 9.38 = 2.81 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5 — Estimate producer gas composition by simplified material balance.**

Assuming all carbon goes to CO (ideal partial oxidation):
- Carbon in: $0.70 / 12 = 0.0583 \; \text{kmol C/kg fuel}$
- O atoms needed: $0.0583 \; \text{kmol O} = 0.0292 \; \text{kmol O}_2 = 0.933 \; \text{kg O}_2$

Available O₂ in air: $2.81 \times 0.232 = 0.652 \; \text{kg O}_2$

Since **available O₂ (0.652 kg) < required for CO₂ (0.933 kg)**, the system operates in the **partial-oxidation regime**, producing **CO + H₂** as intended.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Gasifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Preparation Section] --> B[Feedstock Drying and Grinding]
    B --> C[Gasifier Reactor Core]
    D[Air Blower and Air Supply] --> C
    E[Steam Generator and Steam Injection] --> C
    C --> F[Hot Raw Producer Gas Outlet]
    F --> G[Cyclone Separator for Particulates]
    G --> H[Quench and Gas Cooling Section]
    H --> I[Tar Scrubber and Wet Scrubbing]
    I --> J[Gas Cleaning and Filtration]
    J --> K[Sulfur and Ammonia Removal]
    K --> L[Clean Cold Producer Gas to End Use]
    C --> M[Ash and Char Discharge]
    M --> N[Ash Quench and Disposal]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style M fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram**: The yellow node `C` is the **Gasifier Reactor Core** — the heart of the process. All material and energy flows converge there. The blue node `A` represents upstream solids handling; the green node `L` is the final clean gas dispatch. The orange node `M` is the bottom-ash stream — important for **mass balance closure** in exam problems.

---

## 4.2 Internal Reaction-Zone Architecture (Co-Current Downdraft)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Drying Zone - 100 to 200 C] --> Z2[Pyrolysis Zone - 200 to 700 C]
    Z2 --> Z3[Oxidation Zone - 900 to 1500 C]
    Z3 --> Z4[Reduction Zone - 700 to 1100 C]
    Z4 --> Z5[Ash Bed - Below 700 C]
    Z3 -.exothermic heat.-> Z2
    Z3 -.exothermic heat.-> Z1

    P1[Feedstock Inlet from Top] --> Z1
    A1[Air Inlet at Mid Level] --> Z3
    G1[Producer Gas Exit from Reduction Zone] --> Z4
    ASH1[Ash Discharge at Bottom] --> Z5

    style Z3 fill:#ff7043,stroke:#bf360c,color:#fff
    style Z4 fill:#fff176,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    style Z1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style Z2 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style Z5 fill:#bdbdbd,stroke:#424242,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Process Flow — Three Major Gasifier Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FB[Fixed Bed Updraft Gasifier]
        FB1[Feed from Top] --> FB2[Counter Current Air from Bottom]
        FB2 --> FB3[Producer Gas Exits Top]
    end

    subgraph FD[Fixed Bed Downdraft Gasifier]
        FD1[Feed from Top] --> FD2[Co Current Air from Sides]
        FD2 --> FD3[Producer Gas Exits Below Throat]
    end

    subgraph FLD[Fluidized Bed Gasifier]
        FLD1[Feed from Side] --> FLD2[Bed Material Fluidized by Air]
        FLD2 --> FLD3[Producer Gas Exits Top Through Cyclone]
    end

    subgraph EF[Entrained Flow Gasifier]
        EF1[Feed as Slurry or Dry Powder] --> EF2[Parallel Flow with O2 and Steam]
        EF2 --> EF3[Producer Gas Quenched at Bottom]
    end

    style FB fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style FD fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style FLD fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style EF fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f
```

---

## 4.4 Process Flow Block Diagram — IGCC Power Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal or Biomass Feedstock] --> B[Air Separation Unit]
    A --> C[Gasifier Reactor]
    B -->|Pure O2 supply| C
    C -->|Raw Syngas| D[Gas Cleaning Unit]
    D -->|Clean Syngas| E[Gas Turbine Combustor]
    F[Compressed Air] --> E
    E -->|Hot Flue Gas| G[Gas Turbine]
    G --> H[Generator 1]
    C -->|Hot Exhaust| I[Heat Recovery Steam Generator]
    G -->|Turbine Exhaust| I
    I -->|Steam| J[Steam Turbine]
    J --> K[Generator 2]

    style C fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style E fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style G fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style J fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the IGCC Block Diagram**: The gasifier (yellow) produces syngas, which is cleaned (gas cleaning unit) and burned in a **gas turbine** to generate electricity (Generator 1). The hot exhaust from both the gasifier and the gas turbine is recovered in an **HRSG** (Heat Recovery Steam Generator), which drives a **steam turbine** and **Generator 2**. This combined cycle achieves **45 – 50 %** net electrical efficiency — significantly higher than conventional pulverized-coal plants (**33 – 38 %**).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`
> **Define gasification. How does it differ from combustion in terms of equivalence ratio and primary products?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gasification is a thermo-chemical process that converts carbonaceous feedstocks into a combustible gaseous product (producer gas) at elevated temperatures and **sub-stoichiometric air supply**.

| Aspect | Gasification | Combustion |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | **0.25 – 0.50** (fuel-rich) | **≥ 1.0** (air-rich) |
| Primary Products | **CO, H₂, CH₄** | **CO₂, H₂O** |
| Air Factor | Limited | Excess |
| Energy Output | Chemical (in gas) | Thermal (heat) |

*(3 marks — full credit for tabular comparison + ER value + product gases.)*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`
> **List any three primary chemical reactions occurring in the reduction zone of a gasifier with their enthalpies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Boudouard Reaction:** $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$, $\Delta H = +172.5 \; kJ/mol$ (endothermic)
2. **Water-Gas Reaction:** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$, $\Delta H = +131.4 \; kJ/mol$ (endothermic)
3. **Methanation:** $C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4$, $\Delta H = -74.8 \; kJ/mol$ (exothermic)
4. **Water-Gas Shift:** $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$, $\Delta H = -41.2 \; kJ/mol$ (exothermic)

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for any three correctly stated reactions with enthalpy signs.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (2 × 14 Marks)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **four reaction zones** inside a downdraft biomass gasifier. State the temperature range and dominant chemical reaction in each zone. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fixed-bed gasifier is fed with **500 kg/h** of rice husk ($LHV = 14.5 \; MJ/kg$, ultimate analysis: C = 38 %, H = 5 %, O = 36 %, N = 0.6 %, Ash = 20.4 %). The actual air supplied is **2.0 kg air/kg fuel**. The producer gas yield is **2.4 Nm³/kg fuel** with composition by volume: CO = 18 %, H₂ = 16 %, CH₄ = 3 %, CO₂ = 11 %, N₂ = 52 %.
> Calculate:
>   - (i) The **stoichiometric air required** per kg of fuel.
>   - (ii) The **Equivalence Ratio**.
>   - (iii) The **Cold Gas Efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**The four reaction zones in a downdraft gasifier are:**

| Zone | Temperature | Dominant Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Drying Zone** | **100 – 200 °C** | Evaporation of moisture: $H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$, $\Delta H = +44 \; kJ/mol$ |
| **2. Pyrolysis Zone** | **200 – 700 °C** | Thermal decomposition: $Biomass \rightarrow Char + Volatiles + Tar$ |
| **3. Oxidation Zone** | **900 – 1500 °C** | $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$, $\Delta H = -393.5 \; kJ/mol$ |
| **4. Reduction Zone** | **700 – 1100 °C** | $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ (Water-Gas); $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ (Boudouard) |

**Block diagram (textual):**

```
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  Feedstock Inlet   │
   └────────┬───────────┘
            ↓
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  1. Drying Zone    │  ← 100–200 °C
   └────────┬───────────┘
            ↓
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  2. Pyrolysis Zone │  ← 200–700 °C
   └────────┬───────────┘
            ↓
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  3. Oxidation Zone │  ← 900–1500 °C  ← Air Inlet
   └────────┬───────────┘
            ↓
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  4. Reduction Zone │  ← 700–1100 °C
   └────────┬───────────┘
            ↓
   ┌────────────────────┐
   │  Ash Discharge     │
   └────────────────────┘
            ↓
   Producer Gas Out (below throat)
```

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for zone identification, 1 mark for temperature, 1 mark for reaction, 1 mark for diagram accuracy.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Calculate stoichiometric air.**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{8C + 24H + 3S - 3O}{69.6} \quad [\text{kg air/kg fuel}]$$

(Note: $S \approx 0$ for biomass, $N$ does not combust, and ash is inert.)

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{8(0.38) + 24(0.05) + 0 - 3(0.36)}{69.6}$$

$$A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{3.04 + 1.20 - 1.08}{69.6} = \frac{3.16}{69.6}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{stoich}} = 4.54 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Equivalence Ratio.**

$$ER = \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{stoich}}} = \frac{2.0}{4.54}$$

$$\boxed{ER = 0.44}$$

*(This is in the gasification range — between 0.25 and 0.50. ✓)*

**Step 3 — Calculate Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE).**

First, find the **LHV of producer gas** from composition:

| Component | $y_i$ | $LHV_i$ (MJ/Nm³) | $y_i \cdot LHV_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 0.18 | 12.64 | 2.275 |
| H₂ | 0.16 | 10.79 | 1.726 |
| CH₄ | 0.03 | 35.88 | 1.076 |
| CO₂ | 0.11 | 0 | 0 |
| N₂ | 0.52 | 0 | 0 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — | **5.08** |

$$LHV_g = 5.08 \; MJ/Nm^3$$

Then compute CGE:

$$CGE = \frac{Y_g \cdot LHV_g}{LHV_f} \times 100 = \frac{2.4 \times 5.08}{14.5} \times 100$$

$$CGE = \frac{12.192}{14.5} \times 100 = 84.08 \;\%$$

$$\boxed{CGE \approx 84.1 \;\%}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for stoichiometric air, 1 for ER, 3 for gas LHV and CGE — must show all intermediate steps.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a)** Compare the **updraft, downdraft, fluidized-bed, and entrained-flow gasifiers** with respect to feed size, ash content, tar yield, and gas application. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **thermodynamically** why gasification is favored at **high temperatures and low pressures** for syngas production. Use the **Water-Gas Shift** and **Methanation** reactions to justify. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

| Feature | Updraft | Downdraft | Fluidized Bed | Entrained Flow |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed Particle Size** | 5 – 100 mm | 20 – 100 mm | 0.5 – 5 mm | < 0.1 mm (pulverized) |
| **Ash Content Tolerance** | **High** (>25 %) | Low–Medium (<6 %) | Medium (<15 %) | Low (<5 %) |
| **Tar Yield** | **Very High** (50 g/Nm³) | **Very Low** (<1 g/Nm³) | Medium (5–10 g/Nm³) | Negligible |
| **Gas LHV** | 5 – 6 MJ/Nm³ | 4 – 5 MJ/Nm³ | 4 – 6 MJ/Nm³ | 10 – 18 MJ/Nm³ |
| **Operating T** | 800 – 1000 °C | 1000 – 1200 °C | 800 – 1000 °C | 1200 – 1500 °C |
| **Best Application** | Direct firing | Engine / IC use | Medium-scale power | Chemicals, IGCC |
| **Carbon Conversion** | 80 – 90 % | 90 – 95 % | 90 – 95 % | **>99 %** |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per row × 4 rows for substance; 3 marks for "best application" reasoning and engineering context.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Reaction 1 — Water-Gas Shift:**
$$CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2, \quad \Delta H = -41.2 \; kJ/mol$$

By **Le Chatelier's Principle**:
- Since the reaction is **exothermic**, **increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium backward** (toward CO + H₂O).
- However, both forward and reverse rates accelerate — the **equilibrium constant decreases** but the absolute rates are higher.
- High T favors **higher overall syngas (H₂ + CO) yield** by promoting the competing endothermic **Water-Gas Reaction** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ ($\Delta H = +131.4 \; kJ/mol$).

**Reaction 2 — Methanation:**
$$C + 2H_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_4, \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \; kJ/mol \; (\text{exothermic})$$

By **Le Chatelier's Principle**:
- Since the reaction is **exothermic and reduces mole count** (1 + 2 → 1), **high temperature** shifts equilibrium toward reactants (less CH₄, more H₂) — **desired for syngas**.
- Since the reaction reduces moles (Δn = -2), **low pressure** also shifts equilibrium back toward reactants.

**Combined conclusion:**

For **maximizing H₂ + CO (syngas)**:
- **High Temperature** → ✓ Suppresses methanation; ✓ Promotes endothermic char reactions.
- **Low Pressure** → ✓ Suppresses methanation; ✓ Favors WGS reverse if you want CO-rich syngas.

Hence, gasifiers for **syngas production** typically operate at **1100 – 1500 °C** and **near-atmospheric pressure** (with downstream compression if needed).

**Quantitative justification via van't Hoff equation:**

$$\frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{RT^2}$$

For methanation ($\Delta H^\circ < 0$), $\frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} < 0$, so **K_p decreases as T increases** → less CH₄ produced.

For Water-Gas Reaction ($\Delta H^\circ > 0$), $\frac{d \ln K_p}{dT} > 0$, so **K_p increases as T increases** → more CO + H₂ produced.

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for WGS analysis, Methanation analysis, and combined thermodynamic conclusion; 1 mark for the van't Hoff quantitative justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the molar-mass ratios in stoichiometric air derivation.** Many students write $A_{\text{stoich}} = (C + H + S - O)/0.232$ — this is **wrong**. The correct formula has coefficients $\frac{8}{3}, 8, 1$ derived from $O_2/C = 32/12$, $O_2/H_2 = 16/2$, $O_2/S = 32/32$. **Marks lost: up to 4.**
>
> 2. **Confusing ER and AFR.** $ER = AFR_{\text{actual}} / AFR_{\text{stoich}}$, not the inverse. **Marks lost: up to 2.**
>
> 3. **In CGE problems, using HHV instead of LHV** when the question specifies lower heating value. **Marks lost: up to 2.**
>
> 4. **Not showing unit conversions** (e.g., MJ/kg vs. MJ/Nm³) in CGE — examiners award partial credit only if units are explicit. **Marks lost: up to 1.**
>
> 5. **In zone-diagram questions, omitting the air inlet position** — a major distinction between updraft, downdraft, and crossdraft gasifiers. **Marks lost: up to 2.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Before Walking Into the Exam Hall:**

- [ ] **Definition of gasification** as partial oxidation with **ER = 0.25 – 0.5** and product gases CO + H₂.
- [ ] **Four reaction zones**: **DD-VR-CR-HR** (Drying → Pyrolysis → Combustion → Reduction).
- [ ] **Three reduction-zone reactions**: Boudouard ($+172.5$), Water-Gas ($+131.4$), Methanation ($-74.8$) — values in kJ/mol.
- [ ] **Water-Gas Shift** reaction ($CO + H_2O \rightleftharpoons CO_2 + H_2$) and its role in setting the H₂/CO ratio.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric air formula**: $A_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{8C + 24H + 3S - 3O}{69.6}$ kg/kg fuel.
- [ ] **Equivalence Ratio**: $ER = AFR_{\text{actual}} / AFR_{\text{stoich}}$.
- [ ] **Cold Gas Efficiency**: $CGE = (Y_g \cdot LHV_g / LHV_f) \times 100$ — typical range **70 – 85 %**.
- [ ] **Four gasifier types**: Updraft (high tar), Downdraft (low tar, IC engines), Fluidized Bed (medium-scale), Entrained Flow (high T, IGCC/chemicals).
- [ ] **Operating temperature window**: **800 – 1500 °C**.
- [ ] **Producer gas LHV**: **4 – 18 MJ/Nm³** (low for air-blown, high for O₂-blown).
- [ ] **Effects of ↑T, ↑P, ↑ER, ↑Steam/Fuel** on product gas composition (Section 2.3 table).
- [ ] **Why high T and low P favor syngas**: Le Chatelier's Principle applied to exothermic methanation.
- [ ] **IGCC efficiency**: **45 – 50 %** vs. **33 – 38 %** for conventional coal plants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparative Analysis of Pyrolysis and Gasification
Overview of Biological Conversion Technologies in WTE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Overview of Biological Conversion Technologies in WTE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biological Conversion Technologies in Waste-to-Energy (WTE)** refer to a class of engineered biochemical processes that utilize the metabolic activity of microorganisms (bacteria, archaea, fungi, and algae) under controlled environmental conditions to convert the organic fraction of solid waste, biomass, and effluents into useful energy carriers such as **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)**, **biohydrogen**, **bioethanol**, **electricity (via MFCs)**, and stabilized organic soil amendments (compost/biofertilizer).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic encompasses the **microbial degradation pathways**, **reactor engineering**, **kinetics of substrate utilization**, **mass and energy balances**, and **process integration strategies** that allow municipal solid waste (MSW), agricultural residues, food waste, and industrial organic effluents to act as renewable feedstocks for decentralized and sustainable energy generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECHT867, Module 3):** Biological conversion is positioned as the **biochemical counterpart** of thermal WTE (incineration, gasification, pyrolysis). It is preferred when the moisture content of the feed is high (>50%), when waste streams are biodegradable, and when the goal includes both **energy recovery** and **nutrient recycling (circular bioeconomy)**.

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Biological Conversion Pathways

The principal biological conversion technologies discussed under WTE in KTU Module 3 are:

| Pathway | Key End Products | Operating Environment |
|---|---|---|
| **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂), digestate | Strictly anaerobic, 35–55 °C |
| **Aerobic Composting** | Compost (humus), CO₂, heat | Aerobic, mesophilic–thermophilic |
| **Fermentation (Dark/Photo)** | Bioethanol, biohydrogen, organic acids | Anaerobic/limited O₂, 30 °C |
| **Microbial Fuel Cells (MFC)** | Electricity + H₂O | Anaerobic anode, aerobic cathode |
| **Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery** | CH₄ + CO₂ | In-situ anaerobic, ambient |
| **Bio-methanation (Power-to-Gas)** | Upgraded biomethane | Anaerobic, with H₂/CO₂ injection |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Cow's Stomach is a Living Bioreactor"**
> Imagine the digestive system of a cow. It chews grass (cellulosic biomass) once, swallows it, and then a complex community of microbes in its four-chambered stomach ferments this grass without any oxygen. The cow releases **belches of methane** (biogas) and produces nutrient-rich dung (digestate/compost). An industrial anaerobic digester is essentially a **giant, temperature-controlled, stainless-steel version of the cow's rumen**, fed with kitchen waste, sewage sludge, or farm manure instead of grass. Just as the cow converts grass into milk and methane, the digester converts waste into **clean fuel and biofertilizer**.

This analogy captures the essence of biological conversion: nature's microbes, scaled up inside engineered reactors, perform **catalytic decomposition of organic waste** at near-ambient pressures, producing **renewable energy** with **net-negative carbon footprints** in many cases.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Benchmark Values (KTU Board Memorization Targets)

- **Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP)**: 200–500 mL CH₄/g VS for food waste; 100–250 mL CH₄/g VS for sewage sludge
- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**: 15–40 days for high-rate AD; >60 days for low-rate systems
- **Volatile Solids (VS) destruction efficiency**: 40–70% in mesophilic AD
- **Specific methane yield**: 0.20–0.50 m³ CH₄/kg VS added
- **Lower heating value of biogas**: **18–28 MJ/m³** (depending on CH₄ fraction)
- **Power density (MFC)**: 0.5–5 W/m² (lab scale); up to 200 W/m³ (scalable designs)
- **C:N:P ratio for optimal composting**: **25–35 : 1 : 0.5**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework – The Four Biochemical Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

The **Buswell–Müller–Symons pathway** of anaerobic digestion is universally accepted as the standard framework. It proceeds through **four sequential, syntrophically-coupled microbial stages**. Each stage is catalyzed by a distinct microbial consortium, and **kinetic bottlenecks in any single stage limit the overall reactor performance**.

### Stage I – Hydrolysis (Rate-Limiting for Particulate Feedstocks)

Particulate biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are enzymatically cleaved into soluble monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids, glycerol) via extracellular enzymes (amylases, proteases, lipases).

> **Why it matters:** When the feed is **lignocellulosic** (e.g., rice straw, bagasse), the crystalline cellulose–hemicellulose–lignin matrix is highly recalcitrant. Hydrolysis becomes the **rate-limiting step**, requiring pretreatment (steam explosion, alkaline, or enzymatic) to enhance biodegradability.

### Stage II – Acidogenesis (Acid-Producing Bacteria)

Monomers are fermented into **volatile fatty acids (VFAs)**, alcohols, CO₂, H₂, and lactic acid by facultative anaerobes. The reaction is rapid (kinetic rate constant k ≈ 1–10 day⁻¹), but if VFA accumulation exceeds alkalinity, the pH crashes and the digester fails ("souring").

### Stage III – Acetogenesis (Acetogenic Bacteria)

VFAs and alcohols are oxidized to **acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂** by obligate syntrophs. These microbes require H₂ partial pressure <10⁻⁴ atm (maintained by methanogens), demonstrating **interspecies hydrogen transfer (IHT)** – a textbook example of microbial symbiosis.

### Stage IV – Methanogenesis (Archaea – The Crown Group)

Two pathways converge:
1. **Acetoclastic methanogenesis** (≈70% of CH₄): CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂
2. **Hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis** (≈30%): CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Methanogens are extremely **sensitive to inhibitors**. Free ammonia (>1500 mg/L), sulfides, heavy metals, and VFAs (>6000 mg/L) all suppress methanogenesis. Any reactor design question must address **pH buffering (NaHCO₃ dosing), C:N ratio control (20–30:1), and toxicant monitoring**.

## 2.2 Stoichiometric Buswell Equation – Universal Anaerobic Digestion Balance

For a generic organic substrate of empirical formula **CₙHₐOᵦNᵧ**, the Buswell equation yields the theoretical methane and ammonia yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_nH_aO_bN_c + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} + \frac{3c}{4}\right)H_2O \longrightarrow \\
\left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} + \frac{3c}{8}\right)CH_4 \; + \\
\left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{3c}{8}\right)CO_2 \; + \; c\,NH_3
\end{aligned}$$

This **stoichiometric identity** allows estimation of maximum methane yield without any experimental data and forms the foundation of all WTE biological conversion calculations in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Reactor Engineering & Process Configurations

| Reactor Type | Configuration | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank)** | Single-stage, fully mixed | Sludge digestion, food waste |
| **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** | Tubular, no back-mixing | Crop residue, manure |
| **UASB (Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket)** | Granular sludge bed | High-strength industrial wastewater |
| **Two-Phase / Two-Stage Digestion** | Phase separation | Improved stability, higher CH₄ |
| **CSTR + PBR (Photobioreactor)** | Hybrid dark/photo fermentation | Sequential H₂ + CH₄ |
| **Anaerobic Membrane Bioreactor (AnMBR)** | Membrane-coupled | High effluent quality |
| **Dry Fermentation (Leach-Bed)** | TS > 20% | MSW, garden waste |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Per KTU board guidelines, all variables are written with `$\vert$` for absolute value expressions to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Equation | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_nH_aO_bN_c \;\rightarrow\; CH_4 + CO_2 + NH_3$ | **Buswell stoichiometry** for AD |
| 2 | $Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot V_{reactor} \cdot VS_{added}}$ | Specific methane yield (m³ CH₄/kg VS) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -k \cdot S$ | First-order hydrolysis kinetics |
| 4 | $M(t) = M_{\max} \cdot \exp\!\left\{-\exp\!\left[\dfrac{R_{\max} \cdot e}{M_{\max}}(t_{\text{lag}}-t) + 1\right]\right\}$ | **Modified Gompertz model** for cumulative CH₄ |
| 5 | $\eta_{VS} = \dfrac{VS_{\text{in}} - VS_{\text{out}}}{VS_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Volatile solids destruction efficiency |
| 6 | $\text{OLR} = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{V}$ | Organic Loading Rate (kg VS/m³·d) |
| 7 | $\text{HRT} = V/Q$ | Hydraulic Retention Time |
| 8 | $E_{out} = V_{CH_4} \cdot \rho_{CH_4} \cdot \text{LHV}_{CH_4} \cdot \eta_{CHP}$ | Energy output of combined heat & power |
| 9 | $P_{\text{MFC}} = I^2 \cdot R_{\text{ext}}$ | Power harvested from Microbial Fuel Cell |
| 10 | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Thermodynamic feasibility of methanogenesis |

Where **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** is the universal gas constant, **LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg**, **density of CH₄ at STP = 0.717 kg/m³**, and **η_CHP ≈ 0.35 (electrical) + 0.50 (thermal)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Decentralized rural electrification** via biogas gensets (e.g., India's SATAT scheme targeting 15 MMT of compressed biogas by 2030).
2. **Wastewater treatment** with simultaneous energy recovery in municipal sewage plants (e.g., the famous Strass plant in Austria achieves >100% energy self-sufficiency).
3. **Carbon-negative biofertilizer** production (digestate displaces synthetic NPK, avoiding ~1.5 kg CO₂eq per kg N avoided).
4. **Industrial symbiosis** in sugar mills, breweries, and pulp/paper plants where high-strength effluents become feedstock.
5. **Climate mitigation** via landfill gas capture (methane has GWP₈₀ₖₑ ≈ 28 relative to CO₂).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Methane Yield via the Buswell Equation

We will derive the **theoretical methane yield (TCH₄, m³ CH₄/kg VS)** for a generic organic substrate.

### Step 1 – Write the generic substrate formula

Let the substrate be $C_nH_aO_bN_c$ on a molar basis.

### Step 2 – Apply the Buswell generalized stoichiometry

From the equation in §2.2, the moles of CH₄ produced per mole of substrate are:

$$\nu_{CH_4} = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} + \frac{3c}{8}$$

### Step 3 – Convert moles to volume at STP

By the ideal gas law, at STP (T = 273.15 K, P = 1 atm):

$$V_{CH_4}^{\text{STP}} = \nu_{CH_4} \times 22.415 \; \text{L/mol}$$

### Step 4 – Express yield per unit mass of substrate (kg basis)

The molecular weight of $C_nH_aO_bN_c$ is:

$$M_{\text{sub}} = 12n + a + 16b + 14c \;\; \text{(g/mol)}$$

Therefore the methane yield in L/kg is:

$$\begin{aligned}
TCH_4 \; [\text{L/kg}] &= \frac{\nu_{CH_4} \times 22.415 \times 1000}{M_{\text{sub}}} \\
&= \frac{\nu_{CH_4} \times 22415}{12n + a + 16b + 14c}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 – Numerical Worked Example (Glucose, C₆H₁₂O₆)

For glucose, $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$, $c = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{CH_4} &= \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} + 0 \\
&= 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 \\
&= 3 \;\; \text{mol CH}_4 / \text{mol glucose}
\end{aligned}$$

$$M_{\text{sub}} = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 \; \text{g/mol}$$

$$TCH_4 = \frac{3 \times 22415}{180} = 373.6 \; \text{L CH}_4/\text{kg glucose}$$

> **Sanity check:** This matches the experimentally accepted value of ~370 L CH₄/kg glucose for glucose monophosphate under standard mesophilic AD conditions. ✓

### Step 6 – Mass Balance Verification for Glucose

$$\begin{aligned}
C_6H_{12}O_6 &\rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2 \\
\text{C atoms:} \; 6 &= 3 + 3 \; \checkmark \\
\text{H atoms:} \; 12 &= 12 \; \checkmark \\
\text{O atoms:} \; 6 &= 6 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

Atomic balance confirms the stoichiometry is correctly closed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Modified Gompertz Kinetic Model for Cumulative Methane Production

The Modified Gompertz equation is the **most widely applied empirical kinetic model** for biological WTE systems, used to extract three bio-kinetic descriptors from batch BMP experiments: **(i) M_max**, **(ii) R_max**, and **(iii) t_lag**.

### Starting Equation

$$M(t) = M_{\max} \cdot \exp\!\left\{-\exp\!\left[\frac{R_{\max} \cdot e}{M_{\max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right]\right\}$$

Where:
- $M(t)$ = cumulative methane production (mL CH₄/g VS) at time $t$
- $M_{\max}$ = methane production potential (mL CH₄/g VS)
- $R_{\max}$ = maximum methane production rate (mL CH₄/g VS·d)
- $\lambda$ = lag phase time (days)
- $e = 2.71828$ (Euler's number)
- $t$ = digestion time (days)

### Step 1 – Compute Initial Value at $t = 0$

At $t = 0$, the exponential argument becomes $\left[\frac{R_{\max} \cdot e}{M_{\max}} \cdot \lambda + 1\right]$.

For a long lag phase ($\lambda \to \infty$), $M(0) \to 0$, which is physically consistent (no methane yet).

### Step 2 – Compute Long-Term Asymptote at $t \to \infty$

As $t \to \infty$, the term $\frac{R_{\max} \cdot e}{M_{\max}}(\lambda - t) \to -\infty$, hence $e^{-\infty} \to 0$, and:

$$M(\infty) = M_{\max} \cdot e^{0} = M_{\max}$$

### Step 3 – Locate the Inflection Point (Maximum Rate)

The inflection point occurs where the second derivative is zero. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = R_{\max} \cdot \exp\!\left\{-\exp\!\left[\frac{R_{\max} e}{M_{\max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right] + \frac{R_{\max} e}{M_{\max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right\}$$

Maximum rate occurs at $t = \lambda$, yielding $R_{\max}$.

### Step 4 – Numerical Example (Food Waste, 35 °C Mesophilic)

Given experimental data fitted to Gompertz parameters:
- $M_{\max} = 480$ mL CH₄/g VS
- $R_{\max} = 38$ mL CH₄/g VS·d
- $\lambda = 2.4$ d

Calculate methane production at $t = 15$ days.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Argument} &= \frac{38 \times 2.718}{480}(2.4 - 15) + 1 \\
&= \frac{103.284}{480}(-12.6) + 1 \\
&= (0.2152)(-12.6) + 1 \\
&= -2.711 + 1 = -1.711 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\exp(-1.711) &= 0.1807 \\
\exp(-0.1807) &= 0.8347 \\
M(15) &= 480 \times 0.8347 = 400.66 \;\;\text{mL CH}_4/\text{g VS}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** After 15 days of HRT, the digester has produced ~83.5% of its ultimate methane potential, indicating good performance and suggesting 18–20 days as the optimal HRT.

---

| Criterion | Anaerobic Digestion | Aerobic Composting | Bioethanol Fermentation | Microbial Fuel Cell |
|---|---|---|---|---|

| **KTU examiner emphasis** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |

## 3.4 Design Calculation: Power Output from a 1000 m³ Mesophilic AD Plant

### Given
- Reactor volume $V_r = 1000$ m³
- OLR = 3.5 kg VS/m³·d
- VS loading = 3500 kg VS/d
- Specific methane yield = 0.32 m³ CH₄/kg VS
- CHP electrical efficiency $\eta_e = 35\%$
- LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³ (at typical 60% CH₄ in biogas)

### Step 1 – Daily Methane Production

$$Q_{CH_4} = 0.32 \times 3500 = 1120 \;\; \text{m}^3 \, CH_4/\text{d}$$

### Step 2 – Energy Content of Methane

$$E_{CH_4} = 1120 \times 35.8 = 40100 \;\; \text{MJ/d}$$

### Step 3 – Electrical Energy Output

$$E_e = 40100 \times 0.35 = 14035 \;\; \text{MJ/d} = \frac{14035}{3.6} = 3899 \;\; \text{kWh/d}$$

### Step 4 – Continuous Power Rating

$$P_{continuous} = \frac{3899}{24} \approx 162 \;\; \text{kW}$$

> **Engineering Takeaway:** A modest 1000 m³ digester can continuously power **~160 households** (assuming 1 kW average per home), while simultaneously processing 10–15 tonnes of organic waste per day. This is the **circular bioeconomy in action**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List the four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion and state the rate-limiting stage for lignocellulosic biomass."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four sequential stages of anaerobic digestion are:

1. **Hydrolysis** – breakdown of complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) into soluble monomers by extracellular enzymes. (1 mark)
2. **Acidogenesis** – conversion of monomers into VFAs, alcohols, H₂, and CO₂ by acidogenic bacteria. (0.5 mark)
3. **Acetogenesis** – oxidation of VFAs into acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by syntrophic acetogens. (0.5 mark)
4. **Methanogenesis** – conversion of acetate and H₂/CO₂ into CH₄ by methanogenic archaea. (0.5 mark)

For **lignocellulosic biomass**, the rate-limiting stage is **Hydrolysis** (Stage I), because the crystalline cellulose–hemicellulose–lignin matrix is highly resistant to enzymatic attack, requiring pretreatment to enhance biodegradability. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP). State the typical BMP range for food waste."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP)** is defined as the maximum volume of methane produced per unit mass of volatile solids (VS) added to a batch anaerobic digester under standard conditions (typically 35 °C, infinite HRT). It is expressed in mL CH₄/g VS added or m³ CH₄/kg VS added. (2 marks)

The typical BMP range for **food waste** is **300–500 mL CH₄/g VS** (or 0.30–0.50 m³ CH₄/kg VS). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of the generalized Buswell equation, derive the theoretical methane yield of **glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** and **glycine (C₂H₅NO₂)**. Comment on why proteins yield more methane per kg VS than carbohydrates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2000 m³ CSTR anaerobic digester treats municipal food waste at an OLR of 4 kg VS/m³·d. The specific methane yield is 0.35 m³ CH₄/kg VS added, the biogas contains 65% CH₄, and the CHP electrical efficiency is 38%. Compute:
  (i) Daily methane production in m³/day
  (ii) Energy content of methane in MJ/day
  (iii) Electrical power output in kW (continuous)
  (iv) Number of households that can be served continuously (assume 1.2 kW per household) **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question A

**(a) Buswell equation derivation for glucose and glycine (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** – Here $n = 6$, $a = 12$, $b = 6$, $c = 0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{CH_4} &= \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} + \frac{3c}{8} \\
&= \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} + 0 \\
&= 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \; \text{mol CH}_4 / \text{mol glucose}
\end{aligned}$$

$$M_{\text{glucose}} = 6(12) + 12 + 6(16) = 180 \; \text{g/mol}$$

$$TCH_4 = \frac{3 \times 22415}{180} = 373.6 \; \text{L CH}_4/\text{kg glucose}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Glycine (C₂H₅NO₂)** – Here $n = 2$, $a = 5$, $b = 2$, $c = 1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{CH_4} &= \frac{2}{2} - \frac{5}{8} + \frac{2}{4} + \frac{3}{8} \\
&= 1 - 0.625 + 0.5 + 0.375 = 1.25 \; \text{mol CH}_4 / \text{mol glycine}
\end{aligned}$$

$$M_{\text{glycine}} = 2(12) + 5 + 2(16) + 14 = 75 \; \text{g/mol}$$

$$TCH_4 = \frac{1.25 \times 22415}{75} = 373.6 \; \text{L CH}_4/\text{kg glycine}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Reaction stoichiometries**

$$\text{Glucose: } C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2$$

$$\text{Glycine: } 4\,C_2H_5NO_2 + 3\,H_2O \rightarrow 5\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2 + 4\,NH_3$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4: Why proteins yield more methane per kg VS than carbohydrates** (1.5 marks)

Proteins contain **reduced carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen** in highly electron-rich forms (–CH(NH₂)–COOH bonds). The nitrogen is excreted as NH₃ (not as reduced product), allowing more electrons per carbon atom to flow to CH₄. Carbohydrates like glucose already contain oxygen bound to carbon, lowering the average oxidation state and reducing the electron equivalents available for methanogenesis. Hence proteins show comparable **molar methane yield** but yield a **higher fraction of reduced CH₄** relative to oxidized CO₂.

---

**(b) Digester power calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_r = 2000 \; m^3$, OLR = 4 kg VS/m³·d, $Y_{CH_4} = 0.35 \; m^3 CH_4/kg VS$, CH₄ fraction = 65%, $\eta_e = 38\%$.

**Step (i): Daily methane production**

$$VS_{\text{loaded}} = 4 \times 2000 = 8000 \; \text{kg VS/d}$$

$$Q_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 8000 = 2800 \; m^3 CH_4/\text{d}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step (ii): Energy content of methane**

Using LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/m³:

$$E_{CH_4} = 2800 \times 35.8 = 100240 \; \text{MJ/d}$$ **(1.5 marks)**

**Step (iii): Electrical power output**

$$E_e = 100240 \times 0.38 = 38091 \; \text{MJ/d}$$

$$P_{continuous} = \frac{38091 \times 1000}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{38091000}{86400} \approx 441 \; \text{kW}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step (iv): Households served**

$$N_{HH} = \frac{441}{1.2} \approx 367 \; \text{households}$$ **(1.5 marks)**
