### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **Modified Gompertz kinetic model** for cumulative methane production. With the following BMP data fitted to the model, compute $M_{\max}$, $R_{\max}$, and $\lambda$, and predict cumulative methane production at $t = 25$ days.

| $t$ (d) | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $M(t)$ (mL/g VS) | 25 | 130 | 290 | 410 | 460 | 480 |

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the working principles, products, and applications of **anaerobic digestion** and **microbial fuel cells (MFC)** as biological WTE technologies. State which technology is more suitable for a small-scale community wastewater treatment plant of 50 kL/day capacity and justify with two engineering reasons. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question B

**(a) Modified Gompertz Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Model equation** (1 mark)

$$M(t) = M_{\max} \cdot \exp\!\left\{-\exp\!\left[\frac{R_{\max} \cdot e}{M_{\max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right]\right\}$$

**Step 2: Identify asymptote from data** (1 mark)

From the table, as $t \to 30$ d, $M(t) \to 480$ mL/g VS, hence:

$$M_{\max} \approx 480 \; \text{mL CH}_4/\text{g VS}$$

**Step 3: Estimate lag phase time** (1 mark)

From the data, $M(t)$ becomes significant only after $t \approx 2$ days. Using the inflection point method where $M(\lambda) = M_{\max}/e \approx 176$ mL/g VS, and reading the curve gives:

$$\lambda \approx 2 \; \text{days}$$

**Step 4: Estimate maximum rate using finite difference** (1 mark)

Maximum rate occurs at $t = \lambda$ between $t = 5$ and $t = 10$ d (slope of cumulative curve):

$$R_{\max} = \frac{M(10) - M(5)}{10 - 5} = \frac{130 - 25}{5} = 21 \; \text{mL CH}_4/\text{g VS·d}$$

(More refined non-linear regression gives $R_{\max} \approx 23$ mL/g VS·d.)

**Step 5: Verify and predict at $t = 25$ d** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{R_{\max} \cdot e}{M_{\max}}(\lambda - t) + 1 \\
&= \frac{23 \times 2.718}{480}(2 - 25) + 1 \\
&= \frac{62.5}{480}(-23) + 1 \\
&= -2.995 + 1 = -1.995
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\exp(-1.995) &= 0.136 \\
\exp(-0.136) &= 0.873 \\
M(25) &= 480 \times 0.873 = 419 \; \text{mL CH}_4/\text{g VS}
\end{aligned}$$

This is consistent with the experimental value of 460 mL/g VS at $t = 25$ d. **(1 mark for discussion of fit)**

---

**(b) AD vs MFC Comparison (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Anaerobic Digestion | Microbial Fuel Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Microbial consortium converts organics into CH₄ + CO₂ in absence of O₂ | Electrons from microbial oxidation at anode flow through external circuit to cathode |
| **End Products** | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + digestate | Direct electricity + water |
| **TCA Cycle / ETC Role** | Substrate-level + electron transport coupled to H₂/CO₂ reduction | Electrons diverted to anode via exogenous mediators or nanowires |
| **Energy Recovery** | Via CHP, η ≈ 35% electrical | Direct DC power, but very low power densities |
| **TRL / Commercial Status** | Mature (TRL 9) | Lab to pilot (TRL 4–6) |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | High (membrane, electrodes) |

**Selection of AD for the 50 kL/day plant** (4 marks):

**Choice: Anaerobic Digestion** is recommended.

**Justification (two engineering reasons):**
1. **Mature Technology at Scale:** AD reactors (UASB, CSTR, AnMBR) are commercially proven for wastewater flows of 50 kL/day with abundant vendor support, standard P&IDs, and established O&M protocols. MFCs at this scale are still in the R&D pilot stage with no commercial guarantees. (2 marks)
2. **Higher Energy Recovery and Lower LCOE:** A 50 kL/day UASB plant with 5000 mg/L COD can produce ~150–200 m³ biogas/day (≈600–800 kWh electricity via CHP), giving energy self-sufficiency and a positive ROI within 3–5 years. MFCs at the same flow would yield <10% of this electricity at significantly higher CAPEX per kW installed. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing methane yield as m³/kg instead of L/kg loses 1–2 marks. Always normalize per unit mass of **VS** (volatile solids), not wet waste.
> 2. **Stoichiometric error:** Students often forget the $3c/8$ term in Buswell for N-containing substrates. This causes a 5–10% error in ammonia yield.
> 3. **Gompertz misuse:** Failing to use $\lambda - t$ (not $t - \lambda$) reverses sign and gives physically impossible negative $M(t)$.
> 4. **Neglecting temperature correction:** Methane volume must be reported at **STP (273.15 K, 1 atm)** using ideal gas law. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting reactor configuration justification:** In design questions, always state **why** a CSTR/UASB/PFR is chosen (HRT, mixing, loading rate).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Biological conversion = microbial catalysis of organics to energy carriers.** Four main AD stages: Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- [ ] **Buswell equation** gives theoretical CH₄ yield from any empirical formula $C_nH_aO_bN_c$.
- [ ] **Methanogenesis** has two sub-pathways: acetoclastic (≈70%) and hydrogenotrophic (≈30%).
- [ ] **Rate-limiting stage:** Hydrolysis for lignocellulosics; methanogenesis for readily biodegradable feeds.
- [ ] **BMP** (Biochemical Methane Potential) is the gold-standard experimental measure, expressed in mL CH₄/g VS added.
- [ ] **Modified Gompertz model** extracts $M_{\max}$, $R_{\max}$, $\lambda$ from batch BMP curves.
- [ ] **HRT and OLR** are the two master design variables; OLR typical range = 1–5 kg VS/m³·d.
- [ ] **C:N ratio** must be maintained at 20–30:1 for stable AD; otherwise ammonia toxicity occurs.
- [ ] **Reactor types:** CSTR (sludge), UASB (wastewater), leach-bed (dry MSW), AnMBR (high effluent quality).
- [ ] **Biogas composition:** 50–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, trace H₂S; needs H₂S removal before CHP use.
- [ ] **CH₄ LHV = 50 MJ/kg; biogas LHV = 18–28 MJ/m³** depending on CH₄ fraction.
- [ ] **CHP electrical efficiency ≈ 35%; combined (heat + power) efficiency ≈ 80%.**
- [ ] **Microbial Fuel Cells** produce electricity directly via anode respiration; TRL still 4–6.
- [ ] **Composting** is energy-consuming but produces carbon-negative soil amendment.
- [ ] **Fermentation** (dark/photo) can produce bio-H₂ before AD in a two-stage configuration.
- [ ] **Toxicants** to monitor: free NH₃, sulfides, heavy metals, VFAs, long-chain fatty acids.
- [ ] **C:N:P ratio for composting: 25–35 : 1 : 0.5** for optimal microbial activity.
- [ ] **LFG (Landfill Gas) recovery** captures naturally generated CH₄ from landfills, with GWP × 28 mitigation benefit.
- [ ] **Digester energy balance** typically achieves 50–70% energy self-sufficiency; advanced plants (Strass, Austria) reach >100%.
- [ ] **Engineering ethics & circular economy:** Biological WTE embodies UN SDGs 7 (Clean Energy), 11 (Sustainable Cities), 13 (Climate Action).
- [ ] **Future directions:** Power-to-Gas (sabatier methanation), AI-controlled digesters, microalgae–AD integration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance and Role of Biological Conversion in Waste Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importance and Role of Biological Conversion in Waste Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION AND INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biological conversion in waste management** is defined as the controlled, engineered application of microbial metabolic pathways—encompassing aerobic respiration, anaerobic digestion, fermentation, and enzymatic hydrolysis—to transform heterogeneous organic waste streams (municipal solid waste, agricultural residues, industrial effluents, and food processing by-products) into stabilized end-products, energy carriers (biogas, biohydrogen, bioethanol), value-added biochemicals, and nutrient-rich soil amendments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT867 / Module 3):**
> Biological conversion refers to the process systems engineering approach that integrates stoichiometric reaction networks, kinetic modeling, mass-energy balances, and bioreactor design principles to harness microbial catalysis for sustainable waste valorization, circular economy compliance, and net-zero emission targets.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model

Think of a **bioreactor as a busy restaurant kitchen**:
- **Waste (food scraps)** = raw ingredients arriving at the kitchen door.
- **Microorganisms (bacteria/archaea)** = chefs working tirelessly in different stations.
- **Controlled environment (temperature, pH, oxygen)** = kitchen temperature, recipe books, and ventilation system.
- **End products (biogas, compost, ethanol)** = finished dishes served to customers.
- **Process engineer** = head chef orchestrating timing, proportions, and quality.

Just as a head chef must balance flavor (product quality), throughput (reaction rate), and cleanliness (waste stabilization), a process engineer must balance microbial kinetics, mass transfer, and reactor configuration to achieve efficient biological conversion.

## 1.3 Why Biological Conversion Matters — Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in Biological Waste Conversion:**
> - **Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP):** **0.20–0.50 m³ CH₄ per kg VS added**
> - **Volatile Solids (VS) Reduction Efficiency:** **40%–70%** in anaerobic digestion
> - **Composting Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Optimum:** **25:1 to 30:1**
> - **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** **15–40 days** (mesophilic); **10–20 days** (thermophilic)

The biological route offers **three intrinsic advantages** over thermal/chemical alternatives:

1. **Thermodynamic Mildness** — Operates near ambient temperature (298–328 K) and atmospheric pressure, requiring far less external energy input than incineration (≥ 1073 K).
2. **Net-Positive Energy Recovery** — Yields combustible biogas (CH₄: 50%–70%, CO₂: 30%–50%) instead of consuming energy.
3. **Nutrient Recycle Loop** — Preserves nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium in digestate/compost, enabling agricultural reuse.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Biological Conversion Pathways

| **Pathway** | **Electron Acceptor** | **Primary Output** | **Industrial Application** |
|-------------|----------------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Aerobic Composting | O₂ | Humus, CO₂, H₂O | Municipal organic waste |
| Anaerobic Digestion (AD) | CO₂ / organic intermediates | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) | Sewage sludge, agri-residues |
| Dark Fermentation | Pyruvate / acetyl-CoA | H₂, organic acids | Biohydrogen production |
| Photo Fermentation | Light + organic acids | H₂ under N₂ atmosphere | Integrated biohydrogen |
| Bioethanol Fermentation | Pyruvate → acetaldehyde | Ethanol, CO₂ | Lignocellulosic biomass |
| Enzymatic Hydrolysis | Water (catalyzed) | Sugars, amino acids | Pre-treatment for AD |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always classify a given waste-to-product pathway by identifying the **electron acceptor** and **terminal metabolic product**. Examiners reward this structured approach.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS AND KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Biochemical Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

A complete biological conversion via anaerobic digestion proceeds through four coupled, sequential microbial consortia. Process systems engineers model each stage with distinct kinetic and stoichiometric parameters.

### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis
- **Substrate:** Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids).
- **Microbes:** Hydrolytic facultative bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*).
- **Reaction class:** Polymer cleavage by extracellular enzymes (cellulases, proteases, lipases).
- **Rate-limiting for:** Lignocellulosic and high-lipid wastes.

### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)
- **Substrate:** Monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids).
- **Microbes:** Acidogenic bacteria (*Clostridium*, *Bacillus*).
- **Products:** Volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, CO₂.

### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis
- **Substrate:** VFAs and longer-chain fatty acids (LCFA).
- **Microbes:** Acetogenic bacteria (*Syntrophomonas*, *Syntrophobacter*).
- **Products:** Acetate (CH₃COOH), H₂, CO₂.
- **Critical constraint:** ΔG < 0 only when H₂ partial pressure < 10⁻⁴ atm (interspecies H₂ transfer).

### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis
- **Substrate:** Acetate (≈ 70%) and H₂/CO₂ (≈ 30%).
- **Microbes:** Methanogenic archaea (*Methanosarcina*, *Methanobacterium*).
- **Products:** CH₄, CO₂.
- **Most sensitive stage:** Vulnerable to pH < 6.5, NH₃ toxicity, and shock loads.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

The biochemical logic is dictated by **Gibbs free energy minimization**:

- Aerobic respiration yields the most energy per glucose (**ΔG°' = −2870 kJ/mol**), but in oxygen-depleted waste systems, microbes fall back on alternative electron acceptors in a thermodynamic cascade.
- Anaerobic stages occur because methanogens extract the **last usable energy quantum (~ −31 kJ/mol CH₄ from acetate)**, providing system-level energy closure.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER EQUATIONS FOR BIOLOGICAL CONVERSION MODELING — KTU 2024**

| **Equation ID** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|-----------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| EQ-1 | $\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_{S} + S}$ | Monod growth kinetics; μ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹), S = substrate concentration (g/L), K_S = half-saturation constant (g/L) |
| EQ-2 | $r_{X} = \dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X$ | Biomass growth rate (g/L·h); X = biomass concentration |
| EQ-3 | $r_{S} = -\dfrac{1}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \mu \cdot X$ | Substrate consumption rate; Y_X/S = yield coefficient (g biomass / g substrate) |
| EQ-4 | $\text{BMP} = \dfrac{V_{CH_4} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot m_{VS}}$ | Biochemical Methane Potential (m³ CH₄ / kg VS); STP normalization |
| EQ-5 | $\theta_{c} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_{d}}$ | Solids Retention Time / Mean Cell Residence Time (days); k_d = endogenous decay coefficient (d⁻¹) |
| EQ-6 | $D = \dfrac{1}{\theta} = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Dilution rate (h⁻¹); washout when D > μ_max |
| EQ-7 | $\eta_{VS} = \dfrac{VS_{in} - VS_{out}}{VS_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Volatile solids reduction efficiency (%) |
| EQ-8 | $E_{out} = V_{CH_4} \cdot \rho_{CH_4} \cdot LHV_{CH_4}$ | Energy output (MJ); LHV_CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg |
| EQ-9 | $\text{C/N}_{\text{opt}} \approx 25\text{–}30$ | Composting C/N ratio for microbial assimilation |
| EQ-10 | $\text{VFA/TA} < 0.4$ | Process stability indicator; FOS/TAC ratio in AD |
| EQ-11 | $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -31 \;\text{kJ/mol CH}_4$ | Methanogenic thermodynamic threshold |
| EQ-12 | $C_{bulk} = C^{*} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-k_{L}a \cdot t}\right)$ | Oxygen transfer in aerobic bioreactors (k_La = volumetric mass transfer coefficient, h⁻¹) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Absolute Values in Tables:**
> Always write `K_S`, `Y_X/S`, etc., as **inline math with `\vert` or `\mid`** if magnitude bars are needed. Avoid the pipe character `|` in markdown table cells — it breaks column rendering. KTU digital answer sheets auto-reject malformed tables.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations underpin the design of:
- **Continuous Stirred-Tank Reactors (CSTR)** for biogas plants.
- **Plug Flow Reactors (PFR)** for solid-state fermentation / drum composting.
- **Upflow Anaerobic Sludge Blanket (UASB)** reactors for high-strength industrial effluents.
- **Sequential Batch Reactors (SBR)** for wastewater with biological phosphorus removal.

The Monod framework (EQ-1) integrates with reactor design to predict **substrate breakthrough**, **biomass washout**, and **steady-state conversions** — all standard Part B problems in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS AND ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Monod Kinetics Applied to a CSTR

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A CSTR bioreactor processes a glucose-containing wastewater with:
- Influent substrate concentration: $S_0 = 5.0$ g/L
- Effluent substrate concentration: $S = 0.5$ g/L
- Yield coefficient: $Y_{X/S} = 0.45$ g biomass / g substrate
- Maximum specific growth rate: $\mu_{max} = 0.35$ h⁻¹
- Half-saturation constant: $K_S = 0.20$ g/L
- Decay coefficient: $k_d = 0.05$ h⁻¹

**Required:** (a) Specific growth rate μ at steady state, (b) Steady-state biomass concentration X, (c) Critical dilution rate D_c (washout threshold).

---

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Specific Growth Rate (Monod Equation)**

From EQ-1 in our formula sheet:

$$
\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\mu = 0.35 \;\text{h}^{-1} \cdot \dfrac{0.5 \;\text{g/L}}{0.20 \;\text{g/L} + 0.5 \;\text{g/L}}
$$

$$
\mu = 0.35 \;\text{h}^{-1} \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{0.7}
$$

$$
\mu = 0.35 \;\text{h}^{-1} \cdot 0.7143
$$

$$
\mu = 0.250 \;\text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The microbes operate at **71.4% of their maximum growth capacity**, indicating healthy substrate availability.

---

**Part (b) — Steady-State Biomass Concentration**

For a CSTR at steady state, the substrate mass balance yields:

$$
D(S_0 - S) = \dfrac{\mu \cdot X}{Y_{X/S}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for X:

$$
X = Y_{X/S} \cdot D \cdot (S_0 - S) \cdot \dfrac{K_S + S}{S} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\mu_{\max}}
$$

But a more direct path uses the observed biomass yield corrected for decay. From the steady-state biomass balance:

$$
X = \dfrac{Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - S)}{1 + \dfrac{k_d}{\mu}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
X = \dfrac{0.45 \cdot (5.0 - 0.5)}{1 + \dfrac{0.05}{0.250}}
$$

$$
X = \dfrac{0.45 \cdot 4.5}{1 + 0.20}
$$

$$
X = \dfrac{2.025}{1.20}
$$

$$
X = 1.6875 \;\text{g/L}
$$

---

**Part (c) — Critical Dilution Rate (Washout Threshold)**

At washout, μ equals D (no biomass retention), and S approaches S₀. Thus:

$$
D_c = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S_0}{K_S + S_0}
$$

Substituting:

$$
D_c = 0.35 \;\text{h}^{-1} \cdot \dfrac{5.0}{0.20 + 5.0}
$$

$$
D_c = 0.35 \;\text{h}^{-1} \cdot \dfrac{5.0}{5.20}
$$

$$
D_c = 0.35 \;\text{h}^{-1} \cdot 0.9615
$$

$$
D_c = 0.3365 \;\text{h}^{-1}
$$

**Engineering conclusion:** Operate the CSTR with D < 0.3365 h⁻¹ to avoid biomass washout. A safety factor of 0.6–0.8 is typical in design practice, giving $D_{design} = 0.20$–$0.27$ h⁻¹.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Stoichiometry of Methane Yield (Buswell Equation)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

Using the **generalized Buswell equation**, derive the theoretical methane yield from glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆).

### 3.2.2 Complete Derivation

The Buswell equation for a generic organic compound $C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d$ is:

$$
C_n H_a O_b N_c S_d + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4} + \dfrac{d}{2}\right) H_2O
$$

$$
\rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} + \dfrac{d}{4}\right) CH_4
$$

$$
+ \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} - \dfrac{d}{4}\right) CO_2 + c \, NH_3 + d \, H_2S
$$

**For glucose:** $C_6 H_{12} O_6$ → n = 6, a = 12, b = 6, c = 0, d = 0.

Compute the water coefficient:

$$
n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} = 6 - \dfrac{12}{4} - \dfrac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0
$$

Compute the CH₄ coefficient:

$$
\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} = \dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$

Compute the CO₂ coefficient:

$$
\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} = \dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

Therefore the balanced reaction is:

$$
C_6 H_{12} O_6 \rightarrow 3 \, CH_4 + 3 \, CO_2
$$

**Mass-based methane yield calculation:**

Molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol.
Moles of CH₄ per mole of glucose = 3.
Mass of CH₄ per mole glucose = $3 \times 16 = 48$ g.

Theoretical yield:

$$
Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{48}{180} = 0.267 \;\text{g CH}_4 / \text{g glucose}
$$

Converting to volumetric yield at STP (1 mol = 22.4 L):

$$
V_{CH_4} = \dfrac{48 / 16}{180} \times 22.4 = \dfrac{3}{180} \times 22.4 = 0.373 \;\text{L CH}_4 / \text{g glucose}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
V_{CH_4} = 0.373 \;\text{m}^3 \text{CH}_4 / \text{kg glucose}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize the Buswell stoichiometric coefficients — examiners frequently award 2 marks simply for writing the balanced equation for a given organic substrate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Mass Balance for Aerobic Composting

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A windrow composting facility receives **1000 kg/day** of mixed organic waste with moisture content **65% (wet basis)** and VS content of the dry solids **85%**. The C/N ratio of the mix is **28:1**. Aeration supplies **5.0 m³ air per kg VS destroyed**. Determine the daily volatile solids destroyed and the CO₂ emissions.

### 3.3.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate VS input per day:**

Dry solids = $1000 \times (1 - 0.65) = 350$ kg/day.

Volatile solids input:

$$
VS_{in} = 350 \times 0.85 = 297.5 \;\text{kg VS/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply typical composting VS reduction:**

Industry-typical VS reduction = 50% for well-managed windrow composting:

$$
VS_{destroyed} = 0.50 \times 297.5 = 148.75 \;\text{kg VS/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate CO₂ emitted:**

Using the empirical conversion (1 kg VS destroyed → ~1.83 kg CO₂ via aerobic respiration):

$$
m_{CO_2} = 1.83 \times 148.75 = 272.21 \;\text{kg CO}_2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compost output:**

$$
Compost_{out} = 1000 - (\text{water evaporated}) - (\text{CO}_2 \text{ lost}) \approx 380 - 420 \;\text{kg/day}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Aerobic Composting** | **Anaerobic Digestion** | **Dark Fermentation** |
|---------------|-----------------------|-------------------------|----------------------|
| Electron acceptor | O₂ | Organic / CO₂ | Pyruvate |
| Primary output | Compost + CO₂ | Biogas + digestate | H₂ + organic acids |
| Energy recovery | None (exothermic) | **High** (combustible CH₄) | Moderate (H₂) |
| C/N optimum | 25–30 | 20–30 | 15–25 |
| HRT (days) | 21–60 | 15–40 | 1–5 |
| Net GHG | CO₂ (biogenic) | Avoided CH₄ emissions | Avoided fossil H₂ |
| Pathogen kill | **High** (55–65 °C thermophilic) | Moderate (35–55 °C) | Limited |
| KTU typical question | Mass balance, C/N | Monod + BMP | Yield coefficient |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS AND SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anaerobic Digestion Plant

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of an industrial anaerobic digestion facility, showing material streams, energy loops, and process control nodes. (Per protocol: alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Feedstock Receiving and Pre-Processing] --> A2[Size Reduction and Mixing]
    A2 --> A3[Hydrolysis Reactor Stage 1]
    A3 --> A4[Acidogenesis Reactor Stage 2]
    A4 --> A5[Acetogenesis Reactor Stage 3]
    A5 --> A6[Methanogenesis Reactor Stage 4]
    A6 --> A7[Biogas Collection and Purification]
    A6 --> A8[Digestate Dewatering]
    A7 --> A9[CHP Combined Heat and Power Unit]
    A7 --> A10[Biomethane Upgrading to Grid]
    A8 --> A11[Liquid Fraction N P K Recovery]
    A8 --> A12[Solid Fraction Compost Output]
    A9 --> A13[Heat Recycle to Reactor Stage 4]
    A1 --> P1[Process Control Sensor Node]
    P1 --> A1
    P1 --> A6
    P1 --> A9
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Nodes A3–A6 represent the **four sequential biochemical stages**.
- A7 splits biogas into two parallel value streams: **electricity (CHP)** and **purified methane (grid injection)**.
- A13 closes the **energy loop** by recycling thermal energy to maintain mesophilic conditions (≈ 37 °C).
- P1 is the **process control sensor node**, indicating where pH, temperature, ORP, and VFA sensors are placed for real-time monitoring.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mass and Energy Flow in Biological Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Organic Waste Input] --> B2[Pre-Treatment]
    B2 --> B3[Bioreactor System]
    B3 --> B4[Gas Phase Output]
    B3 --> B5[Liquid Output Digestate]
    B3 --> B6[Solid Output Compost]
    B4 --> B7[Energy Recovery CHP]
    B7 --> B8[Electricity to Grid]
    B7 --> B9[Heat to Reactor]
    B5 --> B10[Nutrient Recovery N P K]
    B6 --> B11[Soil Amendment Market]
    B10 --> B12[Agricultural Reuse]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision-Tree Diagram — Choosing a Biological Conversion Route

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Waste Characterization] --> C2{Moisture Content}
    C2 -- Greater Than 80 percent --> C3[Anaerobic Digestion Wet Route]
    C2 -- Between 50 to 80 percent --> C4[Co-Digestion or Mixed Reactor]
    C2 -- Less Than 50 percent --> C5[Aerobic Composting Windrow or In-Vessel]
    C1 --> C6{BMP Test Value}
    C6 -- Greater Than 300 L CH4 per kg VS --> C7[Prioritize Anaerobic Digestion]
    C6 -- Less Than 100 L CH4 per kg VS --> C8[Composting Preferred]
    C1 --> C9{Target Product}
    C9 -- Energy CH4 or H2 --> C10[AD or Dark Fermentation]
    C9 -- Soil Amendment --> C11[Composting]
    C9 -- Nutrient Recovery --> C12[Struvite Precipitation plus AD]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Process Control Loop for Biogas Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Reactor Temperature Sensor] --> D2[PID Controller]
    D3[Reactor pH Sensor] --> D2
    D4[Gas Flow Meter] --> D2
    D2 --> D5[Actuator Heater Jacket]
    D2 --> D6[Actuator Feed Pump]
    D2 --> D7[Actuator Alkali Dosing Pump]
    D5 --> D1
    D6 --> D3
    D7 --> D3
```

This control architecture directly supports **CO4 (Process Control and Optimization)** of the KTU 2024 PECHT867 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK AND TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** Define **Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP)** and list three factors that affect BMP measurement.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **BMP Definition:** Biochemical Methane Potential (BMP) is the **maximum volume of methane (in m³ or L at STP) produced per unit mass of volatile solids (VS)** added to an anaerobic digester under standard laboratory conditions (35 °C or 55 °C, infinite time, optimal nutrients).

**Three factors affecting BMP:**
1. **Substrate composition** — lignin content, cellulose crystallinity, lipid fraction.
2. **Inoculum-to-substrate ratio (ISR)** — optimum I/S = 1:1 to 2:1 (VS basis).
3. **Operating temperature** — mesophilic (35 °C) vs. thermophilic (55 °C) kinetics.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Explain the significance of the **C/N ratio** in biological waste conversion processes.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **carbon-to-nitrogen (C/N) ratio** governs microbial metabolism in both composting and anaerobic digestion.

- **Too high C/N (> 35):** Nitrogen becomes limiting → slow microbial growth, long retention time.
- **Too low C/N (< 20):** Excess nitrogen → ammonia inhibition, pH rise, methane yield drop.
- **Optimum C/N:** **25:1 to 30:1** for composting; **20:1 to 30:1** for AD.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Module 3:**
> - **Skipping the Monod derivation** costs 2–3 marks even if the final number is correct.
> - **Not stating operating conditions** (mesophilic/thermophilic, HRT) loses 1 mark.
> - **No mass balance closure** (input ≠ output + accumulation) → −2 marks.
> - **Drawing process flow without labeling streams** → −1 mark.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Module 3, Internal Choice Option A)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four biochemical stages of anaerobic digestion**, and write the **Buswell equation** for a waste of composition $C_{10} H_{19} O_3 N$ (assuming d = 0, no sulfur). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Stage 1 — Hydrolysis:**
Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down into monomers by extracellular enzymes (cellulases, amylases, proteases, lipases). This stage is rate-limiting for high-lignocellulosic wastes.

**Stage 2 — Acidogenesis:**
Monomers (sugars, amino acids) are fermented by acidogenic bacteria into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), alcohols, H₂, and CO₂.

**Stage 3 — Acetogenesis:**
VFAs and longer-chain fatty acids are oxidized to acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by syntrophic acetogens. Reaction is thermodynamically feasible only if H₂ partial pressure is low (< 10⁻⁴ atm).

**Stage 4 — Methanogenesis:**
Acetate (≈ 70%) and H₂/CO₂ (≈ 30%) are converted to CH₄ by methanogenic archaea.

**Buswell Equation for C₁₀H₁₉O₃N:**

Substituting n = 10, a = 19, b = 3, c = 1, d = 0:

Water coefficient:
$$
n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2} + \dfrac{3c}{4} = 10 - \dfrac{19}{4} - \dfrac{3}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4} = 10 - 4.75 - 1.5 + 0.75 = 4.5
$$

CH₄ coefficient:
$$
\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4} + \dfrac{3c}{8} = 5 - \dfrac{19}{8} + \dfrac{3}{4} + \dfrac{3}{8} = 5 - 2.375 + 0.75 + 0.375 = 3.75
$$

CO₂ coefficient:
$$
\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{3c}{8} = 5 + 2.375 - 0.75 - 0.375 = 6.25
$$

Balanced reaction:

$$
C_{10} H_{19} O_3 N + 4.5 \, H_2 O \rightarrow 3.75 \, CH_4 + 6.25 \, CO_2 + NH_3
$$

---

**Q. (b)** A CSTR anaerobic digester operates at steady state. Influent COD = 12,000 mg/L, effluent COD = 1800 mg/L. Yield coefficient Y = 0.06 g VSS/g COD, decay coefficient k_d = 0.03 d⁻¹, HRT = 20 days, μ_max = 0.30 d⁻¹, K_S = 800 mg/L. Calculate: **(i)** Specific growth rate μ, **(ii)** Biomass concentration X, **(iii)** Solids Retention Time (SRT/θ_c), and **(iv)** State whether the system is at washout risk. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step (i) — Specific growth rate μ:**

$$
\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_S + S} = 0.30 \cdot \dfrac{1800}{800 + 1800} = 0.30 \cdot \dfrac{1800}{2600}
$$

$$
\mu = 0.30 \cdot 0.6923 = 0.2077 \;\text{d}^{-1}
$$

**Step (ii) — Biomass concentration X:**

For CSTR at steady state (COD balance):
$$
X = \dfrac{Y \cdot (S_0 - S)}{1 + k_d / \mu} = \dfrac{0.06 \cdot (12000 - 1800)}{1 + 0.03 / 0.2077}
$$

$$
X = \dfrac{0.06 \cdot 10200}{1 + 0.1444} = \dfrac{612}{1.1444}
$$

$$
X = 534.78 \;\text{mg VSS/L}
$$

**Step (iii) — Solids Retention Time (SRT):**

$$
\theta_c = \dfrac{1}{\mu - k_d} = \dfrac{1}{0.2077 - 0.03} = \dfrac{1}{0.1777} = 5.628 \;\text{days}
$$

**Step (iv) — Washout analysis:**

$$
D = \dfrac{1}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 \;\text{d}^{-1}
$$

Since D = 0.05 d⁻¹ < μ = 0.2077 d⁻¹, the system operates well below washout. **No washout risk.**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Module 3, Internal Choice Option B)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Q. (a)** Compare **aerobic composting and anaerobic digestion** as biological conversion routes. Discuss their merits, demerits, and process conditions in detail. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| **Criterion** | **Aerobic Composting** | **Anaerobic Digestion** |
|---------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|
| Oxygen requirement | Continuous aeration | Strictly oxygen-free |
| Energy output | None — exothermic losses | High — biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) |
| GHG emissions | CO₂, biogenic | Avoided CH₄, low carbon |
| Process temperature | 55–65 °C (thermophilic) | 35 °C (mesophilic) or 55 °C (thermophilic) |
| HRT | 21–60 days | 15–40 days |
| C/N ratio optimum | 25–30 | 20–30 |
| Pathogen destruction | Excellent (thermophilic phase) | Moderate |
| End-products | Humus-like compost | Biogas + nutrient-rich digestate |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher (gas-tight reactors) |
| Operational complexity | Simple aeration control | Complex multi-stage microbiology |

**Merits of Composting:**
- Low capital investment; simple technology.
- Excellent pathogen kill in thermophilic phase.
- Produces stable, marketable soil conditioner.

**Demerits of Composting:**
- No energy recovery; significant CO₂ and water loss.
- Long retention time; large land footprint.
- Odor control requires active management.

**Merits of Anaerobic Digestion:**
- Net energy positive (biogas); renewable electricity.
- Nutrient recovery in digestate.
- Reduced GHG emissions vs. open dumping.

**Demerits of Anaerobic Digestion:**
- Higher capital cost and skilled operation.
- Sensitive to toxic shocks (ammonia, LCFA, heavy metals).
- Complex microbial ecology requiring process control.

---

**Q. (b)** A food processing industry generates 5,000 kg/day of organic waste with moisture content 75% (wet basis) and VS fraction of 80% (dry basis). This waste is fed to a mesophilic anaerobic digester with HRT = 30 days. The BMP of the waste is 0.42 m³ CH₄/kg VS. Calculate: **(i)** Daily methane production, **(ii)** Daily electricity generation if CHP efficiency is 35% (electrical) and 45% (thermal), and **(iii)** Reactor volume required. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step (i) — Daily methane production:**

Dry solids per day = $5000 \times (1 - 0.75) = 1250$ kg/day.

VS per day = $1250 \times 0.80 = 1000$ kg VS/day.

Methane volume:

$$
V_{CH_4} = 1000 \;\text{kg VS/day} \times 0.42 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg VS} = 420 \;\text{m}^3 \text{CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

**Step (ii) — Daily electricity generation:**

Methane mass = $420 \times \dfrac{16}{22.4} = 420 \times 0.7143 = 300$ kg CH₄/day.

Methane energy content (LHV) = $300 \times 50 = 15{,}000$ MJ/day.

Electrical output:

$$
E_{elec} = 15{,}000 \times 0.35 = 5250 \;\text{MJ/day}
$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$
E_{elec} = \dfrac{5250}{3.6} = 1458.33 \;\text{kWh/day}
$$

Thermal output (recoverable):

$$
E_{therm} = 15{,}000 \times 0.45 = 6750 \;\text{MJ/day}
$$

**Step (iii) — Reactor volume:**

$$
V_{reactor} = HRT \times Q_{daily} = 30 \;\text{days} \times 5{,}000 \;\text{kg/day} = 150{,}000 \;\text{kg}
$$

Assuming density ≈ 1000 kg/m³ (wet waste):

$$
V_{reactor} \approx 150 \;\text{m}^3
$$

In practice, a **safety factor of 1.2–1.5** is applied, giving a design volume of **180–225 m³**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP AND IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Biological Conversion**

- **BMP range:** 0.20–0.50 m³ CH₄/kg VS added (industry benchmark).
- **Monod equation** (EQ-1) is the **universal kinetic backbone** — appear in nearly every KTU Part B question.
- **Four AD stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- **Methanogens are the bottleneck:** Slowest growers, most pH-sensitive, dictate SRT.
- **C/N optimum:** 25–30 (composting); 20–30 (AD). Outside this range → inhibition.
- **FOS/TAC ratio < 0.4** indicates stable AD; > 0.5 signals impending failure.
- **Buswell equation** yields theoretical CH₄ from stoichiometry — must memorize the water, CH₄, CO₂ coefficient formulas.
- **HRT vs. SRT:** HRT = hydraulic retention time; SRT = mean cell residence time = 1/(μ − k_d). In a CSTR, HRT = SRT.
- **Washout condition:** D > μ_max · S₀/(K_S + S₀) → biomass loss.
- **Aerobic vs. Anaerobic trade-off:** Aerobic = faster, no energy recovery; Anaerobic = slower, energy positive.
- **Energy value of biogas:** LHV ≈ 50 MJ/kg CH₄; 1 m³ CH₄ ≈ 10 kWh thermal.
- **Process control nodes:** pH, ORP, temperature, VFA/TA ratio, gas flow rate.
- **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic links to **CO3 (Process Modeling)**, **CO4 (Process Control)**, and **CO5 (Sustainability)**.
- **Examiner pet questions:** Monod in CSTR; Buswell stoichiometry; BMP calculation; CHP energy balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Day Mantra:**
> "Always write the **Monod equation first**, then plug values. Never forget to **state operating temperature** (mesophilic/thermophilic). Close every mass balance with a **conservation statement**: *Input = Output + Accumulation + Reaction.*"
>
> — KTU 2024 PECHT867 Module 3 Anchor Concept

---

topics.title: Anaerobic Digestion: Process, Types, and Products
Technologies and Systems for Anaerobic Digestion
Fermentation: Ethanol Production from Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fermentation: Ethanol Production from Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fermentation** is a metabolic process in which microorganisms (yeast or bacteria) convert carbohydrates—typically sugars such as glucose, sucrose, or fructose—into ethanol and carbon dioxide, generally under anaerobic (or micro-aerobic) conditions. In the context of **Process Systems Engineering**, fermentation is treated as a **heterogeneous, biologically-catalysed reactive system** characterised by coupled mass, energy, and momentum transfer, intracellular kinetics, and dynamic stoichiometry.

**Ethanol Production from Waste** is a biorefinery process route that converts low-cost, non-food biomass residues (e.g., agricultural waste, food waste, lignocellulosic biomass, municipal solid waste) into bioethanol through a sequence of **pretreatment → hydrolysis → fermentation → separation (distillation/dehydration)**. It is formally classified as a **second-generation (2G) biofuel pathway** under renewable energy engineering.

The canonical stoichiometric equation for the alcoholic fermentation of glucose by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \quad \Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -235\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fermentation of sugars to ethanol is the **central reactive step** in any 2G ethanol plant. The reaction is **exergonic** ($\Delta G < 0$), meaning the system releases free energy and proceeds spontaneously under standard biochemical conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Brewery Micro-Factory"**
> Imagine a sealed kitchen where tiny workers (yeast cells) are fed sugary syrup (glucose from broken-down waste). Because there is no air, instead of "breathing normally" and growing big, they switch to a different mode: they break each sugar molecule into two parts. One part they keep as fuel for themselves (ethanol), and the other part they "exhale" as gas (CO₂). The kitchen slowly fills with froth (the bubbles of CO₂), and the syrup becomes a mild alcoholic broth. What the engineer does is scale this "kitchen" to a 100 m³ stainless steel vessel, feed it pre-broken food waste, control the temperature at **30–35 °C**, and continuously extract the alcohol at the other end. That is fermentation in a nutshell.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Bioethanol Engineering

The most important numerical targets that a KTU examiner expects you to remember are listed below:

- **Theoretical maximum ethanol yield:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (≈ **51.1 % w/w**).
- **Practical industrial yield (2G process):** **0.40 – 0.46 g ethanol / g glucose** (~80–90 % of theoretical).
- **Heat released by fermentation:** **ΔH_rxn ≈ –227 kJ per mole of glucose** consumed.
- **Standard operating pH:** **4.0 – 5.0** (to suppress contaminating bacteria).
- **Optimal temperature for *S. cerevisiae*:** **30 – 35 °C** (mesophilic regime).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The number **0.511 g/g** comes directly from molar mass ratios: 2 mol ethanol (46 g/mol) produced from 1 mol glucose (180 g/mol) gives $2 \times 46 / 180 = 0.5111$. This is a **highly-tested KTU value**.

### 1.4 Why Use *Waste*? The Process-Systems Argument

Using waste as feedstock is attractive because it simultaneously solves three engineering problems:

1. **Waste valorisation** — converting a disposal liability into a value-added product.
2. **Food-vs-fuel conflict avoidance** — 1G ethanol uses corn/sugarcane, which competes with food supply.
3. **Negative / low carbon footprint** — the CO₂ released is biogenic, closing the atmospheric carbon cycle within 1–2 growing seasons.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Process Architecture (Why and How)

The conversion of waste biomass to fuel-grade ethanol is a **multi-stage, integrated process system**. Each stage addresses a specific mass-transfer or reaction-engineering bottleneck. Below is the operational logic, structured step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Feedstock Preparation & Size Reduction:**
  - *Why:* Lignocellulosic and food wastes are heterogeneous solids with poor heat/mass-transfer properties.
  - *How:* Mechanical shredding, milling, and sieving reduce particle size to **< 5 mm**, increasing the accessible surface area for downstream reactions.

- **Step 2 — Pretreatment (Physical / Chemical / Biological):**
  - *Why:* Lignocellulose is a recalcitrant composite of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Without pretreatment, enzymes cannot access the cellulose.
  - *How:* Dilute-acid (H₂SO₄, **0.5 – 2 % w/w** at **120 – 200 °C**), steam explosion, alkaline (NaOH), or hydrothermal methods are used to **liberate hemicellulose sugars** and **disrupt the lignin seal**.

- **Step 3 — Hydrolysis (Saccharification):**
  - *Why:* Cellulose is a polymer of glucose; it must be depolymerised into monomeric glucose before yeast can ferment it.
  - *How:* Either **acid hydrolysis** (chemical) or **enzymatic hydrolysis** using cellulase cocktails at **45 – 55 °C**, pH **4.5 – 5.0**, residence time **48 – 72 h**.

- **Step 4 — Fermentation (The Heart of the Process):**
  - *Why:* This is the **bio-catalytic conversion** where sugars become ethanol. It is the unit operation that gives the system its name.
  - *How:* Conducted in a **CSTR / fed-batch / continuous bioreactor** with *S. cerevisiae* or *Zymomonas mobilis* under anaerobic conditions, **30 – 35 °C**, pH **4.5 – 5.0**, residence time **24 – 72 h**.

- **Step 5 — Solid–Liquid Separation & Beer Stripping:**
  - *Why:* Cells, unconverted solids, and inhibitory byproducts (furfural, HMF) must be removed to protect downstream columns.
  - *How:* Centrifugation / filtration followed by beer-column stripping at atmospheric pressure.

- **Step 6 — Distillation & Dehydration (Molecular Sieve / Azeotropic Distillation):**
  - *Why:* Fermentation broth contains only **6 – 12 % w/w ethanol**. Fuel grade requires **> 99.5 %** (anhydrous).
  - *How:* Multi-effect distillation columns followed by a 3-Å molecular sieve to break the **ethanol–water azeotrope at 95.6 % w/w ethanol**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are *tested* in KTU Board Examinations for Process Systems Engineering, Bioprocess Engineering, and Chemical Reaction Engineering modules.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$ | Molar mass basis | Stoichiometric backbone of alcoholic fermentation |
| 2 | $Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511$ | g ethanol / g glucose | Theoretical maximum ethanol yield |
| 3 | $Y_{E/S}^{\text{actual}} = \eta \times 0.511$ | g/g; $\eta$ = process efficiency (0.80 – 0.90) | Practical yield used in plant design |
| 4 | $\mu = \mu_{\max} \dfrac{S}{K_S + S}$ | $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹), $S$ = substrate (g/L) | Monod kinetics for cell growth |
| 5 | $q_P = Y_{P/X} \cdot \mu$ | $q_P$ = specific productivity (g P / g X · h) | Links product formation to growth |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu X - k_d X$ | $X$ = biomass (g/L), $k_d$ = death rate (h⁻¹) | Cell mass balance |
| 7 | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}} X$ | $Y_{X/S}$ = yield coeff. (g X / g S) | Substrate consumption rate |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = q_P X$ | $P$ = ethanol (g/L) | Product formation rate |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = -227\,\text{kJ/mol}$ glucose | Heat released | Used in fermenter cooling duty |
| 10 | $Q_{\text{cool}} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \cdot r_S \cdot V$ | W; $r_S$ = glucose consumption (mol/L·s) | Cooling coil design parameter |
| 11 | $X_{deg} = 1 - e^{-k t}$ | $k$ = sterilisation constant (s⁻¹) | Degree of sterilisation (Del factor) |
| 12 | $\text{Conversion } X = \dfrac{S_0 - S}{S_0}$ | Dimensionless | Single-pass sugar conversion in CSTR |
| 13 | $\text{Selectivity} = \dfrac{Y_{E/S}^{\text{actual}}}{Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}}}$ | Dimensionless (0 – 1) | Catalyst/biological selectivity |
| 14 | $\text{ATP yield} = 2$ mol ATP / mol glucose | — | Energy budget of yeast cells |
| 15 | $\text{Carbon balance:} \quad \dot{n}_{C,\text{in}} = \dot{n}_{C,\text{out}}$ | mol C / h | Mass balance closure check |
| 16 | $R_{\text{CO}_2/E} = 1$ (mol/mol) | — | Biogenic CO₂ footprint per mol ethanol |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and "given that" separators in the table above have been rendered as $\vert$ and $\mid$ respectively — **not** the raw vertical bar `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity. This is a common KTU typesetting error to avoid in your own answer scripts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bioethanol-from-waste plants are not academic exercises. They are **operating commercial facilities** (e.g., POET-DSM *Project Liberty*, Raízen *Costa Pinto*, Praj Industries 2G plants). Process-systems engineers are needed to:

- Develop **steady-state and dynamic process simulations** (Aspen Plus, SuperPro Designer).
- Perform **heat-integration / pinch analysis** to minimise steam consumption in the distillation train (steam accounts for **~ 50 %** of plant energy use).
- Design **robust control systems** for fermenter pH, temperature, dissolved O₂, and feed rate.
- Conduct **LCA (life-cycle analysis)** and techno-economic assessment (TEA) for feasibility studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

We want to rigorously derive the universal constant $Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} = 0.511$ g/g that all KTU examiners expect you to reproduce from first principles.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation.**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Identify molar masses of each species** (these are **standard atomic mass values**, treat as constants).

$$M_{C_6H_{12}O_6} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M_{C_2H_5OH} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 46 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M_{CO_2} = 1(12) + 2(16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the stoichiometric coefficients (moles) to masses** for one mole of glucose reacted.

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 2 \text{ mol} \times 46 \text{ g/mol} = 92 \text{ g}$$

$$\text{Mass of glucose reacted} = 1 \text{ mol} \times 180 \text{ g/mol} = 180 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio (yield definition).**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} &= \frac{\text{Mass of ethanol produced}}{\text{Mass of glucose consumed}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times M_{C_2H_5OH}}{1 \times M_{C_6H_{12}O_6}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 46}{180} \\
&= \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111\ \text{g ethanol / g glucose}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Express the same yield on a percentage basis** (common in engineering reports).

$$Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} \times 100\% = 51.11\ \%\ \text{w/w}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Out of every **100 g of glucose** fed to a perfectly efficient fermenter, a maximum of **51.11 g** of pure ethanol can be produced. The remaining 48.89 g is split between CO₂ (≈ 48.89 g) and trace byproducts. This is the **upper bound**; real fermenters reach **80 – 90 %** of this value.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Balance on a Continuous Stirred-Tank Fermenter (CSTR)

Consider a CSTR operating at steady state with feed glucose concentration $S_0$ (g/L) and volumetric flow rate $F$ (L/h). The volume is $V$ (L), biomass concentration $X$ (g/L), substrate $S$ (g/L), and ethanol $P$ (g/L). The outlet stream is sterile (no cells).

**Component balances at steady state (accumulation = 0):**

For **biomass** (cells are retained, but for a CSTR they leave with the outlet):

$$F X_0 - F X + V \mu X = 0$$

If the feed is sterile ($X_0 = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
-F X + V \mu X &= 0 \\
V \mu X &= F X \\
\mu &= \frac{F}{V} = \frac{1}{\tau}
\end{aligned}$$

This yields the **fundamental dilution-rate identity** for a chemostat: $\mu = D = 1/\tau$, where $D$ is dilution rate (h⁻¹) and $\tau$ is residence time (h).

For **substrate** (glucose):

$$F S_0 - F S - V \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} = 0$$

Substituting $\mu = D$:

$$D(S_0 - S) = \frac{\mu X}{Y_{X/S}} = \frac{D X}{Y_{X/S}}$$

$$\therefore S_0 - S = \frac{X}{Y_{X/S}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S)$$

For **product** (ethanol):

$$F P_0 - F P + V q_P X = 0$$

With $P_0 = 0$ and $q_P = Y_{P/X} \mu$:

$$\begin{aligned}
- F P + V Y_{P/X} \mu X &= 0 \\
P &= \frac{V Y_{P/X} \mu X}{F} = Y_{P/X} \mu X \tau = Y_{P/X} X \\
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $X = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S)$:

$$P = Y_{P/X} Y_{X/S} (S_0 - S) = Y_{P/S} (S_0 - S)$$

**Conclusion:** In a CSTR, the steady-state ethanol concentration equals the **product yield coefficient** $Y_{P/S}$ multiplied by the amount of substrate consumed.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Duty of a Fermenter

The fermentation reaction is exothermic. The cooling duty $Q_{\text{cool}}$ required to maintain isothermal operation is:

$$Q_{\text{cool}} = -\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} \cdot r_S \cdot V$$

For a typical industrial batch where $S_0 = 100$ g/L of glucose in a $V = 100$ m³ fermenter, with $90 \%$ conversion in $\tau = 48$ h:

**Step 1 — Calculate moles of glucose consumed.**

$$n_{S,\text{consumed}} = \frac{100 \text{ g/L} \times 0.90 \times 100{,}000 \text{ L}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} = 50{,}000 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total heat released.**

$$Q_{\text{released}} = 50{,}000 \text{ mol} \times 227 \text{ kJ/mol} = 1.135 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to average cooling power over 48 h.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{cool}} &= \frac{1.135 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ}}{48 \times 3600 \text{ s}} \\
&= \frac{1.135 \times 10^{7}}{172{,}800} \\
&\approx 65.7 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 100 m³ fermenter needs continuous removal of about **65 – 70 kW** of heat. This determines the sizing of cooling coils, jacket area, and chilled-water flow rate.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Process Configurations

| Configuration | Pros | Cons | Typical Yield (g/g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SHF** (Separate Hydrolysis & Fermentation) | Each step at its own optimum T (50 °C hydrolysis, 30 °C fermentation) | End-product inhibition of cellulase | 0.40 – 0.45 |
| **SSF** (Simultaneous Saccharification & Fermentation) | Low capital cost, no end-product inhibition of cellulase | Compromise T (~37 °C), lower enzyme activity | 0.42 – 0.48 |
| **SSCFE** (Simultaneous Saccharification, Co-Fermentation & Extraction) | In-situ product recovery | Complex, expensive | 0.45 – 0.50 |
| **CBP** (Consolidated Bioprocessing) | Single organism, lowest cost | Microbial engineering still in R&D | 0.30 – 0.40 (current) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, **SSF is the most commonly examined** configuration because it integrates two reaction systems into one reactor, which is a classic process-systems-intensification problem.

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Degree of Reduction (Carbon-Electron Balance)

A powerful check on fermentation stoichiometry is the **degree of reduction (γ)** per carbon atom. For each compound, $\gamma$ counts the electrons transferred to oxygen upon complete combustion.

For a general formula $C_a H_b O_c N_d$:

$$\gamma = \frac{4a + b - 2c - 3d}{a}$$

Applied to **glucose** $C_6H_{12}O_6$:

$$\gamma_{C_6H_{12}O_6} = \frac{4(6) + 12 - 2(6)}{6} = \frac{24 + 12 - 12}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4$$

Applied to **ethanol** $C_2H_6O$:

$$\gamma_{C_2H_5OH} = \frac{4(2) + 6 - 2(1)}{2} = \frac{8 + 6 - 2}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6$$

Applied to **CO₂** $CO_2$:

$$\gamma_{CO_2} = \frac{4(1) + 0 - 2(2)}{1} = 0$$

**Balance check** (electrons in = electrons out for the reaction $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2 C_2H_5OH + 2 CO_2$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Electrons in: } & 4 \times 6 = 24 \\
\text{Electrons out: } & (6 \times 2) + (0 \times 2) = 12
\end{aligned}$$

The mismatch (24 vs 12) reflects that only **half** the carbon is reduced; the other half is already fully oxidised to CO₂. This is a **signature of biological systems** that operate near thermodynamic equilibrium and cannot fully reverse oxidation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Bioethanol from Lignocellulosic Waste

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **end-to-end block-level functional architecture** of a 2G ethanol plant, including all major unit operations, recycle streams, and material flows. This is the exact level of detail expected in a KTU 14-mark descriptive answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed1["FEEDSTOCK AGRI WASTE STRAW BAGASSE"] --> prep1
    feed2["FEEDSTOCK MSW FOOD WASTE"] --> prep1
    prep1["SIZE REDUCTION SHREDDER HAMMERMILL"] --> pretreat1
    pretreat1["PRETREATMENT DILUTE H2SO4 150 C STEAM EXPLOSION"] --> sep1
    sep1["SOLID LIQUID SEPARATION"] --> hydro1
    sep1 --> rec1["RECYCLE HEMICELLULOSE HYDROLYSATE"]
    rec1 --> ferm1
    hydro1["ENZYMATIC HYDROLYSIS CELLULASE 50 C 72 H"] --> ferm1
    ferm1["FERMENTER CSTR OR FED BATCH SACCHAROMYCES 32 C ANAEROBIC"] --> sep2
    sep2["CENTRIFUGE CELL RECYCLE"] --> ferm1
    sep2 --> dist1["BEER COLUMN STRIPPING ATMOSPHERIC"]
    dist1 --> dist2["RECTIFYING COLUMN 95 W PERCENT ETHANOL"]
    dist2 --> dehyd1["MOLECULAR SIEVE 3 ANGSTROM ANHYDROUS ETHANOL 99.5 PERCENT"]
    dehyd1 --> prod1["PRODUCT FUEL GRADE ETHANOL"]
    dist2 --> waste1["WASTE WATER STILLAGE"]
    pretreat1 --> waste2["WASTE LIGNIN SOLID FUEL"]
    hydgas1["CO2 OFF GAS VENT OR CAPTURE"] -.-> ferm1
    hydgas1
    ferm1 -.-> hydgas1
```

### 4.2 Reactor-Level Schematic — SSF Configuration

The following diagram illustrates the **coupled kinetics** of simultaneous saccharification and fermentation, the most important reactor scheme in 2G ethanol. It shows the **two competing reaction networks** that operate inside a single vessel.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    celluloseA["CELLULOSE POLYMER SOLID"] --> hydA
    hydA["ENZYMATIC HYDROLYSIS CELLULASE 50 C"] --> glucoseA["GLUCOSE MONOMER"]
    glucoseA --> fermA
    fermA["FERMENTATION YEAST 32 C"] --> ethanolA["ETHANOL PRODUCT"]
    fermA --> co2A["CO2 OFF GAS"]
    fermA --> yeastA["YEAST CELL BIOMASS"]
    glucoseA -. INHIBITS .-> hydA
    ethanolA -. INHIBITS .-> fermA
    noteA["OPTIMAL COMPROMISE T 37 C PH 4.8 35 G PER L ETHANOL"]:::noteClass
    hydA -.- noteA
    fermA -.- noteA

    classDef noteClass fill:#fff5b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram — Dashed Arrows Indicate Inhibition Loops.**
> The glucose product from hydrolysis **end-product inhibits** the cellulase enzyme. The ethanol product from fermentation **end-product inhibits** the yeast. By co-locating both reactions in a single SSF vessel, the glucose is consumed as soon as it forms, **breaking the inhibition loop** on the enzyme. This is a beautiful example of *process intensification* — solving a kinetic problem by reactor design rather than by changing chemistry.

### 4.3 Mass-Flow Sankey Schematic (Text-Based)

For the benefit of KTU students writing short-answer questions, here is a **textual mass-flow schematic** for processing **1000 kg of dry lignocellulosic waste** (40 % cellulose, 30 % hemicellulose, 20 % lignin, 10 % ash), assuming 90 % hydrolysis efficiency and 90 % of theoretical ethanol yield.

- **Cellulose fraction → Glucose:** $1000 \times 0.40 \times 0.90 = 360$ kg cellulose hydrolysed.
- **Glucose produced:** $360 \times (180/162) \approx 400$ kg (anhydrous basis using hydration correction factor 180/162).
- **Theoretical ethanol:** $400 \times 0.511 = 204.4$ kg.
- **Actual ethanol (90 % of theoretical):** $204.4 \times 0.90 = 184.0$ kg.
- **CO₂ released:** $400 \times 0.489 = 195.6$ kg.
- **Lignin (residual solid fuel):** $1000 \times 0.20 = 200$ kg.
- **Ash + unconverted solids:** $1000 \times (0.10 + 0.40 \times 0.10) \approx 140$ kg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Calculate the mass of ethanol obtainable from 1 tonne of waste containing X% cellulose."* Use the chain **kg waste → kg cellulose → kg glucose → kg ethanol**, applying the factors 0.90, 180/162, 0.511, and the practical efficiency $\eta \approx 0.90$ in sequence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define fermentation in the context of ethanol production from waste. State the stoichiometric equation and the theoretical yield in g/g.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fermentation is an anaerobic bio-catalytic process in which microorganisms (typically *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) convert sugars to ethanol and CO₂. The stoichiometric equation is:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

The theoretical mass yield is:

$$Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511\ \text{g ethanol / g glucose}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three lignocellulosic waste feedstocks suitable for second-generation ethanol production. State one pretreatment method and one hydrolysis method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Feedstocks:** Rice straw, sugarcane bagasse, corn stover, wheat straw, municipal solid waste (MSW), food waste, sawdust.
2. **Pretreatment:** Dilute sulfuric acid treatment at 150 – 200 °C, or steam explosion.
3. **Hydrolysis:** Enzymatic hydrolysis using cellulase enzyme cocktail at 45 – 55 °C, pH 4.5 – 5.0, 48 – 72 h residence time.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** — **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Derive the theoretical ethanol yield from glucose stoichiometry, showing all molar mass calculations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A continuous stirred-tank fermenter (CSTR) is fed with $S_0 = 150$ g/L glucose at $F = 100$ L/h, with $V = 1000$ L, $Y_{X/S} = 0.10$ g/g, $Y_{P/S} = 0.45$ g/g. At steady state, calculate (i) the substrate concentration $S$, (ii) the biomass concentration $X$, and (iii) the ethanol concentration $P$ in the outlet. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation of theoretical yield:**

Stoichiometric equation: $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2 C_2H_5OH + 2 CO_2$

Molar masses:
- $M_{\text{glucose}} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180$ g/mol
- $M_{\text{ethanol}} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 46$ g/mol

Yield (mass of ethanol per mass of glucose):

$$Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} = \frac{2 \times 46}{1 \times 180} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111\ \text{g/g} = 51.11\ \%\ \text{w/w}$$

**(b) — CSTR steady-state balances:**

For a CSTR at steady state with sterile feed, mass balances yield:

$$X = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S)$$

$$P = Y_{P/S}(S_0 - S)$$

We are given $Y_{X/S} = 0.10$ g/g and $Y_{P/S} = 0.45$ g/g, and we need a third relation. **Note:** For this problem we are given a *single* substrate balance that must be closed by assuming a typical $S$ value; in real CSTR design one uses the Monod relation $\mu = D$ to compute $S$. Since the problem specifies $Y_{P/S}$ and $Y_{X/S}$ directly, we may compute the conversion from a measured outlet substrate.

**Assuming** a measured $S = 30$ g/L (typical for a well-designed CSTR):

**(i) Substrate concentration:**

$$S = 30\ \text{g/L (as assumed)}$$

**(ii) Biomass concentration:**

$$X = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S) = 0.10 \times (150 - 30) = 0.10 \times 120 = 12\ \text{g/L}$$

**(iii) Ethanol concentration:**

$$P = Y_{P/S}(S_0 - S) = 0.45 \times (150 - 30) = 0.45 \times 120 = 54\ \text{g/L}$$

**Final answers:** $S = 30$ g/L, $X = 12$ g/L, $P = 54$ g/L.

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]** — **Question B (14 marks, alternative choice)**

**(a)** Draw a labelled block flow diagram of a 2G bioethanol plant from lignocellulosic waste. List the function of each block. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A batch fermenter is loaded with 200 g/L glucose. After 36 h, the residual glucose is 20 g/L. The cell mass produced is 18 g/L and the ethanol formed is 75 g/L. Calculate: (i) the substrate conversion $X$, (ii) the actual ethanol yield $Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}$, (iii) the process selectivity with respect to the theoretical yield, and (iv) the heat released assuming $\Delta H = -227$ kJ/mol glucose. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Block flow diagram:** (See Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram of this document; the student must reproduce it as a hand-drawn flow diagram showing feedstock preparation, pretreatment, hydrolysis, fermentation, separation, distillation, dehydration.)

Each block has the following function:
1. **Feedstock preparation:** size reduction, homogenisation.
2. **Pretreatment:** break lignin seal, release hemicellulose sugars.
3. **Hydrolysis:** depolymerise cellulose to glucose.
4. **Fermentation:** biological conversion of glucose to ethanol.
5. **Cell separation:** centrifugation, recycle yeast.
6. **Distillation:** concentrate ethanol from ~10 % to 95 %.
7. **Dehydration:** break azeotrope to produce anhydrous ethanol (>99.5 %).

**(b) — Batch fermenter calculation:**

Given: $S_0 = 200$ g/L, $S = 20$ g/L, $X_{\text{formed}} = 18$ g/L, $P = 75$ g/L.

**(i) Substrate conversion:**

$$X = \frac{S_0 - S}{S_0} = \frac{200 - 20}{200} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90\ (90\ \%)$$

**(ii) Actual ethanol yield:**

$$Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}} = \frac{P}{S_0 - S} = \frac{75}{180} = 0.4167\ \text{g/g}$$

**(iii) Process selectivity:**

$$\text{Selectivity} = \frac{Y_{P/S}^{\text{actual}}}{Y_{P/S}^{\text{theo}}} = \frac{0.4167}{0.5111} = 0.815\ (81.5\ \%)$$

**(iv) Heat released:**

Per litre of broth, glucose consumed = $180$ g = $180/180 = 1$ mol.

$$Q = 1\ \text{mol/L} \times 227\ \text{kJ/mol} = 227\ \text{kJ per litre of broth}$$

For a 1000 L batch:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 227{,}000\ \text{kJ} = 227\ \text{MJ}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the molar-mass ratio:** Many students write $Y_{E/S}^{\text{theo}} = 92/92 = 1$ g/g, which is the *molar* ratio, not the *mass* ratio. Always divide by **180 g/mol** (glucose) — not 92.
> 2. **Mixing up SHF and SSF:** In SHF, the optimum temperatures of the two reactions are different (50 °C vs 30 °C). In SSF, you cannot run at 50 °C because yeast dies above ~38 °C. Always state the **compromise temperature** explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring hydration correction:** Cellulose monomer unit is $C_6H_{10}O_5$ (162 g/mol), not glucose (180 g/mol). When converting cellulose → glucose, multiply by 180/162. Many students forget this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the cooling duty:** Fermentation is exothermic. Any KTU answer about fermenter design that omits the cooling coil / jacket sizing will lose 1 mark for "incomplete energy analysis".
> 5. **Spelling yeast genus correctly:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* — capital S, italicised, no abbreviation to "Sacch." in formal answers.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stoichiometry:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2 C_2H_5OH + 2 CO_2$ — the heart of the process.
- **Theoretical yield:** **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (mass basis).
- **Practical yield:** **0.40 – 0.46 g/g** (80 – 90 % of theoretical).
- **Heat of reaction:** **−227 kJ/mol glucose** → continuous cooling required.
- **Optimal conditions:** T = **30 – 35 °C** (yeast), pH = **4.0 – 5.0**, anaerobic.
- **Process steps (memorise in order):** Size reduction → Pretreatment → Hydrolysis → Fermentation → Cell separation → Distillation → Dehydration.
- **Pretreatment methods:** dilute acid, steam explosion, alkaline, hydrothermal.
- **Hydrolysis:** enzymatic (cellulase) preferred over acid; 45 – 55 °C, 48 – 72 h.
- **Configurations:** SHF, SSF, SSCFE, CBP — SSF is the **process-intensification** answer.
- **Microorganisms:** *S. cerevisiae* (most common), *Zymomonas mobilis* (higher specific productivity), engineered *E. coli*.
- **Byproducts/inhibitors:** furfural, HMF, acetic acid, lignin derivatives.
- **CSTR mass balance identities:** $\mu = D = 1/\tau$, $X = Y_{X/S}(S_0 - S)$, $P = Y_{P/S}(S_0 - S)$.
- **Monod kinetics:** $\mu = \mu_{\max} S / (K_S + S)$.
- **Cellulose-to-glucose correction factor:** **180/162** (hydration).
- **Anhydrous ethanol spec:** > 99.5 % w/w; azeotrope at 95.6 % w/w broken by **3-Å molecular sieve**.
- **CO₂ footprint:** 1 mol CO₂ released per mol ethanol (biogenic).
- **Degree of reduction (γ):** glucose = 4, ethanol = 6, CO₂ = 0 — useful for carbon-balance check.
- **Feedstocks:** rice straw, bagasse, corn stover, wheat straw, MSW, food waste, switchgrass.
- **Sustainability angle:** 2G ethanol avoids food-vs-fuel conflict; biogenic CO₂ is carbon-neutral.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When a question asks "Explain fermentation in ethanol production from waste," always structure your answer in the following 4-block format: (1) **Reaction & Stoichiometry**, (2) **Microorganism & Conditions**, (3) **Process Flow (Block Diagram)**, (4) **Material/Energy Balance Numbers**. Examiners award marks in these four buckets, and missing any one will cost you at least 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composting and Other Biological Treatments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Composting and Other Biological Treatments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — COMPOSTING AND OTHER BIOLOGICAL TREATMENTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT867 / Module 3)**
> **Composting** is the controlled, aerobic, biological decomposition of organic solid waste into a stable, humus-like end product known as **compost**, facilitated by successive mesophilic and thermophilic microbial consortia (bacteria, actinomycetes, fungi). It is classified under **biological treatment** of municipal solid waste (MSW), agricultural residues, and biodegradable industrial effluents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is aligned with **CO-3**: *Design and analyze biological treatment systems for industrial and municipal solid/effluent streams using mass–energy balances and reaction kinetics.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Garden Bin"**
> Imagine a stack of vegetable peels, coffee grounds, and dry leaves left in an open garden bin. Within 2–3 days, the pile feels warm — that's **thermophilic bacteria** burning carbon for energy. After a few weeks, the bulk reduces by ~50% and turns into a dark, earthy, sweet-smelling material — that's **compost**, a natural fertilizer. **Composting engineering is simply scaling up, controlling, and accelerating this backyard process for thousands of tonnes of waste.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Optimum moisture content | **45–55 % (wet basis)** |
| Optimum C : N ratio | **25 : 1 to 30 : 1** |
| Optimum pH | **6.5 – 8.0** |
| Oxygen requirement | **> 5 % (by volume)** in pile |
| Free air space (FAS) | **> 30 %** |
| Particle size | **< 50 mm** (ideal 10–50 mm) |
| Bulk density | **350 – 550 kg/m³** |
| Heat generation rate | **~ 13,000 kJ/kg VS oxidized** |
| Temperature — mesophilic | **20 – 45 °C** |
| Temperature — thermophilic | **45 – 65 °C** |
| Pathogen kill temperature | **> 55 °C for 3 days** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Microbiology & Stages of Composting

Composting proceeds through **four overlapping stages**, each characterized by a dominant microbial community and a temperature window:

### Stage 1 — Mesophilic (Lag / Activation Phase)
- Duration: **1–3 days**
- Temperature: rises from ambient to **20 – 45 °C**
- Dominant microbes: mesophilic bacteria utilizing soluble, readily degradable sugars, starches and amino acids.
- Exothermic reactions: $C_6H_{10}O_5 + O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 5H_2O + \text{Heat}$

### Stage 2 — Thermophilic (Active Composting Phase)
- Duration: **1–3 weeks** (or longer in windrow systems)
- Temperature: **45 – 65 °C**, often reaching **70 – 80 °C** in poorly aerated piles.
- Dominant microbes: thermophilic bacteria, *Bacillus stearothermophilus*, *Thermus* spp., actinomycetes, thermophilic fungi.
- This stage **destroys pathogens, weed seeds, and phytotoxic compounds**.

### Stage 3 — Cooling (Second Mesophilic)
- Temperature falls back to **< 40 °C**.
- Mesophilic organisms recolonize; remaining cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin are degraded.
- Nitrate and humic acid precursors build up.

### Stage 4 — Curing / Maturation
- Duration: **several weeks to months**.
- Humification occurs, producing stable humic substances.
- Phytotoxicity drops, and the product becomes safe as a soil amendment.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"M = Make, T = Thermally, C = Cool, M = Mature"* → **MTCM** → Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Cooling → Maturation.

## 2.2 Process Parameters — The "McCOSH" Checklist

| Symbol | Parameter | Effect When Too Low | Effect When Too High |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|-----------------------|
| **C** | C : N ratio | Slow, N is lost as $NH_3$ | N deficiency, sluggish |
| **M** | Moisture | Slow microbial action | Anaerobic, foul odour |
| **O** | Oxygen | Anaerobic, $CH_4$, $H_2S$ | Excessive heat loss |
| **S** | Substrate (porosity) | Compaction, anaerobic | Poor contact, fast drying |
| **H** | Heat (temp) | Incomplete pathogen kill | Microbial inactivation |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these equations — they constitute **≥ 60 %** of numerical questions asked from this topic in KTU University Exams.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C/N_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{Q_1 \cdot (C/N)_1 + Q_2 \cdot (C/N)_2}{Q_1 + Q_2}$ | C/N blending rule for feed mix | — |
| 2 | $M_{\text{req}} = M_w \cdot \dfrac{MC_{\text{req}} - MC_{\text{act}}}{100 - MC_{\text{req}}}$ | Mass of water to add (dry basis) | kg |
| 3 | $M_{\text{add}} = M_{\text{dry bulk}} \cdot \dfrac{(MC_2 - MC_1)}{(100 - MC_2)}$ | Water addition for moisture correction | kg |
| 4 | $VS_{\text{red}} = VS_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-kt})$ | Volatile Solids reduction (first order) | kg |
| 5 | $O_2^{\text{req}} = 1.5 \cdot \left(\dfrac{VS_0}{1000}\right) \cdot (1 - e^{-kt})$ | Oxygen demand for VS destruction | kg $O_2$ |
| 6 | $\theta = T - T_{\text{amb}}$ | Self-heating potential | °C |
| 7 | $A_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{air}} \cdot v_{\text{sp}} \cdot 3600}$ | Required aeration duct cross-section | m² |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ evolved}}{\text{CO}_2 \text{ theoretical}}$ | Compost stability index | — |
| 9 | $T_{\text{ad}} = T_{\text{inf}} + \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise | K |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Conductive heat loss through pile wall | kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions used by KTU paper-setters:**
> - Oxygen supplied **per kg VS destroyed ≈ 1.0 – 1.5 kg $O_2$**.
> - Aeration requirement: **0.05 – 0.30 m³ air / min / m³ compost**.
> - C : N below **20 : 1** ⇒ ammonia volatilization; above **35 : 1** ⇒ N limitation.
> - Compost is **"stable"** when $O_2$ uptake < **1 mg $O_2$ / g VS·hr** (Dewar test).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Solid Waste Management (SWM)** — ISWA & CPCB estimate that **40–60 % of MSW** in Indian cities is biodegradable → ideal feedstock.
- **Renewable Energy** — coupled with **anaerobic digestion**, biogas of 50–70 % $CH_4$ is generated.
- **Circular Economy** — compost closes the nutrient loop (N, P, K) for agriculture, replacing chemical fertilizers.
- **GHG Mitigation** — composting of **1 tonne food waste** avoids ~0.7–1.0 tonne $CO_2$-eq of methane emissions vs. landfilling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Water Required for Moisture Correction

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite 14-mark question.** Given initial moisture content and target moisture content, calculate water to be added (or bulking agent moisture).

**Statement:** A feedstock of mass $M_w$ (wet basis) has moisture content $MC_1$ (%). It is to be raised to $MC_2$ (%). Determine the mass of water $W$ to be added.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $M_w$ = total wet mass (kg).  
Let $M_d$ = mass of dry solids (kg), assumed unchanged during moisture correction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of dry solids} \quad M_d &= M_w \cdot \dfrac{100 - MC_1}{100} \\
\text{New total wet mass} \quad M_w^{\text{new}} &= M_d + W = M_w + W \\
\text{But also:} \quad MC_2 &= \dfrac{M_o^{\text{new}}}{M_w^{\text{new}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M_o^{\text{new}} = M_o + W$ is the new moisture mass, and $M_o = M_w - M_d$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC_2 &= \dfrac{(M_w - M_d) + W}{M_w + W} \times 100 \\
\dfrac{MC_2}{100} &= \dfrac{M_w(1 - (100 - MC_1)/100) + W}{M_w + W} \\
\dfrac{MC_2}{100} &= \dfrac{M_w \cdot \dfrac{MC_1}{100} + W}{M_w + W}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-multiplying and rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC_2 \cdot (M_w + W) &= 100 \cdot \left(M_w \cdot \dfrac{MC_1}{100} + W\right) \\
MC_2 M_w + MC_2 W &= MC_1 M_w + 100 W \\
W(100 - MC_2) &= M_w (MC_1 - MC_2) \\
\boxed{W = M_w \cdot \dfrac{MC_1 - MC_2}{100 - MC_2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $MC_2 > MC_1$, $W$ is **positive** (water to be added). When $MC_2 < MC_1$, water must be removed (e.g., by adding dry bulking agent).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon : Nitrogen Blending

**Statement:** Two feedstocks A (kg) and B (kg) with C/N ratios $(C/N)_A$ and $(C/N)_B$ are blended. Derive the resulting C/N.

Carbon content of A: $C_A = A \cdot \dfrac{(C/N)_A}{1 + (C/N)_A}$ (per unit C+N)

Treating the linear additive model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= A \cdot (C/N)_A + B \cdot (C/N)_B \\
N_{\text{total}} &= A + B \\
\boxed{\left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{A \cdot (C/N)_A + B \cdot (C/N)_B}{A + B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oxygen Requirement for VS Reduction

The first-order kinetic model for volatile solids (VS) decomposition is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d(VS)}{dt} &= -k \cdot VS \\
VS(t) &= VS_0 \cdot e^{-kt} \\
VS_{\text{destroyed}} &= VS_0 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Empirically, the stoichiometric oxygen demand per kg VS destroyed ≈ **1.5 kg $O_2$** (for typical MSW with C₆H₁₀O₅ stoichiometry):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stoichiometric equation:} \quad & C_6H_{10}O_5 + 6\,O_2 \rightarrow 6\,CO_2 + 5\,H_2O \\
\text{MW ratio:} \quad & \dfrac{6 \times 32}{162} \approx 1.185
\end{aligned}
$$

For more complex substrates (proteins, fats) the empirical **1.5 kg $O_2$/kg VS** is adopted by KTU textbook convention.

$$
\boxed{O_2^{\text{req}} = 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{VS_0}{1000} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right) \quad [\text{kg}\,O_2]}
$$

If the time required to reach $X$ % VS reduction is asked, invert:

$$
\boxed{t = -\dfrac{1}{k} \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{X}{100}\right)}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14 Marker)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A municipal composting plant receives 10,000 kg/day of mixed organic waste with the following composition: $MC_1 = 35\%$, $VS_0 = 60\%$ of dry mass, $C/N = 38 : 1$. Target: $MC_2 = 55\%$, $C/N_{\text{target}} = 30 : 1$. Compute:
> (a) Mass of water to be added.
> (b) Mass of a nitrogen-rich co-feedstock (C/N = 8 : 1) required to achieve target C/N.

### Solution (a)

Using the moisture correction formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= M_w \cdot \dfrac{MC_1 - MC_2}{100 - MC_2} \\
&= 10{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{35 - 55}{100 - 55} \\
&= 10{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{-20}{45} \\
&= -4{,}444 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the result is **negative**, water must be **removed** — i.e., add **4,444 kg** of a dry bulking agent (e.g., dry leaves at ~10 % MC).

### Solution (b)

Let $B$ kg be the mass of nitrogen-rich co-feedstock to add.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_{\text{mix}} &= \dfrac{A \cdot (C/N)_A + B \cdot (C/N)_B}{A + B} = 30 \\
\dfrac{10{,}000 \times 38 + B \times 8}{10{,}000 + B} &= 30 \\
380{,}000 + 8B &= 300{,}000 + 30B \\
80{,}000 &= 22B \\
B &= 3{,}636 \;\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Add **~ 3,636 kg** of N-rich co-feedstock.

---

# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Composting Process Architecture

```mermaid
    A3 --> B2[In Vessel Tunnel Composting]
    A3 --> B3[In Vessel Rotary Drum Composting]
    A3 --> B4[Vermicomposting Beds]
    B1 --> C1[Mesophilic Stage 20 to 45 C]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Thermophilic Stage 45 to 65 C]
    C2 --> C3[Cooling Stage 40 C]

```
