# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define composting. List any four factors affecting the composting process.**

**Model Answer:**
Composting is the **controlled aerobic decomposition** of organic solid waste by microbial action into a humus-like, stable product rich in humic substances, suitable as a soil conditioner.

**Four factors:**
1. **Moisture content** (optimum 45–55 %).
2. **C : N ratio** (optimum 25–30 : 1).
3. **Aeration / Oxygen supply** (> 5 % by volume).
4. **pH** (6.5 – 8.0) and **Temperature** (45 – 65 °C in thermophilic stage).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award full **3 marks** only if all four factors carry correct numeric ranges.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between aerobic composting and anaerobic digestion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Aerobic Composting | Anaerobic Digestion |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Oxygen | Required | Not required |
| End products | Compost, $CO_2$, $H_2O$, heat | Biogas ($CH_4 + CO_2$), digestate |
| Operating temp | 45 – 65 °C | 35 – 55 °C |
| Reaction time | Weeks to months | 15 – 40 days |
| Energy output | Heat | High-grade biogas |
| Greenhouse gas | Mainly $CO_2$ | $CH_4$ (must be captured) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the microbiology of composting in detail, highlighting the role of bacteria, actinomycetes and fungi across the various temperature stages. Sketch a typical temperature–time profile.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composting facility processes 50 tonnes/day of food waste (dry mass = 18 t/d, moisture = 64 %, C/N = 18 : 1) blended with **dry leaves** (dry mass = 12 t/d, moisture = 8 %, C/N = 80 : 1). Calculate the blended C/N ratio and the additional water needed to bring the mixture to 55 % moisture.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Microbiology of Composting:**

1. **Mesophilic bacteria** (20–45 °C) initiate decomposition. Genera include *Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*, *Clostridium*. They consume soluble sugars and proteins, releasing $CO_2$ and ammonia, and producing heat that raises pile temperature.

2. **Thermophilic bacteria** (45–65 °C) take over as temperature rises. Key species: *Bacillus stearothermophilus*, *Thermus* spp. They degrade fats, oils, cellulose, hemicellulose and kill pathogens and weed seeds.

3. **Actinomycetes** appear during the cooling phase. They degrade tough materials like cellulose, lignin and chitin, producing the **earthy smell** (geosmin) of mature compost.

4. **Fungi** (mesophilic and thermophilic) including *Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*, *Trichoderma* dominate during curing. They complete lignin degradation and synthesize humic acids.

**Temperature–Time Profile:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ambient}} &= 25 \to 30 \;\text{°C (day 0)} \\
T_{\text{mesophilic}} &\to 45 \;\text{°C (day 1–3)} \\
T_{\text{thermophilic}} &\to 55 - 65 \;\text{°C (day 3–21)} \\
T_{\text{cooling}} &\to 40 \;\text{°C (day 21–35)} \\
T_{\text{maturation}} &\to 25 - 30 \;\text{°C (day 35 onwards)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects a hand-drawn sketch equivalent of the ASCII profile above. A clear four-stage curve with temperatures marked earns full marks.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1: Total masses**

Food waste: 50 t/d → Dry mass 18 t/d, Water 32 t/d.  
Leaves: dry 12 t, plus 8 % MC → wet mass = $12/0.92 = 13.04$ t, water = $13.04 - 12 = 1.04$ t.

**Step 2: Blended C/N**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{C}{N}\right)_{\text{mix}} &= \dfrac{50 \times 18 + 13.04 \times 80}{50 + 13.04} \\
&= \dfrac{900 + 1043.5}{63.04} \\
&= \dfrac{1943.5}{63.04} \\
&\approx 30.83 : 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **within the optimum 25–30 : 1** window.

**Step 3: Moisture correction to 55 %**

Total wet mass = $63.04$ t, total water = $32 + 1.04 = 33.04$ t.  
Current MC = $33.04 / 63.04 \times 100 = 52.42\%$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= M_w \cdot \dfrac{MC_1 - MC_2}{100 - MC_2} \\
&= 63.04 \cdot \dfrac{52.42 - 55}{100 - 55} \\
&= 63.04 \cdot \dfrac{-2.58}{45} \\
&= -3.61 \;\text{t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

Negative sign ⇒ must **add ~3.6 t/day** of **dry bulking agent** (or remove moisture by drying).

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the design and operational aspects of any **two** composting systems, comparing their merits and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the first-order kinetic expression for volatile solids reduction in composting, and determine the time required for 50 % VS destruction given $k = 0.08$ per day.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**System 1 — Windrow Composting**

- Waste is arranged in long trapezoidal piles (height 1.5–2.5 m, base 3–4 m, length as needed).
- Periodic turning (every 3–7 days) by front-end loader or specialized turner.
- Capital cost: **low**; suitable for large open plots.
- Turnings release $CO_2$, ammonia and heat — partial pathogen kill but **odor nuisance**.
- Land footprint: **high**; vulnerable to weather (heavy rain → anaerobic).
- Typical cycle: **8–12 weeks**.

**System 2 — In-Vessel (Tunnel / Drum) Composting**

- Closed reactor with controlled aeration, temperature and moisture.
- **Positive aeration** via blower → forced oxygen delivery.
- Footprint drastically reduced; **complete containment of odors** via biofilter.
- Cycle time: **2–4 weeks**.
- Capital cost: **high** (5–10× windrow); requires skilled operation.
- Better process control → consistent compost quality.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Aspect | Windrow | In-Vessel |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| Land area | High | Low |
| Odor control | Poor | Excellent |
| Pathogen kill | Moderate | High (controlled T) |
| Capital | Low | High |
| Skill level | Low | High |
| Throughput | Low–Medium | High |

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Derivation of first-order VS reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d(VS)}{dt} &= -k \cdot VS \\
\int_{VS_0}^{VS} \dfrac{d(VS)}{VS} &= -\int_0^t k\,dt \\
\ln(VS) - \ln(VS_0) &= -k\,t \\
VS(t) &= VS_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t} \\
VS_{\text{reduced}} &= VS_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k\,t})
\end{aligned}
$$

For **50 % VS reduction** ($VS(t) = 0.5 \cdot VS_0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5 \cdot VS_0 &= VS_0 \cdot e^{-k\,t_{50}} \\
\ln(0.5) &= -k\,t_{50} \\
t_{50} &= \dfrac{-\ln(0.5)}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.08} \\
\boxed{t_{50} \approx 8.66 \text{ days}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Do **not** confuse **C : N** (mass ratio) with **C + N** totals when blending — KTU paper-setters test this trap deliberately.
> 2. In moisture-correction numerics, students often write $W = M_w \cdot (MC_2 - MC_1) / 100$ — **wrong**, the correct denominator is $(100 - MC_2)$.
> 3. **Sign convention** is critical: negative $W$ ⇒ removal (add bulking agent), not adding water. Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the sign.
> 4. In kinetics, $k$ must always be expressed with **consistent units** (per day here). Mixing $k$ in $h^{-1}$ with time in days costs 2 marks.
> 5. Never use **landfill** and **bioreactor landfill** interchangeably in 14-mark theory answers.

---

## 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ Composting is **aerobic, exothermic**, biologically driven, and produces **compost + $CO_2$ + $H_2O$ + heat**.
- ✅ Four stages: **Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Cooling → Curing**.
- ✅ Optimum operating envelope: **MC 45–55 %, C/N 25–30 : 1, pH 6.5–8.0, $O_2$ > 5 %, T 45–65 °C**.
- ✅ Key derivations to memorize: **moisture correction**, **C/N blending**, **first-order VS kinetics**.
- ✅ Aerobic requirement: **~1.5 kg $O_2$ per kg VS destroyed**.
- ✅ Turned windrow → low cost, high footprint. In-vessel → high cost, controlled, low footprint.
- ✅ Anaerobic digestion yields **biogas (~60 % $CH_4$)** plus digestate that can be further composted.
- ✅ Vermicomposting operates at **20–30 °C**, uses *Eisenia foetida* earthworms, excellent for kitchen-waste scale.
- ✅ Biofiltration treats **volatile organics and odours** from composting off-gases using microbial biofilms.
- ✅ Pathogen kill requires **> 55 °C sustained for ≥ 3 days** during thermophilic stage (WHO/CPCB standard).
- ✅ Final compost is **"stable"** when $O_2$ uptake rate **< 1 mg $O_2$/g VS·h** (Dewar / SOUR test).
- ✅ Mature compost is characterized by **C/N of ~10–15 : 1**, dark brown colour, earthy smell, and absence of phytotoxicity.
- ✅ Coupling AD + composting gives the **maximum circular-economy value**: energy + soil amendment from the same waste.
- ✅ KTU viva favourites: *Why thermophilic stage is critical?*, *Why C/N > 35 : 1 slows composting?*, *Why anaerobic pockets form in wet piles?*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparative Analysis of Fermentation and Composting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Comparative Analysis of Fermentation and Composting # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fermentation — Formal Academic Definition **Fermentation** is an *anaerobic* (or partially anaerobic) biochemical process in which microorganisms such as bacteria, yeasts, or fungi enzymatically transform organic substrates—primarily carbohydrates—into alcohols, organic acids, gases (CO₂, H₂, CH₄), and/or other value-added products in the absence (or near-absence) of molecular oxygen. The process conserves energy through substrate-level phosphorylation and follows a structured microbial metabolism involving glycolysis, the TCA cycle (in facultative systems), and fermentation-specific pathways. The generalized stoichiometry for the most common forms is: $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobic}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 \quad \text{(Ethanol Fermentation)}$$ $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{anaerobic}} 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{CHOHCOOH} \quad \text{(Lactic Acid Fermentation)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Fermentation is a *controlled, product-oriented* bioprocess in which microbial metabolism is deliberately directed to maximize the yield of a specific biochemical product (ethanol, lactic acid, biogas, etc.) under tightly regulated conditions of pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen (< **0.1 mg/L**), and nutrient feed. ## 1.2 Composting — Formal Academic Definition **Composting** is an *aerobic* biochemical process in which heterotrophic microorganisms (bacteria, actinomycetes, fungi) decompose heterogeneous solid organic waste—such as agricultural residues, food waste, manure, and yard trimmings—under controlled conditions of moisture, aeration, and temperature, yielding stabilized humus-like material, CO₂, water, and heat. The overall stoichiometry can be approximated as: $$\text{Organic Matter} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NH}_3 + \text{Heat} + \text{Stable Humus}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Composting is a *waste-stabilization* process aimed at converting biodegradable solid waste into a hygienic, humus-rich soil conditioner. It is governed by the **C/N ratio (ideal: 25–35:1)**, **moisture content (50–60% wet basis)**, and **aeration (O₂ > 5–10% v/v in pore space)**. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies > [!NOTE] > **Analogy for Fermentation** 🥖🍷 > Think of fermentation as a *closed, sealed kitchen jar* where yeast turns grape juice into wine, or a fermenter tank where microbes "breathe" without fresh air and release alcohol and CO₂ as a metabolic compromise. The microbes are working to extract whatever little energy they can without oxygen, producing a specific end product we want. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy for Composting** 🍂🌱 > Composting is like *Nature's recycling bin in your backyard* — when you mix kitchen scraps with dry leaves, turn the pile to let air in, and keep it moist, the microbes "breathe" oxygen, devour the waste, heat up the pile, and slowly transform trash into rich, dark soil amendment (compost) that gardeners love. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Fermentation | Composting | |---|---|---| | **Dissolved O₂** | < **0.1 mg/L** (strict) | > **5–10% v/v** (pore space) | | **Operating pH** | 3.5 – 7.0 (process-dependent) | 5.5 – 8.5 (matures to ~7) | | **Temperature Range** | **30 °C** (mesophilic) to **55 °C** (thermophilic, special cases) | Ambient → **55–65 °C** (thermophilic) | | **Retention Time** | **24–96 h** (industrial) | **3–6 months** (windrow) / **2–4 weeks** (in-vessel) | | **Moisture** | Liquid broth (> **90%** water) | **50–60%** (wet basis) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Fermentation — Process Logic 1. **Substrate Preparation:** Carbon source (glucose, molasses, starch hydrolysate) is sterilized and fed to a closed bioreactor. 2. **Inoculation:** A pure or mixed culture (e.g., *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, *Lactobacillus*) is introduced under aseptic conditions. 3. **Anaerobic Maintenance:** The reactor is sparged with N₂/CO₂ instead of air; dissolved O₂ is maintained at near-zero ppm. 4. **Metabolism:** Microbes run the EMP pathway → pyruvate → ethanol/lactate (or other product), regenerating NAD⁺. 5. **Product Recovery:** Downstream separation via distillation, extraction, ion-exchange, or membrane separation. 6. **Process Control:** Real-time monitoring of pH, temperature, agitation, and substrate concentration via DCS/PLC. ### 2.1.2 Composting — Process Logic 1. **Feedstock Preparation:** Mixing of wet (nitrogen-rich) and dry (carbon-rich) materials to achieve C/N ≈ **30:1**. 2. **Pile/Windrow Formation:** Loose, porous pile constructed to enable passive or forced aeration. 3. **Mesophilic Phase (0–7 days):** Temperature rises from ambient to **40–45 °C**; bacteria dominate. 4. **Thermophilic Phase (7–30+ days):** Temperature peaks at **55–65 °C**; actinomycetes and thermophilic fungi degrade proteins, lipids, cellulose. 5. **Cooling & Curing Phase (1–3 months):** Temperature falls; mesophiles recolonize; humic acids polymerize; phytotoxins degrade. 6. **Maturation:** Stable, dark, earthy-smelling humus is screened and bagged for application. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the difference?** Fermentation is **product-driven** and operates on a *defined substrate* with pure culture; composting is **stabilization-driven** and operates on *heterogeneous solid waste* with a mixed consortium. This is the most testable conceptual point in KTU boards. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Units / Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | C/N Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{C}}{\text{N}} = 25 \text{ to } 35$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Moisture Content (wet basis) | $\%M = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$ | **50–60%** | | 3 | Moisture Content (dry basis) | $\%M_d = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} \times 100$ | 100–150% | | 4 | Free Air Space (FAS) | $\text{FAS} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{\text{bulk}}}{\rho_{\text{particle}}} - \%M \cdot \rho_{\text{bulk}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$ | > **30%** | | 5 | Pore Volume O₂ Concentration | $\%\text{O}_2^{\text{pore}} = 21 \cdot (1 - \text{CO}_2^{\text{pore}}/21)$ | > **10%** | | 6 | Monod Growth Kinetics (μ) | $\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | h⁻¹ | | 7 | Specific Substrate Uptake | $q_S = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}}$ | g/(g·h) | | 8 | Product Yield (Fermentation) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | g/g substrate | | 9 | Biogas Yield (Anaerobic Digestion) | $Y_b = V_{\text{CH}_4} / V_{\text{feed}}$ | m³/kg VS | | 10 | Degree of Compost Stability | $\text{DRI} = \dfrac{\text{C}_{\text{ha}}}{\text{C}_{\text{fa}}}$ | ratio (low = stable) | | 11 | Heat Generation Rate | $q_{th} = \Delta H \cdot k_d \cdot S$ | kJ/m³·h | | 12 | Respiration Rate (SOUR) | $\text{SOUR} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{O}_2}{\text{VS} \cdot t}$ | mg O₂/g VS·h | | 13 | Stover's Equation (biogas) | $C_nH_aO_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4$ | molar | | 14 | Henderson-Hasselbalch pH | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | — | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by waste-management engineers to size **in-vessel composters** (Formula 4), by bioprocess engineers to **design CSTR/PFR fermenters** (Formula 6–8), and by environmental engineers to **forecast biogas plant output** (Formula 13). Master these for any KTU numerical. ## 2.3 Microbial Communities | Process | Dominant Microbes | Physiology | |---|---|---| | Ethanol Fermentation | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* | Facultative anaerobe | | Lactic Acid Fermentation | *Lactobacillus* spp. | Facultative / obligate anaerobe | | Anaerobic Digestion (Biogas) | Methanogens (*Methanobacterium*) | Strict anaerobe | | Composting – Mesophilic | *Bacillus*, *Pseudomonas* | Aerobic mesophiles | | Composting – Thermophilic | *Thermus*, *Actinomyces*, thermophilic fungi | Aerobic thermophiles (50–70 °C) | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Methane Yield via Stover's Equation The empirical formula for organic waste in anaerobic digestion is generalized as $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b\text{N}_c\text{S}_d$. Ignoring N and S for the basic Buswell-Stover stoichiometry, the balanced reaction is: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \dfrac{a}{4} - \dfrac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \left(\dfrac{n}{2} - \dfrac{a}{8} + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\dfrac{n}{2} + \dfrac{a}{8} - \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4$$ **Example:** Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) has $n=6$, $a=12$, $b=6$. **Step 1 — Compute the water stoichiometric coefficient:** $$\left(6 - \dfrac{12}{4} - \dfrac{6}{2}\right) = (6 - 3 - 3) = 0$$ **Step 2 — Compute the CO₂ stoichiometric coefficient:** $$\left(\dfrac{6}{2} - \dfrac{12}{8} + \dfrac{6}{4}\right) = (3 - 1.5 + 1.5) = 3$$ **Step 3 — Compute the CH₄ stoichiometric coefficient:** $$\left(\dfrac{6}{2} + \dfrac{12}{8} - \dfrac{6}{4}\right) = (3 + 1.5 - 1.5) = 3$$ **Step 4 — Final Balanced Equation:** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{CH}_4$$ **Step 5 — Theoretical Methane Yield (per kg glucose, MW = 180 g/mol):** $$Y_{CH_4} = \dfrac{3 \times 22.4\,\text{L (STP)}}{180\,\text{g}} = 0.373\,\text{L CH}_4/\text{g glucose} = 373\,\text{L CH}_4/\text{kg glucose}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the *theoretical* yield. In practice, **60–80%** of this is achieved in real digesters due to microbial growth and energy losses. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moisture Content (Wet Basis) from Lab Data A compost sample has **wet mass** $M_w = 250\,\text{g}$ and **oven-dry mass** ($105\,°\text{C}$, 24 h) $M_d = 110\,\text{g}$. **Step 1 — Mass of water evaporated:** $$M_{H_2O} = M_w - M_d = 250 - 110 = 140\,\text{g}$$ **Step 2 — Moisture on wet basis:** $$\%M_{wb} = \dfrac{140}{250} \times 100 = 56\%$$ This falls within the optimum **50–60%** range. ✓ **Step 3 — Moisture on dry basis:** $$\%M_{db} = \dfrac{140}{110} \times 100 = 127.3\%$$ ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Exhaustive) | Dimension | Fermentation | Composting | KTU Examiner's Focus | |---|---|---|---| | Oxygen Requirement | Anaerobic / microaerobic | Strictly aerobic | ⭐⭐⭐ Very High | | Primary End Product | Defined chemical (ethanol, lactate) | Stabilized humus + CO₂ + H₂O | ⭐⭐ High | | State of Substrate | Liquid (broth) | Solid (porous bulk) | ⭐⭐ High | | Microbial Purity | Mono/defined culture | Mixed consortium | ⭐⭐ High | | Reactor Type | CSTR, PFR, Airlift | Windrow, In-vessel, Static pile | ⭐ Medium | | Sterility | Aseptic (mandatory) | Non-aseptic | ⭐⭐ High | | Temperature Control | Tight (± 0.5 °C) | Wide (ambient → 65 °C) | ⭐ Medium | | Product Recovery | Required (DSP) | Not required (direct use) | ⭐⭐ High | | Capital Cost | High (sterile SS) | Low (concrete pads) | ⭐ Medium | | Process Duration | Hours to days | Weeks to months | ⭐⭐ High | | C/N Ratio Control | By media design | By feedstock mixing | ⭐⭐ High | | Moisture Target | 90–95% (submerged) | 50–60% (porous wet) | ⭐⭐ High | | Heat Output | Limited (≤ 35 °C) | Significant (55–65 °C) | ⭐⭐ High | | Pathogen Kill | Sterile operation | Thermophilic phase kills | ⭐ Medium | | Scale-up Complexity | High (mass transfer, sterility) | Low (geometry, aeration) | ⭐⭐ High | | Net Energy | Net consumer (sterile utilities) | Net producer (self-heating) | ⭐ Medium | | Effluent Streams | Liquid effluent, spent cells | Leachate, odor gases | ⭐ Medium | | Typical Application | Pharma, food, beverage | Solid waste, agriculture | ⭐⭐ High | | Process Goal | Yield / Productivity | Stability / Sanitization | ⭐⭐ High | | Governing Equation | Monod + Product Kinetics | First-order decay + heat balance | ⭐⭐ High | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Process Block Flow — Fermentation ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Substrate
Molasses Glucose] --> B[Heat Sterilization
121 C 20 min] B --> C[Cooling 30 C] C --> D[Seed Bioreactor
Pure Culture] D --> E[Production Bioreactor
CSTR Anaerobic] E --> F[Solid Liquid Separation
Centrifuge] F --> G[Cell Disruption
Optional] G --> H[Purification
Distillation Extraction] H --> I[Polished Product
Ethanol Lactate] E --> J[Waste Broth
DSP Residue] style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style J fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Process Block Flow — Composting ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Green Waste
Nitrogen Wet] --> C[Feedstock Mixing
C N 30 1] A2[Brown Waste
Carbon Dry] --> C C --> D[Shredding and Size Reduction] D --> E[Pile Formation
Windrow In-vessel] E --> F[Mesophilic Phase
0 7 days 40 C] F --> G[Thermophilic Phase
7 30 days 55 65 C] G --> H[Cooling and Curing
30 90 days] H --> I[Maturation and Screening] I --> J[Finished Compost
Soil Amendment] H --> K[Leachate Collection] G --> L[Odor Control
Biofilter] style C fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333 style G fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style L fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Integrated Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FERMENTATION F1[Defined Substrate] --> F2[Sterile CSTR] F2 --> F3[Anaerobic Control] F3 --> F4[Defined Product Ethanol] end subgraph COMPOSTING C1[Mixed Solid Waste] --> C2[Windrow Reactor] C2 --> C3[Aerobic Aeration] C3 --> C4[Stable Humus] end F4 --> OUT[Process Goal Comparison
Yield vs Stability] C4 --> OUT style F3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style OUT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The **core contrast** captured in the diagrams: Fermentation is a *closed-sterile-product* architecture, while Composting is an *open-mixed-stabilization* architecture. This single sentence can earn full marks on a KTU Part A question. ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Decision Logic for Process Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Waste Type} -->|Liquid Soluble| Q2{Desired Output} Q1 -->|Solid Heterogeneous| P1[Choose Composting] Q2 -->|Defined Product| P2[Choose Fermentation] Q2 -->|Energy Recovery| P3[Choose Anaerobic Digestion] Q2 -->|Soil Amendment| P1 P1 --> R1[Low Cost Slow Solid] P2 --> R2[High Capital Fast Liquid] P3 --> R3[Moderate Cost Medium] style P1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style P2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style P3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between aerobic composting and anaerobic fermentation with respect to oxygen requirement, end products, and microbial purity. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criterion | Aerobic Composting | Anaerobic Fermentation | |---|---|---| | **Oxygen** | Strictly aerobic, O₂ > 5–10% v/v | Anaerobic, DO < 0.1 mg/L | | **End Products** | CO₂, H₂O, heat, stable humus | Ethanol / lactate / CH₄ + CO₂ | | **Microbial Purity** | Mixed heterogeneous consortium (bacteria, fungi, actinomycetes) | Defined/pure culture (e.g., *S. cerevisiae*, *Lactobacillus*) | | **Energy Release** | High (complete oxidation) | Low (partial oxidation) | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Why is the C/N ratio critical in composting but relatively unimportant in pure ethanol fermentation? **Model Answer (3 marks):** In **composting**, the C/N ratio governs microbial metabolism on *heterogeneous* substrates. An ideal C/N of **25–35:1** balances carbon (energy source) and nitrogen (protein synthesis). Too low → ammonia odor and N loss; too high → stalled decomposition. In **pure ethanol fermentation**, the substrate is a *defined* sugar medium (e.g., glucose or molasses) with engineered nitrogen content (e.g., yeast extract), so the C/N ratio is **pre-optimized by media design**, not by feedstock mixing. It is still important but controlled upstream in media preparation, not at feedstock stage. --- ## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *composting* with *anaerobic digestion* (which also yields CH₄). Composting is **always aerobic**; biogas/CH₄ is from **anaerobic digestion**. Mixing these will cost you 4–5 marks instantly. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, describe the various phases of aerobic composting. State the optimum ranges of temperature, moisture, C/N ratio, and pH for each phase. **(b) [7 Marks]** A municipal solid waste (MSW) sample of **500 kg** with the following characteristics is to be composted: Carbon = **48%** (dry), Nitrogen = **1.4%** (dry), Moisture = **62%** (wet basis). Determine: 1. The current C/N ratio. 2. The amount of **dry sawdust** (C = **52%**, N = **0.3%**, Moisture = **10%**) needed to bring the C/N ratio to **30:1** (assume dry-basis mixing; neglect sawdust moisture for simplicity). 3. The final moisture content of the mixed feedstock on a wet basis. --- ### **Solution to Question A:** **(a) [7 Marks] Phases of Aerobic Composting:** | Phase | Duration | Temperature | Microbes | Key Process | |---|---|---|---|---| | Mesophilic | 0–7 days | **30–45 °C** | Mesophilic bacteria | Hydrolysis of sugars, starches | | Thermophilic | 7–30 days | **45–65 °C** | Thermophilic bacteria, actinomycetes | Degradation of proteins, fats, cellulose; pathogen kill | | Cooling & Curing | 1–3 months | Cooling to ambient | Mesophiles recolonize | Humification, stabilization | **Optimum Parameters (universal):** - **Moisture:** 50–60% (wet basis) - **C/N ratio:** 25–35:1 - **pH:** 5.5–8.5 - **Aeration:** O₂ > 5–10% v/v in pore space *(Insert a flow chart similar to Section 4.2 — Mesophilic → Thermophilic → Curing → Maturation. Full marks for clear arrows and labelled temperature ranges.)* --- **(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution:** **Given Data:** - MSW mass: $M_{MSW} = 500\,\text{kg}$ (wet) - MSW moisture: $62\%$ wet basis - MSW carbon (dry): $C_{MSW} = 48\%$ - MSW nitrogen (dry): $N_{MSW} = 1.4\%$ - Sawdust carbon (dry): $C_{SD} = 52\%$ - Sawdust nitrogen (dry): $N_{SD} = 0.3\%$ - Target C/N = **30:1** (dry basis) **Step 1 — Dry mass of MSW:** $$M_{MSW,dry} = 500 \times (1 - 0.62) = 500 \times 0.38 = 190\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of Carbon and Nitrogen in MSW (dry basis):** $$C_{MSW} = 190 \times 0.48 = 91.2\,\text{kg C}$$ $$N_{MSW} = 190 \times 0.014 = 2.66\,\text{kg N}$$ **Step 3 — Let $x$ = dry mass of sawdust (kg).** Carbon added by sawdust: $0.52x$ Nitrogen added by sawdust: $0.003x$ **Step 4 — Set up the C/N balance equation (target = 30):** $$\dfrac{91.2 + 0.52x}{2.66 + 0.003x} = 30$$ **Step 5 — Cross-multiply:** $$91.2 + 0.52x = 30 \times (2.66 + 0.003x)$$ $$91.2 + 0.52x = 79.8 + 0.09x$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $x$:** $$0.52x - 0.09x = 79.8 - 91.2$$ $$0.43x = -11.4$$ $$x = -26.51\,\text{kg}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative Result Check:** The MSW alone has C/N = $91.2/2.66 = 34.3$, which is *already* within the optimum 25–35:1 range. Hence, **no sawdust addition is required** — the feedstock can be composted as-is. The negative value simply confirms the C/N is already acceptable. In KTU valuation, this critical interpretation earns full 7 marks; stating "negative mass" loses 3 marks. **Step 7 — Final moisture content of the (un-mixed) feedstock:** The MSW at 62% moisture is slightly above optimum (50–60%). Suggest **air-drying** or blending with dry leaves (without altering C/N significantly) to reduce moisture to ~58%. **Final Answer:** C/N = **34.3** (acceptable); no sawdust required. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of ethanol fermentation by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* with a balanced chemical equation and a process flow diagram. State the operating conditions used industrially. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the kinetics of ethanol fermentation (using Monod model) and composting (using first-order decay model). For a fermentation with $\mu_{max} = 0.45\,\text{h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.5\,\text{g/L}$, $S_0 = 30\,\text{g/L}$, $Y_{X/S} = 0.12$, compute the maximum specific growth rate and the steady-state substrate concentration in a chemostat with dilution rate $D = 0.25\,\text{h}^{-1}$. --- ### **Solution to Question B:** **(a) [7 Marks] Mechanism of Ethanol Fermentation:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* is a facultative anaerobe. Under anaerobic conditions, pyruvate from glycolysis is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde by **pyruvate decarboxylase**, then reduced to ethanol by **alcohol dehydrogenase**, regenerating NAD⁺. **Overall Stoichiometry (Gay-Lussac):** $$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase complex}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta G^\circ = -47\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Theoretical Yield:** 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose (= 51.1% w/w); practical yield ~ **90–95%** of theoretical. **Industrial Operating Conditions:** - Temperature: **30–35 °C** - pH: **4.0–5.0** - Dissolved O₂: < **0.1 mg/L** (anaerobic) - Sugar concentration: **100–200 g/L** (fed-batch preferred) - Agitation: low (100–200 rpm) **Process Flow:** Substrate prep → Sterilization → Cooling → Inoculation → Fermenter (CSTR/Fed-batch) → Centrifugation → Distillation → Dehydration → Anhydrous ethanol. --- **(b) [7 Marks] Kinetic Comparison & Numerical Solution:** **Kinetic Comparison:** | Aspect | Ethanol Fermentation (Monod) | Composting (First-Order Decay) | |---|---|---| | Equation | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | $\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -k \cdot S$ | | Order | Microbial growth, substrate saturation | First-order w.r.t. substrate | | Application | CSTR / Fed-batch / Chemostat design | Windrow / Pile kinetic modelling | | Best for | Liquid defined-substrate systems | Solid heterogeneous waste | **Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $\mu_{max} = 0.45\,\text{h}^{-1}$, $K_s = 0.5\,\text{g/L}$, $S_0 = 30\,\text{g/L}$, $Y_{X/S} = 0.12$, $D = 0.25\,\text{h}^{-1}$. **Step 1 — Monod growth rate at $D$ (chemostat at steady state, $\mu = D$):** $$D = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$$ $$0.25 = 0.45 \cdot \dfrac{S}{0.5 + S}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $S$:** $$\dfrac{0.25}{0.45} = \dfrac{S}{0.5 + S}$$ $$0.5556 = \dfrac{S}{0.5 + S}$$ $$0.5556 \times (0.5 + S) = S$$ $$0.2778 + 0.5556 S = S$$ $$0.2778 = S - 0.5556 S = 0.4444 S$$ $$S = \dfrac{0.2778}{0.4444} = 0.625\,\text{g/L}$$ **Step 3 — Cell (biomass) concentration at steady state:** $$X = Y_{X/S} \cdot (S_0 - S) = 0.12 \times (30 - 0.625)$$ $$X = 0.12 \times 29.375 = 3.525\,\text{g/L}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum specific growth rate:** $$\mu_{max} = 0.45\,\text{h}^{-1} \quad \text{(given, attained when } S \gg K_s\text{)}$$ **Final Answers:** - $\mu_{max} = 0.45\,\text{h}^{-1}$ - Steady-state $S = 0.625\,\text{g/L}$ - $X = 3.525\,\text{g/L}$ - Wash-out check: Since $D (0.25) < \mu_{max} (0.45)$, the chemostat is **stable** (no wash-out). ✓ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistakes:** > 1. Confusing dilution rate $D$ with $\mu_{max}$ — they are equal *only* at steady state. > 2. Forgetting to verify that $D < \mu_{max}$ to avoid wash-out. > 3. Using $S_0$ instead of $S$ in the Monod equation (which would give $\mu = 0.44$, not $0.25$). --- ## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Things to Remember (High-Yield KTU Bullets):** - ✅ **Fermentation is anaerobic** (< 0.1 mg/L DO); **Composting is aerobic** (> 5–10% O₂ in pores). - ✅ **Fermentation** = *product-oriented, aseptic, defined-substrate, fast (h–days)*. - ✅ **Composting** = *stabilization-oriented, non-aseptic, mixed-substrate, slow (weeks–months)*. - ✅ **C/N ratio:** 25–35:1 (composting); engineered in media (fermentation). - ✅ **Moisture:** 50–60% (composting); > 90% (fermentation broth). - ✅ **Three composting phases:** Mesophilic (30–45 °C) → Thermophilic (45–65 °C, pathogen kill) → Curing (humification). - ✅ **Optimum fermentation pH:** 3.5–6.0 (acidic); **composting pH:** 5.5–8.5 (neutral to slightly alkaline). - ✅ **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** → ethanol; **Lactobacillus** → lactic acid; **Methanogens** → CH₄ (anaerobic digestion, NOT composting). - ✅ **Stover's equation** for theoretical CH₄ yield; **Monod kinetics** for fermentation; **first-order decay** for composting. - ✅ **Negative sawdust mass** in numerical = C/N is *already optimal*; do not force-add. - ✅ **Wash-out** in chemostat when $D \geq \mu_{max}$; otherwise steady state is stable. - ✅ **Gay-Lussac equation:** 1 mol glucose → 2 mol ethanol + 2 mol CO₂. - ✅ **Theoretical ethanol yield:** 0.511 g/g glucose; practical = **90–95%** of theoretical. - ✅ **FAS (Free Air Space) > 30%** in compost pile for adequate aeration. - ✅ **SOUR (Specific Oxygen Uptake Rate)** is the key indicator of compost stability (< 1 mg O₂/g VS·h = stable).

topics.title: Transesterification: Fundamentals of Transesterification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transesterification: Fundamentals of Transesterification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transesterification** is a reversible, equilibrium-limited, liquid-phase organic reaction in which an **ester** reacts with an **alcohol** (in the presence of a catalyst) to yield a **new ester** and a **new alcohol**. The alkoxy group of the attacking alcohol displaces the alkoxy group of the original ester. In process systems engineering, this unit operation is a **heterogeneous or homogeneous catalyzed liquid-liquid reactor system** — most commonly applied to convert **triglycerides** (vegetable oils / animal fats) into **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME / biodiesel)** using **methanol** in the presence of an alkali, acid, or enzymatic catalyst.

The canonical generalized reaction is:

$$\text{R}^{1}\text{COOR}^{2} \; + \; \text{R}^{3}\text{OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{R}^{1}\text{COOR}^{3} \; + \; \text{R}^{2}\text{OH}$$

Where:
- **R¹COOR²** = parent ester (e.g., triglyceride)
- **R³OH** = attacking alcohol (e.g., methanol, ethanol)
- **R¹COOR³** = newly formed ester (e.g., FAME)
- **R²OH** = liberated alcohol (e.g., glycerol)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lock-and-Key Exchange" Model

Imagine a **molecular hand-shake** at a wedding reception. Think of the ester as a person (**R¹**) tightly holding hands with a partner (**R²**). A new, more attractive dance partner (**R³**) approaches and politely *cuts in* on the dance floor — releasing the old partner and pairing up with R¹. The old partner (now free) joins the crowd as a spectator.

In biodiesel context:
- The **triglyceride** is R¹ holding hands with glycerol (R²).
- **Methanol** (R³) cuts in.
- The new couple becomes **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)** — i.e., biodiesel.
- The displaced **glycerol** drifts off into the aqueous phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Process Systems Engineering:** Transesterification is the *heart* of any biodiesel plant. Modeling its kinetics, equilibrium, and reactor type (CSTR vs. PFR vs. batch) directly governs plant CAPEX, OPEX, and conversion efficiency.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Transesterification Variants (KTU Board Taxonomy)

| Variant | Catalyst System | Typical Application | Reaction Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkali-catalyzed** | NaOH, KOH, CH₃ONa | Refined vegetable oils (low FFA) | Mild (~60 °C, 1 atm) |
| **Acid-catalyzed** | H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄, HCl | High FFA feedstocks (waste cooking oil) | Severe (~110 °C) |
| **Enzymatic** | Lipases (Novozym 435) | Pharma-grade esters, green chemistry | Mild (~35–45 °C) |
| **Heterogeneous** | CaO, MgO, zeolites, ion-exchange resins | Continuous fixed-bed reactors | Moderate (60–200 °C) |
| **Supercritical** | *No catalyst* | High water/FFA tolerance | Very severe (>250 °C, >80 bar) |
| **In-situ (Reactive extraction)** | Same as alkali/acid | Direct from oilseeds | Mild–Moderate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **alkali-catalyzed methanolysis of triglycerides** as the primary case study, with kinetics, equilibrium, and reactor design as the core learning outcomes.

---

## 1.4 Standard Operating Metrics & Physical Constants

The following **bolded values** are universally cited in KTU textbooks, solved examples, and KTU University Examinations:

- **Standard molar ratio (methanol : oil) = 6 : 1** (stoichiometric = 3 : 1, but excess alcohol drives equilibrium).
- **Catalyst loading = 0.5 – 1.0 % wt/wt** of oil (alkali catalyst).
- **Reaction temperature = 60 °C** (just below the boiling point of methanol, **T_b = 64.7 °C**).
- **Reaction time = 30 – 90 minutes** for batch.
- **Optimum FFA content of feedstock < 2 %** (to prevent soap formation with alkali catalyst).
- **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**.
- **Density of methanol ρ_MeOH ≈ 792 kg/m³** at 25 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Consecutive-Parallel Mechanism

Transesterification of a triglyceride is **not a single-step** reaction. KTU examiners consistently test the student's understanding that it proceeds via **three sequential, reversible, second-order elementary reactions** — each liberating one mole of FAME.

### Step 1 — Conversion of Triglyceride → Diglyceride

$$\text{Triglyceride (TG)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1}\; \text{Diglyceride (DG)} + \text{FAME}$$

### Step 2 — Conversion of Diglyceride → Monoglyceride

$$\text{Diglyceride (DG)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{-2}]{k_2}\; \text{Monoglyceride (MG)} + \text{FAME}$$

### Step 3 — Conversion of Monoglyceride → Glycerol

$$\text{Monoglyceride (MG)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{-3}]{k_3}\; \text{Glycerol (GL)} + \text{FAME}$$

### Overall Net Reaction

$$\text{TG} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Each step produces **one mole of FAME**. The overall reaction releases **three moles of FAME** per mole of triglyceride — hence the stoichiometric **3 : 1** alcohol requirement.

---

## 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" — The Engineering Rationale

- **Why three steps?** Because glycerol (a triol) is *physically bonded* to three fatty acid chains via ester linkages. The catalyst attacks **one ester bond at a time**.
- **Why excess methanol (6:1)?** Because each step is **reversible**. By **Le Chatelier's Principle**, driving the system with **twice the stoichiometric alcohol** shifts equilibrium toward FAME.
- **Why mild temperature (60 °C)?** Higher T accelerates side reactions (saponification), and methanol begins to boil at **64.7 °C**, escaping the liquid phase.
- **Why catalyst choice matters:** Alkali catalysts are ~4000× faster than acid catalysts, but they **saponify** free fatty acids (FFAs), destroying yield. Acid catalysts tolerate FFA but require **higher T and longer time**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars in the following table are rendered using `\vert` (LaTeX) instead of the pipe character to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Expression | Definition / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle r_i = k_i \, C_A \, C_B$ | Second-order rate for step $i$ (alkali-catalyzed, both reactants in liquid phase) | mol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\displaystyle X = \frac{n_{\text{TG},0} - n_{\text{TG}}}{n_{\text{TG},0}}$ | Conversion of triglyceride | dimensionless (%) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle K_{eq} = \frac{[\text{FAME}]^{3}[\text{GL}]}{[\text{TG}][\text{MeOH}]^{3}}$ | Thermodynamic equilibrium constant of overall reaction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\displaystyle K_{eq,i} = \frac{k_i}{k_{-i}}$ | Step-wise equilibrium constant for step $i$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \ln\!\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{-E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Arrhenius temperature dependence (two-temperature method) | — |
| 6 | $\displaystyle k = A\, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius equation | s⁻¹ or L·mol⁻¹·s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \theta = \frac{n_{\text{MeOH},0}}{n_{\text{TG},0}}$ | Molar ratio (stoichiometric = 3, optimum = 6) | dimensionless |

| 12 | $\displaystyle \text{Conversion}_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ | First-order CSTR conversion (limiting case) | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

Transesterification is **the cornerstone unit operation** of the **global biodiesel industry** — a market valued at over **USD 50 billion** annually. Specific engineering applications:

1. **Biodiesel production** from soybean oil, palm oil, rapeseed oil, used cooking oil, and algal lipids.
2. **Pharmaceutical ester synthesis** (e.g., aspirin, methyl salicylate) where transesterification enables green, catalyst-recoverable routes.
3. **Polymer processing** — PET synthesis via transesterification between dimethyl terephthalate and ethylene glycol.
4. **Lubricant base-stock manufacture** — producing bio-lubricants with low ecotoxicity.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Process Systems Engineering, the transesterification reactor is treated as a **canonical case study** for **reactor selection (CSTR/PFR/batch), equilibrium shift analysis, and downstream separation design** (gravity settling, centrifugal separation, water washing, and vacuum drying of FAME).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Equilibrium Constant from Standard Gibbs Free Energy

**Starting thermodynamic identity** — at chemical equilibrium:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q = 0$$

**Step-by-step transformation:**

At equilibrium, the reaction quotient equals the equilibrium constant:

$$Q_{eq} = K_{eq}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K_{eq} = 0$$

Isolating $K_{eq}$:

$$RT \ln K_{eq} = -\Delta G^{\circ}$$

$$\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{RT}$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$\boxed{K_{eq} = e^{-\Delta G^{\circ} / RT}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** For typical alkali-catalyzed methanolysis, $\Delta G^{\circ} \approx -15$ to $-30$ kJ/mol at 333 K, yielding $K_{eq} \approx 250$ to $80{,}000$ — confirming that the reaction is **strongly product-favored** at mild temperatures.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Pseudo-First-Order Kinetics in Excess Methanol

When methanol is held in large molar excess ($\theta = 6$ or higher), its concentration is approximately **constant** during the reaction. The elementary rate law for Step 1 becomes:

$$r_1 = k_1\, [\text{TG}]\, [\text{MeOH}]$$

Since $[\text{MeOH}] \approx [\text{MeOH}]_0$ is effectively constant, we define a **pseudo-first-order rate constant**:

$$k_1^{\prime} = k_1 \, [\text{MeOH}]_0 \quad \left[\text{units: min}^{-1}\right]$$

The rate of triglyceride disappearance becomes:

$$-\frac{d[\text{TG}]}{dt} = k_1^{\prime}\, [\text{TG}]$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{d[\text{TG}]}{[\text{TG}]} = -k_1^{\prime}\, dt$$

Integrating from $t=0$ to $t$, with $[\text{TG}] = [\text{TG}]_0$ at $t=0$:

$$\int_{[\text{TG}]_0}^{[\text{TG}]} \frac{d[\text{TG}]}{[\text{TG}]} = -k_1^{\prime}\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{[\text{TG}]}{[\text{TG}]_0}\right) = -k_1^{\prime}\, t$$

Exponentiating:

$$[\text{TG}] = [\text{TG}]_0\, e^{-k_1^{\prime}\, t}$$

Conversion expression:

$$\boxed{X = 1 - e^{-k_1^{\prime}\, t}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Step-Wise Rate Equations (Full Mechanistic Model)

Defining dimensionless concentrations $C_i = [\text{species}_i] / [\text{TG}]_0$, the **forward rate of FAME appearance** in the mechanistic model is the sum of all three steps:

$$\frac{dC_{\text{FAME}}}{dt} = k_1\, C_{\text{TG}}\, C_M - k_{-1}\, C_{\text{DG}}\, C_{\text{FAME}} + k_2\, C_{\text{DG}}\, C_M - k_{-2}\, C_{\text{MG}}\, C_{\text{FAME}} + k_3\, C_{\text{MG}}\, C_M - k_{-3}\, C_{\text{GL}}\, C_{\text{FAME}}$$

where $C_M$ is the dimensionless methanol concentration.

The governing **mass balances** on a batch reactor basis:

$$\frac{dC_{\text{TG}}}{dt} = -k_1\, C_{\text{TG}}\, C_M + k_{-1}\, C_{\text{DG}}\, C_{\text{FAME}}$$

$$\frac{dC_{\text{DG}}}{dt} = k_1\, C_{\text{TG}}\, C_M - k_{-1}\, C_{\text{DG}}\, C_{\text{FAME}} - k_2\, C_{\text{DG}}\, C_M + k_{-2}\, C_{\text{MG}}\, C_{\text{FAME}}$$

$$\frac{dC_{\text{MG}}}{dt} = k_2\, C_{\text{DG}}\, C_M - k_{-2}\, C_{\text{MG}}\, C_{\text{FAME}} - k_3\, C_{\text{MG}}\, C_M + k_{-3}\, C_{\text{GL}}\, C_{\text{FAME}}$$

$$\frac{dC_{\text{GL}}}{dt} = k_3\, C_{\text{MG}}\, C_M - k_{-3}\, C_{\text{GL}}\, C_{\text{FAME}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **system of coupled ODEs** is the analytical workhorse for KTU numerical/analytical problems on transesterification kinetics. It is solved numerically (e.g., Runge–Kutta 4th order) in MATLAB/Python/SciPy when analytical solutions are not sought.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Equilibrium Conversion via Stoichiometric Table (Methanol-to-Oil Ratio)

**System:** Stoichiometric ratio $\theta = n_{M,0}/n_{TG,0}$; equilibrium extent $\varepsilon$.

**Initial moles:** $n_{TG,0} = 1$, $n_{M,0} = \theta$, $n_{\text{FAME},0} = 0$, $n_{\text{GL},0} = 0$.

**At equilibrium** (overall stoichiometry TG + 3 MeOH ⇌ 3 FAME + GL):

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---|---|---|---|
| TG | 1 | $-\varepsilon$ | $1-\varepsilon$ |
| MeOH | $\theta$ | $-3\varepsilon$ | $\theta-3\varepsilon$ |
| FAME | 0 | $+3\varepsilon$ | $3\varepsilon$ |
| GL | 0 | $+\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$ |

**Equilibrium constant in terms of extent:**

$$K_{eq} = \frac{(3\varepsilon)^{3}\,(\varepsilon)}{(1-\varepsilon)\,(\theta-3\varepsilon)^{3}}$$

For typical alkali-catalyzed systems at 333 K, $K_{eq} \gg 1$, so $\varepsilon \to 1$. The **practical constraint** becomes the **mass transfer limitation** between methanol (polar) and oil (non-polar), not equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Favorite:** Given $\theta = 6$ and $K_{eq} = 250$, solve the cubic in $\varepsilon$. Expect $\varepsilon \approx 0.97$ — i.e., 97 % conversion.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Arrhenius Parameters from Two-Temperature Kinetic Data

If a student is given $k_1$ at $T_1$ and $T_2$, the activation energy is obtained from the two-point Arrhenius form:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{k_1}{k_2}\right) = \frac{-E_a}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Rearranging for $E_a$:

$$\boxed{E_a = \frac{R\, \ln(k_1/k_2)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)}}$$

Typical literature values for NaOH-catalyzed transesterification: $E_a \approx 26$–$47$ kJ/mol.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A batch transesterification reactor is charged with **1000 mol** of soybean oil (assumed pure TG) and **6000 mol** of methanol with **1 % wt NaOH** catalyst at **333 K**. The pseudo-first-order rate constant is $k' = 0.08$ min⁻¹. Calculate the conversion after **(i)** 10 min and **(ii)** 60 min.

**Solution:**

**Part (i) — $t = 10$ min:**

$$X = 1 - e^{-0.08 \times 10} = 1 - e^{-0.8}$$

$$e^{-0.8} = 0.4493$$

$$X_1 = 1 - 0.4493 = 0.5507 \approx 55.07\%$$

**Part (ii) — $t = 60$ min:**

$$X = 1 - e^{-0.08 \times 60} = 1 - e^{-4.8}$$

$$e^{-4.8} = 0.00823$$

$$X_2 = 1 - 0.00823 = 0.9918 \approx 99.18\%$$

**Interpretation:** The reaction reaches **~99 % conversion in 1 hour** under pseudo-first-order conditions — which is why commercial batch reactors operate for 60–90 min.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Transesterification Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end process architecture** of an industrial biodiesel plant via alkali-catalyzed transesterification. It captures every unit operation from feedstock preparation to FAME polishing, including the recycle loop for unreacted methanol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feedstock Storage: Vegetable Oil or Used Cooking Oil] --> B[Pre-treatment: Degumming, Deacidification, Drying]
    B --> C[FFA Content Check: Acid Value less than 2 mg KOH per g]
    C --> D[Methanol Preparation Tank with NaOH or KOH Dissolution]
    D --> E[Reactor 1: CSTR or Batch at 60 deg C, 1 atm, 400 rpm]
    E --> F[Reactor 2: Second Stage for 99 percent Conversion]
    F --> G[Gravity Settler: Phase Separation of FAME and Glycerol]
    G --> H[Glycerol Phase: Neutralization, Purification, Sale]
    G --> I[FAME Phase: Methanol Recovery via Vacuum Distillation]
    I --> J[Water Wash Column: Removal of Residual Catalyst and Soap]
    J --> K[Vacuum Dryer: Moisture below 500 ppm]
    K --> L[Final Polish: Adsorption on Activated Carbon or Magnesol]
    L --> M[Biodiesel Storage Tank: ASTM D6751 or EN 14214 Compliant]
    I --> N[Methanol Recovery Column: Distillation at 64.7 deg C]
    N --> D
```

```mermaid
    M -->|Attack Step 2| DG
    M -->|Attack Step 3| MG
    F1[FAME 1] -->|Released| GL
    F2[FAME 2] -->|Released| GL
    F3[FAME 3] -->|Released| GL

    MG -->|Liberates| F3
```
