# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Define transesterification. Write the overall stoichiometric reaction for the production of biodiesel from triglycerides.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transesterification** is a reversible chemical reaction in which the alkoxy group of an ester is exchanged with the alkoxy group of an alcohol in the presence of a catalyst. In biodiesel production, triglycerides (the ester) react with a short-chain alcohol such as methanol or ethanol to produce fatty acid alkyl esters (biodiesel) and glycerol as a byproduct.

The overall stoichiometric reaction:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}}\; 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$

Key points to remember:
- Reaction is **reversible**, hence excess alcohol (typically 6:1 molar ratio) is used.
- The reaction is **three-step** (TG → DG → MG → GL).
- It is **equilibrium-limited** but **kinetically fast** with alkali catalysts.

**(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for catalyst/conditions)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**List any three factors that affect the yield of biodiesel in alkali-catalyzed transesterification and explain one in detail.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three primary factors:

1. **Methanol-to-oil molar ratio** — Excess methanol shifts equilibrium toward FAME. Optimum is 6:1.
2. **Reaction temperature** — Optimum is 60 °C; below this, kinetics are slow; above this, methanol vaporizes and saponification increases.
3. **Catalyst concentration** — Typical loading is 0.5–1 % wt NaOH/KOH. Too little gives low conversion; too much promotes soap formation.

**Detailed explanation of methanol-to-oil ratio:**

The stoichiometric requirement is 3 mol methanol per mol triglyceride. Because the reaction is reversible, an **excess of methanol (6:1)** is industrially used to push equilibrium toward FAME per Le Chatelier's principle. However, excessively high ratios (>9:1) complicate glycerol separation and inflate recovery costs. Therefore, **6:1** is the engineering optimum.

**(Total: 3 marks — 1 mark each for three factors + 1 bonus mark reserved for the detailed explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the rate expression for an alkali-catalyzed transesterification under pseudo-first-order conditions in a batch reactor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A batch reactor is charged with oil and methanol at a 1:6 molar ratio with 1 % NaOH catalyst at 60 °C. The pseudo-first-order rate constant is **k′ = 0.075 min⁻¹**. Calculate **(i)** the time required for **95 % conversion**, and **(ii)** the reactor volume if **1000 kg of oil** (MW ≈ 885 g/mol) is processed per batch with a **liquid volumetric loading of 850 L per 1000 kg oil**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Pseudo-First-Order Rate Expression

**Step 1: Write the elementary rate law** for TG + MeOH → DG + FAME:

$$r = k_1\, [\text{TG}]\, [\text{MeOH}]$$

**Step 2: Apply the excess-methanol assumption.** With $\theta \geq 6$, methanol concentration remains approximately constant:

$$[\text{MeOH}] \approx [\text{MeOH}]_0 = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3: Define pseudo-first-order constant:**

$$k_1' = k_1\, [\text{MeOH}]_0$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the rate law:**

$$-\frac{d[\text{TG}]}{dt} = k_1'\, [\text{TG}]$$

**Step 5: Integrate** with $[\text{TG}] = [\text{TG}]_0$ at $t = 0$:

$$\int_{[\text{TG}]_0}^{[\text{TG}]} \frac{d[\text{TG}]}{[\text{TG}]} = -k_1'\int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{[\text{TG}]}{[\text{TG}]_0}\right) = -k_1' t$$

**Step 6: Convert to conversion** $X = 1 - \frac{[\text{TG}]}{[\text{TG}]_0}$:

$$\boxed{X = 1 - e^{-k_1' t}}$$

**(Full 7 marks: 1 mark each for steps 1–6 + 1 mark for final boxed equation)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculations

**Part (i) — Time for 95 % conversion:**

Rearranging $X = 1 - e^{-k_1' t}$:

$$e^{-k_1' t} = 1 - X$$

$$-k_1' t = \ln(1 - X)$$

$$t = -\frac{\ln(1 - X)}{k_1'}$$

Substituting $X = 0.95$ and $k_1' = 0.075$ min⁻¹:

$$t = -\frac{\ln(0.05)}{0.075} = -\frac{-2.9957}{0.075}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 39.94 \text{ min} \approx 40 \text{ min}}$$

**Part (ii) — Reactor volume:**

Given:
- $m_{\text{oil}} = 1000$ kg
- $\text{MW}_{\text{oil}} = 885$ g/mol = 0.885 kg/mol
- $n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{1000}{0.885} = 1129.94$ mol
- Volumetric loading = 850 L per 1000 kg oil
- Total batch liquid volume (oil + methanol at 6:1):

Methanol mass:
$$n_{\text{MeOH}} = 6 \times 1129.94 = 6779.66 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{\text{MeOH}} = 6779.66 \times 0.03204 \text{ kg/mol} = 217.22 \text{ kg}$$

Total liquid volume (oil + methanol), assuming additive volumes (typical approximation):

$$V_{\text{oil}} = \frac{1000}{920 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 1.087 \text{ m}^3 = 1087 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{\text{MeOH}} = \frac{217.22}{792 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.2743 \text{ m}^3 = 274.3 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{\text{total}} = 1087 + 274.3 \approx 1361 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{reactor}} \approx 1.36 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (with 20 \% headspace} \Rightarrow 1.63 \text{ m}^3)}$$

**(Full 7 marks: 3 marks for Part (i) + 4 marks for Part (ii))**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **three-step consecutive mechanism** of alkali-catalyzed transesterification with the help of reaction equations. Why is each step reversible, and how does the choice of catalyst affect the rate of each step? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **acid-catalyzed** vs **alkali-catalyzed** transesterification on the basis of (i) reaction rate, (ii) FFA tolerance, (iii) temperature, (iv) catalyst loading, (v) reactor material, and (vi) industrial preference. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Three-Step Consecutive Mechanism

**Step 1 — Conversion of triglyceride to diglyceride:**

$$\text{TG} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1}\; \text{DG} + \text{FAME}_1$$

**Step 2 — Conversion of diglyceride to monoglyceride:**

$$\text{DG} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{-2}]{k_2}\; \text{MG} + \text{FAME}_2$$

**Step 3 — Conversion of monoglyceride to glycerol:**

$$\text{MG} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[k_{-3}]{k_3}\; \text{GL} + \text{FAME}_3$$

**Why each step is reversible:** All three steps are **elementary nucleophilic acyl substitution reactions**. The catalyst lowers the activation energy for both forward and reverse directions equally. Since no step produces a thermodynamically stable, irreversible product (e.g., gas evolution, phase separation), all steps remain equilibrium-limited.

**Effect of catalyst on each step:**

- **Alkali catalyst (NaOH, KOH):** Generates the **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)** in situ — a strong nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the ester rapidly. Speeds up all three steps roughly equally by ~4000× compared to the uncatalyzed reaction.
- **Acid catalyst (H₂SO₄):** Protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbonyl carbon more electrophilic. Slower but tolerates FFAs.
- **Enzymatic (lipase):** Highly selective for the outer positions (sn-1, sn-3) of the glycerol backbone; step 3 is rate-limiting.

**(7 marks distributed as: 3 marks for equations, 2 marks for reversibility rationale, 2 marks for catalyst effect)**

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table

| Parameter | Acid-Catalyzed | Alkali-Catalyzed |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Reaction rate | Slow ($k_1$ ~ 4000× slower) | Very fast |
| (ii) FFA tolerance | High — converts FFAs to FAME | Low — FFAs form soap |
| (iii) Temperature | 100–120 °C | 50–65 °C |
| (iv) Catalyst loading | 3–5 % wt H₂SO₄ | 0.5–1 % wt NaOH |
| (v) Reactor material | Corrosion-resistant (SS316, glass-lined) | Mild steel acceptable |
| (vi) Industrial preference | Used for low-grade feedstocks | Preferred for refined oils |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake:**
> Students frequently write *"acid catalyst is preferred because it is faster"* — this is **FALSE**. Alkali catalysts are preferred for refined oils due to higher rate and lower temperature, but acid catalysts are **mandatory for high-FFA feedstocks** (e.g., waste cooking oil with FFA > 2 %). A **two-stage acid–alkali hybrid** process is the industrial standard for low-grade feeds.
> **Marks lost if student conflates rate with industrial preference.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSING PITFALLS IN TRANSESTERIFICATION QUESTIONS:**
> 1. **Forgetting reversibility** — writing a single arrow instead of $\rightleftharpoons$. Loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing FFA with WFA** — FFA = Free Fatty Acid, NOT "Waste Fatty Acid". Saponification side reaction depends on FFA content, not on total acidity.
> 3. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio** — Stoichiometric is 3:1, NOT 6:1. The 6:1 ratio is an engineering choice for equilibrium shift.
> 4. **Ignoring catalyst selection logic** — Always check FFA content FIRST before choosing NaOH vs. H₂SO₄.
> 5. **Units mismatch in kinetics** — If $k$ is in L·mol⁻¹·min⁻¹, concentration must be in mol/L, not kg/m³. Convert carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — TRANSESTERIFICATION FUNDAMENTALS**

- ✅ **Definition:** Ester + Alcohol ⇌ New Ester + New Alcohol (catalyzed).
- ✅ **Industrial significance:** Biodiesel (FAME) from triglycerides + methanol.
- ✅ **Overall stoichiometry:** TG + 3 MeOH ⇌ 3 FAME + Glycerol.
- ✅ **Mechanism:** Three consecutive, reversible, second-order elementary steps.
- ✅ **Optimum operating point:** 6:1 molar ratio, 60 °C, 1 % wt NaOH/KOH, 60 min.
- ✅ **Catalyst hierarchy:** Alkali (fast, sensitive) > Acid (slow, robust) > Enzyme (mild, expensive) > Heterogeneous (continuous, complex).
- ✅ **Pseudo-first-order assumption:** Valid when $[\text{MeOH}]_0 \gg [\text{TG}]_0$.
- ✅ **Conversion formula (pseudo-first-order):** $X = 1 - e^{-k' t}$.
- ✅ **Equilibrium constant:** $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[\text{FAME}]^{3}[\text{GL}]}{[\text{TG}][\text{MeOH}]^{3}}$.
- ✅ **Le Chatelier lever:** Excess methanol or removal of glycerol shifts equilibrium forward.
- ✅ **FFA rule:** FFA < 2 % → alkali; FFA > 2 % → acid pre-esterification then alkali.
- ✅ **Side reactions:** Saponification (alkali + FFA → soap), hydrolysis (water + TG → DAG + FFA).
- ✅ **Reactor types:** Batch (small scale), CSTR cascade (medium), PFR with heterogeneous catalyst (large).
- ✅ **Downstream:** Glycerol settles by gravity (density 1.26 g/cm³); FAME is washed, dried, polished.
- ✅ **Activation energy:** $E_a \approx 26$–$47$ kJ/mol for alkali systems.
- ✅ **Standard reference temperature:** 333 K (60 °C).
- ✅ **Biodiesel standard:** ASTM D6751 (USA) or EN 14214 (Europe).
- ✅ **Catalyst recovery:** Acid neutralized with NaOH → Na₂SO₄; alkali removed by water washing.
- ✅ **Mass transfer issue:** Methanol-oil two-phase system requires intense mixing (400–600 rpm) or co-solvent (e.g., THF, n-hexane).

---

**End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 4, Transesterification Fundamentals.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Challenges of Transesterification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Advantages and Challenges of Transesterification ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Transesterification** (also called **alcoholysis**) is a reversible chemical reaction in which the alkyl group of an ester is exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol in the presence of an acid, base, or enzymatic catalyst. In the context of **Process Systems Engineering** and **biofuel manufacturing**, it most commonly refers to the conversion of triglycerides (vegetable oils / animal fats) into **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME — biodiesel)** and a valuable co-product, **glycerol**, by reacting the triglyceride with a short-chain alcohol (typically **methanol**) in the presence of a homogeneous alkaline catalyst (e.g., NaOH or KOH). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT867 – Module 4):** > The unit-level learning outcome demands that the student must be able to *evaluate the advantages (renewability, lower emissions, biodegradability, cetane improvement) and engineering challenges (kinetic limitations, equilibrium constraints, mass-transfer resistance, catalyst recovery, feedstock variability, and downstream separation economics) of large-scale transesterification processes.* ### 1.2 The Overall Stoichiometry (Single-Stage Representation) For the base-catalyzed methanolysis of a generic triglyceride (TG): $$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{catalyst}]{\Delta}\; 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} $$ The reaction proceeds through three sequential, reversible steps: **Triglyceride → Diglyceride → Monoglyceride → Glycerol**, releasing one mole of FAME at each step. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lego Block Exchange" Imagine a **triglyceride molecule as a 3-pronged Lego "trident"** with a glycerol "base" holding three long hydrocarbon "tail-bricks" (fatty acid chains). Now introduce a swarm of small **methanol "scissors-handles"** that, in the presence of a **catalyst "butler"**, repeatedly cut one tail-brick at a time off the trident. After three cuts: - The three tail-bricks (now called **FAME / biodiesel**) are released. - The trident's base remains as **glycerol** (a sweet, viscous by-product used in pharma and cosmetics). - The methanol "scissors" come out unchanged — they are *not consumed*. > [!NOTE] > **Key takeaway:** Transesterification is essentially a **molecular rebuilding exercise** — the carbon skeleton of the glycerol remains intact; only the fatty-acid "passengers" are swapped for a methyl group. This simple swap changes a viscous, high-viscosity oil into a low-viscosity, engine-friendly fuel. ### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics to Remember | Metric | Typical Value (Biodiesel Context) | |---|---| | Molar ratio (methanol : oil) | 6 : 1 (stoichiometric) → 9–12 : 1 (industrial practice) | | Reaction temperature | 323 K – 338 K (50 °C – 65 °C) | | Catalyst loading (NaOH) | 0.5 – 1.5 % wt. of oil | | Reaction time | 30 min – 2 h (batch) | | Conversion target | > 96 % (ASTM D6751) | | **Glycerol purity (by-product)** | **≥ 80 % (crude)** → 99.7 % (refined pharma grade) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Sequential Reaction Steps Because each step of the transesterification is reversible, the overall process is equilibrium-limited. The stepwise mechanism is: $$ \text{Triglyceride (TG)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Diglyceride (DG)} + \text{FAME}_1 $$ $$ \text{Diglyceride (DG)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Monoglyceride (MG)} + \text{FAME}_2 $$ $$ \text{Monoglyceride (MG)} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Glycerol (GL)} + \text{FAME}_3 $$ > [!TIP] > **Why three steps and not one?** The triglyceride has three ester linkages, and the catalyst (e.g., methoxide ion, CH₃O⁻) attacks them **one at a time**. The intermediate DG and MG are always present in measurable quantities and must be tracked for accurate kinetic modelling. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Engineering Choices - **Why excess methanol (6–12 : 1 instead of 3 : 1)?** Le Chatelier's principle: a reversible reaction is pushed forward by increasing reactant concentration. Stoichiometric methanol gives only ~70 % conversion; industrial excess pushes it past 96 %. - **Why 60 °C and not 100 °C?** Methanol boils at 64.7 °C. Going higher risks **vapor locking**, pressure-build-up in batch reactors, and energy penalties for condensation. The optimum sits just below the boiling point to maximize kinetic rate without phase change. - **Why an alkaline catalyst?** Base catalysis is **~4000× faster** than acid catalysis at 60 °C and uses milder equipment (carbon-steel reactors instead of exotic alloys). However, the feedstock must be dry and have a low free fatty acid (FFA) content (< 2 %). - **Why vigorous mixing?** Transesterification is a **liquid–liquid two-phase system** (polar methanol / non-polar oil). Mass transfer between the phases is the **rate-limiting step** before the kinetic regime is reached. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | Overall: $TG + 3\,ROH \rightleftharpoons 3\,FAME + GL$ | Mass-balance baseline | | 2 | Equilibrium constant: $K_{eq} = \dfrac{[FAME]^{3}[GL]}{[TG][ROH]^{3}}$ | Reactor design and conversion prediction | | 3 | Conversion: $X = \dfrac{n_{TG,0}-n_{TG}}{n_{TG,0}}$ | Quantifying reaction progress | | 4 | Yield: $Y = \dfrac{m_{FAME,actual}}{m_{FAME,theoretical}}$ | Process economics | | 5 | Selectivity: $S = \dfrac{n_{FAME}}{n_{FAME}+n_{DG}+n_{MG}}$ | Quality control | | 6 | Excess alcohol ratio: $R_{excess} = \dfrac{n_{ROH,actual}}{n_{ROH,stoich}}$ | Optimizing recycle streams | | 7 | Pseudo-first-order rate (alkaline): $-r_{TG} = k\,C_{TG}\,C_{ROH}$ | Kinetic modelling | | 8 | Mass balance on FAME: $F_{FAME,out} = F_{FAME,in} + 3\,F_{TG,conv}$ | Steady-state design | | 9 | Activation energy (alkaline): $E_a \approx 26$–$47\;\text{kJ/mol}$ | Temperature sensitivity | | 10 | Purity of crude glycerol: $\%\;GL = \dfrac{m_{GL,crude}}{m_{GL,crude}+m_{impurities}}\times 100$ | By-product economics | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Examiner Pattern:** A 14-mark Part-B question on this module **almost always** combines a stoichiometric / conversion calculation with a 3–4 mark discussion of *either* advantages *or* challenges. Memorize Equations 3, 4, and 6 — they are the most frequently tested. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Transesterification underpins the **global biodiesel industry** (> 45 billion litres/year capacity as of 2024), which is a cornerstone of process systems engineering case studies. Process engineers use this reaction to: - Design **continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR)** and **plug-flow reactors (PFR)** cascades. - Specify **decanter / centrifuge** separation trains for glycerol removal. - Perform **life-cycle assessment (LCA)** comparing biodiesel to petroleum diesel. - Develop **process flow diagrams (PFDs)** and **P&IDs** that integrate catalyst recovery, methanol recovery (distillation), and glycerol purification (ion-exchange + vacuum distillation). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Yield of FAME from a Triglyceride **Problem Statement:** A feed of **1000 kg** of soybean oil (assumed pure triolein, MW = 885.4 g/mol) reacts completely with methanol in a 6 : 1 molar ratio. Calculate the theoretical mass of FAME (methyl oleate, MW = 296.5 g/mol) and glycerol (MW = 92.1 g/mol) produced. **Step 1 — Moles of triglyceride charged:** $$ n_{TG} = \dfrac{m_{TG}}{MW_{TG}} = \dfrac{1\,000\,000\;\text{g}}{885.4\;\text{g/mol}} = 1129.4\;\text{mol} $$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometric methanol required (3 mol per mole TG):** $$ n_{ROH,\,stoich} = 3 \times 1129.4 = 3388.2\;\text{mol} $$ **Step 3 — Actual methanol charged (6 : 1 ratio):** $$ n_{ROH,\,actual} = 6 \times 1129.4 = 6776.4\;\text{mol} $$ **Step 4 — FAME produced (3 mol per mole TG):** $$ n_{FAME} = 3 \times 1129.4 = 3388.2\;\text{mol} $$ **Step 5 — Mass of FAME:** $$ m_{FAME} = n_{FAME} \times MW_{FAME} = 3388.2 \times 296.5 = 1\,004\,601\;\text{g} \approx 1004.6\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 6 — Mass of glycerol (1 mol per mole TG):** $$ m_{GL} = 1129.4 \times 92.1 = 104\,017\;\text{g} \approx 104.0\;\text{kg} $$ **Step 7 — Mass-balance check (input = output, ignoring unreacted methanol):** $$ \sum m_{in} = 1000 + (6776.4 \times 32.04)/1000 = 1000 + 217.1 = 1217.1\;\text{kg} $$ $$ \sum m_{out} = 1004.6 + 104.0 = 1108.6\;\text{kg} $$ The difference of **108.5 kg** is the **unreacted excess methanol** that must be recovered by distillation (mass-balance closure achieved). > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Notice that **1000 kg of oil → 1004.6 kg of FAME**, a mass *gain* because three methyl groups replace the glycerol backbone's hydrogen atoms. This is a classic KTU trick question — students often wrongly assume mass is conserved on the ester side. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion vs. Equilibrium Relationship For the pseudo-first-order approximation at constant methanol activity, the integrated rate law gives the **conversion of TG as a function of time**: $$ X_{TG}(t) = 1 - \exp(-k_{\text{obs}}\,t) $$ where $k_{\text{obs}} = k\,C_{ROH,\,excess}$ is the **observed pseudo-first-order rate constant** (s⁻¹). At 60 °C with 1 % NaOH and 6 : 1 methanol ratio, $k_{\text{obs}} \approx 0.6$–$1.2\;\text{min}^{-1}$. | Time (min) | $X_{TG}$ (calc.) | Industrial status | |---|---|---| | 5 | 0.95 | Quasi-equilibrium reached | | 15 | 1.00 | 99 % completion | | 30 | 1.00 | Plateau | | 60 | 1.00 | No further progress (residual MG/DG) | **Mathematical justification** — combining the rate law with the stoichiometric relation between TG and FAME (3 mol FAME per mol TG): $$ X_{FAME}(t) = \dfrac{n_{FAME}(t)}{3\,n_{TG,0}} = 1 - \exp(-k_{\text{obs}}\,t) $$ This proves that **bulk FAME production tracks TG conversion linearly** — a key identity for sizing downstream settlers. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Requirement for Heating the Reactor **Given:** $V_{reactor} = 5\;\text{m}^3$, feed enters at 298 K, target 333 K, oil density $\rho = 880\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $C_{p,\,oil} = 1.9\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $C_{p,\,methanol} = 2.5\;\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. $$ m_{oil} = 5 \times 880 = 4400\;\text{kg}, \quad m_{methanol} = 0.20 \times 4400 = 880\;\text{kg} $$ $$ Q = m_{oil}\,C_{p,\,oil}\,\Delta T + m_{methanol}\,C_{p,\,methanol}\,\Delta T $$ $$ Q = (4400 \times 1.9 \times 35) + (880 \times 2.5 \times 35) = 292\,600 + 77\,000 = 369\,600\;\text{kJ} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Insight:** This 369.6 MJ of heat input represents about **4–5 %** of the **calorific value** of the FAME produced. It is the single largest *energy penalty* in the transesterification process, making **waste-heat recovery from glycerol purification** a critical engineering decision. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Challenges (Quantitative Lens) | Dimension | Advantage | Quantitative Benefit | Challenge | Quantitative Penalty | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Renewability** | Biomass-based feedstock | $CO_2$ closed-loop; 50–80 % GHG reduction vs. diesel | Feedstock seasonal variation | ±15 % capacity utilization | | **Fuel properties** | Higher cetane (~50–60) | Better ignition quality | Cold-flow issues (cloud point 0–15 °C) | Limited to tropical/temperate blends | | **Emissions** | Lower SOx, particulate, CO | 90 % SOx, 47 % PM reduction | Slightly higher NOx (10 %) | After-treatment needed | | **Biodegradability** | Non-toxic, rapidly degraded | 28 days vs. petroleum's years | Water contamination risk | Microbial growth in storage | | **Reaction kinetics** | Fast under base catalysis | 30–60 min batch time | Two-phase mass-transfer limited | First 5 min: 70 % of total time | | **Catalyst cost** | NaOH is cheap (USD 0.3/kg) | Catalyst cost < 1 % of product | Catalyst loss to glycerol phase | 5–8 % catalyst drag | | **Glycerol co-product** | 1 kg glycerol per 10 kg FAME | Pharma-grade revenue (USD 0.6/kg) | Impure crude (80 %); purification capex | 20 % of plant capital cost | | **Process scale-up** | Continuous reactors available | 60–250 ML/year per plant | CSTR-PFR cascade design complex | Capital intensity: USD 0.5–1.0 per litre annual capacity | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Block Flow Diagram of Industrial Biodiesel Plant via Transesterification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feedstock Storage
Vegetable Oil or Animal Fat] --> B[Pre-treatment
FFA Removal Degumming Drying] B --> C[Reactor 1 CSTR
Alkaline Transesterification 333 K] C --> D[Reactor 2 PFR
Polishing Stage] D --> E[Gravity Decanter
Glycerol Phase Separation] E --> F[Centrifuge Wash
Water Washing of FAME] F --> G[Methanol Recovery
Distillation Column] G --> H[Finished FAME
ASTM D6751 Quality] E --> I[Crude Glycerol 80 percent] I --> J[Glycerol Refining
Vacuum Distillation Ion Exchange] J --> K[Pharma Grade Glycerol 99.7 percent] L[Catalyst Makeup NaOH Methoxide] --> C M[Process Steam 4 bar] --> C M --> G N[Waste Water Treatment] --> F ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = process streams; the dashed inputs (catalyst, steam) are utility streams. The reactor cascade (CSTR → PFR) is the industry standard for pushing conversion beyond 99 % while keeping residence time short. ### 4.2 Reaction Mechanism Flow — Sequential Transesterification ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Triglyceride TG] -- Step 1 with methanol --> A2[Diglyceride DG + FAME 1] A2 -- Step 2 with methanol --> A3[Monoglyceride MG + FAME 2] A3 -- Step 3 with methanol --> A4[Glycerol GL + FAME 3] A4 --> A5[Total 3 FAME per TG molecule] P1[Catalyst CH3O minus] -.-> A1 P1 -.-> A2 P1 -.-> A3 P1 -.-> A4 ``` ### 4.3 Advantages-vs-Challenges Decision Matrix (Mermaid Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ADV[Advantages of Transesterification] A1[Renewable Feedstock] A2[Lower GHG Emissions] A3[High Cetane Number] A4[Biodegradable Fuel] A5[Valuable Glycerol By product] A6[Compatible with Diesel Engines] end subgraph CHA[Engineering Challenges] C1[Feedstock Variability FFA Water] C2[Equilibrium Limited Reversible] C3[Mass Transfer Resistance Two Phase] C4[Catalyst Recovery and Disposal] C5[Excess Methanol Recovery] C6[Glycerol Purification Capex] end ADV --- CHA A1 --- C1 A2 --- C1 A5 --- C6 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define transesterification. Write the overall stoichiometric equation for the production of biodiesel from triglycerides using methanol.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Transesterification is the chemical reaction in which the alkyl group of an ester is exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol in the presence of a catalyst. For biodiesel production, triglycerides react with methanol to produce fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) and glycerol: $$ \text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH / KOH}]{333\,\text{K}}\; 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol} $$ It is a reversible, three-step reaction proceeding through diglyceride and monoglyceride intermediates. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *List any three advantages and three challenges of the transesterification process at industrial scale.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages (1½ marks):** 1. Renewable, biomass-derived feedstock. 2. Higher cetane number (50–60) and better combustion quality. 3. Produces valuable glycerol as co-product. **Challenges (1½ marks):** 1. Reversible, equilibrium-limited reaction — needs excess methanol. 2. Two-phase (oil-methanol) system causes mass-transfer resistance. 3. Crude glycerol contains catalyst, water, and methanol — needs costly purification. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the *stoichiometric* methanol:oil ratio (3:1) instead of the *industrial* ratio (6–12:1). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for not justifying the excess. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) with FAMEs (plural) or with ethanol-based FAEE. Use **FAME** consistently. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Listing advantages without a corresponding *engineering rationale* (e.g., "high cetane" alone is worth 1 mark; "high cetane → better cold-start → lower emissions" earns 2). > - **Common Mistake 4:** In mass-balance problems, ignoring the **mass gain** in FAME due to methyl-group addition. Always re-derive the mass balance, do not assume conservation on the ester side. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Advantages-Focused Module Question** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Part B, Module 4, Set A]** **(a)** Explain the stepwise mechanism of base-catalyzed transesterification of triglycerides. Why is an **excess of methanol (6:1 to 12:1 molar ratio)** used industrially, although the stoichiometric requirement is only 3:1? Discuss the role of the **alkaline catalyst** and the **typical operating conditions**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A continuous biodiesel plant processes **5000 kg/day** of refined soybean oil (MW = 885.4) with **methanol at 9:1 molar ratio** and **1 % wt NaOH catalyst** at 333 K. The reactor achieves **96 % conversion** of triglyceride. Calculate: (i) Daily mass of FAME produced. (ii) Daily mass of crude glycerol (assume stoichiometric from the reacted TG). (iii) Mass of excess methanol to be recovered. (iv) Comment on whether a **second reactor** (PFR polishing stage) would be economically justified. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Mechanism (4 marks) — The three steps:** $$ TG + ROH \rightleftharpoons DG + RCOOCH_3 $$ $$ DG + ROH \rightleftharpoons MG + RCOOCH_3 $$ $$ MG + ROH \rightleftharpoons GL + RCOOCH_3 $$ Each step is reversible; methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻) attacks the carbonyl carbon, forming a tetrahedral intermediate that collapses to release the FAME and a smaller glyceride. **Why excess methanol (1½ marks):** Le Chatelier's principle pushes the equilibrium toward the product side. At stoichiometric 3:1, conversion stalls at ~70 %. At 6:1 → ~90 %; at 9:1 → ~96 %; beyond 12:1, the marginal conversion gain does not justify the additional methanol-recovery cost. **Catalyst and conditions (1½ marks):** NaOH/KOH (or the in-situ-generated CH₃ONa) gives **~4000× faster** kinetics than acid catalysis. Operating at 333 K (just below methanol's 64.7 °C boiling point) maximizes rate without vapor-locking. Catalyst loading 0.5–1.5 % wt. is the optimum: less gives slow kinetics; more promotes **saponification** (soap formation, an undesired side reaction that consumes FAME and reduces yield). **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Moles of TG per day:** $$ n_{TG,\,feed} = \dfrac{5\,000\,000}{885.4} = 5647.4\;\text{mol/day} $$ **Step 2 — Moles of TG reacted (96 % conversion):** $$ n_{TG,\,reacted} = 0.96 \times 5647.4 = 5421.5\;\text{mol/day} $$ **(i) Mass of FAME (3 mol FAME per mol TG reacted, MW = 296.5):** $$ n_{FAME} = 3 \times 5421.5 = 16\,264.5\;\text{mol} $$ $$ m_{FAME} = 16\,264.5 \times 296.5 = 4\,822\,424\;\text{g} \approx 4822.4\;\text{kg/day} $$ **(ii) Mass of glycerol (1 mol per mol TG reacted, MW = 92.1):** $$ m_{GL} = 5421.5 \times 92.1 = 499\,320\;\text{g} \approx 499.3\;\text{kg/day} $$ **(iii) Mass of excess methanol to recover:** Total methanol charged = $9 \times 5647.4 = 50\,826.6$ mol = $50\,826.6 \times 32.04 / 1000 = 1628.5$ kg Methanol consumed (stoichiometric) = $3 \times 5421.5 \times 32.04 / 1000 = 521.1$ kg Unreacted methanol = $1628.5 - 521.1 = 1107.4$ kg/day → must be recovered by distillation. **(iv) Second reactor justification (2 marks):** The remaining 4 % unreacted TG corresponds to ~226 mol/day ≈ 200 kg of oil-in-product. A polishing PFR with another 30 min residence and a methanol top-up (to maintain 6:1 in the residue) would push conversion to ~99 %, recovering an additional 120 kg FAME/day. The payback period on the additional capex is **< 1 year** for a 5000 kg/day plant — **economically justified**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Challenges-Focused Module Question** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Part B, Module 4, Set B]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **major engineering challenges** of large-scale transesterification: (i) Reversibility and equilibrium limitation. (ii) Two-phase mass-transfer resistance. (iii) Feedstock variability (FFA and water content). (iv) Catalyst recovery and soap-formation (saponification). Suggest **two engineering strategies** to overcome each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same 5000 kg/day soybean oil plant, suppose the **crude glycerol** is 80 % pure and contains 10 % water, 5 % catalyst residue, 3 % unreacted methanol, and 2 % residual FAME. The glycerol selling price is **USD 0.6/kg for crude** and **USD 1.2/kg for pharma-grade (99.7 %)**. The purification train costs **USD 0.25/kg crude glycerol** to operate. (i) Calculate the **daily crude glycerol mass** (using your result from Q-A). (ii) Compute the **revenue from crude sales** vs. **revenue from refined sales** (assume 95 % recovery in refining). (iii) Recommend whether to refine on-site or sell as crude. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Engineering Challenges (7 marks):** **(i) Reversibility (1¾ marks):** - **Challenge:** $\Delta G^{\circ} \approx 0$ to slightly positive; reaction is genuinely equilibrium-limited. - **Strategy 1:** Use 6–12:1 methanol excess. - **Strategy 2:** Continuous removal of glycerol (decantation between reactor stages) to shift equilibrium. **(ii) Mass-transfer resistance (1¾ marks):** - **Challenge:** Oil and methanol are immiscible; reaction rate is initially controlled by interfacial area, not kinetics. - **Strategy 1:** High-shear mixing (Reynolds > 10⁴) and use of **co-solvents** (e.g., tetrahydrofuran) to form a single phase. - **Strategy 2:** Use of **ultrasonic / static-mixer reactors** that create fine emulsions and intensify mass transfer. **(iii) Feedstock variability (1¾ marks):** - **Challenge:** FFA > 2 % → soap formation; water > 0.1 % → catalyst deactivation. - **Strategy 1:** Pre-treat feedstock with **acid esterification** (H₂SO₄ + methanol) to convert FFA to FAME first. - **Strategy 2:** Implement **feedstock-blending strategy** and online NIR sensors to monitor FFA & moisture. **(iv) Catalyst recovery / saponification (1¾ marks):** - **Challenge:** Homogeneous NaOH ends up in glycerol phase, hard to recover; forms soap with FFA, losing FAME. - **Strategy 1:** Switch to **heterogeneous solid catalysts** (CaO, MgO, hydrotalcites) for easy filtration. - **Strategy 2:** Use **membrane separation** or **ion-exchange resins** to recover catalyst from glycerol. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Crude glycerol mass (2 marks):** Total crude glycerol stream = 499.3 kg (pure GL) / 0.80 = **624.1 kg/day**. **(ii) Revenue comparison (3 marks):** *Option A — Sell as crude:* $$ \text{Revenue}_A = 624.1 \times 0.6 = \$374.5/\text{day} $$ *Option B — Refine to pharma grade:* - Glycerol recovered in refining = $0.95 \times 499.3 = 474.3$ kg/day of pharma-grade. - Refining cost = $624.1 \times 0.25 = \$156.0/\text{day}$. - Revenue from pharma-grade = $474.3 \times 1.2 = \$569.2/\text{day}$. - Net revenue B = $569.2 - 156.0 = \$413.2/\text{day}$. **(iii) Recommendation (2 marks):** Net gain from refining = $413.2 - 374.5 = \$38.7/\text{day}$ (~$14,000/year for 5000 kg/day plant). This marginal gain must be weighed against **capital cost of the refining train (ion-exchange + vacuum distillation, ~USD 1–2 million)** and **payback of ~100+ years** without subsidies. **Recommendation: sell as crude** unless the plant is co-located with a pharmaceutical glycerol consumer or benefits from a government value-addition incentive. **Conclusion:** The decision is **not justified** on standalone economics — refining is viable only at >50,000 kg/day scale. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Definition:** Transesterification = alkyl exchange between ester + alcohol → new ester + new alcohol. - ✅ **Overall reaction:** $TG + 3\,ROH \rightleftharpoons 3\,FAME + GL$. - ✅ **Industrial methanol ratio:** 6:1 to 12:1 (NOT 3:1 stoichiometric). - ✅ **Catalyst:** NaOH / KOH (alkaline), 0.5–1.5 % wt., at **333 K**. - ✅ **Three sequential steps:** TG → DG → MG → GL (each releases 1 FAME). - ✅ **Mass gain in FAME:** 1000 kg oil → ~1004.6 kg FAME (methyl groups added). - ✅ **Glycerol yield:** ~10 % of oil mass; crude purity 80 %, pharma 99.7 %. - ✅ **Advantages:** renewable, low emissions, high cetane, biodegradable, valuable co-product. - ✅ **Challenges:** reversible equilibrium, two-phase mass transfer, FFA/water sensitivity, catalyst loss, methanol recovery cost, glycerol purification capex. - ✅ **Reactor configuration:** CSTR (bulk conversion) → PFR (polishing to >99 %). - ✅ **Separation train:** Decanter (gravity) → centrifuge wash → methanol distillation → glycerol refining. - ✅ **Activation energy (alkaline):** ~26–47 kJ/mol. - ✅ **Examiner traps:** stoichiometric vs. industrial ratio; mass gain in FAME; saponification side reaction; forget to include methanol recovery in mass balance. - ✅ **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 m³ oil ≈ 880 kg; 1 kmol methanol = 32.04 kg; 1 kmol glycerol = 92.1 kg. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always frame your answers in three parts — **(1) Chemistry (mechanism + stoichiometry), (2) Engineering (reactor + separation), (3) Economics (cost + by-product revenue).** This 3-pillar structure is what differentiates an 8-mark answer from a 14-mark answer in the KTU valuation key.

topics.title: Chemical Reactions Involved
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chemical Reactions Involved

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Process Systems Engineering (PSE)**, the term *Chemical Reactions Involved* refers to the systematic identification, representation, and mathematical modeling of the chemical transformations occurring within a process. These reactions are characterized by their **stoichiometry** (the quantitative relationship between reactants and products), **kinetics** (the rate at which reactants are converted to products), **thermodynamics** (the feasibility and equilibrium position of the reaction), and **mechanism** (the elementary steps through which the overall transformation occurs).

For KTU 2024 Scheme (Outcome-Based Education), the reactions are studied not in isolation but as an integrated part of the **process flow sheet**, where they influence mass balances, energy balances, equipment sizing, and overall process economics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Chemical reactions form the *core* of any chemical process. Without understanding the reactions involved, one cannot design reactors, optimize yields, or perform meaningful dynamic simulation. The reactions provide the link between the *raw material streams* entering a process and the *product streams* leaving it.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Stoichiometry** | The mole-balanced relationship describing the proportion in which reactants combine to form products. |
| **Reaction Rate ($-r_A$)** | The rate at which a species *A* is consumed per unit volume (for homogeneous) or per unit mass of catalyst (for heterogeneous). Units: $\text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$. |
| **Conversion ($X_A$)** | The fraction of reactant *A* that has been consumed in the reactor. |
| **Selectivity ($S$)** | The ratio of the desired product formed to the total reactant consumed. |
| **Yield ($Y$)** | The fraction of the limiting reactant that has been converted into the desired product. |
| **Extent of Reaction ($\xi$)** | A single scalar quantity that measures the progress of a reaction; it changes by the same amount for all species when scaled by their stoichiometric coefficients. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — "The Kitchen Cooking Model"**
> Imagine a restaurant kitchen. The *chef* (the reactor) takes raw ingredients (reactants) and transforms them into dishes (products). The *recipe* is the stoichiometry (it tells you how much flour, sugar, and eggs you need to bake a cake). The *speed of cooking* is the kinetics (how fast the cake bakes at 180 °C vs. 100 °C). The *maximum taste quality* is the thermodynamic equilibrium (the cake can't get "tastier" beyond a certain point). The *cooking equipment* is the reactor type (frying pan = batch, conveyor oven = continuous). Understanding the recipe, the cooking rate, and the equipment allows the restaurant owner to predict how many cakes can be made per hour and at what cost — exactly the same way a process engineer predicts the output of a chemical plant.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$ (= $0.08314 \ \text{bar}\cdot\text{L}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$)
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_{\text{ref}} = 298.15 \ \text{K}$ (**25 °C**)
- **Standard Reference Pressure:** $P_{\text{ref}} = 1 \ \text{atm}$ (= **101.325 kPa**)
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Chemical Reactions

Chemical reactions encountered in process systems are classified along multiple dimensions. Each classification dictates the type of mathematical model used.

#### 2.1.1 By Number of Phases Involved

- **Homogeneous Reactions:** All reactants and products exist in a single phase (typically gas or liquid). Example: The aqueous-phase neutralization $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \to \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$.
- **Heterogeneous Reactions:** Reaction occurs at the interface of two or more phases. Example: Catalytic cracking of hydrocarbons on a solid zeolite catalyst.

#### 2.1.2 By Molecularity and Order

- **Elementary Reaction:** Occurs in a single step; molecularity equals order. Example: $2\text{NO}_2 \to 2\text{NO} + \text{O}_2$ is a second-order elementary reaction.
- **Non-Elementary Reaction:** Proceeds via a multi-step mechanism; the observed rate law does not follow the stoichiometry. Example: The overall hydrogenation of ethylene appears second order but follows the Hougen-Watson mechanism on a metal surface.

#### 2.1.3 By Thermal Behavior

- **Exothermic Reactions:** Release heat ($\Delta H < 0$). Example: Combustion of methane.
- **Endothermic Reactions:** Absorb heat ($\Delta H > 0$). Example: Steam reforming of methane.

#### 2.1.4 By Reversibility

- **Reversible Reactions:** Proceed in both forward and reverse directions, reaching dynamic equilibrium. Example: $\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NH}_3$.
- **Irreversible Reactions:** Proceed essentially to completion in the forward direction. Example: Precipitation of $\text{AgCl}$ from aqueous solution.

### 2.2 Reaction Stoichiometry — The Algebraic Foundation

For a general single reaction:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \nu_i A_i = 0
$$

where $A_i$ denotes the chemical species and $\nu_i$ is the **stoichiometric coefficient** (negative for reactants, positive for products). The relationship between the changes in moles of each species is:

$$
\frac{n_{i} - n_{i0}}{\nu_i} = \frac{n_{j} - n_{j0}}{\nu_j} = \xi \quad \text{(extent of reaction)}
$$

For multiple reactions $j = 1, 2, \ldots, M$ with species $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N_s$, the generalized relation becomes:

$$
n_i = n_{i0} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} \nu_{ij}\,\xi_j
$$

This formulation is the **cornerstone of process simulation** — it links the reaction progress to the species mole balance.

### 2.3 Reaction Rate Laws

The rate of a reaction depends on the state of the system. The most general form is:

$$
-r_A = f(C_A, C_B, \ldots, T, P, \text{catalyst}, \text{etc.})
$$

For a power-law (or **law of mass action**) rate expression, an irreversible reaction $\text{A} + \text{B} \to \text{Products}$ follows:

$$
-r_A = k \, C_A^{\alpha} \, C_B^{\beta}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the **orders** with respect to species *A* and *B* respectively, and $k$ is the **rate constant**.

The **Arrhenius Equation** describes the temperature dependence of $k$:

$$
k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

where:
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = activation energy ($\text{J/mol}$)
- $R$ = universal gas constant
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### 2.4 Reversible Reaction Rate — The Net Rate Concept

For the reversible reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$, the **net rate** equals the forward rate minus the reverse rate:

$$
-r_A = k_f C_A - k_r C_B
$$

At equilibrium, $-r_A = 0$, giving the **equilibrium constant** in terms of rate constants:

$$
K_C = \frac{k_f}{k_r} = \frac{C_{B,\text{eq}}}{C_{A,\text{eq}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link to Thermodynamics:** The thermodynamic equilibrium constant $K$ is related to the standard Gibbs free energy change: $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$. This is a constant KTU board favorite — it connects the kinetic description with the thermodynamic feasibility.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extent of Reaction | $\xi = \dfrac{n_i - n_{i0}}{\nu_i}$ | mol (or kmol) |
| 2 | Conversion | $X_A = \dfrac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le X_A \le 1$ |
| 3 | Selectivity | $S_{P/U} = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired } P \text{ formed}}{\text{moles of undesired byproduct}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Yield | $Y_P = S_P \cdot X_A$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Power Law Rate | $-r_A = k\,C_A^{\alpha}\,C_B^{\beta}$ | $\text{kmol}\cdot\text{m}^{-3}\cdot\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Arrhenius | $k = A\,e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $k$ in consistent units |
| 7 | Net Rate (Reversible) | $-r_A = k_f C_A - k_r C_B$ | At eq.: $k_f/k_r = K_C$ |
| 8 | Van't Hoff (K vs T) | $\dfrac{d \ln K}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R T^2}$ | True only if $\Delta H^\circ \approx$ const. |
| 9 | Thermodynamic Constraint | $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ | $K$ dimensionless; $\Delta G^\circ$ in J/mol |
| 10 | Molar Flow Relation | $F_i = F_{i0} + \displaystyle\sum_j \nu_{ij}\,\xi_j$ | Continuous reactors, mol/s |
| 11 | Space Velocity (heterogeneous) | $SV = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}}{V_{\text{reactor}}}$ | $s^{-1}$ or $h^{-1}$ |
| 12 | Space Time | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{\text{reactor}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{feed}}} = \dfrac{1}{SV}$ | $s$ or $h$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the chemical reactions involved is the **gateway** to:

- **Reactor Design:** CSTR, PFR, batch, semi-batch, packed-bed, fluidized-bed — each selected based on reaction kinetics and phase behavior.
- **Process Optimization:** Maximizing yield/selectivity reduces raw material cost, a dominant factor in chemical plant economics.
- **Safety Analysis:** Exothermic reactions may lead to *thermal runaway*; knowing the heat of reaction and activation energy is essential for emergency relief design (DIERS methodology).
- **Dynamic Simulation & Control:** The reaction sub-model sits inside the dynamic mass/energy balance for transient studies.
- **Catalyst Selection:** Heterogeneous reactions require kinetic models (e.g., Langmuir-Hinshelwood, Eley-Rideal) that include adsorption equilibria.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linking Mole Changes to Conversion

**Problem Setup:** For a single reaction $aA + bB \to cC + dD$, derive the relation between the change in moles of *B* and the change in moles of *A* using the *extent of reaction* approach.

**Step 1 — Write the general stoichiometric form:**

$$
\nu_A A + \nu_B B + \nu_C C + \nu_D D = 0
$$

By IUPAC convention, $\nu_A = -a$, $\nu_B = -b$, $\nu_C = c$, $\nu_D = d$.

**Step 2 — Express the moles of each species at extent $\xi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_A &= n_{A0} + \nu_A \, \xi \\
n_B &= n_{B0} + \nu_B \, \xi \\
n_C &= n_{C0} + \nu_C \, \xi \\
n_D &= n_{D0} + \nu_D \, \xi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $\xi$ by equating the expressions for *A* and *B*:**

$$
\frac{n_A - n_{A0}}{\nu_A} = \frac{n_B - n_{B0}}{\nu_B} = \xi
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the change in moles of B in terms of change in moles of A:**

$$
n_B - n_{B0} = \frac{\nu_B}{\nu_A}\,(n_A - n_{A0}) = \frac{b}{a}\,(n_A - n_{A0})
$$

This gives the **stoichiometric ratio** between any two species — a direct, fast, and reliable KTU board calculation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Concentration as a Function of Conversion (Constant Density / Ideal Gas Batch)

**Problem Setup:** For a single reaction consuming species *A* in a batch reactor with constant total volume, derive the relation $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and then extend to the case of a gas-phase reaction at constant $T$ and $P$.

#### Case 1: Constant-Density Liquid-Phase Batch

The conversion is defined by:

$$
X_A = \frac{N_{A0} - N_A}{N_{A0}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
N_A = N_{A0}(1 - X_A)
$$

If the volume $V$ is constant, divide both sides by $V$:

$$
C_A = \frac{N_A}{V} = \frac{N_{A0}}{V}(1 - X_A) = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)
$$

This is the **standard textbook relation** for liquid-phase reactors.

#### Case 2: Gas-Phase Reaction at Constant $T$ and $P$ (Variable Volume)

For a gas-phase system, the volume changes as the reaction proceeds because the total number of moles changes. We define the **fractional change in volume** as:

$$
\varepsilon_A = \frac{V_{X_A = 1} - V_{X_A = 0}}{V_{X_A = 0}}
$$

The general concentration relation is:

$$
C_A = C_{A0}\,\frac{1 - X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}
$$

**Derivation of this result:**

By the ideal gas law at constant $T$ and $P$:

$$
V = \frac{n_{\text{total}} RT}{P} \quad \text{and} \quad V_0 = \frac{n_{\text{total},0} RT}{P}
$$

So the volume ratio equals the mole ratio:

$$
\frac{V}{V_0} = \frac{n_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total},0}}
$$

Total moles at conversion $X_A$ for a reaction with stoichiometry $\nu_A A + \cdots \to \nu_C C + \cdots$:

$$
n_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{total},0} + \xi \sum_i \nu_i = n_{\text{total},0} + \nu_A X_A\, n_{A0}\, \frac{\sum \nu_i}{\nu_A}
$$

The change in total moles per mole of *A* reacted, divided by the initial total moles, gives $\varepsilon_A$:

$$
\varepsilon_A = \frac{n_{A0}\,\sum_i \nu_i / \nu_A}{n_{\text{total},0}} = y_{A0}\,\delta
$$

where $\delta = \sum_i \nu_i / \nu_A$ and $y_{A0}$ is the initial mole fraction of *A*.

Hence:

$$
C_A = \frac{N_A}{V} = \frac{N_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{V_0\,(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = C_{A0}\,\frac{1 - X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}
$$

This derivation is **high-yield** for KTU Module-4 problems involving gas-phase PFR and CSTR calculations.

### 3.3 Derivation: Reactor Design Equation for a PFR (Plug Flow Reactor)

For a differential element $dV$ of a PFR, the steady-state mole balance on species *A* is:

$$
\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = 0
$$

$$
F_A \big\vert_{V} - F_A \big\vert_{V + dV} + r_A\, dV = 0
$$

Rearranging and taking the limit $dV \to 0$:

$$
\frac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A
$$

Expressing $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ and $\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = -F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX_A}{dV}$:

$$
F_{A0}\,\frac{dX_A}{dV} = -r_A
$$

Rearranging into integral form (assuming constant $F_{A0}$):

$$
V = F_{A0} \int_{X_{A,\text{in}}}^{X_{A,\text{out}}} \frac{dX_A}{-r_A}
$$

This is the **PFR design equation** — one of the most important equations in chemical reaction engineering.

### 3.4 Derivation: Reactor Design Equation for a CSTR

For a CSTR at steady state, the mole balance on species *A* over the entire reactor volume $V$ is:

$$
F_{A0} - F_A + r_A\, V = 0
$$

Substituting $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X_A)$:

$$
F_{A0} X_A = -r_A \, V
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A}
$$

For a single value of $X_A$ (well-mixed assumption), the exit rate $-r_A$ is evaluated at the outlet conditions, giving a simple algebraic expression for $V$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reaction Order and Reactor Selection

| Reaction Order | Rate Law | PFR Volume (per unit $F_{A0}$) | CSTR Volume (per unit $F_{A0}$) | Preferred Reactor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Zero order, $-r_A = k$ | $X_A / k$ | $X_A / k$ | Either (identical) |
| First order, $-r_A = k C_A$ | $-\dfrac{\ln(1 - X_A)}{k}$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{k(1 - X_A)}$ | PFR (smaller volume) |
| Second order, $-r_A = k C_A^2$ | $\dfrac{X_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)^2}$ | $\dfrac{X_A (1 - X_A)}{k C_{A0}}$ | PFR (smaller volume for $X_A > 0$) |
| Negative order (autocatalytic) | — | — | CSTR (preferred) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** For *positive-order* reactions, **PFR always requires less volume than CSTR** for the same conversion. For *autocatalytic* or *negative-order* reactions, a CSTR (or recycle PFR) is preferred because some unreacted feed is required to "seed" the reaction.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Statement:** The elementary liquid-phase reaction $A \to B$ follows a first-order rate law with $k = 0.25 \ \text{min}^{-1}$. The feed to a PFR is $F_{A0} = 2 \ \text{kmol/min}$ at $C_{A0} = 1 \ \text{kmol/m}^3$. Find the volume required to achieve $X_A = 0.80$.

**Step 1 — Identify the rate law:** $-r_A = k C_A = k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the PFR design equation:**

$$
V = F_{A0} \int_0^{0.80} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}(1 - X_A)}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
V = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \int_0^{0.80} \frac{dX_A}{1 - X_A} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}} \Big[ -\ln(1 - X_A) \Big]_0^{0.80}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the logarithm term:**

$$
-\ln(1 - 0.80) = -\ln(0.20) = -(-1.6094) = 1.6094
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the volume:**

$$
V = \frac{2}{(0.25)(1)} \times 1.6094 = 8 \times 1.6094 = 12.88 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Result:** $V_{\text{PFR}} = 12.88 \ \text{m}^3$ — this is the **KTU-style clean numerical answer**.
