## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
> Define the *extent of reaction*. How is it used to relate the change in moles of different species in a multi-reaction system?

**Model Answer:**
The extent of reaction, denoted by $\xi$, is a scalar quantity that measures the progress of a single reaction. For a single reaction involving $N_s$ species, the mole change of species $i$ is given by:
$$n_i = n_{i0} + \nu_i \xi$$
where $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric coefficient (negative for reactants, positive for products). For a system of $M$ simultaneous reactions, the relation generalizes to:
$$n_i = n_{i0} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} \nu_{ij} \xi_j$$
This formulation is central to process simulation because a single scalar $\xi_j$ uniquely characterizes the progress of each reaction $j$, allowing mass balances over the entire reactor to be expressed in a compact form. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
> Distinguish between *selectivity* and *yield*. Why are these concepts important in industrial reactor design?

**Model Answer:**
*Selectivity* ($S$) is the ratio of the moles of the desired product formed to the moles of a particular reactant (often the limiting one) consumed. *Yield* ($Y$) is the ratio of the moles of desired product obtained to the theoretical maximum possible from the limiting reactant, and is given by the product of conversion and selectivity: $Y_P = S_P \cdot X_A$. These are important because most industrial processes involve **multiple parallel or consecutive reactions**, and the operating conditions (temperature, pressure, residence time, catalyst) must be tuned to maximize the selectivity toward the desired product, which directly affects raw-material cost, downstream separation load, and waste generation. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
>
> **(a)** The elementary gas-phase reaction $2A \to B + C$ is carried out in an isothermal PFR at constant temperature and pressure. Pure *A* enters the reactor at $F_{A0} = 5 \ \text{kmol/hr}$ and $C_{A0} = 0.5 \ \text{kmol/m}^3$. The rate law is $-r_A = k C_A^2$ with $k = 0.08 \ \text{m}^3/(\text{kmol}\cdot\text{hr})$. Determine the reactor volume required to achieve 70 % conversion. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the gas-phase concentration relation $C_A = C_{A0}\,\dfrac{1 - X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$. Discuss the physical significance of $\varepsilon_A$ and its effect on reactor design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the stoichiometry and compute $\varepsilon_A$:**
For $2A \to B + C$, the change in total moles per mole of A reacted is:
$$\delta = \frac{\sum \nu_i}{\nu_A} = \frac{(-2) + 1 + 1}{-2} = 0$$
Since pure A is fed, $y_{A0} = 1$, so $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0} \cdot \delta = 0$. The reaction proceeds at constant volume.

**Step 2 — Express $C_A$ in terms of $X_A$:**
$$C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$$

**Step 3 — Write the PFR design equation:**
$$V = F_{A0} \int_0^{0.70} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$V = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^2} \int_0^{0.70} \frac{dX_A}{(1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^2} \left[ \frac{1}{1 - X_A} \right]_0^{0.70}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$V = \frac{5}{(0.08)(0.5)^2} \left[ \frac{1}{0.30} - 1 \right] = \frac{5}{0.02} \times 2.333 = 250 \times 2.333 = 583.3 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Result:** $V_{\text{PFR}} \approx 583.3 \ \text{m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the total moles at any extent:**
For the reaction $2A \to B + C$ with initial moles $n_{A0}$ and $n_{i0} = 0$ for B and C:
$$n_A = n_{A0} - 2\xi, \quad n_B = \xi, \quad n_C = \xi$$
$$n_{\text{total}} = n_{A0} - 2\xi + 2\xi = n_{A0}$$

Since the total moles are constant and pure A is fed, $\varepsilon_A = 0$, so $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$.

**Step 2 — General derivation:**
At any conversion $X_A$, $\xi = n_{A0} X_A / |\nu_A|$. The total moles:
$$n_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{total},0} + \xi \sum_i \nu_i$$
Dividing by the initial total moles and using the ideal gas law at constant $T$ and $P$:
$$\frac{V}{V_0} = 1 + \varepsilon_A X_A$$
Hence:
$$C_A = \frac{N_A}{V} = \frac{N_{A0}(1 - X_A)}{V_0(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)} = C_{A0}\,\frac{1 - X_A}{1 + \varepsilon_A X_A}$$

**Step 3 — Physical significance of $\varepsilon_A$:**
$\varepsilon_A$ is the fractional change in volume (at constant $T$ and $P$) when $A$ is completely converted. It is positive if the reaction produces more moles than it consumes, negative otherwise. A larger $|\varepsilon_A|$ implies greater volumetric change, which affects the local concentration profile and hence the design equation. **(7 marks)**

---

> ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
>
> **(a)** For the elementary reversible reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ carried out in a CSTR, derive the design equation and discuss the effect of equilibrium conversion on reactor size. The rate law is $-r_A = k_f C_A - k_r C_B$, with $k_f = 1.0 \ \text{hr}^{-1}$, $k_r = 0.2 \ \text{hr}^{-1}$, $C_{A0} = 2 \ \text{kmol/m}^3$, $\dot{V} = 1.5 \ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$. Pure *A* is fed. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the volume requirement of a PFR and a CSTR for a second-order liquid-phase reaction $A + B \to \text{Products}$ with $-r_A = k C_A C_B$, $k = 0.5 \ \text{L}/(\text{mol}\cdot\text{min})$, equimolar feed, $C_{A0} = C_{B0} = 1 \ \text{mol/L}$, $\dot{V}_0 = 1 \ \text{L/min}$, target $X_A = 0.90$. State which reactor is preferred and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — CSTR design equation:**
$$V = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{-r_A} = \frac{\dot{V} C_{A0} X_A}{k_f C_{A0}(1 - X_A) - k_r C_{A0} X_A}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium conversion:**
At equilibrium, $-r_A = 0$:
$$X_{A,\text{eq}} = \frac{K_C}{1 + K_C} = \frac{k_f/k_r}{1 + k_f/k_r} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833$$

**Step 3 — Required conversion (use $X_A = 0.70$ as a typical operating target):**
$$V = \frac{(1.5)(2)(0.70)}{(1.0)(2)(0.30) - (0.2)(2)(0.70)} = \frac{2.1}{0.6 - 0.28} = \frac{2.1}{0.32} \approx 6.56 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Effect of equilibrium on design:**
As $X_A \to X_{A,\text{eq}} = 0.833$, the denominator $-r_A \to 0$ and the required volume $V \to \infty$. Therefore, the operating conversion must be kept **strictly below** the equilibrium conversion. Reactors are often sized to operate at $X_A \approx 0.85 X_{A,\text{eq}}$ to balance volume cost against diminishing returns. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — CSTR volume:**
$$V_{\text{CSTR}} = \frac{F_{A0} X_A}{k C_{A0} C_{B0}(1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{(1)(0.90)}{(0.5)(1)(1)(0.10)^2} = \frac{0.90}{0.005} = 180 \ \text{L}$$

**Step 2 — PFR volume:**
For a PFR with equimolar feed ($C_A = C_B$ throughout):
$$V_{\text{PFR}} = F_{A0} \int_0^{0.90} \frac{dX_A}{k C_{A0}^2 (1 - X_A)^2} = \frac{F_{A0}}{k C_{A0}^2} \left[ \frac{1}{1 - X_A} - 1 \right]$$
$$V_{\text{PFR}} = \frac{1}{(0.5)(1)} \left[ \frac{1}{0.10} - 1 \right] = 2 \times 9 = 18 \ \text{L}$$

**Step 3 — Compare and conclude:**
$V_{\text{PFR}} = 18 \ \text{L}$ versus $V_{\text{CSTR}} = 180 \ \text{L}$. The PFR requires **one-tenth** the volume of the CSTR for the same conversion. For a positive-order reaction, the **PFR is strongly preferred**. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign convention for $\nu$:** Many students forget that stoichiometric coefficients are *negative for reactants*. Using $+2$ instead of $-2$ for the reactant *A* in $2A \to B + C$ will give a wrong $\varepsilon_A$ and hence a wrong concentration profile.
> 2. **Forgetting the gas-phase correction:** A very common mistake is to use $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ for *gas-phase* reactions where $\varepsilon_A \neq 0$. Always check the phase of the reaction and the feed composition before applying this formula.
> 3. **Confusing selectivity and yield:** Yield = Conversion × Selectivity. Examiners often give partial credit if you write the correct relationship, even if the numerical values are wrong.
> 4. **Reversible vs irreversible:** For an irreversible reaction, you cannot reach $X_A = 1$ in finite time/volume. For a reversible reaction, you cannot exceed the *equilibrium* conversion. Confusing these two limits is a frequent cause of lost marks.
> 5. **Units consistency:** Always carry the units in the design equation calculation. KTU examiners often deduct 1 mark simply for inconsistent or missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Definition of $\xi$:** A single scalar that measures reaction progress. Negative coefficients for reactants, positive for products.
>
> ✅ **Conversion:** $X_A = (N_{A0} - N_A)/N_{A0}$, always between 0 and 1 (for positive-order reactions).
>
> ✅ **Selectivity & Yield:** $S$ = desired product formed / limiting reactant consumed; $Y = S \cdot X_A$.
>
> ✅ **Power-law rate law:** $-r_A = k C_A^{\alpha} C_B^{\beta} \cdots$; the *order* is determined experimentally, not from stoichiometry (except for elementary reactions).
>
> ✅ **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ — temperature accelerates the rate.
>
> ✅ **Net reversible rate:** $-r_A = k_f C_A - k_r C_B$; at equilibrium, $k_f/k_r = K_C$.
>
> ✅ **Constant-volume batch:** $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)$ — apply only to liquids or to gases with $\varepsilon_A = 0$.
>
> ✅ **General gas-phase batch:** $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X_A)/(1 + \varepsilon_A X_A)$, where $\varepsilon_A = y_{A0} \cdot \delta$, $\delta = \sum \nu_i / \nu_A$.
>
> ✅ **PFR design:** $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int dX_A/(-r_A)$ — *integral* in general.
>
> ✅ **CSTR design:** $V = F_{A0} X_A / (-r_A)$ — *algebraic*, evaluated at exit.
>
> ✅ **Reactor comparison:** For positive-order kinetics, PFR $<$ CSTR volume; for autocatalytic, CSTR or recycle PFR is preferred.
>
> ✅ **Thermodynamic link:** $\Delta G^\circ = -RT \ln K$ — connects feasibility to kinetics.
>
> ✅ **Van't Hoff equation:** $d\ln K / dT = \Delta H^\circ / RT^2$ — exothermic reactions have $K$ decreasing with $T$.
>
> ✅ **Catalytic reactions:** Use Langmuir-Hinshelwood or Eley-Rideal forms; the rate includes adsorption terms.
>
> ✅ **Safety:** Exothermic reactions with high $E_a$ are vulnerable to *thermal runaway*; design of relief systems depends on reaction kinetics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catalysts Used (Acid, Base, and Enzyme Catalysts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Catalysts Used (Acid, Base, and Enzyme Catalysts)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Catalyst?

A **catalyst** is a substance that **increases the rate of a chemical reaction** without being consumed in the overall process, by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a **lower activation energy (Ea)**. The catalyst participates in the elementary steps of the mechanism but is regenerated at the end of the catalytic cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Strict Recall Version):**
> "A catalyst is a substance which alters the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being consumed in the reaction. It functions by providing an alternative reaction pathway of lower activation energy, thereby increasing the rate at which equilibrium is approached but not the position of the equilibrium itself."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Tunnel"

Imagine you are a hiker trying to cross a tall mountain (this represents the **activation energy barrier** of a chemical reaction). 

- **Without a catalyst:** You must climb all the way to the peak before reaching the other side. This is slow and energy-expensive.
- **With a catalyst:** A tunnel is bored through the mountain. You walk through the tunnel, taking far less time and energy, and emerge on the other side safely.

The mountain tunnel is **not consumed** in the journey — it remains intact for the next hiker. Similarly, a catalyst is **regenerated** after each reaction cycle, and only a small amount is needed to process large quantities of reactants.

## 1.3 Classification of Catalysts Used in Process Industries

In Process Systems Engineering (PSE), the three principal classes of industrial catalysts are:

| Class | Example | Typical Application |
|:-----|:--------|:--------------------|
| **Acid Catalysts** | H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄, Zeolites, Sulfated Zirconia, Amberlyst-15 | Alkylation, Cracking, Isomerization, Esterification |
| **Base Catalysts** | NaOH, KOH, CaO, MgO, Anion-Exchange Resins | Transesterification, Aldol Condensation, Biodiesel Production |
| **Enzyme Catalysts** | Lipase, Amylase, Protease, Cellulase, Zymase | Pharmaceutical Synthesis, Starch Hydrolysis, Fermentation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Homogeneous vs. Heterogeneous:**
> - **Homogeneous catalyst:** Exists in the *same phase* as the reactants (e.g., liquid H₂SO₄ catalyzing liquid-phase esterification).
> - **Heterogeneous catalyst:** Exists in a *different phase* (e.g., solid zeolite catalyzing vapor-phase cracking).
> Enzymes are technically a third category — **biocatalysts** — operating under mild aqueous (or biphasic) conditions at near-ambient temperatures.

## 1.4 Why Study Catalysts in Process Systems Engineering?

In **PECHT867 — Process Systems Engineering**, catalysts are studied not just for their chemistry, but for their impact on:

- **Reactor design** (size, configuration, heat/mass transfer limitations)
- **Process economics** (CAPEX for catalyst, OPEX for replacement/regeneration)
- **Process intensification** (e.g., reactive distillation, microreactors)
- **Sustainability** (green chemistry, biocatalytic routes replacing toxic reagents)
- **Process safety** (acid/base handling, thermal runaway potential)

The **selection of catalyst** is one of the first and most critical decisions a process engineer makes when synthesizing a new flow sheet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Acid Catalysts

### 2.1.1 Brønsted Acid Catalysts (Proton Donors)

A **Brønsted acid** donates a proton (H⁺) to a substrate, generating a more reactive **carbocation** or **oxocarbenium ion** intermediate. The reaction is termed **specific acid catalysis** when only the H⁺ species is active.

**Mechanism (Generalized for an alkene + acid HA):**
1. Protonation of alkene → carbocation intermediate
2. Nucleophilic attack on the carbocation
3. Deprotonation to yield the final product + regenerated HA

**Industrial Examples:**
- **H₂SO₄ (98%)** → Alkylation of isobutane with butenes → high-octane gasoline
- **H₃PO₄ on Kieselguhr (solid)** → Polymerization of propylene → polypropylene
- **p-Toluene Sulfonic Acid (PTSA)** → Esterification of acetic acid with ethanol
- **HF** → Alkylation (Phillips process)
- **Nafion-H / Amberlyst-15 (ion-exchange resins)** → MTBE synthesis

### 2.1.2 Lewis Acid Catalysts (Electron-Pair Acceptors)

A **Lewis acid** accepts an electron pair from a substrate, polarizing bonds and activating them. 

**Mechanism (Friedel–Crafts Alkylation, Generic):**
1. Lewis acid (e.g., AlCl₃) coordinates to alkyl halide → polarized complex
2. Carbocation generation (or ion-pair)
3. Electrophilic attack on aromatic ring
4. Proton loss → product + regenerated AlCl₃

**Industrial Examples:**
- **AlCl₃, BF₃, ZnCl₂, FeCl₃** → Friedel–Crafts alkylation/acylation
- **Zeolites (H-ZSM-5, H-Y, H-Beta)** → FCC, hydrocracking, MTO (methanol-to-olefins)
- **Sulfated zirconia (SO₄²⁻/ZrO₂)** → n-Butane isomerization at low T

### 2.1.3 Solid Acid Catalysts (Heterogeneous) — The Workhorses of Refining

Modern refining and petrochemical processes rely heavily on **solid acid catalysts** because of ease of separation, regenerability, and continuous operation in fixed/fluidized beds.

**Key Properties Required:**
- Strong and tunable acid strength
- High surface area (>100 m²/g)
- Thermal/hydrothermal stability
- Shape selectivity (zeolites)
- Resistance to coking, S, and N poisoning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zeolites — KTU Favourite Topic:** Zeolites are crystalline aluminosilicates with uniform micropores (0.3–1.5 nm). The **Si/Al ratio** controls acid strength: lower Si/Al → stronger and denser acid sites. They are widely asked about in KTU due to their use in **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)**.

---

## 2.2 Base Catalysts

### 2.2.1 Homogeneous Base Catalysts

A **Brønsted base** (OH⁻, alkoxide, amide) abstracts a proton from an acidic substrate, generating a reactive **carbanion** or **enolate**. The generic rate expression is:

$$r = k \cdot [\text{Substrate}] \cdot [\text{Base}]$$

**Common Industrial Examples:**
- **NaOH, KOH** → Biodiesel (transesterification of triglycerides with methanol), aldol condensations
- **NaOCH₃, KOt-Bu** → Fine chemicals, Michael additions
- **Amines (DBU, DABCO)** → Polymerization catalysts (e.g., polyurethanes)

### 2.2.2 Heterogeneous Base Catalysts

Heterogeneous base catalysts avoid the separation problem of liquid bases and allow continuous fixed-bed operation.

| Catalyst | Active Site | Application |
|:---------|:------------|:------------|
| CaO, MgO | Surface O²⁻ | Transesterification (biodiesel) |
| Hydrotalcite (Mg-Al-O) | Layered OH⁻ | Aldol condensation, Knoevenagel |
| KF/Al₂O₃ | Basic F⁻ sites | Michael addition, epoxidation |
| Anion-exchange resins (Amberlite IRA-400) | Quaternary N⁺OH⁻ | Biodiesel, epoxide ring-opening |
| Alkali-modified zeolites | Occluded Na₂O clusters | Side-chain alkylation of toluene |

> [!NOTE]
> **Biodiesel KTU Hook:** KTU frequently asks about **heterogeneous base catalysts for biodiesel (FAME)** — advantages include reusability, no saponification (compared to NaOH), and easier product separation. The standard reaction is triglyceride + 3 MeOH → glycerol + 3 FAME (fatty acid methyl esters), catalyzed by CaO at 60–65 °C.

---

## 2.3 Enzyme Catalysts (Biocatalysts)

### 2.3.1 Nature of Enzymes

Enzymes are **high-molecular-weight proteins** (often 15,000–500,000 Da) produced by living cells. They catalyze biochemical reactions with **extraordinary specificity** (regio-, stereo-, enantio-) under **mild conditions** (pH 5–9, 25–60 °C, aqueous medium).

**Classification by Reaction Type (Enzyme Commission — EC Nomenclature):**
1. **Oxidoreductases** — redox reactions
2. **Transferases** — group transfer
3. **Hydrolases** — hydrolysis
4. **Lyases** — addition/elimination
5. **Isomerases** — isomerization
6. **Ligases** — bond formation with ATP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** For PSE applications, **hydrolases (lipases, proteases, amylases)** are most important industrially, accounting for the majority of bulk biocatalytic production.

### 2.3.2 The Active Site and Lock-and-Key / Induced-Fit Models

The **active site** is a 3-D pocket where the substrate binds. Two classical models:
- **Lock-and-Key (Fischer, 1894):** Rigid fit between substrate and active site.
- **Induced-Fit (Koshland, 1958):** Active site reshapes upon substrate binding. This is the **accepted model** and is more accurate.

**Enzyme Features (Koshland's Six Postulates for Specificity):**
1. The enzyme is complementary to the **transition state**, not the substrate.
2. Substrate binding distorts it toward the transition state.
3. Catalysis proceeds via the transition-state complex.
4. Specificity arises from the geometry of enzyme-substrate interaction.
5. The enzyme is a flexible, dynamic template.

### 2.3.3 Michaelis–Menten Kinetics (The Core Equation for Enzymes)

The single most important equation in enzyme kinetics is the **Michaelis–Menten equation**:

$$v = \frac{V_{max} \cdot [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

where:
- $v$ = reaction rate (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹)
- $V_{max}$ = maximum rate when enzyme is saturated
- $[S]$ = substrate concentration (mol L⁻¹)
- $K_M$ = Michaelis constant (mol L⁻¹) = substrate concentration at which $v = V_{max}/2$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** $K_M$ is the **[S] at half Vmax**. Lower $K_M$ → higher enzyme affinity for substrate.

The **Lineweaver–Burk** double-reciprocal form (linearization) is:

$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_M}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

This is used to determine $V_{max}$ and $K_M$ experimentally from a straight line of $1/v$ vs. $1/[S]$.

### 2.3.4 Enzyme Inhibition (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Effect on K_M | Effect on V_max |
|:-----|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Competitive** | ↑ K_M (apparent) | Unchanged |
| **Uncompetitive** | ↓ K_M (apparent) | ↓ V_max |
| **Non-competitive** | Unchanged | ↓ V_max |
| **Mixed** | Changes | ↓ V_max |

### 2.3.5 Immobilization of Enzymes (Industrial Practice)

Free enzymes are water-soluble, expensive, and not reusable. **Immobilization** solves this by attaching enzymes to a solid support.

**Methods:**
1. **Adsorption** on activated carbon, silica, ion-exchange resin
2. **Covalent bonding** to functionalized carriers (e.g., glutaraldehyde-activated chitosan)
3. **Entrapment** in polymer gel (Ca-alginate, polyacrylamide)
4. **Encapsulation** in microcapsules or liposomes
5. **Cross-linking** (CLE — cross-linked enzyme aggregates, CLEAs)

**Advantages of Immobilization:**
- Easy separation from product
- Reusability (5–100 cycles)
- Continuous operation in packed-bed reactors
- Enhanced thermal and operational stability

---

## 2.4 Selection Criteria for Industrial Catalysts (Engineering View)

A process engineer must weigh multiple competing factors when selecting a catalyst:

1. **Activity** — rate per unit mass/volume of catalyst
2. **Selectivity** — proportion of desired product
3. **Stability / Life** — time-on-stream before regeneration
4. **Cost** — including precious-metal (Pt, Pd) or enzyme cost
5. **Regenerability** — ease of coke removal, redox reactivation
6. **Mechanical strength** — attrition resistance for fluidized beds
7. **Toxicity / Environmental impact** — REACH, green chemistry compliance
8. **Compatibility with reactor type** — slurry, fixed bed, fluidized bed, membrane

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes |
|:------------------|:----------------------|:--------------|
| **Catalyst Effect on Ea** | $E_{a,\text{cat}} < E_{a,\text{non-cat}}$ | kJ/mol |
| **Arrhenius Rate Law** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| **Turnover Number (TON)** | $\text{TON} = \dfrac{\text{moles product}}{\text{moles catalyst}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\text{TON}}{\Delta t}$ | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ |
| **Michaelis–Menten** | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]}$ | $V_{max}$ in mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹; $K_M$ in mol L⁻¹ |
| **Lineweaver–Burk** | $\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{K_M}{V_{max}}\dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Linear plot |
| **Enzyme Mass Balance** | $V_{max} = k_{cat} \cdot [E]_0$ | $k_{cat}$ = catalytic constant (s⁻¹) |
| **Catalyst Effectiveness Factor** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{actual rate}}{\text{rate at bulk conditions}}$ | 0 < η ≤ 1 (η < 1 → pore diffusion limited) |
| **Space Time Yield** | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{\text{mass product}}{\text{mass catalyst} \cdot \text{time}}$ | kg/(kg·h) |
| **Thiele Modulus** | $\phi = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{eff}}}$ | $L$ = characteristic length, $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity |
| **Acid Strength (Hammett)** | $H_0 = pK_{a,\text{indicator}} + \log\left(\dfrac{[B]}{[BH^+]}\right)$ | More negative → stronger acid |
| **Bioconversion Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{moles product formed}}{\text{moles substrate consumed}} \times 100\%$ | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No pipes inside table cells.** Note the use of `\dfrac` and `\cdot` for clean LaTeX rendering inside markdown.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refining:** FCC units use **zeolite Y + ZSM-5** to crack heavy gas oil into gasoline, diesel, and LPG. The catalyst circulates between the **riser reactor** and the **regenerator** at 700–800 °C.
- **Polymer Industry:** Ziegler–Natta (TiCl₄/MgCl₂) and metallocene catalysts produce isotactic polypropylene and LLDPE.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** **Lipase B from Candida antarctica** (Novozym 435, immobilized) is the industrial workhorse for chiral resolution and dynamic kinetic resolution of racemic alcohols.
- **Green Chemistry:** **Enzymatic hydrolysis of cellulose** to glucose replaces acid hydrolysis, eliminating corrosive waste streams.
- **Biodiesel:** **Heterogeneous CaO/MgO catalysts** enable continuous biodiesel production from used cooking oil, avoiding soap formation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius Form and the Effect of a Catalyst on Rate

We begin with the Arrhenius equation. Let us show that a catalyst's ability to lower $E_a$ exponentially accelerates the reaction.

### Step 1: Define the Arrhenius equation
The temperature dependence of a rate constant is given by:
$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

### Step 2: Ratio of catalyzed to uncatalyzed rate constants
At a fixed temperature $T$, the ratio of the catalyzed to the uncatalyzed rate constant is:
$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{non-cat}}} = \frac{A_{\text{cat}} \cdot e^{-E_{a,\text{cat}} / RT}}{A_{\text{non-cat}} \cdot e^{-E_{a,\text{non-cat}} / RT}}$$

### Step 3: Assume pre-exponentials are of similar magnitude
For a simplified engineering estimate, $A_{\text{cat}} \approx A_{\text{non-cat}} = A$. Then:
$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{non-cat}}} = e^{-(E_{a,\text{cat}} - E_{a,\text{non-cat}}) / RT} = e^{\Delta E_a / RT}$$

where $\Delta E_a = E_{a,\text{non-cat}} - E_{a,\text{cat}}$ (a positive number, since the catalyst lowers the barrier).

### Step 4: Numerical sanity check (KTU-style plug-and-show)
Take $T = 600$ K, $E_{a,\text{non-cat}} = 150$ kJ/mol, $E_{a,\text{cat}} = 100$ kJ/mol. Then:
$$\Delta E_a = 50{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$$
$$\frac{k_{\text{cat}}}{k_{\text{non-cat}}} = \exp\left(\frac{50{,}000}{8.314 \times 600}\right) = \exp(10.02) \approx 22{,}500$$

So even a modest $\Delta E_a$ of 50 kJ/mol multiplies the rate by roughly **four orders of magnitude**. This is why catalysts are *essential*, not optional, in industrial chemistry.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Michaelis–Menten Equation from the Quasi-Steady-State Approximation (QSSA)

Consider the classical enzyme mechanism:
$$E + S \xrightleftharpoons[k_{-1}]{k_1} ES \xrightarrow{k_2} E + P$$

### Step 1: Define the elementary rate constants
- $k_1$: forward rate for enzyme-substrate binding (M⁻¹ s⁻¹)
- $k_{-1}$: reverse rate for unbinding (s⁻¹)
- $k_2$: catalytic turnover (s⁻¹), also called $k_{cat}$

### Step 2: Write the rate of product formation
The rate of appearance of product is the rate of breakdown of the enzyme-substrate complex:
$$v = \frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_2 \cdot [ES]$$

### Step 3: Apply the Quasi-Steady-State Approximation
The Briggs–Haldane QSSA states that after a brief transient, $[ES]$ changes negligibly slowly, so:
$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 [E][S] - k_{-1}[ES] - k_2[ES] \approx 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $[ES]$
Rearranging the QSSA equation:
$$k_1 [E][S] = (k_{-1} + k_2) [ES]$$
$$[ES] = \frac{k_1 [E][S]}{k_{-1} + k_2}$$

### Step 5: Introduce $K_M$
By definition, the Michaelis constant is:
$$K_M = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$

Substituting:
$$[ES] = \frac{[E][S]}{K_M}$$

### Step 6: Use the enzyme mass balance
The total enzyme concentration is conserved (the catalyst is not consumed):
$$[E]_0 = [E] + [ES]$$

Hence, the free enzyme concentration is:
$$[E] = [E]_0 - [ES]$$

### Step 7: Substitute into the expression for $[ES]$
$$[ES] = \frac{([E]_0 - [ES])[S]}{K_M}$$
$$K_M [ES] = [E]_0 [S] - [ES][S]$$
$$[ES]\,(K_M + [S]) = [E]_0 [S]$$
$$[ES] = \frac{[E]_0 [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

### Step 8: Substitute into the rate expression
$$v = k_2 [ES] = \frac{k_2 [E]_0 [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

### Step 9: Define $V_{max}$
At saturation, $[S] \to \infty$ and the active sites are fully occupied: $V_{max} = k_2 [E]_0 = k_{cat} [E]_0$. Therefore:
$$v = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

This is the **Michaelis–Menten equation**, the foundation of enzyme kinetics and a guaranteed KTU exam topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case:** If $k_2 \ll k_{-1}$ (i.e., catalysis is slow and unbinding is fast), then $K_M \approx k_{-1}/k_1 = K_d$, the **thermodynamic dissociation constant** of the ES complex. In this limit, $K_M$ is a true equilibrium constant.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lineweaver–Burk Linearization

Starting from the Michaelis–Menten equation:
$$v = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

### Step 1: Invert both sides
$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_M + [S]}{V_{max} [S]} = \frac{K_M}{V_{max} [S]} + \frac{[S]}{V_{max} [S]}$$

### Step 2: Simplify
$$\frac{1}{v} = \frac{K_M}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

This is in the form $y = mx + c$ with:
- $y = 1/v$
- $x = 1/[S]$
- Slope $m = K_M / V_{max}$
- Intercept $c = 1 / V_{max}$

Plotting $1/v$ vs. $1/[S]$ yields a straight line whose slope and intercept allow $K_M$ and $V_{max}$ to be extracted. The **x-intercept** equals $-1/K_M$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Catalyst Comparison

| Property | Acid Catalyst | Base Catalyst | Enzyme Catalyst |
|:---------|:--------------|:--------------|:----------------|
| **Phase** (typical) | Liquid (H₂SO₄) or Solid (zeolite) | Liquid (NaOH) or Solid (CaO) | Aqueous / immobilized on solid |
| **Operating T** | 50–800 °C | 50–400 °C | 20–70 °C |
| **Operating pH** | pH < 7 | pH > 7 | pH 5–9 (narrow window) |
| **Selectivity** | Moderate (poor for sensitive substrates) | Moderate | **Very high** (regio-, stereo-) |
| **Reusability** | High (heterogeneous) | High (heterogeneous) | Limited; needs immobilization |
| **Byproducts** | Salt waste (H₂SO₄) | Soap (homogeneous NaOH) | H₂O, CO₂ (clean) |
| **Sensitivity to poisons** | S, N compounds, coking | CO₂, H₂O | Heat, organic solvents, heavy metals |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low to moderate | **High** (but often offset by selectivity) |
| **Scale-up maturity** | Mature (refining) | Mature (biodiesel) | Growing (pharma, food) |
| **Environmental impact** | Corrosive waste | Corrosive waste | **Low** (green) |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Effectiveness Factor for a Porous Catalyst (Thiele Approach)

Consider a first-order reaction $A \to B$ in a spherical catalyst pellet of radius $R$.

### Step 1: Mass balance on a thin shell of radius $r$
$$D_{eff} \left( \frac{d^2 C_A}{dr^2} + \frac{2}{r} \frac{dC_A}{dr} \right) = k \cdot C_A$$

### Step 2: Boundary conditions
- At $r = R$: $C_A = C_{A,s}$ (surface concentration)
- At $r = 0$: $dC_A/dr = 0$ (symmetry)

### Step 3: Non-dimensionalize
Let $\rho = r/R$ and $\psi = C_A / C_{A,s}$. Then:
$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{d\rho^2} + \frac{2}{\rho} \frac{d\psi}{d\rho} = \phi^2 \psi$$

where the **Thiele modulus** is:
$$\phi = R \sqrt{\frac{k}{D_{eff}}}$$

### Step 4: Solve and compute the effectiveness factor
For a first-order reaction in a sphere, the analytical solution is:
$$\eta = \frac{3}{\phi} \left[ \frac{1}{\tanh(\phi)} - \frac{1}{\phi} \right]$$

### Step 5: Limiting cases
- **Reaction-limited** ($\phi \to 0$): $\eta \to 1$ (entire pellet used)
- **Diffusion-limited** ($\phi \to \infty$): $\eta \to 3/\phi$ (only outer shell used)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Insight:** Industrial pellets are designed with $\phi \approx 0.5$–$2$ to balance activity (high $\eta$) and pressure drop (which scales with $1/R^2$). This is a classic PSE optimization problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Question Pattern Recap:**
> - Part A: Short-answer questions, 3 marks each.
> - Part B: Descriptive questions, 14 marks each, with internal choice. Sub-parts are usually 7 + 7 marks.
> - Module-end exam typically includes **2 marks for neat sketch / diagram**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a catalyst. How does it differ from an inhibitor? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **catalyst** is a substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without being consumed in the overall process, by providing an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.

- A **catalyst speeds up** the reaction (decreases $E_a$).
- An **inhibitor slows down** or stops the reaction (often by deactivating active sites, decreasing the effective catalyst concentration, or increasing the apparent activation energy).
- A catalyst is regenerated at the end of the cycle; an inhibitor is generally consumed or remains bound.

### Q2. State the Michaelis–Menten equation and explain the significance of $K_M$. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Michaelis–Menten equation is:
$$v = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

**Significance of $K_M$:**
- It is numerically equal to the substrate concentration at which the reaction rate is half the maximum: $v = V_{max}/2$ when $[S] = K_M$.
- It is an inverse measure of the **affinity** of the enzyme for its substrate: a low $K_M$ means high affinity.
- Its units are **mol L⁻¹** (concentration units).
- When $k_2 \ll k_{-1}$, $K_M$ approaches the dissociation constant $K_d$ of the ES complex.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Acid and Base Catalysts

**Q. (a)** Discuss the mechanism of **Brønsted acid catalysis** with a suitable example. Explain how the **Hammett acidity function $H_0$** characterizes the strength of a solid acid catalyst. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the use of **heterogeneous base catalysts** in the **transesterification reaction for biodiesel production**. Compare its advantages over homogeneous NaOH. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Mechanism of Brønsted Acid Catalysis (3 marks):**

Consider the acid-catalyzed esterification of acetic acid with ethanol:

Step 1 — Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen by the Brønsted acid HA:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{HA} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(OH}^+\text{)OH} + \text{A}^-$$

Step 2 — Nucleophilic attack of ethanol on the activated carbonyl carbon, forming a tetrahedral intermediate:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{C(OH}^+\text{)OH} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(OH)(OC}_2\text{H}_5\text{)OH}^+$$

Step 3 — Proton transfer and loss of water:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{C(OH)(OC}_2\text{H}_5\text{)OH}^+ \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{H}^+$$

Step 4 — Regeneration of HA from the released proton and the conjugate base:
$$\text{H}^+ + \text{A}^- \rightarrow \text{HA}$$

The catalyst HA is regenerated, completing the cycle.

**Hammett Acidity Function $H_0$ (2 marks):**

The Hammett function quantifies the acid strength of a solid (or superacid) catalyst using an indicator base B:
$$H_0 = pK_{a,BH^+} + \log\left(\frac{[B]}{[BH^+]}\right)$$

where $pK_{a,BH^+}$ is the $pK_a$ of the conjugate acid of the indicator. **More negative $H_0$ → stronger acidity**. For reference, $100\%$ H₂SO₄ has $H_0 \approx -10$, while a superacid like HF–SbF₅ reaches $H_0 \approx -23$.

**Examples in Industry (2 marks):**
- Friedel–Crafts alkylation with AlCl₃ (Lewis)
- FCC with zeolite Y (solid Brønsted)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Transesterification Reaction (2 marks):**
$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

This is the heart of **biodiesel production**. CaO (calcined limestone) catalyzes the reaction at 60–65 °C, atmospheric pressure, with a 6:1 methanol-to-oil molar ratio.

**Mechanism on CaO (2 marks):**
- Methanol adsorbs on the basic CaO surface, generating **methoxide (CH₃O⁻)** species.
- Methoxide attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride.
- Tetrahedral intermediate collapses to release FAME and a diglyceride, which continues to react in two more steps.

**Advantages of Heterogeneous CaO over Homogeneous NaOH (3 marks):**

| Feature | Homogeneous NaOH | Heterogeneous CaO |
|:--------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| **Separation** | Difficult (washing, water wash) | **Easy — filtration** |
| **Reusability** | Single use | **5–10 cycles** |
| **Saponification** | Severe (forms soap) | Negligible (no free OH⁻ in solution) |
| **FFA tolerance** | Poor (saponification) | Better |
| **Wastewater** | High (acid neutralization) | Low |
| **Continuous operation** | No | **Yes (packed bed)** |
| **Drawback** | Corrosion | Slow kinetics, possible leaching of Ca²⁺ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A & B):**
> - Forgetting to **regenerate the catalyst** explicitly in a catalytic cycle — KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark**.
> - Conflating **Arrhenius pre-exponential factor $A$** with rate — $A$ is a *constant*, not a "rate".
> - Confusing $K_M$ with $K_d$ — they are **not** the same unless $k_2 \ll k_{-1}$.
> - Using "$|x|$" syntax in a markdown table — the KTU answer sheet is plain text; write "abs(x)" or use a separate equation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Enzyme Catalysts

**Q. (a)** Derive the **Michaelis–Menten equation** using the quasi-steady-state approximation. Discuss the physical significance of $K_M$ and $V_{max}$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the various **types of enzyme inhibition** with suitable plots. What is **enzyme immobilization**, and why is it important industrially? **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The derivation follows the QSSA steps shown in Section 3.2. The key final result is:
$$v = \frac{V_{max} [S]}{K_M + [S]}$$

with $V_{max} = k_{cat} [E]_0$ and $K_M = (k_{-1} + k_2)/k_1$.

**Physical Significance (2 marks):**
- $V_{max}$ is the **maximum attainable rate**, observed when all enzyme molecules are bound to substrate (saturation). It is directly proportional to the total enzyme concentration.
- $K_M$ has two interpretations:
  (i) It is the **[S] at which $v = V_{max}/2$** — a useful experimental yardstick.
  (ii) It is a measure of **enzyme–substrate affinity** — lower $K_M$ → tighter binding → higher affinity.
- Units: $V_{max}$ in mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹; $K_M$ in mol L⁻¹.

**Limiting cases (2 marks):**
- At low $[S] \ll K_M$: $v \approx (V_{max}/K_M) [S]$ — **first-order** in substrate.
- At high $[S] \gg K_M$: $v \approx V_{max}$ — **zero-order** in substrate (saturation).

**Experimental determination (1 mark):** Use the **Lineweaver–Burk** double-reciprocal plot ($1/v$ vs. $1/[S]$); slope $= K_M / V_{max}$, intercept $= 1/V_{max}$, x-intercept $= -1/K_M$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Types of Enzyme Inhibition (3 marks):**

1. **Competitive Inhibition:** Inhibitor resembles substrate; binds to the **active site**. Increases apparent $K_M$; $V_{max}$ unchanged. Example: malonate inhibiting succinate dehydrogenase.
2. **Uncompetitive Inhibition:** Inhibitor binds only to the **ES complex**. Decreases both apparent $K_M$ and $V_{max}$.
3. **Non-competitive (pure) Inhibition:** Inhibitor binds to a site **other than the active site**, with equal affinity for E and ES. $K_M$ unchanged; $V_{max}$ decreased.
4. **Mixed Inhibition:** Inhibitor binds to both E and ES with different affinities; both $K_M$ and $V_{max}$ are affected.

(Refer to the Lineweaver–Burk plot: competitive inhibitors rotate the line about the y-intercept; non-competitive ones rotate about the x-intercept; uncompetitive ones shift both intercepts.)

**Enzyme Immobilization (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Immobilization is the technique of physically confining or localizing enzyme molecules on a solid support while retaining their catalytic activity.

**Methods (with examples):**
- **Adsorption** (e.g., lipase on silica)
- **Covalent bonding** (e.g., glucose isomerase on porous glass with glutaraldehyde)
- **Entrapment** (e.g., enzyme in Ca-alginate beads)
- **Encapsulation** (e.g., in liposomes or microcapsules)
- **Cross-linked enzyme aggregates (CLEAs)**

**Industrial Importance (1 mark):**
- Reusability (saves cost since enzymes are expensive)
- Continuous operation in **packed-bed or fluidized-bed bioreactors**
- Easy product purification (no enzyme in product stream)
- Enhanced stability against pH, temperature, and solvents

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Skipping the QSSA justification** in the Michaelis–Menten derivation will cost you **2 marks** — KTU expects the assumption to be explicitly stated.
> - Forgetting to mention **units** of $K_M$ and $V_{max}$ — a guaranteed 0.5 mark loss.
> - Drawing Lineweaver–Burk plots **without labeling axes** — examiners deduct a full mark for sloppy diagrams.
> - Confusing "competitive" with "non-competitive" inhibition patterns — please memorize the $K_M$ and $V_{max}$ behavior table from Section 2.3.4.
> - Using $V_{max} = k_{cat}$ (without $[E]_0$) — this is a classic KTU trap; $V_{max}$ depends on enzyme concentration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print or pin this for the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Catalyst definition** — alters rate without being consumed; lowers $E_a$; does **not** shift equilibrium.
- [ ] **Three classes** — acid (Brønsted/Lewis/solid), base (homogeneous/heterogeneous), enzyme (biocatalyst).
- [ ] **Brønsted acid** donates H⁺; **Lewis acid** accepts an electron pair.
- [ ] **Hammett $H_0$** — more negative = stronger acid (used for superacids, solid acids).
- [ ] **Zeolites** — crystalline aluminosilicates; tunable Si/Al ratio; shape selectivity; used in FCC.
- [ ] **CaO** — heterogeneous base catalyst of choice for biodiesel; avoids saponification.
- [ ] **Enzymes** are proteins; high specificity; mild conditions; **Michaelis–Menten kinetics** central.
- [ ] **Michaelis–Menten** — $v = V_{max}[S]/(K_M + [S])$; $K_M$ = [S] at $V_{max}/2$; lower $K_M$ = higher affinity.
- [ ] **Lineweaver–Burk** — $1/v = (K_M/V_{max})(1/[S]) + 1/V_{max}$.
- [ ] **Inhibition types** — Competitive (↑$K_M$, same $V_{max}$); Non-competitive (same $K_M$, ↓$V_{max}$); Uncompetitive (both ↓); Mixed (both change).
- [ ] **QSSA** — assumes $d[ES]/dt \approx 0$ after a brief transient; **must** be stated explicitly in derivations.
- [ ] **Immobilization** — methods: adsorption, covalent, entrapment, encapsulation, cross-linking. Industrial benefits: reusability, continuous operation, easy separation.
- [ ] **Effectiveness factor $\eta$** — measures how much of the internal catalyst pellet is utilized; depends on Thiele modulus $\phi$.
- [ ] **TOF** (turnover frequency) and **TON** (turnover number) are key catalyst-performance metrics — memorize their definitions.
- [ ] **Arrhenius impact** — a 50 kJ/mol decrease in $E_a$ can speed up a reaction by **~4 orders of magnitude** at 600 K.
- [ ] **Common KTU values to remember** — $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); typical $K_M$ range = 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻² mol/L; typical operating T for enzymes = 25–60 °C.
- [ ] **Drawings matter** — always label axes, mark intercepts, and state assumptions in any kinetic plot.
- [ ] **Reactor-catalyst coupling** — solid acids → fixed/fluidized bed; homogeneous bases → stirred tank with neutralization; enzymes → packed-bed bioreactor with immobilized pellets.

**All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biodiesel Production from Waste Oils and Fats: Raw Materials and Feedstock
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biodiesel Production from Waste Oils and Fats: Raw Materials and Feedstock

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Biodiesel** is defined as a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids derived from a renewable lipid feedstock, such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or waste cooking oils, produced through a process called **transesterification** (or alcoholysis). According to international standards (ASTM D6751 / EN 14214), biodiesel is a fuel composed of **Fatty Acid Methyl Esters (FAME)** when methanol is used as the alcohol.

**Waste oils and fats** (also termed **second-generation feedstocks** or **used lipid feedstocks**) refer to lipid-based materials that have already served a primary purpose (e.g., cooking) and are now destined for disposal, recycling, or energy recovery. They are categorized based on origin, purity, and free fatty acid (FFA) content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The emphasis is on the **characterization**, **pre-treatment**, and **selection** of low-cost lipid feedstocks such as *Yellow Grease*, *Brown Grease*, *Waste Cooking Oil (WCO)*, *Tallow*, *Lard*, *Trap Grease*, and *Sewage Sludge Lipids* for sustainable biodiesel synthesis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of waste oils and fats as **"second-hand gold"** for the biodiesel industry.

- **Virgin Vegetable Oil** = Gold ore freshly mined from the earth — pure, but expensive (₹80–120/kg in India) and competes with the human food chain.
- **Waste Cooking Oil (WCO)** = Recycled jewelry — it has been "used" (contaminated with water, food particles, free fatty acids) but is still chemically rich in **triglycerides**, the very molecules that can be converted into biodiesel.

Just as a jeweler must clean and purify second-hand gold before reshaping it, a chemical engineer must **pre-treat** waste oils (remove water, filter solids, neutralize or esterify free fatty acids) before subjecting them to the **transesterification reactor** to produce high-purity FAME.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Approximately **70–80%** of the total biodiesel production cost comes from the feedstock itself. Hence, waste oils are economically essential, even though they introduce significant **process complexity** due to high FFA, moisture, and impurities.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Feedstock Characterization

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free Fatty Acid** | FFA | % (as oleic acid) | Determines pre-treatment route (acid vs. base catalysis) |
| **Acid Value** | AV | mg KOH/g oil | Quantifies acidity / hydrolytic degradation |
| **Saponification Value** | SV | mg KOH/g oil | Indicates average molecular weight of triglycerides |
| **Iodine Value** | IV | g I₂/100 g oil | Measures degree of unsaturation (affects cetane & oxidation stability) |
| **Moisture Content** | M | wt% | Causes soap formation in base catalysis; must be **<0.1%** |
| **Density** | ρ | kg/m³ | Affects fuel injection and energy density |
| **Viscosity** | μ | mm²/s | Biodiesel target: **3.5–5.0 mm²/s** at 40 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Threshold for KTU Exam:** If **FFA > 2%** (or AV > 4 mg KOH/g), the feedstock **cannot** be directly transesterified using a homogeneous base catalyst (e.g., NaOH, KOH). A two-step **acid-catalyzed esterification followed by base-catalyzed transesterification** becomes mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Waste Oils and Fats

Waste lipid feedstocks are systematically classified based on their **origin** and **purity level**:

#### A. Vegetable Oil-Based Waste Feedstocks
1. **Used Cooking Oil (UCO) / Waste Cooking Oil (WCO):**
   - Generated from household, restaurant, and food-industry frying operations.
   - Typical FFA: **2–15%** (depending on frying cycles, temperature, and storage time).
   - Color: dark brown; contains polymerized compounds, water, food particulates.
   - Most widely used second-generation biodiesel feedstock globally.

2. **Crude Vegetable Oil Refining By-products:**
   - **soapstock**, **acid oil**, **gums**, and **spent bleaching earth lipids**.
   - Very high FFA (often **>20%**); require extensive pre-treatment.

#### B. Animal Fat-Based Waste Feedstocks
1. **Tallow** (rendered bovine fat) — FFA: 1–12%.
2. **Lard** (rendered porcine fat) — FFA: 0.5–5%.
3. **Poultry Fat** (chicken) — FFA: 1–8%.
4. **Fish Oil** (from fish processing waste) — high unsaturation, prone to oxidation.

#### C. Grease-Based Waste Feedstocks (Recovered from Traps/Sewers)
1. **Yellow Grease** — recovered waste oil with FFA **<15%** (high-quality, often from restaurant traps).
2. **Brown Grease** — recovered from sewer traps and grease interceptors; FFA **>15%**, highly contaminated (debris, water, calcium soaps).
3. **Trap Grease** — similar to brown grease; used in municipal wastewater treatment plants.

#### D. Other Industrial Wastes
1. **Sewage Sludge Lipids** (extracted via flotation from municipal WWTPs).
2. **Microalgae Lipids** (third-generation, not strictly "waste" but low-cost).

### 2.2 Why Feedstock Pre-Treatment is Mandatory

When WCO is heated repeatedly in the presence of moisture and air, the following reactions occur:

- **Hydrolysis** of triglycerides → diglycerides + monoglycerides + **FFA** (acid value rises).
- **Oxidation** at C=C double bonds → aldehydes, ketones, peroxides, polymers.
- **Thermal polymerization** at high frying temperatures (>180 °C) → cyclic and aromatic compounds (high viscosity, gum formation).

These degradation products cause:
- **Soap formation** (FFA + NaOH → soap), which consumes catalyst, emulsifies product, and reduces yield.
- **Poor phase separation** between glycerol and FAME.
- **Engine fouling** from polymerized deposits and high viscosity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam high-yield. Memorize the constants, the variable definitions, and the units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Constants / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free Fatty Acid (as oleic acid)** | $\text{FFA\,\%} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 28.2}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | 28.2 = equivalent weight of oleic acid / 10 |
| 2 | **Acid Value (AV)** | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 56.1}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | 56.1 = equivalent weight of KOH |
| 3 | **Saponification Value (SV)** | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}}) \times N_{\text{HCl}} \times 56.1}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | Higher SV → lower molecular weight triglyceride |
| 4 | **Average Molecular Weight of Oil** | $M_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} + 3 \times 18$ | Three fatty acids + glycerol backbone |
| 5 | **Iodine Value (Wijs method, simplified)** | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}}) \times N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} \times 12.69}{W_{\text{oil}}}$ | 12.69 = equivalent weight of I₂ / 10 |
| 6 | **Theoretical Biodiesel Yield (mass basis)** | $Y_{\text{theo}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{FAME}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} \times W_{\text{oil}}$ | Assumes 100% conversion of all 3 fatty acids |
| 7 | **Stoichiometric Methanol Ratio** | $n_{\text{MeOH}} : n_{\text{oil}} = 3 : 1$ | Practically, **6:1 to 12:1** is used to shift equilibrium |
| 8 | **Saponification (Soap) Reaction Mass Balance** | $\text{RCOOH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{RCOONa} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | 1 mol FFA consumes 1 mol NaOH catalyst |
| 9 | **Esterification (Pre-treatment) Reaction** | $\text{RCOOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Reduces FFA below **2%** before base step |
| 10 | **Transesterification (Main Reaction)** | $\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{NaOH}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$ | Reversible; excess MeOH drives forward |

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Step

1. **Step 1 — Sample Drying:** Heat WCO to **105 °C** for 30 min or use vacuum dehydration to drive off water to **<0.1 wt%**. Water is a poison for base catalysts because it hydrolyzes FAME back to FFA.

2. **Step 2 — Filtration:** Pass oil through mesh (60–100 µm) or filter press to remove food particulates, charred solids, and insoluble gums.

3. **Step 3 — Acid-Catalyzed Esterification (if FFA > 2%):** Add H₂SO₄ (1–5 wt%) and methanol (10–20 wt%) at **55–65 °C** for 1–2 h. FFA → FAME. Reduce FFA to **<1%**.

4. **Step 4 — Base-Catalyzed Transesterification:** Add NaOH (0.5–1.5 wt%) dissolved in methanol (6:1 molar ratio) at **60 °C** for 1 h. Triglyceride → 3 FAME + glycerol.

5. **Step 5 — Phase Separation & Purification:** Settle (gravity) → separate glycerol (bottom) → wash FAME with warm water to remove soap/catalyst → dry under vacuum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This two-step process is the **industry standard** for high-FFA waste feedstocks, and forms the backbone of commercial biodiesel plants in India, the EU, and the US.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Biodiesel Yield from a Waste Cooking Oil Sample

**Problem Setup:**
A WCO sample has a measured **saponification value (SV) = 195 mg KOH/g oil**. Calculate:
(a) The average molecular weight of the triglyceride.
(b) The theoretical mass of FAME (biodiesel) obtainable from 1000 kg of this WCO, assuming the average FAME molecular weight is **296 g/mol** (methyl oleate equivalent).
(c) The stoichiometric and practical (6:1) methanol requirement.

#### Part (a): Average Molecular Weight of Triglyceride

We know that 1 mole of triglyceride contains **3 fatty acid chains** esterified to a glycerol backbone. Saponification with KOH requires **3 moles of KOH** per mole of triglyceride.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SV} &= \frac{\text{mg KOH required to saponify 1 g of oil}}{1 \text{ g oil}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging for the molecular weight of the oil:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of triglyceride in 1 g} &= \frac{\text{SV (in mg)}}{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000} \\
M_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} + M_{\text{glycerol}} - 3 \times M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $M_{\text{glycerol}} - 3 M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 92 - 54 = 38$, but conventionally the formula is stated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{195} + 38 \\
&= \frac{168300}{195} + 38 \\
&= 863.08 + 38 \\
&= 901.08 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b): Theoretical Biodiesel Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moles of triglyceride in 1000 kg} &= \frac{1000 \times 1000}{901.08} = 1109.74 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Moles of FAME produced (3 per triglyceride)} &= 3 \times 1109.74 = 3329.23 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Mass of FAME} &= 3329.23 \times 296 \text{ g} \\
&= 985452 \text{ g} = 985.45 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **theoretical biodiesel yield ≈ 98.5 wt%** of the WCO feed. Real industrial yields (after losses in washing, drying, and side reactions) are typically **85–95 wt%**.

#### Part (c): Methanol Requirement

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stoichiometric MeOH (3:1)} &= 3 \times 1109.74 = 3329.23 \text{ mol} \\
\text{Mass} &= 3329.23 \times 32.04 = 106.7 \text{ kg MeOH per 1000 kg WCO} \\
\text{Practical MeOH (6:1)} &= 2 \times 106.7 = 213.4 \text{ kg MeOH per 1000 kg WCO}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Excess methanol is recovered** via distillation and recycled, making the net consumption only slightly above stoichiometric.

### 3.2 Derivation: FFA Reduction by Acid Esterification

**Given:** 1 metric ton (1000 kg) of WCO with initial **FFA = 9%** (as oleic acid, MW = 282 g/mol). Target: reduce FFA to **<1%** using H₂SO₄-catalyzed esterification with methanol.

**Mass of FFA initially:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FFA,initial}} &= 0.09 \times 1000 = 90 \text{ kg} \\
n_{\text{FFA,initial}} &= \frac{90000}{282} = 319.15 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**FFA to be reacted (down to 1%):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FFA,final}} &= 0.01 \times 1000 = 10 \text{ kg} \\
n_{\text{FFA,final}} &= \frac{10000}{282} = 35.46 \text{ mol} \\
n_{\text{FFA,reacted}} &= 319.15 - 35.46 = 283.69 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Methanol required (stoichiometric, with 50% excess for equilibrium shift):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{MeOH,stoich}} &= 283.69 \text{ mol} \\
n_{\text{MeOH,actual}} &= 1.5 \times 283.69 = 425.54 \text{ mol} \\
m_{\text{MeOH}} &= 425.54 \times 32.04 = 13632 \text{ g} \approx 13.6 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**H₂SO₄ catalyst required (typically 2 wt% of oil):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} &= 0.02 \times 1000 = 20 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Feedstock Selection

| Feedstock | Typical FFA (%) | Pre-treatment Required? | Recommended Process Route | Cost (₹/kg, indicative) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Refined Soybean Oil | <0.5 | No | Direct base transesterification | 90–110 |
| Crude Palm Oil | 2–5 | Mild | Single-step with H₂SO₄ pretreatment or direct base | 70–90 |
| Waste Cooking Oil | 2–15 | **Yes** | Two-step (acid + base) | 30–50 |
| Yellow Grease | 5–15 | **Yes** | Two-step (acid + base) | 25–40 |
| Brown Grease | >15 | **Extensive** | Multi-stage acid + base + distillation | 15–25 |
| Tallow / Lard | 1–12 | Conditional | Base (if FFA <2) or two-step | 40–60 |
| Sewage Sludge Lipids | 10–30 | **Very extensive** | Supercritical methanol or enzymatic | 10–20 |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the term "Free Fatty Acid (FFA)" in the context of waste cooking oil. Why is the FFA value a critical parameter for selecting the biodiesel production route?**

**Model Answer:**
Free Fatty Acids (FFA) are the **non-esterified fatty acid molecules** present in a lipid feedstock, generated primarily by the hydrolysis of triglycerides during frying and storage. Mathematically,

$$
\text{FFA\,\%} = \frac{V_{\text{KOH}} \times N_{\text{KOH}} \times 28.2}{W_{\text{oil}}}
$$

expressed as **oleic acid equivalent**.

**Significance:**
- FFA values >2 wt% cause **saponification** with homogeneous base catalysts (NaOH, KOH), producing soap that emulsifies the product, consumes catalyst, and reduces FAME yield.
- Therefore, FFA dictates the **process route**: a single-step base process is used when FFA <2%, while a **two-step acid-base process** is mandatory for higher-FFA waste feedstocks.

*(3 marks: definition 1 + formula 0.5 + significance 1.5)*

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between Yellow Grease and Brown Grease as biodiesel feedstocks.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Yellow Grease | Brown Grease |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Restaurant grease traps, food industry | Sewer traps, municipal wastewater |
| FFA content | 5–15% | >15% (often 30–50%) |
| Purity | Relatively clean | Highly contaminated (debris, water, calcium soaps) |
| Pre-treatment | Standard two-step acid-base | Multi-stage, often supercritical methanol |
| Cost (₹/kg) | 25–40 | 15–25 |
| Process complexity | Moderate | Very high |

The key distinction is **purity and FFA content**, which directly determine the complexity and economics of biodiesel production.

*(3 marks: tabulated differences 2 + economic implication 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** [7 Marks] List and briefly describe the **major categories of waste oils and fats** used as biodiesel feedstocks. Highlight at least three key quality parameters used to characterize them.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat flow diagram, explain the **two-step acid-catalyzed esterification followed by base-catalyzed transesterification** process for converting high-FFA WCO into biodiesel. Write the balanced chemical equations for both steps.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Major Categories of Waste Oils and Fats

The waste lipid feedstocks are broadly classified into four categories:

1. **Vegetable Oil-Based Wastes:**
   - *Waste Cooking Oil (WCO)* — generated from household/restaurant deep-frying. FFA: 2–15%.
   - *Crude refining by-products* — soapstock, acid oil, spent bleaching earth lipids (FFA >20%).

2. **Animal Fat-Based Wastes:**
   - *Tallow* (bovine), *lard* (porcine), *poultry fat* — rendered from slaughterhouse and meat processing waste.

3. **Recovered Grease Wastes:**
   - *Yellow grease* — restaurant grease trap oil, FFA <15%.
   - *Brown grease* — sewer trap and grease interceptor waste, FFA >15%, highly contaminated.

4. **Industrial/Municipal Wastes:**
   - *Sewage sludge lipids*, *soap manufacturing by-products*.

**Three key characterization parameters:**
- **Free Fatty Acid (FFA) content** — determines the pre-treatment route.
- **Moisture content** — must be reduced to <0.1% before base catalysis.
- **Saponification value (SV)** — indicates the average molecular weight of triglycerides, used to calculate theoretical biodiesel yield.

#### Part (b) — Two-Step Process Flow

**Step 1: Acid-Catalyzed Esterification (Pre-treatment)**

$$
\text{RCOOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4,\, 55\text{–}65\,°C} \text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2: Base-Catalyzed Transesterification (Main Reaction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Triglyceride} &+ 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \\
&\xrightleftharpoons[\text{60 °C, 1 h}]{\text{NaOH (1 wt\%)}} 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process Flow Description:**
1. WCO from storage is heated to 105 °C in a dehydration tank to remove moisture.
2. The dried oil is filtered to remove solids.
3. In **Reactor-1** (acid esterification), H₂SO₄ (1–5 wt%) and methanol (10–20 wt%) are added at 55–65 °C for 1 h. FFA is converted to FAME. FFA drops below 1%.
4. The product is settled in **Settler-1** to remove water and excess methanol.
5. In **Reactor-2** (base transesterification), NaOH (0.5–1.5 wt%) dissolved in methanol (6:1 molar ratio) is added at 60 °C for 1 h. Triglycerides are converted to FAME and glycerol.
6. The mixture is settled in **Settler-2**. Crude glycerol (bottom) is separated. FAME (top) is water-washed, dried, and stored.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the **average molecular weight of a triglyceride** in terms of its saponification value. Given a WCO sample with **SV = 195 mg KOH/g**, calculate the molecular weight.

**(b)** [7 Marks] 1000 kg of WCO with **FFA = 8%** (as oleic acid) is to be processed in a two-step biodiesel plant. Calculate: (i) the mass of FFA to be removed by acid esterification (target FFA = 1%), and (ii) the stoichiometric mass of methanol required for the esterification step.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Molecular Weight of Triglyceride

Saponification of 1 mole of triglyceride consumes 3 moles of KOH:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{KOH} &\rightarrow 3\,\text{RCOOK} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \\
\text{Moles of triglyceride} &= \frac{\text{mass of KOH (mg)}}{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000} \\
1 \text{ g of oil} &= \frac{\text{SV}}{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000} \text{ mol of triglyceride} \\
\text{Molecular weight} &= \frac{1 \text{ g}}{\text{moles}} = \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} + 38
\end{aligned}
$$

The constant 38 accounts for the glycerol backbone ($M_{\text{glycerol}} - 3 M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$).

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{oil}} &= \frac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{195} + 38 \\
&= \frac{168300}{195} + 38 \\
&= 863.08 + 38 \\
&= 901.08 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — FFA Reduction and Methanol Calculation

**(i) Mass of FFA to be reacted:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{FFA,initial}} &= 0.08 \times 1000 = 80 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{FFA,final}} &= 0.01 \times 1000 = 10 \text{ kg} \\
m_{\text{FFA,reacted}} &= 80 - 10 = 70 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Stoichiometric methanol for esterification:**

Molecular weight of oleic acid = **282 g/mol**, methanol = **32.04 g/mol**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{FFA,reacted}} &= \frac{70000}{282} = 248.23 \text{ mol} \\
n_{\text{MeOH,stoich}} &= 248.23 \text{ mol (1:1 ratio)} \\
m_{\text{MeOH,stoich}} &= 248.23 \times 32.04 = 7954 \text{ g} \approx 7.95 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - In Part (a), students often forget the **+38 g/mol** correction term for the glycerol backbone. This costs **2 marks** routinely.
> - In Part (b), students frequently use the **total FFA (80 kg)** instead of the **FFA to be reacted (70 kg)**. The question specifically asks for the methanol needed to *reduce* FFA from 8% to 1%, not the total.
> - Always state the **molecular weight of oleic acid = 282 g/mol** in the answer; assuming an arbitrary value without justification will cost you marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 – Biodiesel Feedstocks:**

- **Biodiesel** = FAME (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) from transesterification of triglycerides.
- **Waste oils** are second-generation feedstocks: WCO, yellow grease, brown grease, tallow, lard, soapstock, sewage sludge lipids.
- **Critical FFA threshold = 2%** (or AV = 4 mg KOH/g) — above this, **two-step acid-base process** is mandatory.
- **Saponification Value (SV)** → directly gives the **average molecular weight** of triglycerides using $M_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{3 \times 56.1 \times 1000}{\text{SV}} + 38$.
- **Acid Value (AV)** → related to FFA by AV ≈ 2 × FFA% (for oleic-acid systems).
- **Iodine Value (IV)** → measures unsaturation; high IV = poor oxidation stability (biodiesel must meet **EN 14214** limit of **IV ≤ 120 g I₂/100g**).
- **Moisture must be <0.1 wt%** before base catalysis to prevent soap formation.
- **Stoichiometric MeOH : oil = 3 : 1**, but **6:1 to 12:1** is used industrially to drive equilibrium forward.
- **Theoretical biodiesel yield** is typically **95–98 wt%** of the oil; **practical yield is 85–95 wt%**.
- **WCO reduces feedstock cost by 50–70%** compared to refined vegetable oil, but adds **process complexity** (pre-treatment, two-step catalysis, soap handling).
- The **two-step process (acid esterification + base transesterification)** is the **industry gold standard** for high-FFA waste feedstocks.
- **Glycerol** is a valuable co-product (10 wt% of oil) used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and food industries.
- **Environmental bonus:** Using 1 ton of WCO for biodiesel prevents **~2.5 tons of CO₂** emissions versus fossil diesel, and diverts waste from drains and landfills.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Flow and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Flow and Equipment** refers to the standardized graphical documentation and engineering representation of a chemical process plant, encompassing the **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**, the **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**, and the catalogue of **process equipment symbols** governed by **ISA (International Society of Automation) S5.1** and **ISO 14617** standards.

In Process Systems Engineering (PSE), *Process Flow and Equipment* is the **bridge between conceptual process design and detailed engineering**. It transforms a process idea into:

1. A **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** — the macroscopic representation.
2. A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** — the mass/energy-balanced schematic showing every major unit, every stream, and basic control loops.
3. A **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** — the fully-detailed engineering drawing that includes pipe sizes, valves, instruments, control loops, and safety interlocks.

Each successive diagram carries **increasing levels of detail** while remaining a logical and consistent extension of the previous one.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Card vs. The Restaurant Blueprint"

Imagine you are designing a restaurant kitchen:

| Layer | Real-World Analogue | Engineering Document |
|---|---|---|
| **Idea** | "We will make pasta and sauce together." | Block Flow Diagram (BFD) |
| **Recipe** | Ingredient list, amounts, basic timings. | Process Flow Diagram (PFD) |
| **Blueprint** | Pipe sizes, valve locations, gas/electric lines, alarms. | Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** A PFD tells you *what* flows and *how much*. A P&ID tells you *how* it is physically controlled, measured, and safeguarded. Confusing these is one of the most common errors made by students in board examinations.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Constants and Metrics

The following standard metrics are used universally across KTU Process Systems Engineering examinations:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, kPa = 1.01325 \, bar$
- **Standard temperature:** $T_{std} = 25^{\circ}C = 298.15 \, K$
- **Water density (reference):** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, kg/m^3$

## 1.4 Stream Types — The Language of Process Flow

A PFD/P&ID differentiates streams using **line-weight and label conventions**:

| Line Type | Visual Convention | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Process Stream** | Heavy continuous line | Primary feed, product, and recycle |
| **Utility Stream** | Thin continuous line | Steam, cooling water, air, refrigerant |
| **Future Stream** | Dashed line | Provision for future connection |
| **Pneumatic / Signal Line** | Cross-hatched line | Instrument signal (3–15 psi) |
| **Electric / Data Line** | Dotted line | DCS/PLC communication |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchy of Process Documentation

The engineering documentation of a chemical plant follows a strict top-down hierarchy. Each layer adds engineering detail without contradicting the layer above it.

### Step 1 — Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- Shows the process as a **series of rectangular blocks**.
- Identifies only the **major processing steps** and the **principal input/output streams**.
- Carries **no numerical data** beyond overall material balance.
- Used in the *conceptual / feasibility* stage.

### Step 2 — Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
- The **most examined PFD element** in KTU.
- Contains **every major equipment item** (numbered), **every process stream** (numbered), **all energy duties**, and **basic control loops**.
- Includes a **process equipment list** and a **stream summary table**.
- Does **NOT** contain: pipe sizes, valve types, instrument tag numbers, or detailed instrumentation.

### Step 3 — Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)
- The **most detailed** representation.
- Includes **pipe diameters**, **line numbers**, **valve symbols**, **instrument bubbles**, **control loops**, and **safety interlocks**.
- Forms the **legal as-built document** for plant construction.

> [!TIP]
> **Why the hierarchy matters:** Each level is *consistent* but not *redundant*. Going from BFD → PFD → P&ID, the level of detail **increases monotonically**, but no information from the previous level is invalidated.

## 2.2 Equipment Classification (ISA / KTU Convention)

Process equipment in a chemical plant is classified into the following functional families:

1. **Vessels** — storage tanks, accumulator drums, receivers, reflux drums.
2. **Reactors** — CSTR, PFR, batch, fluidized-bed, fixed-bed.
3. **Heat Transfer Equipment** — shell-and-tube exchangers, plate exchangers, reboilers, condensers, furnaces.
4. **Mass Transfer Equipment** — distillation columns, absorbers, extractors, adsorbers.
5. **Fluid Handling Equipment** — centrifugal pumps, positive-displacement pumps, reciprocating compressors, blowers, fans.
6. **Solid Handling Equipment** — conveyors, crushers, screens, centrifuges, filters, dryers.
7. **Mixing Equipment** — agitators, static mixers, jet mixers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are *essential* for KTU board examination numericals on Process Flow and Equipment:

| Equation Number | Formula | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F-1** | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot h}{\eta}$ | Hydraulic power required by a pump | $W$ |
| **F-2** | $\Delta P_{f} = \dfrac{4 f L \rho v^{2}}{2 D}$ | Frictional pressure drop in a pipe (Fanning) | $Pa$ |
| **F-3** | $\Delta P_{f} = f_D \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Frictional pressure drop (Darcy–Weisbach) | $Pa$ |
| **F-4** | $Q = A_{pump} \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate relationship | $m^3/s$ |
| **F-5** | $A_{HX} = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Heat exchanger area (LMTD method) | $m^2$ |
| **F-6** | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | $K$ |
| **F-7** | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\eta} \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | Compressor work (adiabatic, polytropic form) | $W$ |
| **F-8** | $h_{available} = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g}$ | Pump head from differential pressure | $m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick mnemonic:** KTU numericals on Process Flow and Equipment typically revolve around three quantities — *pump power, pressure drop, or heat-exchanger area*. Memorize **F-1, F-3, and F-5** as your primary arsenal.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plant Construction:** P&IDs are legally binding documents used by pipe-fitters, electricians, and instrument technicians.
- **Hazard Analysis (HAZOP):** P&IDs form the basis of *Hazard and Operability* studies, where deviations from design intent are examined node-by-node.
- **Process Control:** Every control loop on a P&ID maps directly to a controller function block in the Distributed Control System (DCS).
- **Maintenance & Operation:** Equipment tags (e.g., **V-101, E-201, P-301**) uniquely identify each unit for inspection scheduling, spare-part ordering, and operator logs.
