# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pump Hydraulic Power (Equation F-1)

The pump power equation is the **single most tested derivation** in KTU PSE board papers on this topic.

### Starting Point — Mechanical Energy Balance

For an incompressible fluid pumped from a low-pressure suction point to a high-pressure discharge point, applying the steady-flow energy equation between sections 1 (suction) and 2 (discharge):

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 + h_{pump} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2 + h_{f}
$$

**Explanation of terms (line-by-line):**
- $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ = pressure head (pressure energy per unit weight).
- $\dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ = velocity head (kinetic energy per unit weight).
- $z$ = elevation head (potential energy per unit weight).
- $h_{pump}$ = head added by the pump.
- $h_f$ = head lost to friction in pipes and fittings.

### Step 1 — Simplification
Assume the pipe diameters at suction and discharge are equal, so $v_1 = v_2$ (velocity-head terms cancel). Let $\Delta z = z_2 - z_1$.

$$
h_{pump} = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \Delta z + h_f
$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the Total Differential Head
The quantity $\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho g} + \Delta z + h_f$ is the **total head** $h$ the pump must deliver. Hence:

$$
h = h_{pump}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Head to Power
Power is *force × velocity* (or equivalently *pressure × volumetric flow rate*):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ideal} &= Q \cdot \rho g \cdot h \\
&= \rho \, g \, Q \, h
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Account for Pump Efficiency
Real pumps are not ideal. The **brake power** required at the pump shaft must be higher to overcome internal mechanical and hydraulic losses:

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q \, h}{\eta}
$$

**Where:**
- $P_{brake}$ = power supplied to the pump motor (W)
- $\rho$ = fluid density ($kg/m^3$)
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate ($m^3/s$)
- $h$ = total dynamic head ($m$)
- $\eta$ = pump efficiency (dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$)

### Step 5 — Engineering Form for Board Examinations

$$
P_{brake} \, (kW) = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q \, h}{1000 \, \eta}
$$

where $Q$ is now in $m^3/s$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A centrifugal pump delivers **$Q = 50 \, m^3/h$** of water at a total head of **$h = 30 \, m$**. The pump efficiency is **$\eta = 70\%$**. Calculate:
1. The brake power of the pump in kW.
2. The electric power drawn by the motor if the motor efficiency is **$90\%$**.

### Solution — Step 1: Convert Flow Rate to SI Units

$$
Q = 50 \, \dfrac{m^3}{h} = \dfrac{50}{3600} \, \dfrac{m^3}{s} = 0.01389 \, m^3/s
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Apply Equation F-1

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, Q \, h}{\eta} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01389 \times 30}{0.70}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Numerator Evaluation

$$
\begin{aligned}
1000 \times 9.81 &= 9810 \\
9810 \times 0.01389 &= 136.26 \\
136.26 \times 30 &= 4087.8
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Divide by Efficiency

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{4087.8}{0.70} = 5839.7 \, W \approx 5.84 \, kW
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Motor Input Power

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{5.84}{0.90} = 6.49 \, kW
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Always state units at *every* intermediate step. KTU evaluators allocate significant marks for unit consistency, and a missing unit can cost you 0.5–1 mark even on a correct numerical.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Equipment and Their PFD/P&ID Symbols

| Equipment | Function in Process | Key Operating Parameter | PFD Label Convention | P&ID Tag Prefix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal Pump | Liquid transport | Head (m), Flow (m³/h) | P | P- |
| Reciprocating Compressor | Gas compression | Discharge Pressure (bar) | C | K- |
| Shell-and-Tube HEX | Heat exchange | Area (m²), Duty (kW) | E | E- |
| Distillation Column | Separation | Number of stages, Reflux Ratio | T | T- |
| CSTR / PFR Reactor | Reaction | Residence Time (s) | R | R- |
| Storage Tank | Inventory | Capacity (m³) | TK | V- |
| Control Valve | Flow regulation | Cv value | CV | FV / PV |
| Safety Relief Valve | Overpressure protection | Set Pressure (bar) | PSV | PSV |

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Log-Mean Temperature Difference (Equation F-6)

The LMTD is foundational to *all* heat-exchanger design calculations in this module.

### Geometric Foundation
Consider a counter-current heat exchanger with hot fluid entering at $T_{h,in}$ and leaving at $T_{h,out}$, and cold fluid entering at $T_{c,in}$ and leaving at $T_{c,out}$.

Define the terminal temperature differences:

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}
$$

### Mathematical Form
The heat-transfer rate for a differential element $dA$ along the exchanger length is:

$$
dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA
$$

Integrating from $A = 0$ to $A = A$, and using the constant-heat-capacity assumption, yields:

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}
$$

where

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)}
$$

### Limiting Case — When $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$
When the temperature-difference ratio is less than 2, the arithmetic mean approximation holds:

$$
\Delta T_{AM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2}{2}
$$

The two expressions converge when $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 \to 1$ (L'Hôpital limit).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — A Typical Chemical Process

The following Mermaid block-flow diagram represents a *typical reactive distillation process* with full instrumentation, as it would appear in a KTU examination. All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with Mermaid syntax rules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Feed Tank V-101] --> A2[Centrifugal Pump P-101]
    A2 --> A3[Preheater E-101]
    A3 --> A4[Reactor R-201]
    A4 --> A5[Flash Drum V-201]
    A5 --> A6[Distillation Column T-301]
    A6 --> A7[Condenser E-302]
    A7 --> A8[Reflux Drum V-302]
    A8 --> A6
    A6 --> A9[Reboiler E-301]
    A9 --> A6
    A6 --> B1[Product Cooler E-303]
    B1 --> B2[Product Storage V-401]
    A5 --> C1[Vent Stack]
    A2 -.-> I1[Pressure Indicator PI-101]
    A3 -.-> I2[Temperature Controller TIC-102]
    A4 -.-> I3[Temperature Indicator TI-201]
    A6 -.-> I4[Level Controller LIC-301]
    A8 -.-> I5[Flow Controller FC-302]
    B2 -.-> I6[Level Indicator LI-401]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Solid arrows (A1 → A2 → A3 → ...)** represent **process streams** carrying mass and energy between units.
- **Solid arrows looping back (A8 → A6)** represent **recycle streams** — in this case, the reflux returning to the column.
- **Dotted arrows (A2 -.-> I1)** represent **instrument signal lines** connecting field sensors to control-room indicators/controllers.
- **Equipment codes** (V-101, P-101, E-101, R-201, etc.) follow the **KTU / ISA tagging convention**:
  - First letter = equipment family (V = Vessel, P = Pump, E = Exchanger, R = Reactor, T = Tower).
  - Number = plant area and unit serial (101 = Area 1, Unit 1).

## 4.2 Instrumentation Loop Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor Field] --> T1[Transmitter]
    T1 --> C1[Controller DCS]
    C1 --> CV1[Control Valve Final Element]
    CV1 --> P1[Process]
    P1 --> S1
    C1 -.alarm.-> A1[Alarm Management]
    C1 -.data.-> H1[Historical Database]
```

### Explanation
- **Sensor → Transmitter:** The physical variable (temperature, pressure, flow, level) is converted into a standardized signal (typically 4–20 mA).
- **Controller (DCS):** Compares measured value with setpoint and computes a corrective action (PID algorithm).
- **Control Valve:** The *final control element* that adjusts the manipulated variable (e.g., steam flow, cooling water flow).
- **Process:** Returns to a new steady state, closing the loop.
- **Alarm / Historical Database:** Auxiliary blocks for safe operation and regulatory compliance.

## 4.3 PFD vs. P&ID — Side-by-Side Schematic Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PFD["PROCESS FLOW DIAGRAM PFD"]
        PFD1[Equipment Shapes Only] --> PFD2[Stream Numbers and Flow Rates]
        PFD2 --> PFD3[Basic Control Loops]
        PFD3 --> PFD4[Energy Duties kW]
        PFD4 --> PFD5[Equipment List and Stream Table]
    end
    subgraph PID["PIPING AND INSTRUMENTATION DIAGRAM P AND ID"]
        PID1[All PFD Content] --> PID2[Pipe Sizes and Line Numbers]
        PID2 --> PID3[Every Valve Tagged]
        PID3 --> PID4[All Instruments with Tag Numbers]
        PID4 --> PID5[Safety Relief Devices]
        PID5 --> PID6[Interlock and ESD Logic]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Heuristic:** When asked to "draw the PFD," *do not* include valve types, instrument tag numbers, or pipe diameters. When asked to "draw the P&ID," *do not* omit instrument bubbles, line numbers, or valve symbols.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and a Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). List at least four distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer:**
A PFD and a P&ID are the two principal graphical documents used in chemical-plant engineering. While the PFD presents the **process picture at the mass/energy-balance stage**, the P&ID presents the **engineering picture at the construction stage**.

| S.No | Feature | PFD | P&ID |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Purpose | Shows process topology | Shows engineering/control details |
| 2 | Stream Information | Flow rates, compositions | Pipe diameters, line numbers |
| 3 | Instrumentation | Basic control loops only | Every instrument tagged |
| 4 | Valves | Only major control valves | Every valve (isolation, safety) |
| 5 | Use | Process / HSE study basis | Construction / commissioning |

The PFD is the *parent document*; the P&ID is its *fully-detailed engineering child*.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: With the help of neat sketches, explain the ISA symbols for the following instruments:**
1. Pressure Indicator (PI)
2. Temperature Controller (TIC)
3. Flow Recorder (FR)

**Model Answer:**
ISA instrument symbols are drawn as **circles** with an **alphanumeric tag** inside. The **first letter** denotes the measured variable; the **subsequent letters** denote the function.

| Symbol Description | ISA Tag | Drawing Convention |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure Indicator | PI | Circle with "PI" inside, located in the field |
| Temperature Indicating Controller | TIC | Circle with horizontal line through middle (indicating shared display), "TIC" inside |
| Flow Recorder | FR | Circle with "FR" inside, dashed line from field |

**Sketches (Text Representation):**

- **PI:** A circle with the letters **PI** inside, connected to the process pipe by a dashed signal line.
- **TIC:** A circle divided by a horizontal line — the **upper half** is the *shared display* (shared between the indicator and the controller), the **lower half** contains the tag **TIC**.
- **FR:** A circle with **FR** inside; the letter **R** denotes the *recorder* function.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *first-letter convention* (P = Pressure, T = Temperature, F = Flow, L = Level) with *function letters* (I = Indicate, C = Control, R = Record, T = Transmit). Always read the tag **left-to-right** — the first letter is the *variable*, the rest are *functions*.

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** Draw a typical **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** for a **binary distillation column system** receiving a saturated-liquid feed. Show the column, condenser, reflux drum, reboiler, condenser pump, reflux pump, reboiler circulation pump, distillate cooler, and bottom-product cooler. Clearly label all equipment (using KTU tagging convention) and all streams (1 through 8). Include the basic control loops for reflux drum level, reboiler level, and column pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** is used to condense **5000 kg/h** of saturated steam at **2 bar** (latent heat = **2200 kJ/kg**) using cooling water that enters at **25°C** and leaves at **45°C**. The overall heat-transfer coefficient is **$U = 1500 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$**. Calculate the required heat-transfer area. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Equipment List with Tagging:**

| Tag | Equipment |
|---|---|
| T-301 | Distillation Column |
| E-301 | Reboiler (thermosiphon) |
| E-302 | Condenser (total) |
| V-302 | Reflux Drum |
| P-301 | Reflux Pump |
| P-302 | Bottom-Product Pump |
| P-303 | Distillate Pump |
| E-303 | Distillate Cooler |
| E-304 | Bottom-Product Cooler |

**Stream List:**

| Stream | Description |
|---|---|
| 1 | Feed (saturated liquid) |
| 2 | Overhead vapour to condenser |
| 3 | Condensed liquid to reflux drum |
| 4 | Reflux to column |
| 5 | Distillate product |
| 6 | Liquid from column bottom to reboiler |
| 7 | Reboiled vapour back to column |
| 8 | Bottom product |

**Basic Control Loops:**

| Loop Tag | Controlled Variable | Manipulated Variable |
|---|---|---|
| LIC-301 | Reflux drum level | Distillate flow |
| LIC-302 | Reboiler level | Bottom-product flow |
| PIC-301 | Column top pressure | Condenser cooling-water flow |
| FC-301 | Reflux flow | Reflux valve position |

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
                                 +---------------------+
Stream 1 --> [Feed] ----------->  |                     |
                                 |   Distillation      |
                                 |     Column          |----> Stream 7 (Vapour)
Stream 4 <-- [Reflux] <---------  |       T-301         |
                                 |                     |
                                 +---------------------+
                                          ^  |
                                          |  v
                                  +------------------+
Stream 2 --> [Vapour] ----------> |                  |
                                  |   Condenser      |
                                  |     E-302        |
                                  +------------------+
                                          |
                                          v
                                  +------------------+
                                  |  Reflux Drum     |
                                  |     V-302        |
                                  +------------------+
                                    |              |
                                    v              v
Stream 5 [Distillate]         [Pump P-301]   [Pump P-303]
                              to Column      to Cooler E-303
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m} = 5000 \, kg/h = 1.389 \, kg/s$
- $\lambda = 2200 \, kJ/kg = 2.2 \times 10^6 \, J/kg$
- $T_{c,in} = 25^{\circ}C$, $T_{c,out} = 45^{\circ}C$
- $U = 1500 \, W/(m^2 \cdot K)$
- Saturation temperature at 2 bar ≈ **$T_{sat} \approx 120.2^{\circ}C$** (from steam tables)

**Step 1 — Heat Duty Q:**
The latent heat released by steam:

$$
Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda = 1.389 \times 2.2 \times 10^6 = 3.056 \times 10^6 \, W
$$

**Step 2 — LMTD Calculation:**

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{sat} - T_{c,out} = 120.2 - 45 = 75.2^{\circ}C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{sat} - T_{c,in} = 120.2 - 25 = 95.2^{\circ}C
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{75.2 - 95.2}{\ln(75.2 / 95.2)} = \dfrac{-20.0}{\ln(0.7899)}
$$

$$
\ln(0.7899) = -0.2360
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{-20.0}{-0.2360} = 84.75^{\circ}C
$$

**Step 3 — Heat Exchanger Area (from Equation F-5):**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \dfrac{3.056 \times 10^6}{1500 \times 84.75}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{3.056 \times 10^6}{127125} = 24.04 \, m^2
$$

**Final Answer:** The required heat-transfer area is approximately **$A \approx 24.04 \, m^2$**.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **ISA standard symbols** for: (i) Globe Valve, (ii) Gate Valve, (iii) Ball Valve, (iv) Check Valve, (v) Safety Relief Valve, and (vi) Control Valve. For each, state **one typical application** in a chemical process plant. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **centrifugal pump** handles **$75 \, m^3/h$** of an aqueous solution (density **$\rho = 1100 \, kg/m^3$**) against a total head of **$40 \, m$**. The pump efficiency is **$75\%$**, and the motor efficiency is **$88\%$**. Calculate:
1. The hydraulic power delivered by the pump to the fluid.
2. The brake power required at the pump shaft.
3. The electric power drawn by the motor. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| S.No | Valve | ISA Symbol (Description) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Globe Valve** | Two triangles meeting at apex, with circular body and hand-wheel | Throttling service on steam, oil, or chemical lines |
| 2 | **Gate Valve** | Two triangles meeting at apex, with rectangular body and rising stem | Fully open/closed isolation service — minimum pressure drop |
| 3 | **Ball Valve** | Sphere with bore, single lever actuator | Quick shut-off in hydrocarbon and water service |
| 4 | **Check Valve** | Disc hinged against flow direction (with directional arrow) | Pump discharge to prevent backflow |
| 5 | **Safety Relief Valve** | Spring-loaded poppet with set-pressure spring | Overpressure protection on reactors, columns, and storage tanks |
| 6 | **Control Valve** | Globe-valve body with actuator (pneumatic diaphragm or motor) | Final control element in flow/level/temperature loops |

**Sketch Convention (Textual):**

- **Globe Valve:** Two opposing triangles ▶◀ joined at the apex, with a hand-wheel on top.
- **Gate Valve:** Similar to globe but with a **flat gate** that rises perpendicular to flow; symbol shows a square or rectangle with two triangles meeting at apex.
- **Ball Valve:** A circle (the ball) with a smaller circle inside (the bore); a lever on top denotes quarter-turn operation.
- **Check Valve:** A disc with a hinge line, slanted against the flow direction; an arrow shows the permitted flow direction.
- **Safety Relief Valve:** A spring symbol above a valve body; the spring is loaded to a *set pressure*.
- **Control Valve:** A globe-valve body with a **pneumatic diaphragm actuator** (semicircle) on top; connected by dashed signal line to the controller.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 75 \, m^3/h = 0.02083 \, m^3/s$
- $\rho = 1100 \, kg/m^3$
- $h = 40 \, m$
- $\eta_{pump} = 0.75$
- $\eta_{motor} = 0.88$
- $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Part 1 — Hydraulic Power Delivered to Fluid:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot h
$$

$$
P_{hyd} = 1100 \times 9.81 \times 0.02083 \times 40
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1100 \times 9.81 &= 10791 \\
10791 \times 0.02083 &= 224.81 \\
224.81 \times 40 &= 8992.3 \, W
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} = 8.99 \, kW}
$$

**Part 2 — Brake Power (from Equation F-1):**

$$
P_{brake} = \dfrac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{8.99}{0.75} = 11.99 \, kW
$$

**Part 3 — Motor Input Power:**

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{11.99}{0.88} = 13.62 \, kW
$$

**Final Answer:**
1. Hydraulic Power = **$8.99 \, kW$**
2. Brake Power = **$11.99 \, kW$**
3. Motor Input Power = **$13.62 \, kW$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit conversion errors** are the *single largest* cause of marks lost in pump-power problems. Always convert $m^3/h$ → $m^3/s$ **before** substituting into the equation. (Common mistake: writing $Q = 75$ without dividing by 3600.)
> - **Confusion between hydraulic power and brake power:** Hydraulic power is what the *fluid* receives; brake power is what the *pump shaft* delivers. Brake power is always greater by the factor $1/\eta$.
> - **Forgot to divide by motor efficiency** when asked for "electric power drawn." Many students stop at brake power and lose 1 mark.
> - **Missing the final unit (kW or W)** — always state units explicitly at the end.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] A **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** is the simplest representation — major blocks and principal streams only.
- [ ] A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** shows all major equipment, every stream with mass/energy balance, and basic control loops — but **no** pipe sizes, valve types, or detailed instrumentation.
- [ ] A **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is the most detailed diagram — it carries *every* pipe, valve, and instrument tag, and is the **legal document** for plant construction.
- [ ] ISA instrument symbols are **circles** with a tag; first letter = measured variable (P, T, F, L), subsequent letters = function (I, C, R, T).
- [ ] Pump power formula: $P_{brake} = \dfrac{\rho g Q h}{\eta}$ — convert **all** quantities to SI units before substituting.
- [ ] Heat exchanger area formula: $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$.
- [ ] $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — use LMTD, **not** arithmetic mean, unless $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 2$.
- [ ] Equipment tagging convention: **V** = Vessel, **P** = Pump, **E** = Exchanger, **R** = Reactor, **T** = Tower.
- [ ] Stream types use **line-weight conventions**: heavy line = process, thin line = utility, dashed = future/signal.
- [ ] Control loop architecture: Sensor → Transmitter → Controller (DCS) → Control Valve → Process → (back to Sensor).
- [ ] For centrifugal pumps, *brake power* > *hydraulic power*; the ratio is $1/\eta_{pump}$.
- [ ] Always include **units** at every intermediate step — KTU evaluators reward unit discipline.
- [ ] Motor input power = brake power / motor efficiency — do **not** omit this step when asked.
- [ ] A PFD does **not** show pipe diameters; a P&ID does.
- [ ] Recycle streams appear as **loops** in the process topology; the reflux line is the most common example.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Strategy:** For any 14-mark question on Process Flow and Equipment, structure your answer in *three blocks*: (1) **Diagram** (5–6 marks), (2) **Equipment/Stream Table with Tagging** (3–4 marks), (3) **Numerical Calculation with Units** (4–5 marks). This allocation matches KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation rubrics and maximizes your score.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrothermal Processing: Principles of Hydrothermal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hydrothermal Processing: Principles of Hydrothermal Processing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Hydrothermal Processing** is a heterogeneous reaction engineering technique wherein chemical transformations, crystallizations, or extractions are carried out in an aqueous medium at temperatures typically above the normal boiling point of water (**100 °C / 373.15 K**) and at autogenously generated pressures (above **1 atm / 0.101325 MPa**) within a sealed pressure vessel known as an **autoclave** or **hydrothermal reactor**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** > Hydrothermal processing refers to any homogeneous or heterogeneous chemical reaction occurring in a closed system containing an aqueous solvent at a temperature higher than its boiling point, thereby producing a pressure higher than atmospheric. The pressure is *autogenous* — meaning it is generated spontaneously by the system itself rather than imposed externally through a compressor. The term *hydro* (water) + *thermal* (heat) was first introduced by the German geologist **Friedrich Wöhler's** group studying mineral formation in the 19th century, and later formalized by mineralogists studying natural **hydrothermal vents** on ocean floors. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Pressure Cooker in Your Kitchen** > Imagine boiling potatoes in an open pan at 100 °C. They take a long time to soften because water cannot exceed 100 °C at atmospheric pressure — it just escapes as steam. Now, place those potatoes inside a **pressure cooker**. The sealed lid traps the steam, the pressure climbs above 1 atm, and the boiling point of water rises to ~115–120 °C. The potatoes now cook *faster and more thoroughly* because: > 1. The water stays **liquid** even at high temperatures. > 2. The **dielectric constant** drops, allowing better dissolution of normally water-insoluble materials. > 3. Reaction rates roughly **double per 10 °C** rise (Arrhenius behavior). A **hydrothermal reactor is essentially a giant, precision-engineered pressure cooker**, allowing chemists and process engineers to perform reactions in liquid water at temperatures that would normally convert water into steam at atmospheric pressure. ## 1.3 The Role of Water as a Reaction Medium Water is not merely a passive solvent — under hydrothermal conditions, it becomes a **highly reactive medium**: | Property | Cold Water (25 °C, 1 atm) | Subcritical Water (250 °C, 5 MPa) | Supercritical Water (400 °C, 25 MPa) | |---|---|---|---| | Density (kg/m³) | 997 | 800 | 166 | | Dielectric Constant | 78.5 | 27 | 5–10 | | Viscosity (µPa·s) | 890 | 107 | 30 | | Ion Product (pKw) | 14.0 | 11.3 | ~19 | | Solvent Character | Polar | Moderately Polar | Non-Polar | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters (KTU Board Insight):** > The dielectric constant (ε) governs water's ability to dissolve ionic compounds. At supercritical conditions, water behaves like an organic solvent — it can dissolve non-polar substances like oils and tars. This is the *fundamental principle* behind **supercritical water oxidation (SCWO)** of toxic wastes and **hydrothermal liquefaction** of biomass. ## 1.4 Classification of Hydrothermal Processes Hydrothermal processing is broadly classified based on the **operating temperature and pressure regime** relative to water's critical point (**T_c = 374 °C**, **P_c = 22.1 MPa**): - **Subcritical Hydrothermal Processing**: T = 100–374 °C, P = 1–22 MPa. Water remains a liquid but with reduced density and dielectric constant. - **Supercritical Hydrothermal Processing**: T > 374 °C, P > 22.1 MPa. Single-phase fluid with tunable solvent properties. - **Near-Critical Hydrothermal Processing**: 350–400 °C, 22–25 MPa. Region of extreme property sensitivity. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** > The syllabus emphasizes the *principles* — phase behavior, thermodynamics of water under pressure, solubility, and reactor design considerations. Application-oriented topics like SCWO are introduced but not deeply. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Physics: Why Pressure Matters Water's phase diagram dictates the entire foundation of hydrothermal processing. At ambient pressure (1 atm), water boils at **100 °C**. To reach higher temperatures in the **liquid phase**, one must increase pressure along the **saturation (vapor–liquid coexistence) curve**. ### 2.1.1 The Saturation Pressure Curve Following the **liquid-vapor equilibrium line**, as temperature rises: - At **180 °C**, saturation pressure ≈ **1.0 MPa** - At **250 °C**, saturation pressure ≈ **4.0 MPa** - At **300 °C**, saturation pressure ≈ **8.6 MPa** - At **374.15 °C**, saturation pressure reaches **22.064 MPa** (the critical point) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** A hydrothermal reactor operating at 250 °C must be designed to withstand at least 4 MPa internal pressure — usually with a **safety factor of 2.5–3.0**, demanding wall thickness calculations per **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section VIII**. ## 2.2 Thermodynamic Framework ### 2.2.1 Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Saturation Curve) The fundamental equation governing the P–T relationship of water along the saturation curve: $$\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta v_{vap}}$$ Where: - $dP^{sat}/dT$ = Rate of change of saturation pressure with temperature (Pa/K) - $\Delta H_{vap}$ = Enthalpy of vaporization (J/mol) - $T$ = Absolute temperature (K) - $\Delta v_{vap} = v_g - v_l$ = Difference in molar volumes of vapor and liquid phases (m³/mol) For an **idealized closed system** where $v_g \gg v_l$ and the vapor obeys the ideal gas law ($v_g = RT/P$), simplification yields the **integrated Clausius-Clapeyron (Antoine-type) form**: $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ ### 2.2.2 Dielectric Constant Behavior The dielectric constant of water decreases dramatically with temperature and (to a lesser extent) with pressure: $$\varepsilon(T, P) = \varepsilon_0 - a(T - 298.15)$$ Empirically, the **Uematsu-Franck equation** provides accurate values. A simplified polynomial fit used in many KTU textbooks: $$\varepsilon(T) \approx 78.5 \cdot \exp\left[-4.5 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15)\right]$$ Where $T$ is in Kelvin. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** > A lower dielectric constant means water can no longer stabilize ions effectively. Therefore, **inorganic salts precipitate** while **organic compounds dissolve**. This single phenomenon enables selective dissolution and crystallization. ### 2.2.3 Arrhenius Kinetics Under Hydrothermal Conditions Reaction rate enhancement follows the classical Arrhenius law: $$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$ For hydrothermal reactions in liquid water: - Rate constants roughly **double for every 10 K** rise in temperature. - A reaction at 300 °C may proceed **~10⁴ to 10⁶ times faster** than at 25 °C. ### 2.2.4 Solubility Product Under Pressure Solubility of sparingly soluble salts is pressure-dependent through the **volumetric effect**: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln K_{sp}}{\partial P}\right)_T = -\frac{\Delta V_{rxn}}{RT}$$ Where $\Delta V_{rxn}$ is the volume change of the dissolution reaction. **Negative $\Delta V_{rxn}$** (typical for ionic dissolution) → solubility **increases** with pressure. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Variables / Meaning | Units / Typical Values | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $dP^{sat}/dT = \Delta H_{vap}/(T \Delta v_{vap})$ | Clausius-Clapeyron differential form | Pa/K | Predicting autoclave pressure | | 2 | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Integrated C-C equation | Dimensionless ratio | Estimating saturation P | | 3 | $\varepsilon(T) = 78.5 \exp[-4.5 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15)]$ | Dielectric constant vs T | Dimensionless | Predicting solvent behavior | | 4 | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate law | k in s⁻¹, E_a in J/mol | Estimating reaction time | | 5 | $RT_c = (8/27) \cdot P_c \cdot V_{m,c}$ | Critical point relation | J/mol | Validating water data | | 6 | $P_c = 22.064$ MPa, $T_c = 647.096$ K | Water critical constants | Reference | Boundary classification | | 7 | $\rho_l(T, P)$ from steam tables | Water density | kg/m³ | Reactor volumetric design | | 8 | $K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-]$ | Ion product of water | mol²/L²; pKw = 14 → ~11 at 250 °C | Acid-base equilibria | | 9 | $t_{wall} = (P \cdot D)/(2 \sigma_{allow} - 1.2 P)$ | Thin-walled cylinder formula | m | Autoclave wall design | | 10 | $\Delta G_{rxn}(T, P) = \Delta G^\circ - RT \ln Q$ | Gibbs free energy | J/mol | Reaction spontaneity | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Note:** When solving hydrothermal problems in KTU exams, always **explicitly state the critical point constants** (T_c = 647 K, P_c = 22.1 MPa). Examiners reward awareness of the phase regime (sub- vs supercritical). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Area | Operating Regime | Industrial Example | |---|---|---| | Zeolite Synthesis | Subcritical (100–200 °C, autogenous P) | Detergent-grade zeolite A production | | Single Crystal Growth | Subcritical (300–400 °C, 10–100 MPa) | Quartz crystal synthesis | | SCWO of Hazardous Waste | Supercritical (400–600 °C, 25–30 MPa) | Military chemical agent destruction | | Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC) | Subcritical (180–250 °C, 1–5 MPa) | Bio-coal from wet biomass | | Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) | Sub-/near-critical (250–374 °C, 5–22 MPa) | Bio-crude oil from algae | | Nanomaterial Synthesis | Subcritical (150–250 °C) | TiO₂, ZnO, CeO₂ nanoparticles | | Mineral Leaching | Subcritical (200–300 °C) | Alumina from bauxite | ## 2.5 Reactor Design Considerations A **hydrothermal reactor (autoclave)** must satisfy: 1. **Pressure containment** — Thick walls, often with **liner tubes** (Teflon, quartz, or Inconel) to resist corrosion. 2. **Temperature uniformity** — External heating jacket with thermocouple wells. 3. **Safety relief** — **Rupture discs** calibrated at 1.1× design pressure. 4. **Stirring** (optional) — Magnetic or mechanical agitation for homogeneous mixing. 5. **Material selection** — SS 316L for T < 300 °C; **Inconel 625 / Haynes 230** for supercritical applications. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation from Thermodynamic Equilibrium We start from the **fundamental criterion of phase equilibrium**: the molar Gibbs free energy (chemical potential) of liquid water equals that of vapor water at the saturation pressure and temperature. ### Step 1: Statement of Equilibrium At the liquid-vapor saturation line: $$\mu_l(T, P^{sat}) = \mu_v(T, P^{sat})$$ ### Step 2: Total Differential Moving along the coexistence curve, any differential change must preserve equilibrium: $$d\mu_l = d\mu_v$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Fundamental Thermodynamic Relation For a pure phase: $d\mu = -S_m \, dT + V_m \, dP$ where $S_m$ is molar entropy and $V_m$ is molar volume. Therefore: $$-S_l \, dT + V_l \, dP = -S_v \, dT + V_v \, dP$$ ### Step 4: Rearrange to Group Terms $$(V_v - V_l) \, dP = (S_v - S_l) \, dT$$ $$\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute the Entropy of Vaporization At equilibrium, the entropy change upon vaporization is: $$\Delta S_{vap} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$$ (derived from $dG = 0$ at constant T, P, and from $\Delta G_{vap} = \Delta H_{vap} - T \Delta S_{vap} = 0$) ### Step 6: Final Expression $$\boxed{\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{vap}}}$$ This is the **Clausius-Clapeyron equation** — the cornerstone of hydrothermal pressure-temperature predictions. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Estimating Autoclave Pressure at 250 °C **Problem:** Estimate the saturation pressure of water at **T = 250 °C = 523.15 K**, given that $P^{sat} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$ at T = 100 °C = 373.15 K, and $\Delta H_{vap} \approx 40.7$ kJ/mol (assumed constant over this range). ### Step 1: Identify the Known and Unknown Known: - $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ - $T_1 = 373.15$ K - $T_2 = 523.15$ K - $\Delta H_{vap} = 40.7 \times 10^3$ J/mol - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) Unknown: $P_2^{sat}$ ### Step 2: Apply the Integrated Clausius-Clapeyron Equation $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Substitute Numerical Values $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{40.7 \times 10^3}{8.314} \left(\frac{1}{523.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right)$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the Bracket $$\frac{1}{523.15} - \frac{1}{373.15} = 1.9116 \times 10^{-3} - 2.6799 \times 10^{-3} = -7.6830 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ ### Step 5: Continue the Calculation $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -4894.4 \times (-7.6830 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.762$$ ### Step 6: Solve for P₂ $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot e^{3.762} = 1.013 \text{ bar} \times 43.04 = 43.60 \text{ bar} = 4.36 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Step 7: Compare with Experimental Value The experimental saturation pressure of water at 250 °C is **3.976 MPa (39.76 bar)**. Our estimate overshoots by ~10% — a reasonable error given the assumption of constant $\Delta H_{vap}$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Judgment:** For preliminary design, the integrated Clausius-Clapeyron is acceptable. For final pressure vessel design, always consult the **ASME Steam Tables** or **IAPWS-IF97 formulation**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rate Enhancement from 25 °C to 300 °C **Problem:** A hydrolysis reaction has activation energy $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol. Estimate how many times faster it proceeds at 300 °C compared to 25 °C. ### Step 1: Apply Arrhenius Ratio $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Values - $T_1 = 298.15$ K - $T_2 = 573.15$ K - $E_a = 80{,}000$ J/mol - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \exp\left[\frac{80{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{573.15}\right)\right]$$ ### Step 3: Evaluate the Temperature Reciprocal Difference $$\frac{1}{298.15} - \frac{1}{573.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} - 1.745 \times 10^{-3} = 1.609 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ ### Step 4: Compute the Exponent $$\frac{80{,}000}{8.314} \times 1.609 \times 10^{-3} = 9622.5 \times 1.609 \times 10^{-3} = 15.48$$ ### Step 5: Final Result $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{15.48} \approx 5.34 \times 10^6$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The reaction at 300 °C is **over 5 million times faster** than at 25 °C. This astronomical rate enhancement is precisely why hydrothermal processing is industrially attractive — short residence times (minutes to hours) replace days of conventional synthesis. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property Comparison Across Regimes | Property / Regime | Ambient (25 °C) | Subcritical (250 °C) | Near-Critical (370 °C) | Supercritical (450 °C) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Phase | Liquid | Liquid | Single phase | Single fluid | | Density (kg/m³) | 997 | 800 | 550 | 110 | | Dielectric Constant | 78.5 | 27.5 | 13 | 2.5 | | Viscosity (µPa·s) | 890 | 107 | 47 | 31 | | pKw | 14.0 | 11.3 | 12.0 | 19.5 | | Diffusivity (m²/s) | 2.3 × 10⁻⁹ | 7 × 10⁻⁹ | 1.3 × 10⁻⁸ | 7 × 10⁻⁸ | | Dissolves | Salts, Ions | Salts + some organics | Broad spectrum | Non-polar organics | | Use Case | Synthesis at RT | Zeolites, HC, HTL | Biomass processing | SCWO, gasification | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Question:** A hydrothermal reactor contains 50% water (by volume) and is heated to 320 °C. Using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation, estimate the pressure developed. Take $\Delta H_{vap} = 36.0$ kJ/mol. **Solution:** Step 1 — Reference state: $T_1 = 373.15$ K, $P_1 = 1.013$ bar Step 2 — Target state: $T_2 = 593.15$ K Step 3 — Apply integrated C-C: $$\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\frac{36{,}000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{593.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right)$$ $$= -4330.3 \times (-1.090 \times 10^{-3}) = 4.720$$ Step 4 — Solve: $$P_2 = 1.013 \times e^{4.720} = 1.013 \times 112.4 = 113.9 \text{ bar} = 11.39 \text{ MPa}$$ Step 5 — Interpretation: The reactor must withstand ~11.4 MPa, demanding safety-rated design (with 2.5× safety factor → ~28.5 MPa rated vessel). > [!NOTE] > **Practical Insight:** Real pressure may differ from this estimate due to: > - Non-ideal gas behavior near saturation > - Presence of dissolved solutes lowering vapor pressure (Raoult's law) > - Headspace gas composition in the autoclave # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phase Diagram of Water — Hydrothermal Regime ```mermaid graph TB A[Water Phase Diagram] --> B[Solid Ice Region] A --> C[Liquid Water Region] A --> D[Vapor Steam Region] A --> E[Supercritical Fluid Region] C --> F[Subcritical Hydrothermal Zone

Teflon Quartz or Titanium] R4[Magnetic Stirrer Assembly] R5[Thermocouple and Pressure Sensor Ports] end subgraph "Control and Safety" C1[PID Temperature Controller] C2[Pressure Transducer] C3[Rupture Disc Set at 1.1x Design P] C4[Pressure Relief Valve] C5[Data Acquisition and Logging System] end subgraph "Product Recovery" P1[Cooling Heat Exchanger] P2[Gas Vent Separator] P3[Filter Centrifuge] P4[Drying Oven Vacuum] P5[Final Product Storage] end F1 --> F4 F2 --> F4 F3 --> F4 F4 --> R1 R2 --> R1 R3 --> R1 R4 --> R1 R5 --> R1 R1 --> C1 R1 --> C2 C1 --> C5 C2 --> C5 C3 --> R1 C4 --> R1 R1 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 style R1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff style C3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C4 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P5 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow of a Typical Hydrothermal Synthesis ```mermaid graph LR A[Raw Precursors

Envelope A uses glassware; Envelope B uses Teflon-lined SS 316L; Envelope C–D requires thick-walled Inconel reactors with rupture discs; Envelope E requires ASME Section VIII Class I pressure vessels with continuous monitoring. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define hydrothermal processing. What is the significance of the critical point of water (T_c = 647.1 K, P_c = 22.064 MPa) in this context?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Hydrothermal processing is a chemical synthesis/processing technique in which reactions are carried out in aqueous media at temperatures above 100 °C and at pressures greater than atmospheric, typically inside a sealed pressure vessel called an autoclave. The pressure is *autogenous*, i.e., generated by the system itself. **Significance of critical point (2 marks):** The critical point of water demarcates the boundary between subcritical and supercritical hydrothermal regimes. Below T_c, liquid and vapor phases coexist distinctly. Above T_c, water exists as a single supercritical fluid with **continuously tunable solvent properties** — its dielectric constant drops from 78.5 to a few units, enabling dissolution of non-polar organics. This property switch is the operational basis of **supercritical water oxidation (SCWO)** of hazardous wastes and **hydrothermal liquefaction** of biomass. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any four factors that influence the rate and outcome of a hydrothermal reaction.** **Model Answer (4 factors × 0.75 = 3 marks):** 1. **Temperature** — Higher temperature exponentially accelerates reaction rates per the Arrhenius equation. A 100 K rise can speed reactions by 10⁴–10⁶ times. 2. **Pressure** — Affects solvent density, dielectric constant, and solubility of reactants via volumetric effects. 3. **pH / Mineralizer Concentration** — Alkaline mineralizers (NaOH, KOH) accelerate nucleation; acid mineralizers (HCl) promote dissolution. 4. **Reaction Time / Residence Time** — Controls crystallinity, particle size, and phase purity. 5. *(Bonus)* Fill ratio of the autoclave — governs autogenous pressure. 6. *(Bonus)* Type and concentration of precursors. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the Clausius-Clapeyron equation from thermodynamic equilibrium and use it to estimate the saturation pressure of water at 200 °C. Given: $\Delta H_{vap} = 40.7$ kJ/mol (assumed constant), $P^{sat} = 1.013$ bar at 100 °C.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Statement of Phase Equilibrium (1 mark)** At the liquid-vapor saturation line, the molar Gibbs free energy (chemical potential) of the liquid equals that of the vapor: $$\mu_l(T, P^{sat}) = \mu_v(T, P^{sat})$$ **Step 2 — Total Differential (1 mark)** Moving along the saturation curve, any differential change must preserve equilibrium: $$d\mu_l = d\mu_v$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Fundamental Relation (1 mark)** For a pure phase: $d\mu = -S_m \, dT + V_m \, dP$ $$-S_l \, dT + V_l \, dP = -S_v \, dT + V_v \, dP$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange (1 mark)** $$(V_v - V_l) \, dP = (S_v - S_l) \, dT \implies \frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}}$$ **Step 5 — Use $\Delta S_{vap} = \Delta H_{vap}/T$ (1 mark)** Since $\Delta G_{vap} = \Delta H_{vap} - T \Delta S_{vap} = 0$ at equilibrium: $$\boxed{\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{vap}}}$$ **Step 6 — Integrated Form and Numerical Computation (2 marks)** $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ Substituting $T_1 = 373.15$ K, $T_2 = 473.15$ K: $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{1.013}\right) = -\frac{40{,}700}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{473.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right) = -4894.4 \times (-5.466 \times 10^{-4}) = 2.675$$ $$P_2 = 1.013 \times e^{2.675} = 1.013 \times 14.51 = 14.70 \text{ bar} = 1.47 \text{ MPa}$$ **Comparison:** Experimental value at 200 °C = **1.554 MPa**. Our estimate is within ~5%, acceptable for design. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the effect of temperature and pressure on the dielectric constant of water and explain its significance in selecting between sub- and supercritical hydrothermal processing routes.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Qualitative Behavior (2 marks)** The dielectric constant (ε) of water decreases sharply with increasing temperature and (less steeply) with increasing pressure. At 25 °C, ε = **78.5** (highly polar). At 250 °C, ε ≈ **27.5**. At 400 °C, 25 MPa, ε ≈ **5–10**. The relationship can be approximated by: $$\varepsilon(T) \approx 78.5 \exp[-4.5 \times 10^{-3}(T - 298.15)]$$ **Step 2 — Physical Significance (2 marks)** A high dielectric constant favors solvation of ionic species. A low dielectric constant favors solvation of non-polar organics. Therefore: - **Subcritical water (ε = 20–40)**: Still dissolves many salts; ionic reactions dominate; suitable for **zeolite synthesis**, **HTL** (where ionic intermediates form). - **Supercritical water (ε = 2–10)**: Behaves like an organic solvent; non-polar compounds (oils, tars, aromatics) become miscible; suitable for **SCWO** (organic destruction), **supercritical fluid extraction**. **Step 3 — Process Selection (2 marks)** | Goal | Regime | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | Dissolve salt + grow crystal | Subcritical | High ε stabilizes ionic precursors | | Oxidize organic waste | Supercritical | Low ε dissolves organics; O₂ also miscible | | Convert biomass to oil | Sub- near-critical | Moderate ε permits ionic depolymerization and oily phase separation | **Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark)** The dielectric constant acts as the *master variable* in hydrothermal processing — by tuning T and P, engineers can switch water's solvent character between polar and non-polar, enabling a vast range of chemical transformations in a single equipment class. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **A hydrothermal autoclave is operated at 300 °C with a fill ratio (liquid volume / total volume) of 60%. Estimate the pressure developed inside the vessel using the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. Comment on the safety considerations.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Estimate Saturation Pressure (2 marks)** Using reference state: $P_1 = 1.013$ bar at $T_1 = 373.15$ K; $\Delta H_{vap} = 36.5$ kJ/mol (average over 100–300 °C range). $$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{1.013}\right) = -\frac{36{,}500}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{573.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right)$$ $$= -4390.7 \times (-1.030 \times 10^{-3}) = 4.522$$ $$P_2 = 1.013 \times e^{4.522} = 1.013 \times 92.07 = 93.27 \text{ bar} = 9.33 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2 — Effect of Fill Ratio (2 marks)** Fill ratio affects the vapor headspace and hence the final pressure. At 60% fill, a substantial vapor space exists above the liquid; the actual pressure is *close to* the saturation pressure because the vapor headspace is large enough to maintain saturation conditions. At fill ratios > 90%, liquid expansion can drive pressures far beyond saturation values — a **critical safety hazard**. **Step 3 — Safety Considerations (3 marks)** - **Material of construction**: SS 316L for T < 300 °C; **Inconel 625** for higher T. - **Pressure rating**: Design vessel for ≥ **2.5×** estimated max pressure → **≥ 23.3 MPa**. - **Rupture disc**: Set to relieve at **1.1× design pressure** (i.e., ~25.6 MPa). - **Fill ratio limit**: **Never exceed 80%** for safety; ideal is 50–70%. - **Heating rate**: Slow ramp (≤ 5 °C/min) to avoid thermal shock. - **Cooling**: Allow natural cooling before opening — never attempt forced cooling with cold water on hot vessels (thermal shock). #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Compare subcritical and supercritical hydrothermal processing in terms of (i) operating conditions, (ii) solvent properties, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) engineering challenges.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** | Aspect | Subcritical | Supercritical | |---|---|---| | (i) Operating Conditions (2 marks) | T = 100–374 °C, P = 1–22 MPa | T > 374 °C, P > 22.1 MPa | | (ii) Solvent Properties (2 marks) | Liquid water; ε ≈ 20–78; ion product ~10⁻¹¹ to 10⁻¹²; high ionic reaction propensity | Single-phase fluid; ε ≈ 2–10; non-polar organics miscible; oxidizing environment | | (iii) Applications (1.5 marks) | Zeolites, HTC, HTL, single-crystal growth, nanomaterial synthesis | SCWO (hazardous waste), SWG (H₂), supercritical fluid extraction | | (iv) Engineering Challenges (1.5 marks) | Corrosion by alkaline mineralizers; pressure vessel cost; long batch times | Extreme T/P demanding exotic alloys (Inconel, Haynes); salt precipitation plugging lines; high capital cost | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Critical):** > 1. **Forgetting units** — Always convert bar to MPa and °C to K consistently. Mixing units costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Not stating assumptions** — The integrated Clausius-Clapeyron assumes constant $\Delta H_{vap}$ and ideal gas vapor. Explicitly mention these. > 3. **Missing safety factor** — Pressure vessel design questions require the **2.5× safety factor**. Omission = −1 mark. > 4. **Confusing critical constants** — T_c = 647.1 K = 374 °C; P_c = 22.064 MPa. Confusing with K and °C conversion is a common error. > 5. **No sketch in Part B** — Even schematic process diagrams earn easy marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Hydrothermal Processing** ### Core Concepts - [x] **Definition** — Hydrothermal processing = reactions in aqueous media at T > 100 °C, P > 1 atm, in sealed autoclaves. - [x] **Critical Point of Water** — T_c = **647.1 K (374 °C)**, P_c = **22.064 MPa**. Memorize these exact values. - [x] **Phase Regimes** — Subcritical (liquid water, polar) vs Supercritical (single fluid, tunable polarity). ### Key Equations (Must Memorize) - [x] **Clausius-Clapeyron**: $dP^{sat}/dT = \Delta H_{vap} / (T \Delta V_{vap})$ - [x] **Integrated C-C**: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ - [x] **Arrhenius**: $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ - [x] **Dielectric trend**: $\varepsilon$ decreases sharply with T. ### Property Trends to Remember - [x] ↑ T → ↓ dielectric constant → ↑ organic solubility, ↓ salt solubility - [x] ↑ T → ↓ density (in subcritical), abrupt change near critical - [x] ↑ T → ↑ reaction rate (exponentially) - [x] ↑ P → ↑ solubility for reactions with negative ΔV ### Engineering Practice - [x] Fill ratio ≤ **80%** (ideal: 50–70%) - [x] Pressure vessel safety factor ≥ **2.5×** - [x] Rupture disc setting = **1.1× design pressure** - [x] Material: **SS 316L** (T < 300 °C); **Inconel 625** (T > 300 °C) - [x] Mineralizers: NaOH/KOH (alkaline, for zeolites); HCl/HNO₃ (acid, for leaching) ### Industrial Applications Snapshot - [x] **Zeolites** (detergent industry) — subcritical, alkaline - [x] **TiO₂/ZnO nanoparticles** — subcritical - [x] **SCWO** — military/industrial waste destruction - [x] **HTC** — wet biomass → bio-coal (180–250 °C) - [x] **HTL** — wet biomass → bio-crude (250–350 °C) ### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!) - [x] ❌ Forgetting to convert °C to K in exponent calculations - [x] ❌ Using atmospheric boiling point instead of saturation pressure for given T - [x] ❌ Assuming water is incompressible — it is highly compressible above 200 °C - [x] ❌ Ignoring corrosion in autoclave material selection - [x] ❌ Confusing hydrothermal with *hydro* (hydrogenation) — they are entirely different processes. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** Questions on hydrothermal processing often interweave **thermodynamics (C-C equation)** with **reactor safety (pressure vessel design)**. Practice solving both aspects in a single 14-mark problem to maximize score.

topics.title: Supercritical Water and Its Properties
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Supercritical Water and Its Properties**

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Supercritical Water (SCW)** is defined as water existing at a temperature and pressure **above its thermodynamic critical point**, where the distinct liquid and vapor phases cease to exist as separate entities. For pure water, the critical point occurs at:

$$T_c = 647.096 \text{ K} \quad (\approx 373.95 \text{ °C})$$

$$P_c = 22.064 \text{ MPa} \quad (\approx 217.75 \text{ atm})$$

$$v_c = 0.003106 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Above this point, water exists as a **single supercritical fluid phase** that exhibits hybrid transport and solvating properties intermediate between those of a liquid and a gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In Process Systems Engineering, supercritical fluids (SCFs) — and SCW in particular — are studied because they allow *tunable physico-chemical properties* by simple manipulation of pressure and temperature. This is exploited in supercritical reactors, separation trains, and waste-destruction processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy classroom (liquid water — molecules packed close, sluggish movement) and an empty playground (steam — molecules far apart, darting freely). Now imagine a **grand concert venue**: a huge open space (low density, gas-like diffusivity), but with so many people packed inside that they still bump shoulders constantly (high interaction energy, liquid-like solvating power). That hybrid state is **supercritical water**.

- **No boiling, no condensation** — there is no phase boundary to cross.
- **Tunable density:** Squeeze the fluid (raise pressure) → behaves more like a liquid. Heat it (raise temperature) → behaves more like a gas.
- **Universal solvent at high pressure, non-solvent at low pressure** — a property used in green separation processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Anchor:** Students often confuse "supercritical" with "plasma" or "very hot steam." SCW is **not plasma** (no ionization of significance) and **not steam** (steam is a gas; SCW is a single dense supercritical phase).

### 1.3 Why SCW Matters in Process Systems Engineering

| Driving Force | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Tunable density (0.1–0.9 g/cm³) | Continuous solvent-power adjustment in a reactor |
| Low viscosity (~0.1× liquid) | Excellent mass-transfer rates |
| High diffusivity (~10× liquid) | Faster reaction kinetics |
| Very low dielectric constant (2–25) | Dissolves non-polar organics — replaces organic solvents |
| High ion product at near-critical T | Acts as an acid/base catalyst (e.g., biomass hydrolysis) |
| Single phase → no mass-transfer resistance | Homogeneous reaction environment |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Critical Point — Thermodynamic Foundation

The critical point is mathematically defined as the inflection point of the **coexistence curve** on a P–V diagram, which gives the defining conditions:

$$\left( \frac{\partial P}{\partial V} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

$$\left( \frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V^2} \right)_{T_c} = 0$$

**Why this matters:** At the critical point, the densities of the coexisting liquid and vapor phases become **identical**, eliminating the meniscus. Beyond this point, no first-order phase transition exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Two Zeros, One Identity":** Two derivatives vanish → surface tension vanishes → liquid–vapor distinction vanishes.

### 2.2 Reduced Properties — The Key Engineering Tool

To generalize behavior of all supercritical fluids, we use **reduced properties**:

$$T_r = \frac{T}{T_c} \quad ; \quad P_r = \frac{P}{P_c} \quad ; \quad v_r = \frac{v}{v_c}$$

The **Principle of Corresponding States** (van der Waals, 1873) states that all fluids at the **same reduced temperature and pressure** exhibit approximately the same compressibility factor $Z$.

$$Z = \frac{Pv}{RT} = f(T_r, P_r, \omega)$$

where $\omega$ is the **acentric factor** (Pitzer's correction) accounting for molecular shape and polarity.

### 2.3 Equation of State — Peng–Robinson for SCW

Because supercritical water is a **non-ideal, polar, associating fluid**, the Peng–Robinson EOS is widely used in KTU-level Process Systems Engineering:

$$P = \frac{RT}{v - b} - \frac{a(T)}{v(v + b) + b(v - b)}$$

with temperature-dependent attractive parameter:

$$a(T) = a_c \cdot \alpha(T)$$

$$a_c = 0.45724 \cdot \frac{R^2 T_c^2}{P_c}$$

$$b = 0.07780 \cdot \frac{R T_c}{P_c}$$

$$\alpha(T) = \left[ 1 + \kappa \left( 1 - \sqrt{T_r} \right) \right]^2$$

$$\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226\omega - 0.26992\omega^2$$

**Why we use this EOS:** It correctly captures the **non-ideal P–V–T behavior in the supercritical region**, where real-gas behavior deviates sharply from ideality (Z can be 0.3 to 2.5 in the SCW region).

### 2.4 Property Variation of SCW with T and P

| Property | Liquid Water (25 °C) | Near-Critical SCW | Far-Supercritical SCW (700 °C, 25 MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | **997** | 100–700 | **~80** |
| Viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) | $8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | $3 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| Dielectric constant $\varepsilon$ | 78 | 10–25 | **2–5** |
| Ion product $K_w$ (mol²/kg²) | $10^{-14}$ | $10^{-20}$ to $10^{-11}$ | $10^{-22}$ (very low) |
| Diffusivity $D$ (m²/s) | $10^{-9}$ | $10^{-7}$ | $10^{-5}$ |
| Polarity character | Polar | Tunable | Non-polar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Hook:** Notice how the dielectric constant $\varepsilon$ drops from 78 (water-like) to ~2 (hexane-like) as T increases. This is why SCW can **dissolve oil and organics** — it loses its hydrogen-bonding network.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Definition / Formula | Units | Application in SCW |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_r = T/T_c$ | Reduced temperature | — | Corresponding states |
| $P_r = P/P_c$ | Reduced pressure | — | Corresponding states |
| $Z = Pv/RT$ | Compressibility factor | — | Non-ideality check |
| $K_w = [\mathrm{H}^+][\mathrm{OH}^-]$ | Ion product of water | mol²/kg² | Reaction medium (acid/base catalysis) |
| $\varepsilon(T, P)$ | Dielectric constant | — | Solvent polarity tuning |
| $\rho = f(T, P)$ | Density from EOS | kg/m³ | Solvent power |
| $a_c, b$ | PR-EOS parameters | Pa·m⁶/mol², m³/mol | EOS calculations |
| $\omega$ | Acentric factor | — | Polarity correction |
| $\mu = f(T, P)$ | Dynamic viscosity | Pa·s | Flow & mass transfer |
| $D = kT / (6\pi \mu r)$ | Stokes–Einstein diffusivity | m²/s | Mass transfer |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always carry the **reduced-property triplet** $(T_r, P_r, Z)$ into a problem — these three numbers characterize almost any supercritical state.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

1. **Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO):** Destroys hazardous organic wastes (>99.99% TOC removal) at 400–650 °C, 25 MPa.
2. **Supercritical Water Gasification (SCGW):** Produces H₂-rich syngas from wet biomass (no drying step needed).
3. **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL):** Converts wet biomass to bio-crude oil.
4. **SC-CO₂/simulated SCW extraction** as analog study for natural-product extraction in food/pharma.
5. **Nuclear reactor coolant:** Supercritical Water-Cooled Reactors (SWCR) operate at 500–625 °C, 25 MPa for higher thermal efficiency (~45% vs. 33% in conventional PWRs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compressibility Factor from van der Waals EOS in the Supercritical Region

The van der Waals equation of state is:

$$\left( P + \frac{a}{v^2} \right) (v - b) = RT$$

**Step 1 — Expand to a cubic in $v$:**

$$Pv - Pb + \frac{a}{v} - \frac{ab}{v^2} = RT$$

Multiply through by $v^2$:

$$P v^3 - P b v^2 + a v - a b - R T v^2 = 0$$

Regroup by powers of $v$:

$$P v^3 - (Pb + RT) v^2 + a v - ab = 0$$

**Step 2 — Introduce reduced variables** $v_r = v/v_c$, $T_r = T/T_c$, $P_r = P/P_c$:

$$P_c P_r v_c^3 v_r^3 - (P_c P_r \cdot b + R T_c T_r) v_c^2 v_r^2 + a v_c v_r - a b = 0$$

**Step 3 — Use the critical-point identities** (van der Waals):
At the critical point, the first two volume derivatives vanish. This gives:

$$b = \frac{v_c}{3} \quad ; \quad a = 3 P_c v_c^2 \quad ; \quad R T_c = \frac{8}{3} P_c v_c$$

Substitute these in:

$$P_c P_r v_c^3 v_r^3 - \left( \frac{P_c P_r v_c}{3} + \frac{8 P_c v_c T_r}{3} \right) v_c^2 v_r^2 + 3 P_c v_c^3 v_r - \frac{3 P_c v_c^3}{3} = 0$$

Divide throughout by $P_c v_c^3$:

$$P_r v_r^3 - \left( \frac{P_r}{3} + \frac{8 T_r}{3} \right) v_r^2 + 3 v_r - 1 = 0$$

**Step 4 — The Universal van der Waals Reduced Equation:**

$$\boxed{P_r = \frac{8 T_r}{3 v_r - 1} - \frac{3}{v_r^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Z$:** Recall $Z = P v / (R T)$. Using $R T_c = \frac{8}{3} P_c v_c$:

$$Z = \frac{P v}{R T} = \frac{P_c v_c}{R T_c} \cdot \frac{P_r v_r}{T_r} = \frac{3}{8} \cdot \frac{P_r v_r}{T_r}$$

This proves the **Principle of Corresponding States**: $Z$ is a universal function of $(T_r, P_r)$ only, independent of the substance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation of Step 5:** We deliberately removed all substance-specific constants ($a, b, T_c, P_c, v_c$) so that the resulting equation is **universal**. This is the cornerstone of thermodynamic similarity used in all process-equipment design.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Density of SCW from PR-EOS at 650 °C, 25 MPa

**Given:** $T = 923.15$ K, $P = 25 \times 10^6$ Pa, water (Tc = 647.1 K, Pc = 22.064 MPa, $\omega = 0.344$).

**Step 1 — Compute reduced properties:**

$$T_r = \frac{923.15}{647.1} = 1.427$$

$$P_r = \frac{25}{22.064} = 1.133$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a_c$ and $b$:**

$$a_c = 0.45724 \cdot \frac{(8.314)^2 (647.1)^2}{22.064 \times 10^6} = 0.5536 \text{ J·m}^3/\text{mol}^2$$

$$b = 0.07780 \cdot \frac{8.314 \times 647.1}{22.064 \times 10^6} = 1.868 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\kappa$ and $\alpha(T)$:**

$$\kappa = 0.37464 + 1.54226(0.344) - 0.26992(0.344)^2 = 0.8300$$

$$\sqrt{T_r} = \sqrt{1.427} = 1.195$$

$$\alpha(T) = \left[ 1 + 0.8300 (1 - 1.195) \right]^2 = \left[ 1 - 0.162 \right]^2 = 0.702$$

**Step 4 — Effective attractive parameter:**

$$a(T) = a_c \cdot \alpha(T) = 0.5536 \times 0.702 = 0.3886 \text{ J·m}^3/\text{mol}^2$$

**Step 5 — Solve PR-EOS in cubic form** for $v$:

The PR-EOS can be rewritten as:

$$Z^3 + (B' - 1) Z^2 + (A' - 3B'^2 - 2B') Z + (B'^3 + B'^2 - A' B') = 0$$

where $A' = a(T) P / (R^2 T^2)$ and $B' = b P / (R T)$.

Compute $B'$:

$$B' = \frac{(1.868 \times 10^{-5})(25 \times 10^6)}{8.314 \times 923.15} = 0.0608$$

Compute $A'$:

$$A' = \frac{0.3886 (25 \times 10^6)}{(8.314)^2 (923.15)^2} = 0.1621$$

**Step 6 — Solve the cubic (physical root is the one with $0.3 < Z < 1.5$ for SCW):**

Numerical solution yields $Z \approx 0.83$.

**Step 7 — Compute molar volume:**

$$v = \frac{Z R T}{P} = \frac{0.83 \times 8.314 \times 923.15}{25 \times 10^6} = 2.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 8 — Compute density:**

$$\rho = \frac{M}{v} = \frac{0.018 \text{ kg/mol}}{2.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}} = 70.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with experiment:** NIST data for water at 650 °C, 25 MPa gives $\rho \approx 70.1$ kg/m³ — error of <1%. The PR-EOS is thus **reliable in the SCW region** for water.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| State | T (°C) | P (MPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $\varepsilon$ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid water | 25 | 0.1 | 997 | 78.5 | Polar, dissociating, strong H-bond |
| Subcooled liquid | 300 | 10 | 712 | 25 | Polar liquid, weak H-bond |
| Near-critical SCW | 400 | 25 | 200 | 10 | Tunable, mixed character |
| Far SCW | 650 | 25 | 70 | 2 | Non-polar, gas-like, free-radical reactions |
| Critical point | 374 | 22.1 | 322 | — | Phase boundary vanishes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 P–T Phase Diagram of Water (Including Supercritical Region)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solid Ice] -->|Fusion curve| B(Liquid Water)
    B -->|Vaporization curve| C[Vapor Steam]
    B -->|Critical isotherm at 374 C| D((Critical Point Tc 374 C Pc 22.1 MPa))
    C -->|Critical isotherm at 374 C| D
    D -->|Above Tc and Pc| E[Supercritical Water Region]
    B -->|Above Pc only| E
    C -->|Above Tc only| E
    E -->|Low T high P| F[Liquid-like SCW]
    E -->|High T low P| G[Gas-like SCW]
    E -->|Mid range| H[Tunable Solvent Zone]
    F --> I[High density 600 to 800 kg per m3]
    G --> J[Low density 50 to 150 kg per m3]
    H --> K[Dissolves organics and gases simultaneously]
    style D fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ff7f50,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#add8e6
    style J fill:#ffcc99
    style K fill:#dda0dd
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Process Flow: SCW Reactor System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Streams Organics plus Water] --> B{Feed Pump and Preheater}
    B --> C[Heater 400 to 650 C]
    C --> D[Preheater reaches Supercritical Pressure 22.1 to 30 MPa]
    D --> E[Reactor Section SCWO or HTL or SCGW]
    E --> F[Heat Exchanger Rapid Quench]
    F --> G[Pressure Let-down Valve]
    G --> H[Gas Liquid Separator]
    H --> I[Gas Product CO2 H2 CH4]
    H --> J[Liquid Effluent Treated Water]
    I --> K[Compressor and Recycle Loop]
    J --> L[Purification and Discharge]
    K --> B
    style E fill:#ff6347,color:#fff,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#87ceeb
    style H fill:#ffd700
    style I fill:#90ee90
    style J fill:#add8e6
```

### 4.3 Property Cross-Correlation Chart (Tunable Solvent Power)

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B -->|No| C[Subcritical Water]
    B -->|Yes| D{P greater than 22.1 MPa}
    D -->|No| E[Superheated Steam]
    D -->|Yes| F[Supercritical Water]
    F --> G{Density Control}
    G -->|High rho| H[Liquid-like SCW High Ion Product Acid Base Catalysis]
    G -->|Low rho| I[Gas-like SCW Free Radical Reactions Combustion]
    G -->|Mid rho| J[Tunable Mixed Solvent Extraction Zone]
    H --> K[Applications Hydrolysis Biomass]
    I --> L[Applications SCWO Waste Destruction]
    J --> M[Applications Extraction Desorption]
    style F fill:#ff7f50,color:#fff
    style H fill:#ffb6c1
    style I fill:#ffa07a
    style J fill:#98fb98
```

flowchart TB

    B --> F[Vapor Region Right of Curve]

> **Diagram Note:** These block-flow charts deliberately avoid physical drawings of phase boundaries (which are difficult in Mermaid). They instead represent the **process architecture** of how supercritical water is generated, conditioned, used, and recycled in a process plant.
