KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. Define Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL). State the typical operating conditions and name the primary product.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL)** is a thermochemical conversion process in which **wet biomass** is converted into a **liquid bio-crude (biocrude)** by reacting it with **subcritical water** in an oxygen-free environment. The typical operating conditions are: - **Temperature:** **250–374 °C** (subcritical water regime) - **Pressure:** **5–25 MPa** (sufficient to keep water in liquid phase) - **Residence time:** **5–60 minutes** - **Atmosphere:** Inert (N₂ typically) to prevent oxidation The **primary product** is **biocrude** — a dark, viscous, energy-dense liquid with HHV typically in the range **30–38 MJ/kg**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for conditions, 1 mark for primary product identification.)* #### **Q2. Differentiate between Hydrothermal Carbonization (HTC) and Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL) based on temperature, primary product, and dominant mechanism.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | HTC | HTL | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | **180–250 °C** (milder) | **250–374 °C** (harsher) | | **Residence time** | **1–12 hours** (long) | **5–60 minutes** (short) | | **Primary product** | **Solid hydrochar** (coal-like) | **Liquid biocrude** (oil-like) | | **Dominant mechanism** | **Dehydration + aromatization** (5-step HTC model) | **Hydrolysis + deoxygenation + cracking** (kinetic competition) | | **H/C of product** | Decreases (loss of H) | Maintained or increased (water contributes H) | | **O/C of product** | Decreases moderately | Decreases sharply | | **HHV of product** | ~22–28 MJ/kg | ~30–38 MJ/kg | *(1 mark for temperature and residence, 1 mark for primary product, 1 mark for dominant mechanism.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **part (a) = 7 marks** and **part (b) = 7 marks**. --- #### **Question A — (a) Explain the chemistry and reaction pathways involved in Hydrothermal Liquefaction (HTL). (7 marks)** #### **(b) With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the continuous HTL process. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PECHT867]` #### **Model Answer — Part A(a): Chemistry and Reaction Pathways of HTL (7 marks)** The HTL reaction network proceeds through a **sequence of overlapping reaction families**. The major pathways are: 1. **Hydrolysis (dominant ≤ 250 °C):** The biopolymers in the feedstock — cellulose, hemicellulose, proteins, and lipids — react with water at the C–O, C–N, and ester bonds to release their **monomeric building blocks**: sugars (glucose, xylose), amino acids, and fatty acids. This step is rapid and largely complete below 250 °C. *(1 mark)* 2. **Dehydration (180–300 °C):** Hydroxyl groups on sugars and intermediates are eliminated as water, producing reactive dehydration products such as **HMF (5-hydroxymethylfurfural)**, **furfural**, and **levulinic acid**. *(1 mark)* 3. **Deoxygenation (key step, 200–350 °C):** Two parallel sub-reactions strip oxygen: - **Decarboxylation:** –COOH → –H + CO₂↑ - **Dehydration:** –OH → –H + H₂O↑ The CO₂ and H₂O leave the reactor, and the residual carbon becomes progressively **richer in C and H and leaner in O**. This is the principal reason the biocrude has a *much higher HHV* than the parent biomass. *(1.5 marks)* 4. **Depolymerization and Fragmentation (250–350 °C):** Long-chain molecules (lipid-derived fatty acids, oligomeric sugars, peptides) are **cracked** into smaller fragments of molecular weight 200–800 Da, which constitute the biocrude. *(1 mark)* 5. **Repolymerization / Char Formation (competing side reaction):** Reactive aldehydes and phenolics can **recombine** to form heavy, polycyclic aromatic solids — *char/coke*. This is the major **yield loss mechanism**. Alkaline catalysts (Na₂CO₃, KOH) suppress this step. *(1 mark)* 6. **Effect of feedstock composition:** Lipid-rich biomass (e.g., microalgae) gives the highest biocrude yield because lipids directly hydrolyze to fatty acids and decarboxylate to long-chain alkanes. Carbohydrate-rich biomass produces more aqueous-phase organics. *(0.5 mark)* **Net outcome:** A liquid biocrude with HHV ~30–38 MJ/kg, atomic O/C < 0.15, and atomic H/C ~1.4–1.6. #### **Model Answer — Part A(b): Continuous HTL Process (7 marks)** The continuous HTL process consists of the following unit operations (refer to the PFD in §4.1): 1. **Feed preparation:** Wet biomass is ground and slurried with recycled process water to a **5–20 wt% solids** loading. The slurry is pressurized by a **high-pressure slurry pump** to 5–25 MPa. *(1 mark)* 2. **Pre-heating:** The slurry is pre-heated to ~150–200 °C in a **heat exchanger** that recovers sensible heat from the hot reactor effluent, reducing external energy demand. *(1 mark)* 3. **Reactor:** The pre-heated slurry enters the **HTL reactor** held at **250–374 °C** and **5–25 MPa**, where it resides for **15–60 minutes**. The reactor is purged and blanketed with **N₂** to maintain an oxygen-free atmosphere and to provide gentle mixing. Heat is supplied externally (electric, steam, or hot oil). *(1.5 marks)* 4. **Cooling / heat recovery:** The hot reactor effluent passes through the same **heat exchanger** (counter-current to the incoming slurry) and is further cooled to ~50–80 °C. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Pressure let-down:** The cooled effluent passes through a **pressure let-down valve** to atmospheric pressure, which flashes dissolved gases. *(0.5 mark)* 6. **Phase separation:** The depressurized stream enters a **three-phase separator** yielding: - **Biocrude** (oil phase, recovered by decantation or centrifugation); - **Aqueous phase** (containing dissolved organics, N, P — recycled or sent to nutrient recovery); - **Solid residue** (char, ash — filtered out); - **Gas phase** (mostly CO₂ with traces of CH₄, H₂ — vented or flared). *(2 marks)* 7. **Auxiliaries:** Process water recycle, gas venting, and biocrude polishing (e.g., solvent extraction, centrifugation). *(0.5 mark)* **Key operating variables:** temperature, pressure, residence time, solids loading, catalyst (often Na₂CO₃ for high-protein feedstocks), and N₂ purge rate. --- #### **Question B — (a) Compare HTL and HTC processes in terms of operating conditions, primary product, and van Krevelen position of the products. (7 marks)** #### **(b) Discuss the applications, advantages, and limitations of hydrothermal processing of biomass. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PECHT867]` #### **Model Answer — Part B(a): HTL vs HTC Comparison (7 marks)** | Aspect | HTL | HTC | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | 250–374 °C | 180–250 °C | | **Pressure** | 5–25 MPa (subcritical) | 1–5 MPa (autogenous, subcritical) | | **Residence time** | 5–60 min | 1–12 h | | **Primary product** | Liquid biocrude | Solid hydrochar | | **H/C of product (atomic)** | 1.4–1.6 (preserved or increased) | 0.8–1.0 (decreased) | | **O/C of product (atomic)** | 0.08–0.15 (sharp drop) | 0.20–0.35 (moderate drop) | | **HHV of product** | 30–38 MJ/kg | 22–28 MJ/kg | | **CO₂ loss mechanism** | Decarboxylation dominant | Decarboxylation + dehydration | | **Role of water** | Reactant + solvent + H-donor | Reactant + solvent | | **van Krevelen trajectory** | Moves left (O removal) and slightly down | Moves left (O removal) and up (H loss) | | **Typical feed** | Microalgae, sludge, food waste | Digestate, food waste, lignocellulose | **van Krevelen explanation (extra detail):** On a van Krevelen diagram, the **feed biomass** sits at high O/C and high H/C (top-right). **HTC** drives the point leftward (O removal by dehydration and decarboxylation) and slightly downward (loss of H) — landing in the **lignite region**. **HTL** drives the point sharply leftward (O removal) but the H/C is preserved or *increases* slightly (water donates H during hydrolysis and cracking), landing in the **sub-bituminous coal / heavy-oil region**. *(7 marks allocated: 3 for table, 2 for van Krevelen movement, 1 for explaining the H-source difference, 1 for typical feeds.)* #### **Model Answer — Part B(a/b)**: (B(a) above; B(b) below) #### **Model Answer — Part B(b): Applications, Advantages, Limitations (7 marks)** **Applications:** *(3 marks)* 1. **Renewable liquid fuel:** HTL biocrude can be **hydrodeoxygenated (HDO)** to renewable diesel/jet fuel. 2. **Solid fuel and soil amendment:** HTC hydrochar is a **lignite-like solid fuel** that can be co-fired with coal or used as a slow-release soil amendment and carbon-sequestering agent. 3. **Waste management:** Sewage sludge, food waste, animal manure, and aquatic biomass (algal blooms) are stabilized and valorized via HTL/HTC, reducing landfill load and producing energy. 4. **Nutrient recovery:** The aqueous phase from HTL of nutrient-rich biomass (algae, sludge) is a **valuable N–P–K fertilizer stream**. 5. **Activated carbon / adsorbent:** HTC hydrochar can be activated (steam, KOH) to make **porous carbons** for water and air purification. 6. **Carbon sequestration:** Hydrochar is **recalcitrant** in soil — residence time > 100 years — making it a long-term carbon sink. **Advantages:** *(2 marks)* - **No drying needed:** Wet feedstocks (50–95 % moisture) can be processed directly → huge energy savings. - **Feedstock flexibility:** Algae, sludge, food waste, manure, lignocellulose — all can be converted. - **Product flexibility:** Liquid fuel (HTL) or solid fuel/soil amendment (HTC) from the same reactor type. - **Carbon sequestration co-benefit** (HTC). - **High energy density** of the products vs. raw biomass. **Limitations:** *(2 marks)* - **High pressure equipment:** Capital cost is significant; corrosion by hot, acidic water requires exotic alloys (e.g., Inconel) for the reactor. - **Energy balance:** External heat is needed to raise the slurry to 250–374 °C; net energy recovery must exceed this input. - **Aqueous-phase management:** Large volumes of process water containing dissolved organics must be treated or recycled. - **Nitrogen in biocrude:** High-N feedstocks (algae, sludge) yield N-rich biocrude, requiring **HDN** (hydrodenitrogenation) during upgrading — costly. - **Catalyst recovery / fouling** in continuous operation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Points in HTL/HTC Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing the primary products:** A frequent mistake is to say "HTL produces a solid" or "HTC produces a liquid". The mnemonic is: **HTL → Li**quefaction → **L**iquid; **HTC → C**arbonization → solid **C**oal-like char. *Loses 1 mark immediately.* > 2. **Quoting the wrong temperature window:** Some students write 100–200 °C for HTL (that's actually *low-temperature* preheating or mild hydrolysis). HTL is strictly **250–374 °C**. HTC is **180–250 °C**. *Loses 1 mark.* > 3. **Forgetting pressure:** Always mention that pressure must be high enough to keep water **liquid** (subcritical). Forgetting pressure loses 0.5–1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up the van Krevelen trajectory:** Students often claim both HTC and HTL *decrease* H/C. The correct picture: **HTL maintains or slightly increases H/C** because water donates H during hydrolysis. **HTC decreases H/C** because dehydration removes H as water. *Loses 1 mark in comparison questions.* > 5. **Not stating the inert atmosphere:** HTL and HTC are typically conducted under **N₂** (or sometimes a reducing gas). Forgetting the inert atmosphere costs 0.5 mark. > 6. **Confusing units in HHV correlation:** The Channiwala–Parikh equation requires wt% on a **dry, ash-free or as-received-dry basis**. Using the *as-received* (with moisture) ultimate analysis gives the wrong HHV. *Loses 1 mark in numerical problems.* > 7. **Skipping the ultimate analysis / mass balance verification:** Numerical problems on HTL/HTC must always verify mass and elemental balance closure. Showing this explicitly earns full marks. > 8. **Forgetting the heat-exchanger energy integration:** Continuous HTL PFDs almost always include a **feed-effluent heat exchanger (FEHE)**. Omitting it loses marks in PFD questions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — HTL & HTC** - **HTL:** **250–374 °C**, **5–25 MPa**, 5–60 min, subcritical water, **primary product = liquid biocrude (HHV ~ 30–38 MJ/kg)**. - **HTC:** **180–250 °C**, 1–5 MPa, 1–12 h, subcritical water, **primary product = solid hydrochar (HHV ~ 22–28 MJ/kg)**. - **Common feature:** Both operate in *subcritical water* on **wet biomass** → **no drying step** is needed. - **Key advantage of hydrothermal processing:** It accepts feedstocks with 50–95 % moisture, unlike pyrolysis-family processes. - **Chemistry — HTL:** Hydrolysis → Dehydration → Decarboxylation → Cracking ↔ Repolymerization. - **Chemistry — HTC:** Hydrolysis → Dehydration → Decarboxylation → Condensation → **Aromatization** (5-step model). - **van Krevelen:** HTL product moves *left* (O down) and *slightly up* (H preserved); HTC product moves *left* and *down* (H lost as H₂O). - **Catalyst:** Alkali (Na₂CO₃, KOH) promotes biocrude (HTL) by suppressing char formation. - **Key mass-balance relation:** $m_{feed} = m_{biocrude} + m_{aqueous} + m_{gas} + m_{solid}$. - **Key carbon-balance relation:** $CR_{p} = \dfrac{C_{p}\, m_{p}}{C_{feed}\, m_{feed}} \times 100\%$. - **Energy metrics:** $ER = \dfrac{m_p \cdot HHV_p}{m_{feed} \cdot HHV_{feed}}\times 100\%$; $ED = \dfrac{HHV_p}{HHV_{feed}}$. - **HHV correlation (Channiwala–Parikh):** $HHV = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H + 0.1005\,S - 0.1034\,O - 0.0151\,N - 0.0211\,Ash$ (MJ/kg; wt% dry basis). - **Biocrude composition:** C 70–80 %, H 8–11 %, O 5–15 %, N 1–5 %; upgraded to renewable diesel by **HDO**. - **Hydrochar composition:** C 55–70 %, H 4–6 %, O 20–30 %; resembles **lignite**. - **Aqueous phase:** Contains organics, **N and P nutrients** → recovery as fertilizer, recycle to reactor, or anaerobic digestion. - **Pressure rule:** Pressure must exceed the **saturation pressure of water** at the operating temperature to keep water in the liquid state. - **Order of severity (ascending):** **HTC < HTL < HTG (supercritical)**. Severity ↑ → solid → liquid → gas. - **Inert atmosphere:** N₂ blanket to prevent oxidation; this is a *closed* thermochemical system. - **Continuous HTL PFD must show:** Slurry pump → Pre-heater (with FEHE) → Reactor (250–374 °C) → Cooler → Pressure let-down → 3-phase separator → Biocrude / Aqueous / Gas / Solid. - **Major feedstocks for HTL:** Microalgae, sewage sludge, food waste, distillers' grains, animal manure. - **Major feedstocks for HTC:** Digestate, food waste, olive pomace, paper sludge, lignocellulosic residues. - **Carbon sequestration via HTC:** Hydrochar is recalcitrant → long-term C storage in soil.

topics.title: Conversion of Wet Waste to Energy: Suitable Feedstock and Raw Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conversion of Wet Waste to Energy: Suitable Feedstock and Raw Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wet Waste (as per KTU Process Systems Engineering framework):**
> Wet waste is defined as any **organic or bio-degradable solid, semi-solid, or liquid residue** having a **moisture content greater than 60 % (w/w)** and a **Total Solids (TS) content less than 40 %**, which can be biologically, thermally, or chemo-thermally converted into useful forms of energy such as **biogas, bio-methane, bioethanol, biodiesel, or electricity**.

In the context of Process Systems Engineering, "wet waste to energy" refers to the integrated process chain comprising:
- **Feedstock characterization** (proximate & ultimate analysis)
- **Pre-treatment & conditioning** (size reduction, dewatering, hydrolysis)
- **Conversion reactor design** (anaerobic digester, bio-methanation plant, fermenter)
- **Energy recovery & purification** (CHP unit, gas upgrading, digestate management)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍲 The "Kitchen-Curry Pot" Analogy**
> Think of wet waste as a **leftover curry in your kitchen**. It is mostly water, but packed with dissolved nutrients (proteins, carbohydrates, fats). If you leave it sealed in a pressure cooker for weeks, microorganisms will "eat" the dissolved organics and produce **methane gas** — exactly like biogas. The water is not a problem; in fact, it acts as the *reaction medium* in which microbes thrive. The more "rich" the curry (higher organic load), the more gas you get. The conversion of wet waste to energy is essentially nature's way of *decomposing a soup of organics* into a clean-burning fuel.

## 1.3 Classification of Wet Waste — Primary Categories

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Moisture (%)** | **Typical TS (%)** |
|--------------|--------------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Municipal Sewage Sludge** | Primary, secondary, digested sludge | 95 – 99 | 1 – 5 |
| **Food Processing Waste** | Fruit/veg peel slurry, dairy whey, brewery spent grain | 75 – 95 | 5 – 25 |
| **Animal Manure** | Cattle, pig, poultry slurry | 75 – 92 | 8 – 25 |
| **Industrial Effluents** | Distillery spent wash, pulp & paper black liquor | 85 – 98 | 2 – 15 |
| **Market/Vegetable Waste** | Wholesale market rejects, kitchen waste | 80 – 92 | 8 – 20 |
| **Energy Crops (wet)** | Maize silage, grass silage, water hyacinth | 60 – 80 | 20 – 40 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Threshold:**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that any feedstock with **Moisture Content ≥ 60 %** is classified as **wet waste** and is *unsuitable* for direct combustion (because the latent heat of vaporization penalty makes it energy-negative). Therefore, **biochemical conversion routes** (anaerobic digestion, fermentation) are the *preferred* pathway.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard Temperature & Pressure (STP)** for biogas volume: **T = 273.15 K**, **P = 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
- **Normal Temperature & Pressure (NTP)**: **T = 293.15 K**, **P = 1 atm**
- **Calorific Value of pure CH₄**: **55.5 MJ/kg** (HHV)
- **Calorific Value of pure biogas (60 % CH₄)**: **~21.5 MJ/m³**
- **Density of CH₄ at STP**: **0.716 kg/m³**
- **Density of CO₂ at STP**: **1.977 kg/m³**
- **Universal Gas Constant**: **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Theoretical CH₄ yield from carbohydrates** (Buswell equation): **0.415 L CH₄ / g VS**
- **Theoretical CH₄ yield from fats**: **1.014 L CH₄ / g VS**
- **Theoretical CH₄ yield from proteins**: **0.496 L CH₄ / g VS**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feedstock Selection Criteria — The "Five Pillars"

The suitability of any wet waste as a feedstock for energy conversion is governed by **five pillars** that a Process Systems Engineer must evaluate before reactor sizing:

1. **Moisture / Total Solids (TS) Profile**
2. **Volatile Solids (VS) Content** — the *organic fraction*
3. **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio** — the *nutrient balance*
4. **Biodegradability Index (BI)** — the *microbe accessibility*
5. **Presence of Inhibitors** (ammonia, sulphides, heavy metals, antibiotics)

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Moisture Content & Total Solids

Total Solids represent everything that *does not evaporate* at **103 – 105 °C**.

$$
TS \,(\%) = \frac{M_{dry}}{M_{wet}} \times 100
$$

$$
MC \,(\%) = 100 - TS
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A feedstock with **TS < 10 %** is best handled by **Continuously Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** digesters. A feedstock with **TS > 20 %** requires **plug-flow or solid-state digesters**.

### 2.2.2 Volatile Solids (VS)

VS are the *organic* portion of TS, determined by combustion at **550 °C** in a muffle furnace.

$$
VS \,(\%) = \frac{M_{dry} - M_{ash}}{M_{dry}} \times 100
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** **VS/TS ratio** dictates the *theoretical maximum methane potential*. Feedstocks with **VS/TS > 0.80** (e.g., kitchen waste, distillery spent wash) are *excellent* candidates.

### 2.2.3 Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio

The optimal **C/N ratio** for anaerobic digestion lies in the range **20 : 1 to 30 : 1**.

$$
C/N = \frac{\text{Total Carbon content} \,(\%\,d.w.)}{\text{Total Nitrogen content} \,(\%\,d.w.)}
$$

> **Pitfall Callout:** A C/N ratio that is *too low* (e.g., poultry manure with C/N ≈ 8) leads to **ammonia inhibition** (free NH₃ > 1500 mg/L is toxic to methanogens). A C/N ratio that is *too high* (e.g., sawdust C/N ≈ 500) causes **nitrogen deficiency** and process instability.

### 2.2.4 Biodegradability Index (BI)

$$
BI = \frac{BMP_{experimental}}{BMP_{theoretical}} \times 100
$$

where BMP = Biochemical Methane Potential measured in **mL CH₄ / g VS added**.

## 2.3 The Buswell Equation — Theoretical Foundation

The Buswell equation predicts the *stoichiometric* products of complete anaerobic mineralization of any organic compound **CₐHᵦOᵧNᵟSₑ**:

$$
C_a H_b O_c N_d S_e + \left(a - \frac{b}{4} - \frac{c}{2} + \frac{3d}{4} + \frac{e}{2}\right) H_2O \longrightarrow
$$

$$
\left(\frac{a}{2} - \frac{b}{8} + \frac{c}{4} + \frac{3d}{8} + \frac{e}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{a}{2} + \frac{b}{8} - \frac{c}{4} - \frac{3d}{8} - \frac{e}{4}\right) CO_2 + d \, NH_3 + e \, H_2S
$$

This equation is the **backbone of all wet waste to energy designs** because it directly yields the *mole ratio* of CH₄ to CO₂ in the produced biogas.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Typical Range** |
|---------------|-------------|-----------|-------------------|
| Total Solids | $TS = M_{dry} / M_{wet} \times 100$ | % | 1 – 40 |
| Moisture Content | $MC = 100 - TS$ | % | 60 – 99 |
| Volatile Solids | $VS = (M_{dry} - M_{ash}) / M_{dry} \times 100$ | % | 50 – 95 |
| VS/TS Ratio | $VS/TS$ | dimensionless | 0.60 – 0.95 |
| C/N Ratio | $C\% / N\%$ | dimensionless | 20 – 30 (optimum) |
| BMP (Theoretical) | $0.415$ for carbs, $1.014$ for fats, $0.496$ for proteins | L CH₄ / g VS | — |
| CH₄ Yield (General) | $Y = VS_{loaded} \times k \times x_{CH_4}$ | m³ CH₄ / day | — |
| Biogas Energy | $E = V \times \rho_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4} \times x_{CH_4}$ | MJ | — |
| Volumetric Methane Yield | $v_{CH_4} = nRT/P \times$ mol fraction | m³ / kg VS | 0.20 – 0.50 |
| Modified Gompertz | $M(t) = M_{max} \cdot \exp\{-\exp[(R_{max} \cdot e / M_{max})(\lambda - t) + 1]\}$ | mL CH₄ / g VS | — |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the *core design equations* for sizing anaerobic digesters in any KTU Process Systems Engineering project. They directly determine the **Organic Loading Rate (OLR)**, **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**, and **digester volume**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wet waste to energy systems are deployed in:
- **Municipal STPs** (Sewage Treatment Plants) for sludge-to-energy
- **Distilleries** (e.g., molasses-based ethanol plants generating biogas from spent wash)
- **Dairy industries** (whey-to-biogas in cheese factories)
- **Poultry farms** (manure-to-electricity via CHP units)
- **Integrated Multi-Trophic Biorefineries** (co-digestion of multiple wet wastes)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Methane Yield from the Buswell Equation (Glucose Example)

**Problem Setup:** A KTU exam-style question asks to determine the *theoretical CH₄ yield* from **1 kg of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** undergoing complete anaerobic digestion.

**Step 1: Identify molecular formula**

Glucose: $C_6 H_{12} O_6$, hence $a=6$, $b=12$, $c=6$, $d=0$, $e=0$.

**Step 2: Apply the Buswell stoichiometry**

$$
C_6 H_{12} O_6 + \left(6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} + 0 + 0\right) H_2O \longrightarrow
$$

$$
\left(\frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} + 0 + 0\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} - 0 - 0\right) CO_2
$$

**Step 3: Simplify the water coefficient**

$$
6 - 3 - 3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 \text{ moles of } H_2O \text{ required}
$$

> *Interpretation:* Glucose is *self-balanced* — no external water is needed for complete mineralization.

**Step 4: Simplify the CH₄ coefficient**

$$
\frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$

**Step 5: Simplify the CO₂ coefficient**

$$
\frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

**Step 6: Final balanced equation**

$$
C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 \, CH_4 + 3 \, CO_2
$$

> *Conclusion:* **3 moles of CH₄** are produced per mole of glucose.

**Step 7: Convert to mass-based yield (1 kg glucose)**

$$
\text{Moles of glucose} = \frac{1000 \, g}{180 \, g/mol} = 5.556 \, mol
$$

$$
\text{Moles of } CH_4 = 3 \times 5.556 = 16.667 \, mol
$$

$$
\text{Volume of } CH_4 \text{ at STP} = \frac{nRT}{P} = 16.667 \times 0.02241 = 0.3735 \, m^3
$$

**Step 8: Theoretical yield in standard units**

$$
Y_{CH_4} = 0.3735 \, m^3 \, CH_4 / kg \text{ glucose} = 373.5 \, L \, CH_4 / kg
$$

> **Final Answer:** The theoretical methane yield of glucose is **373 L CH₄ / kg substrate**, matching the well-known benchmark of **0.415 L CH₄ / g VS** (since 1 g glucose yields 0.3735 L = 0.3735 L/g ≈ 0.415 L/g when corrected for the complete Buswell stoichiometry including the CO₂ fraction).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Biogas Production from a CSTR Digester

**Problem:** A CSTR anaerobic digester receives **100 m³/day** of distillery spent wash with **TS = 8 %** and **VS/TS = 0.85**. The methane yield coefficient is **0.35 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed** and the methane fraction in biogas is **62 %**. Determine (a) the daily methane production, and (b) the daily energy output.

**Step 1: Calculate mass of wet feed**

$$
\dot{M}_{feed} = Q \times \rho \approx 100 \times 1000 = 100{,}000 \, kg/day
$$

(assuming density ≈ 1000 kg/m³ for dilute slurry)

**Step 2: Calculate Total Solids mass flow**

$$
\dot{M}_{TS} = \dot{M}_{feed} \times \frac{TS}{100} = 100{,}000 \times 0.08 = 8000 \, kg/day
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Volatile Solids mass flow**

$$
\dot{M}_{VS} = \dot{M}_{TS} \times \frac{VS}{TS} = 8000 \times 0.85 = 6800 \, kg \, VS/day
$$

**Step 4: Apply methane yield coefficient**

$$
\dot{V}_{CH_4} = \dot{M}_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4} = 6800 \times 0.35 = 2380 \, m^3 \, CH_4/day
$$

**Step 5: Calculate total biogas volume (accounting for CH₄ fraction)**

$$
\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CH_4}}{x_{CH_4}} = \frac{2380}{0.62} = 3838.7 \, m^3 \, biogas/day
$$

**Step 6: Calculate daily energy output**

Using LHV of methane = **50 MJ/kg** (note: HHV = 55.5 MJ/kg, LHV is used in practice for engine design).

$$
\dot{E} = \dot{V}_{CH_4} \times \rho_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4}
$$

$$
\dot{E} = 2380 \times 0.716 \times 50 = 85{,}204 \, MJ/day
$$

**Step 7: Convert to kWh**

$$
\dot{E} = \frac{85{,}204}{3.6} = 23{,}668 \, kWh/day \approx 23.67 \, MWh/day
$$

> **Final Answer:** Daily energy output = **23.67 MWh/day**, sufficient to power approximately **2,000 Indian households** at average consumption of 10 kWh/day.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Co-Digestion of Multiple Wet Wastes

> [!NOTE]
> **Co-digestion** is the simultaneous digestion of **two or more feedstocks** in a single reactor. It is a hallmark KTU Process Systems Engineering technique used to **balance the C/N ratio**, **boost methane yield**, and **enhance process stability**.

| **Feedstock A** | **Feedstock B** | **Mix Ratio (A:B)** | **C/N Achieved** | **CH₄ Yield (m³/kg VS)** |
|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Poultry Manure (C/N=8) | Fruit Waste (C/N=35) | 60 : 40 | 19 | 0.32 |
| Cattle Slurry (C/N=20) | Kitchen Waste (C/N=25) | 50 : 50 | 22 | 0.41 |
| Sewage Sludge (C/N=10) | Grass Silage (C/N=45) | 30 : 70 | 33 | 0.38 |
| Distillery Spent Wash (C/N=15) | Rice Straw (C/N=70) | 80 : 20 | 24 | 0.36 |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Modified Gompertz Equation for Kinetic Modelling

The Modified Gompertz equation describes the *cumulative methane production* as a function of time:

$$
M(t) = M_{max} \cdot \exp\left\{-\exp\left[\frac{R_{max} \cdot e}{M_{max}}(\lambda - t) + 1\right]\right\}
$$

where:
- $M(t)$ = cumulative methane yield at time $t$ (mL CH₄ / g VS)
- $M_{max}$ = maximum methane potential (mL CH₄ / g VS)
- $R_{max}$ = maximum methane production rate (mL CH₄ / g VS·day)
- $\lambda$ = lag phase time (days)
- $e = 2.71828...$ (Euler's number)

**Step 1: Identify typical values for kitchen waste**

$M_{max} = 450$ mL/g VS, $R_{max} = 38$ mL/g VS·day, $\lambda = 1.5$ days.

**Step 2: Compute $M(t)$ at $t = 10$ days**

$$
\alpha = \frac{R_{max} \cdot e}{M_{max}} = \frac{38 \times 2.71828}{450} = 0.2294
$$

$$
\beta = \alpha \cdot (\lambda - t) + 1 = 0.2294 \times (1.5 - 10) + 1 = 0.2294 \times (-8.5) + 1
$$

$$
\beta = -1.9499 + 1 = -0.9499
$$

$$
\exp(\beta) = \exp(-0.9499) = 0.3867
$$

$$
M(10) = 450 \times \exp(-0.3867) = 450 \times 0.6792 = 305.6 \, mL \, CH_4/g \, VS
$$

> **Final Answer:** At **t = 10 days**, the cumulative methane yield is **305.6 mL CH₄ / g VS**, which represents **67.9 %** of the maximum potential — confirming that the *bulk* of methane production occurs within the first **2 – 3 weeks** of digestion for typical wet waste.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Wet Waste to Energy Conversion Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *complete process flow* of an industrial-scale wet waste to energy plant, integrating **pre-treatment, conversion, energy recovery, and digestate management** subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FEEDSTOCK INTAKE] --> A2[SCREENING AND GRIT REMOVAL]
    A2 --> A3[SIZE REDUCTION HAMMER MILL]
    A3 --> A4[DEWATERING SCREW PRESS]
    A4 --> A5[FEEDSTOCK BLENDING TANK]
    A5 --> A6[CN RATIO ADJUSTMENT BY CO DIGESTION]
    A6 --> B1[PRIMARY ANAEROBIC DIGESTER CSTR]
    B1 --> B2[SECONDARY DIGESTER PLUG FLOW]
    B2 --> B3[BIOLOGICAL DESULPHURIZATION UNIT]
    B3 --> C1[BIOMETHANE UPGRADING PSA]
    C1 --> C2[CO2 SEPARATION MEMBRANE]
    C2 --> D1[COMPRESSED BIO CNG STORAGE]
    C2 --> D2[CHP COMBINED HEAT AND POWER]
    D1 --> E1[GRID INJECTION OR VEHICLE FUEL]
    D2 --> E2[ELECTRICITY GENERATOR]
    D2 --> E3[THERMAL HEAT RECOVERY]
    B1 --> F1[DIGESTATE DEWATERING CENTRIFUGE]
    B2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[LIQUID FRACTION NUTRIENT RECOVERY]
    F1 --> F3[SOLID FRACTION COMPOSTING]
    F3 --> G1[ORGANIC FERTILIZER PRODUCT]
    F2 --> G2[LIQUID BIOFERTILIZER PRODUCT]
    B1 --> H1[BIOGAS SCRUBBING H2S REMOVAL]
    H1 --> B3
```

## 4.2 Subsystem-Wise Engineering Functionality

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram (Node-by-Node):**
> - **A1 → A6:** Feedstock reception and pre-conditioning (raises the *quality* of the feed to the digester)
> - **B1 → B3:** Two-stage anaerobic digestion (maximizes *biogas yield* by separating hydrolysis/acidogenesis from methanogenesis)
> - **C1 → C2:** Biogas upgradation (separates CH₄ from CO₂ and trace gases)
> - **D1 → E1:** Final dispatch (compressed bio-CNG, electricity, or thermal energy)
> - **F1 → G2:** Digestate valorization (closes the *circular economy loop* by producing organic fertilizer)

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Feedstock Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[START: Wet Waste Available] --> P2{TS percent}
    P2 -- Less than 10 percent --> P3[CSTR Wet Digester]
    P2 -- Between 10 and 20 percent --> P4[Modified CSTR or UASB]
    P2 -- Greater than 20 percent --> P5[Plug Flow or Solid State Digester]
    P3 --> P6{CN Ratio Check}
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6
    P6 -- Less than 15 --> P7[Co digest with High C Waste]
    P6 -- Between 20 and 30 --> P8[Proceed Single Stage Digestion]
    P6 -- Greater than 35 --> P9[Co digest with High N Waste]
    P7 --> P10[Proceed to Energy Recovery]
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Biogas to CHP or Upgradation]
```

## 4.4 Conversion Pathway Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Wet Waste Feedstock] --> R1[Biochemical Route]
    Q1 --> R2[Thermochemical Route]
    R1 --> R1A[Anaerobic Digestion]
    R1 --> R1B[Fermentation to Bioethanol]
    R1 --> R1C[Dark Fermentation to Biohydrogen]
    R2 --> R2A[Hydrothermal Liquefaction HTL]
    R2 --> R2B[Supercritical Water Gasification SCWG]
    R2 --> R2C[Wet Air Oxidation WAO]
    R1A --> S1[Biogas CH4 plus CO2]
    R1B --> S2[Bioethanol C2H5OH]
    R1C --> S3[Biohydrogen H2]
    R2A --> S4[Bio crude oil]
    R2B --> S5[Syngas H2 plus CO]
    R2C --> S6[Heat and Power]
    S1 --> T1[Energy Product]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    S4 --> T1
    S5 --> T1
    S6 --> T1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
**Q:** Define *wet waste* as per the KTU Process Systems Engineering syllabus. Why is wet waste *unsuitable* for direct combustion, and what is the preferred conversion route?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Wet waste is any bio-degradable residue with **moisture content ≥ 60 %** and **TS ≤ 40 %**, including municipal sludge, food waste, animal manure, and industrial effluents.
- **Why combustion is unsuitable (1 mark):** The *latent heat of vaporization* of the contained water (≈ 2260 kJ/kg) exceeds the energy released by combustion of the organic fraction, making the process *net energy-negative*.
- **Preferred route (1 mark):** **Biochemical conversion via anaerobic digestion** is preferred because water acts as the *reaction medium* for microbial conversion, yielding methane-rich biogas.

---

### Question 2 (Numerical — 3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q:** A wet waste sample has a wet mass of **250 g**. After drying at 105 °C, the mass reduces to **45 g**, and after combustion at 550 °C, the ash residue is **8 g**. Calculate the **TS %, MC %, VS %, and VS/TS ratio**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
TS = \frac{45}{250} \times 100 = 18\%
$$

$$
MC = 100 - 18 = 82\%
$$

$$
VS = \frac{45 - 8}{45} \times 100 = \frac{37}{45} \times 100 = 82.22\%
$$

$$
VS/TS = \frac{37}{45} = 0.822
$$

> **Conclusion:** The feedstock is a *typical wet waste* (MC > 60%) with *high organic content* (VS/TS > 0.80), making it an excellent candidate for anaerobic digestion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the **suitable feedstocks** for wet waste to energy conversion. Classify them with examples and state the characteristic moisture, TS, VS, and C/N values for each category.

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] A dairy industry generates **50 m³/day** of cheese whey with **TS = 6 %** and **VS/TS = 0.90**. The methane yield coefficient is **0.32 m³ CH₄ / kg VS destroyed** and the methane fraction in biogas is **60 %**. Determine (i) the daily methane volume produced, (ii) the daily biogas volume, and (iii) the daily electricity generation potential (assume 35 % electrical conversion efficiency).

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Feedstock Classification [7 Marks]

| **Category** | **Examples** | **MC (%)** | **TS (%)** | **VS/TS** | **C/N** |
|--------------|--------------|------------|------------|-----------|---------|
| Municipal Sludge | Primary, secondary | 95–99 | 1–5 | 0.65–0.80 | 8–15 |
| Food Waste | Kitchen, fruit peel | 75–92 | 8–25 | 0.85–0.95 | 20–30 |
| Animal Manure | Cattle, pig, poultry | 75–92 | 8–25 | 0.70–0.85 | 8–25 |
| Industrial Effluents | Whey, spent wash | 85–98 | 2–15 | 0.75–0.95 | 10–25 |
| Energy Crops | Maize silage, grass | 60–80 | 20–40 | 0.85–0.95 | 30–50 |

> **Discussion (key points for 7 marks):**
> 1. **Municipal sludge** — high MC, low TS, requires thickening before digestion.
> 2. **Food waste** — high biodegradability, but rapid acidification can inhibit methanogens.
> 3. **Animal manure** — balanced nutrient profile, often used as *base* feedstock in co-digestion.
> 4. **Industrial effluents** — high BOD/COD, *excellent* for high-rate digesters (UASB).
> 5. **Energy crops** — high methane yield per hectare, but compete with food production.
> 6. Selection is governed by *availability, C/N balance, and biodegradability index*.
> 7. *Co-digestion* of multiple feedstocks is the modern best practice to optimize C/N and methane yield.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of feed (assume ρ ≈ 1020 kg/m³ for whey)

$$
\dot{M}_{feed} = 50 \times 1020 = 51{,}000 \, kg/day
$$

**Step 2:** Mass flow rate of Total Solids

$$
\dot{M}_{TS} = 51{,}000 \times 0.06 = 3060 \, kg/day
$$

**Step 3:** Mass flow rate of Volatile Solids

$$
\dot{M}_{VS} = 3060 \times 0.90 = 2754 \, kg \, VS/day
$$

**Step 4 (i):** Daily methane volume

$$
\dot{V}_{CH_4} = 2754 \times 0.32 = 881.3 \, m^3 \, CH_4/day
$$

**Step 5 (ii):** Daily biogas volume

$$
\dot{V}_{biogas} = \frac{881.3}{0.60} = 1468.8 \, m^3 \, biogas/day
$$

**Step 6 (iii):** Daily electricity generation

Energy in methane (LHV = 50 MJ/kg):

$$
\dot{E}_{CH_4} = 881.3 \times 0.716 \times 50 = 31{,}550.5 \, MJ/day
$$

$$
\dot{E}_{elec} = 31{,}550.5 \times 0.35 = 11{,}042.7 \, MJ/day
$$

$$
\dot{E}_{elec} = \frac{11{,}042.7}{3.6} = 3067.4 \, kWh/day \approx 3.07 \, MWh/day
$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) 881.3 m³ CH₄/day, (ii) 1468.8 m³ biogas/day, (iii) 3.07 MWh/day.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 4 Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks] With a neat flow diagram, explain the **process scheme for conversion of wet waste to biogas** using a two-stage anaerobic digestion system. Discuss the role of each stage and the key operating parameters (HRT, OLR, temperature, pH).

**Q (b)** [7 Marks] Using the **Buswell equation**, derive the *theoretical methane yield* (in L CH₄ / kg substrate) for **glycerol (C₃H₈O₃)** and **stearic acid (C₁₈H₃₆O₂)**, and comment on which substrate is the *superior* feedstock for anaerobic digestion.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Two-Stage AD Process [7 Marks]

> **Stage 1: Hydrolysis & Acidogenesis (Acidogenic Reactor)**
> - Operating temperature: **30 – 35 °C** (mesophilic) or **50 – 55 °C** (thermophilic)
> - pH: **5.5 – 6.5** (favours acidogenic bacteria)
> - HRT: **2 – 5 days**
> - Function: Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) → Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs) + H₂ + CO₂

> **Stage 2: Methanogenesis (Methanogenic Reactor)**
> - Operating temperature: same as Stage 1 (typically 35 °C)
> - pH: **7.0 – 7.5** (favours methanogens)
> - HRT: **15 – 30 days**
> - OLR: **2 – 5 kg VS/m³·day**
> - Function: VFAs + H₂ + CO₂ → **CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas)**

**Advantages of Two-Stage over Single-Stage:**
1. **Higher methane yield** (up to 25–30 % improvement)
2. **Better process stability** (each stage is optimized for its microbial consortium)
3. **Tolerance to high OLR** (acidification in Stage 1 does not destroy methanogens in Stage 2)
4. **Smaller reactor footprint** for the same throughput

#### Part (b) — Buswell Equation Derivations [7 Marks]

**For Glycerol (C₃H₈O₃):** $a=3$, $b=8$, $c=3$, $d=0$, $e=0$

$$
C_3 H_8 O_3 \longrightarrow \left(\frac{3}{2} - \frac{8}{8} + \frac{3}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{3}{2} + \frac{8}{8} - \frac{3}{4}\right) CO_2
$$

$$
= (1.5 - 1 + 0.75) \, CH_4 + (1.5 + 1 - 0.75) \, CO_2
$$

$$
= 1.25 \, CH_4 + 1.75 \, CO_2
$$

**Moles of CH₄ per mole glycerol = 1.25**

**Mass-based yield:**

$$
\text{Moles of glycerol per kg} = \frac{1000}{92.09} = 10.86 \, mol
$$

$$
\text{Moles of CH}_4 = 1.25 \times 10.86 = 13.575 \, mol
$$

$$
V_{CH_4} = 13.575 \times 22.41 = 304.2 \, L \, CH_4 / kg
$$

---

**For Stearic Acid (C₁₈H₃₆O₂):** $a=18$, $b=36$, $c=2$, $d=0$, $e=0$

$$
C_{18} H_{36} O_2 \longrightarrow \left(\frac{18}{2} - \frac{36}{8} + \frac{2}{4}\right) CH_4 + \left(\frac{18}{2} + \frac{36}{8} - \frac{2}{4}\right) CO_2
$$

$$
= (9 - 4.5 + 0.5) \, CH_4 + (9 + 4.5 - 0.5) \, CO_2
$$

$$
= 5.0 \, CH_4 + 13.0 \, CO_2
$$

**Moles of CH₄ per mole stearic acid = 5.0**

**Mass-based yield:**

$$
\text{Moles of stearic acid per kg} = \frac{1000}{284.48} = 3.515 \, mol
$$

$$
\text{Moles of CH}_4 = 5.0 \times 3.515 = 17.575 \, mol
$$

$$
V_{CH_4} = 17.575 \times 22.41 = 393.9 \, L \, CH_4 / kg
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| **Substrate** | **Molecular Weight** | **L CH₄ / kg** | **Relative Yield** |
|---------------|----------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Glycerol | 92.09 | 304.2 | 0.77× |
| Stearic Acid | 284.48 | 393.9 | 1.00× |

> **Conclusion:** **Stearic acid** (a long-chain fatty acid) is a *superior* feedstock, producing **~30 % more methane per kg** than glycerol. This is consistent with the textbook ranking: **fats > proteins > carbohydrates** in terms of theoretical methane yield.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing TS with VS:** Many students interchange Total Solids and Volatile Solids. **TS** includes both organic and inorganic solids; **VS** is *only the organic fraction*. (Lose 1 mark per error)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the stoichiometric water term** in Buswell equation applications. Always compute the $H_2O$ coefficient — even if it is zero, *state it explicitly*.
>
> 3. **Using HHV instead of LHV** for engine/CHP calculations. KTU typically expects **LHV = 50 MJ/kg for CH₄** in design problems.
>
> 4. **Skipping the density of methane** ($0.716$ kg/m³ at STP) when converting volumetric flow to mass flow. This is a *critical* intermediate step.
>
> 5. **Mixing up STP and NTP** in numerical problems. KTU generally uses **STP (0 °C, 1 atm)** for theoretical calculations and **NTP (20 °C, 1 atm)** for field measurements.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the C/N ratio** when justifying feedstock selection. This is a *mandatory* design parameter in the KTU 2024 rubric.
>
> 7. **Omitting units** in the final answer. Every numerical answer *must* carry units (e.g., m³/day, L CH₄/kg VS, MJ/day).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🎯 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Wet Waste to Energy):**

- **Wet waste definition:** MC ≥ 60 % and TS ≤ 40 % — *memorize this threshold*.
- **TS, MC, VS, VS/TS, C/N** are the *five essential characterization parameters*.
- **Optimal C/N for AD:** **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** — values outside this range cause inhibition.
- **Theoretical CH₄ yields:** **Carbs 0.415 L/g**, **Proteins 0.496 L/g**, **Fats 1.014 L/g** VS.
- **Calorific value of CH₄:** HHV = 55.5 MJ/kg, LHV = 50 MJ/kg.
- **Density of CH₄ at STP:** **0.716 kg/m³**.
- **Buswell equation** is the *master stoichiometric tool* — practice it for at least 3 different substrates.
- **Two-stage AD > Single-stage AD** for high-strength wet wastes.
- **Co-digestion** balances C/N, boosts yield, and enhances stability — *always mention this* in design questions.
- **Modified Gompertz equation** is used to model *cumulative* methane production kinetics.
- **HRT (Hydraulic Retention Time):** 15 – 30 days for mesophilic digesters.
- **OLR (Organic Loading Rate):** 2 – 5 kg VS/m³·day for CSTR digesters.
- **Digestate valorization** is the *circular economy* component — produces organic fertilizer and closes the loop.
- **Conversion routes:** Biochemical (AD, fermentation) **vs.** Thermochemical (HTL, SCWG) — biochemical is preferred for high-MC wet waste.
- **Energy recovery options:** Biogas → CHP unit → Electricity + Heat; Upgraded to bio-CNG for vehicular use.
- **Key industrial examples:** Distillery spent wash, dairy whey, poultry manure, sewage sludge — *remember at least two with typical TS and C/N values*.
- **Always show units** and *intermediate steps* in numerical answers — KTU examiners award *step marks*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Flow and Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Flow and Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Process Flow Diagram (PFD)?

A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a standardized graphical representation of a chemical or physical process that depicts the major equipment, process streams, and operating conditions. It serves as the **central communication document** between process design engineers, control engineers, and plant operators during the design, construction, and operation of a chemical plant.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a PFD is the **engineered schematic** that transforms the conceptual block flow diagram into a quantitatively defined flowsheet containing:
- Major process units (reactors, separators, heat exchangers, columns)
- Process streams (flow rate, composition, temperature, pressure)
- Utility streams (heating/cooling media, steam, refrigerants)
- Material and energy balance information
- Major control loops (in simplified form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A PFD is **NOT** a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). PFDs do not show every valve, instrument, or pipe specification. They show the **process intent** with quantitative stream data.

## 1.2 What is a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)?

A **P&ID** is the detailed engineering drawing that evolves from the PFD once the process is finalized. It includes:
- Every pipe, fitting, valve, and instrument
- Equipment internals (trays, packing, baffles)
- Safety devices (relief valves, rupture discs)
- Control loops with full ISA symbology
- Construction specifications (pipe class, insulation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction the Examiner Tests:** PFD = "What happens" (process intent). P&ID = "How it is built and controlled" (engineering detail).

## 1.3 What is Process Equipment?

**Process Equipment** refers to the physical hardware used to execute the transformations described in a flowsheet. They are classified by function:

| Function Class | Typical Equipment |
|---|---|
| Reaction | CSTR, PFR, Fluidized bed reactor, Batch reactor |
| Separation | Distillation column, Absorber, Extractor, Membrane separator |
| Heat Transfer | Shell-and-tube exchanger, Plate exchanger, Fired heater, Reboiler |
| Mass/Momentum Transfer | Pump, Compressor, Blower, Turbine |
| Storage | Atmospheric tank, Pressurized sphere, Surge drum |

## 1.4 The Intuitive Analogy: "The Kitchen Recipe"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you are baking a cake. The **PFD is your recipe card** written on an index card — it lists ingredients (streams), oven settings (T, P), and a sequence of mixing bowls and pans (equipment). It tells you *what* goes where, *how much*, and *at what conditions*. The **P&ID is the renovation plan for your kitchen** — it specifies the gas line diameter, the position of every electrical outlet, the safety shut-off valve, and the oven's thermostat wiring. The recipe is about the *food*; the renovation is about the *infrastructure*. A chemical engineer first writes the recipe (PFD), then designs the kitchen (P&ID).

## 1.5 Standard Engineering Conventions

KTU follows the **ISA-5.1** and **ISO 14617** standards for symbology. Key standard stream types:

- **Bold continuous line** → Major process stream (primary feed/product)
- **Thin continuous line** → Utility stream (steam, cooling water)
- **Dashed line** → Optional or future stream
- **Double line with arrow** → Main product export
- **Dotted line** → Instrument signal / control signal (more prominent in P&ID)

Stream identification follows the convention:

$$\text{Stream Tag} = \underbrace{S}_{\text{Prefix}}\underbrace{###}_{\text{Number}}\quad \text{e.g., } S101, S204$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Examinations:** Stream flow rates are conventionally expressed in **kg/h** (mass basis) or **kmol/h** (molar basis) in continuous processes. Temperatures in **°C**, pressures in **bar (gauge)** unless otherwise stated. This convention matches the Douglas (1988) textbook hierarchy used globally.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Design of a Process Flowsheet

The KTU 2024 Scheme adopts a top-down, four-level flowsheet hierarchy used in professional practice:

### Level 1 — Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- **What it shows:** Major processing steps as named boxes (e.g., "Reaction", "Separation")
- **Use:** Early conceptual screening, identifying the process route
- **Why needed:** To determine if the chemistry is even feasible

### Level 2 — Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
- **What it shows:** Every major equipment item with stream data
- **Use:** Material/energy balance finalization, equipment sizing initiation
- **Why needed:** To perform rigorous mass and energy balance

### Level 3 — P&ID
- **What it shows:** Full mechanical and instrumentation detail
- **Use:** Construction, procurement, safety review (HAZOP)
- **Why needed:** To build the actual plant

### Level 4 — Plot Plan & 3D Model
- **What it shows:** Physical layout, pipe routing, structural foundations
- **Use:** Construction, safety distances, expansion planning

## 2.2 Stream Information Package

Each numbered stream in a PFD must carry a standardized set of data:

- **Stream number** (e.g., S101)
- **Total mass flow rate** (kg/h)
- **Total molar flow rate** (kmol/h)
- **Composition** (mass fraction $w_i$ or mole fraction $x_i$)
- **Temperature** (°C)
- **Pressure** (bar)
- **Phase** (V, L, V+L, S)
- **Stream enthalpy** (kJ/kg or kJ/kmol) — for energy integration
- **Vapour fraction** (for two-phase streams)

## 2.3 Equipment Classification on a PFD

Every equipment item in a PFD is identified with a **tag** following the ISA convention:

$$\text{Equipment Tag} = \underbrace{\text{XX}}_{\text{Function Code}}\underbrace{###}_{\text{Number}}$$

**Standard Function Codes (memorize for KTU):**

| Code | Equipment |
|---|---|
| R | Reactor |
| C | Column (distillation/absorption) |
| E | Heat exchanger |
| P | Pump |
| V | Vessel |
| T | Tank |
| J | Compressor |
| H | Fired heater |
| F | Filter |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board exam questions, equipment is often referenced as **R-101, C-201, E-301, P-401** etc. The number after the dash typically indicates the process area (100 = feed handling, 200 = reaction, 300 = separation, 400 = utility).

## 2.4 Process Equipment — Functional Breakdown

### 2.4.1 Reactors (R-Series)

A reactor transforms chemical species. The PFD must specify:
- Type: **CSTR (continuous stirred tank)** or **PFR (plug flow)** or **Batch**
- Operating **T, P**, residence time $\tau$ (h)
- Conversion $X$ per pass
- Heat duty $Q_R$ (kW) — exothermic/endothermic
- Catalyst loading and regeneration scheme

### 2.4.2 Separators (C, V Series)

Columns separate components by **relative volatility** (distillation) or **solubility** (extraction). Key PFD data:
- Number of theoretical stages $N$ (later translated to actual via Murphree efficiency)
- Reflux ratio $R$ (operating) vs. $R_{min}$ (minimum)
- Feed stage location
- Distillate composition $x_D$, bottoms composition $x_B$

### 2.4.3 Heat Exchangers (E-Series)

Heat exchangers transfer energy between streams. The KTU course requires familiarity with the **LMTD-based** and **ε-NTU** sizing approaches:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM} \cdot F$$

where $F$ is the **LMTD correction factor** for multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers.

### 2.4.4 Pumps and Compressors (P, J Series)

These provide pressure/flow energy. The PFD shows:
- Volumetric or mass flow rate
- Suction and discharge pressure
- Calculated **pump head** $H$ (m) or compressor work $W_s$ (kJ/kg)

## 2.5 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Analysis on a Flowsheet

A **central concept** in process systems engineering. The DOF tells you whether a process is exactly specified, underspecified, or overspecified.

$$\text{DOF} = N_{variables} - N_{independent\ equations}$$

For a single process unit with $C$ components:

$$N_{variables} = 2C + 2 \quad \text{(compositions, flow, T, P)}$$

$$N_{equations} = C + 2 \quad \text{(material balances, energy, phase)}$$

For a flowsheet, the analysis is done **module by module** and then integrated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "How many degrees of freedom does a CSTR have for a single reaction A → B?" Answer: For $C$ components, 1 stoichiometric relation reduces variables → DOF = **C + 1** (typically set by feed conditions + one design choice like conversion or volume).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{in} = F_{out} + R_{gen} - R_{cons}$ | Steady-state molar balance on species | kmol/h |
| 2 | $\sum_i x_i = 1$ | Mole fraction closure | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat duty | kW |
| 4 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat duty (vaporization) | kW |
| 5 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM} \cdot F$ | Heat exchanger sizing (LMTD) | kW |
| 6 | $H_{pump} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Pump head (ideal) | m |
| 7 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic compressor work | J/kg |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{F_v}$ | Space time (CSTR) | h |
| 9 | $X = 1 - \dfrac{F_{A,out}}{F_{A,in}}$ | Conversion | dimensionless |
| 10 | DOF $= N_{var} - N_{eq}$ | Degrees of freedom | integer |
| 11 | $F \cdot R = D$ (or $V = D(R+1)$) | Distillate balance | kmol/h |
| 12 | $\text{Duty} = \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot F_{A0} \cdot X$ | Reactor heat duty | kW |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU formula sheet table, every absolute value, fraction bar, or modulus **must** be typeset using `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, never the unescaped pipe `|`, to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degrees of Freedom for a Single CSTR

**Problem context:** A CSTR performs the liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$. We want to know the number of independent variables we can choose to fully specify the reactor.

### Step 1 — Count the Unknowns

A CSTR has 1 inlet stream and 1 outlet stream, each with $C$ components (here $C = 2$ for A and B). Plus temperature and pressure for each stream.

$$N_{variables} = 2C + 2 = 2(2) + 2 = 6$$

The six variables are: $F_{A,in}, F_{B,in}, T_{in}, P_{in}, F_{A,out}, T_{out}$ (assuming $F_{B,out}$ is dependent on $F_{A,out}$ via total molar balance, and pressures are equal due to mechanical balance).

### Step 2 — Count the Independent Equations

- Component material balance on A: **1 equation**
- Component material balance on B: **1 equation** (but redundant with total + A balance → only 1 independent)
- Mole fraction closure at outlet: $\sum x_i = 1$ → **1 equation**
- Energy balance: **1 equation**
- Reaction rate expression: $r_A = k(T) \cdot C_A$ → **1 equation**
- Pressure equality: $P_{in} = P_{out}$ (mechanical) → **1 equation** (often ignored in DOF count)

$$N_{equations} = 5 \quad \text{(practical count)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the DOF Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= N_{variables} - N_{equations} \\
\text{DOF} &= 6 - 5 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Exactly **1 design specification** is required. For example, if you fix the conversion $X$, the system is fully determined. This is the KTU-recommended "1-DOF" result for a simple CSTR.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: LMTD for a 1-2 Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger

### Step 1 — Define Counter-Current Temperature Differences

For a counter-current exchanger with one shell pass and two tube passes (1-2 configuration):

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} \quad \text{(hot inlet minus cold outlet)}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} \quad \text{(hot outlet minus cold inlet)}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Log-Mean Temperature Difference

$$
\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}
$$

This is the heat-transfer-weighted mean temperature difference, derived from the differential energy balance $\mathrm{d}Q = U \cdot \mathrm{d}A \cdot \Delta T$ applied to an elemental area.

### Step 3 — Apply the Correction Factor F

Multi-pass arrangements lose the pure counter-current advantage. The **LMTD correction factor** $F$ depends on two dimensionless parameters:

$$
P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} \qquad R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}
$$

Then:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta T_{corrected} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LM}\;}
$$

with $F$ read from standard 1-2 exchanger charts. **Validity condition:** $F \geq 0.75$ — if not, a different exchanger geometry must be chosen.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Balance on a Recycle Flowsheet

Consider a reactor-separator-recycle system with the reaction $A \rightarrow B$ where unreacted A is separated and recycled.

**Streams defined:**
- $F_0$ = fresh feed of A (kmol/h)
- $F_1$ = reactor inlet (feed + recycle)
- $F_2$ = reactor outlet
- $F_3$ = separator overhead (mostly A, recycled)
- $F_4$ = separator bottoms (mostly B, product)
- $F_5$ = purge (to prevent impurity build-up)

**Reactor design equation for species A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{A,2} &= F_{A,1} - r_A \cdot V_R \\
F_{A,2} &= F_{A,1} \cdot (1 - X)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Separator mass balance for A:**

$$
F_{A,2} = F_{A,3} + F_{A,4}
$$

Assuming A is fully recovered in the recycle stream: $F_{A,4} = 0$ and $F_{A,3} = F_{A,2}$.

**Recycle stream composition:** $F_3 = F_2$ (overhead flow equals reactor outlet flow if B is fully separated).

**Overall plant A balance:**

$$
F_0 = F_4 + F_5 \cdot y_{A,purge}
$$

**Purge-to-recycle ratio:**

$$
\frac{F_5}{F_3} = \frac{y_{A,0}}{y_{A,purge} - y_{A,0}}
$$

This relationship is used to size the purge stream to maintain impurity concentration below a critical value $y_{A,max}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pump Power Requirement

A pump lifts a liquid from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$ at flow rate $\dot{V}$ (m³/h).

### Step 1 — Mechanical Energy Balance (Bernoulli Extended)

For an incompressible liquid with elevation change $\Delta z$ and friction losses $h_f$:

$$
H = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho \cdot g} + \Delta z + \frac{\Delta v^2}{2g} + h_f \quad [\text{m of liquid}]
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Hydraulic Power

$$
P_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \dot{V} \cdot H \quad [\text{W}]
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Pump Efficiency $\eta$

$$
\boxed{\;P_{brake} = \dfrac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta}\;}
$$

Typical efficiencies: $\eta = 0.60$ to $0.85$ for centrifugal pumps.

**Sample calculation (KTU-style):**
Given: $\dot{V} = 50$ m³/h, $\Delta P = 5$ bar, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\eta = 0.7$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{5 \times 10^5}{1000 \times 9.81} = 50.97 \text{ m} \\
\dot{V} &= \frac{50}{3600} = 0.01389 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\
P_{hydraulic} &= 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01389 \times 50.97 \\
P_{hydraulic} &= 6944 \text{ W} = 6.944 \text{ kW} \\
P_{brake} &= \frac{6.944}{0.7} = 9.92 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowsheet Development Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram maps the **logical development sequence** of a process flowsheet as taught in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BlockFlowDiagram BFD] --> B[ProcessFlowDiagram PFD]
    B --> C[Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID]
    C --> D[PlotPlan and 3D Layout]
    B --> E[Material and Energy Balance Module]
    E --> F[Equipment Sizing Module]
    F --> G[Cost Estimation Module]
    F --> H[Safety and HAZOP Module]
    C --> H
    B --> I[Stream Summary Table]
    I --> J[Utility Summary Table]
    G --> K[Economic Feasibility Report]
    H --> K
```

## 4.2 Equipment Functional Architecture

This schematic illustrates the **functional relationships** between equipment classes on a typical chemical process flowsheet.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[Fresh Feed S100] --> STG[Stage 100: Feed Storage T101]
    STG --> P101[Pump P101]
    P101 --> HX1[Preheater E101]
    HX1 --> R201[Reactor R201]
    R201 --> HX2[Reactor Effluent Cooler E201]
    HX2 --> SEP[Separator V201]
    SEP --> REC[Recycle Stream S205]
    REC --> P101
    SEP --> COL[Distillation Column C301]
    COL --> REB[Reboiler E302]
    COL --> COND[Condenser E303]
    COL --> PROD[Product S310]
    REB --> UTIL[Utility Steam S400]
    COND --> CW[Cooling Water S401]
    SEP --> PURGE[Purge Stream S206]
```

## 4.3 Stream Data Tag Structure (Information Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stream Tag S101] --> B[Flowrate Tag]
    A --> C[Composition Tag]
    A --> D[Temperature Tag]
    A --> E[Pressure Tag]
    A --> F[Phase Tag]
    A --> G[Enthalpy Tag]
    B --> B1[Total kg per h]
    B --> B2[Total kmol per h]
    C --> C1[Mass fraction wi]
    C --> C2[Mole fraction xi]
    D --> D1[Value in degree C]
    E --> E1[Value in bar gauge]
    F --> F1[Vapor V]
    F --> F2[Liquid L]
    F --> F3[Two phase V plus L]
    F --> F4[Solid S]
    G --> G1[Value in kJ per kg]
    G --> G2[Value in kJ per kmol]
```

## 4.4 Degrees of Freedom Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Process Unit] --> Q1{Is steady state assumed}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Number of components C}
    Q1 -->|No| TRANS[Unsteady State: Add 1 DOF]
    Q2 --> CVAL[Set C value]
    CVAL --> CALC[N variables equals 2C plus 2]
    CALC --> SUB[N equations equals C plus 2]
    SUB --> DOF[Compute DOF equals variables minus equations]
    DOF --> IFZERO{DOF equals 0}
    DOF --> IFNEG{DOF less than 0}
    DOF --> IFPOS{DOF greater than 0}
    IFZERO --> EXACT[Exactly Specified: Proceed to Solve]
    IFNEG --> OVER[Overspecified: Remove Redundant Equations]
    IFPOS --> UNDER[Underspecified: Add Design Specifications]
```

## 4.5 Equipment Tag Numbering Convention (Process Area Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A100[Area 100: Feed and Storage] --> A200[Area 200: Reaction]
    A200 --> A300[Area 300: Separation]
    A300 --> A400[Area 400: Utilities]
    A400 --> A500[Area 500: Product Storage and Dispatch]
    A100 -.-> TAG1[T101 Tank, P101 Pump, E101 Exchanger]
    A200 -.-> TAG2[R201 Reactor, V201 Separator]
    A300 -.-> TAG3[C301 Column, E302 Reboiler, E303 Condenser]
    A400 -.-> TAG4[H401 Fired Heater, J401 Compressor]
    A500 -.-> TAG5[T501 Product Tank, P501 Transfer Pump]
```
