# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and a Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). List any three differences.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PFD | P&ID |
|---|---|---|
| Level of detail | Shows major equipment and stream data | Shows every pipe, valve, instrument |
| Purpose | Material/energy balance, process intent | Construction, operation, maintenance |
| Instrumentation | Simplified control loops only | Full ISA symbology with tag numbers |
| Valves | Manual isolation valves only | Every valve: isolation, control, safety |
| Used in | Conceptual and basic engineering | Detailed engineering and procurement |
| Pipe specs | Not specified | Pipe class, size, material specified |

The PFD is therefore the **process engineer's document**, while the P&ID is the **multi-discipline document** used by piping, instrumentation, electrical, and safety engineers. *(3 marks: 1 for correct distinction, 1 for tabulated difference, 1 for concluding statement)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Degrees of Freedom" in the context of process flow analysis. Why is DOF analysis performed before writing material balance equations?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Degrees of Freedom (DOF) is the difference between the number of unknown variables and the number of independent equations describing a process unit or flowsheet.

$$\text{DOF} = N_{variables} - N_{equations}$$

**Why DOF is performed first:**

1. **Identifies the solvability status** of the system: DOF = 0 → exactly specified (solvable); DOF > 0 → underspecified (need more equations); DOF < 0 → overspecified (redundant data or conflicting constraints).
2. **Determines design specifications** required: For DOF = 0, no further input is needed; for DOF > 0, the engineer must decide which design choices to make (e.g., conversion, product purity, recycle ratio).
3. **Prevents wasted effort** on a mathematically under-determined or over-determined system.
4. **Provides a structured approach** to flowsheet synthesis by systematically resolving each unit's DOF before integrating.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for mathematical expression, 1 for any two reasons)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Option Set 1*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Coverage]**

A process flowsheet is being designed for the production of **B from A** via the liquid-phase reaction $A \rightarrow B$, conducted in a CSTR. The reactor effluent is sent to a distillation column where pure B is recovered as bottoms and unreacted A is recycled back to the reactor. A purge stream is taken from the recycle loop to prevent accumulation of an inert impurity.

**(a)** Develop the complete PFD for the above system. Identify all equipment, label all streams (S101 to S106), and state the function of each equipment item. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform a **DOF analysis on the CSTR alone** for the binary system A and B. Clearly list the unknown variables, the independent equations, and state the design specification required. Show your work step by step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

### Part (a) Solution: PFD Development

**Process Description and Equipment Identification:**

1. **T-101 (Feed Storage Tank):** Stores fresh feed of component A and trace inerts. Provides surge capacity and decouples supply from production rate.
2. **P-101 (Feed Pump):** Boosts the pressure of the liquid feed from atmospheric to reactor pressure (e.g., 5 bar), overcoming line and equipment pressure drops.
3. **E-101 (Feed Preheater):** Raises the feed temperature to the optimal reactor inlet temperature using steam or a hot utility stream.
4. **R-201 (CSTR):** Performs the reaction $A \rightarrow B$ at constant temperature, pressure, and intensive mixing. Heat of reaction is removed by a cooling jacket or external exchanger.
5. **V-201 (Flash Separator / Decanter):** Removes dissolved gases, water, or heavy byproducts from the reactor effluent before distillation.
6. **C-301 (Distillation Column):** Separates B (high boiler) as bottoms product from unreacted A (low boiler) as distillate.
7. **E-302 (Reboiler):** Supplies vapor to the column by boiling the bottoms liquid using steam.
8. **E-303 (Condenser):** Condenses the overhead vapor and provides reflux to the column.
9. **P-302 (Reflux Pump):** Returns condensed distillate to the column as reflux.
10. **P-303 (Recycle Pump):** Pumps the condensed distillate (mostly A) back to the reactor feed line.
11. **V-301 (Purge Drum / Splitting Tee):** Diverts a small purge stream (S106) from the recycle loop.

**Stream Numbering:**

- **S101:** Fresh feed of A from storage → Pump
- **S102:** Pressurized feed → Preheater
- **S103:** Hot feed → Reactor
- **S104:** Reactor effluent → Separator
- **S105:** Separator bottoms → Distillation Column
- **S106:** Column distillate → Reflux/Recycle split
- **S107:** Reflux return to column
- **S108:** Recycle to reactor feed
- **S109:** Purge stream (waste)
- **S110:** Pure B product (column bottoms)

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S101[S101 Fresh Feed A] --> T101[T101 Feed Tank]
    T101 --> P101[P101 Feed Pump]
    P101 --> E101[E101 Preheater]
    E101 --> S103[S103 Hot Feed]
    S103 --> R201[R201 CSTR Reactor]
    S108[S108 Recycle A] --> MIX[Mixing Tee]
    MIX --> S103
    R201 --> S104[S104 Reactor Effluent]
    S104 --> V201[V201 Separator]
    V201 --> S105[S105 Crude Product]
    S105 --> C301[C301 Distillation Column]
    C301 --> E302[E302 Reboiler]
    C301 --> E303[E303 Condenser]
    E303 --> P302[P302 Reflux Pump]
    P302 --> S107[S107 Reflux Return]
    S107 --> C301
    E303 --> V301[V301 Purge Splitter]
    V301 --> S106[S106 Purge Stream]
    V301 --> P303[P303 Recycle Pump]
    P303 --> S108
    C301 --> S110[S110 Pure B Product]
    E302 --> UTIL[Steam Utility]
    E303 --> CW[Cooling Water]
```

*(7 marks: 2 for equipment identification with functions, 2 for stream numbering, 2 for Mermaid / schematic, 1 for completeness and clarity)*

### Part (b) Solution: DOF Analysis on the CSTR

**Step 1 — Define System Boundary**

The system is the CSTR with one inlet (S103) and one outlet (S104). Components: A, B, and an inert I (3 components). However, the inert is conserved through the reactor, so it is treated as part of the binary A-B analysis with an inert carry-through.

For a **binary A-B system** in the CSTR:

**Step 2 — Count Unknown Variables**

For each of the 2 streams, we have 2 compositions + 1 total flow + 1 T + 1 P → 5 variables per stream. Two streams → 10 variables. However, pressure equality across a reactor (negligible $\Delta P$) and mole fraction closure (already counted) simplify the practical count:

$$N_{variables} = 2(C + 2) = 2(2 + 2) = 8$$

These 8 are: $F_{A,in}, F_{B,in}, T_{in}, F_{A,out}, F_{B,out}, T_{out},$ plus 2 phase indicators (L-L assumption on both sides). In practice:

- Stream compositions: 2 each side → 4
- Stream flow rates: 1 each side → 2  
- Temperatures: 1 each side → 2
- **Total: 8 variables**

**Step 3 — Count Independent Equations**

1. Mole fraction closure on inlet: $x_{A,in} + x_{B,in} = 1$ → **1 equation**
2. Mole fraction closure on outlet: $x_{A,out} + x_{B,out} = 1$ → **1 equation**
3. Material balance on A: $F_{A,in} - F_{A,out} - r_A V_R = 0$ → **1 equation**
4. Material balance on B (or total): $F_{B,out} = F_{B,in} + r_A V_R$ → **1 equation**
5. Energy balance: $Q = -\Delta H_{rxn} r_A V_R$ → **1 equation**
6. Rate expression: $r_A = k_0 e^{-E/RT_{out}} C_A$ → **1 equation**

$$N_{equations} = 6$$

**Step 4 — Compute DOF**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= N_{variables} - N_{equations} \\
\text{DOF} &= 8 - 6 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and Design Specifications**

The CSTR has **2 degrees of freedom**, meaning **2 design specifications** must be made to fully determine the system. Common choices are:

- **Specification 1:** Conversion $X = 0.80$ (or any other value justified by economics)
- **Specification 2:** Reactor volume $V_R$ (determines production rate) **OR** reactor temperature $T_{out}$ (determines rate constant)

If both $X$ and $V_R$ are specified, then $T_{out}$ is fixed by the energy balance (i.e., heat removal requirement is determined).

*(7 marks: 2 for variable count, 2 for equation count, 1 for DOF calculation, 2 for interpretation and design specs)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Coverage]**

A feed stream of 10,000 kg/h of a binary mixture containing **60 wt% methanol and 40 wt% water** at 25 °C is to be separated in a distillation column operating at 1 atm. The desired products are **95 wt% methanol (distillate)** and **2 wt% methanol (bottoms)**.

**(a)** Compute the **distillate and bottoms flow rates** in kg/h, and determine the **reflux ratio** if the column operates at $R = 1.5 \cdot R_{min}$ and $R_{min} = 0.8$ (use the given value as the operating minimum). Calculate the **vapor boil-up rate** $V$ in kmol/h, assuming an average molecular weight of the distillate as 28 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the reboiler of this distillation column, compute the **heat duty** if the bottoms mass flow rate is 4118 kg/h, the bottoms is vaporized from 95 °C to its bubble point, and the heat of vaporization is 2200 kJ/kg. Use an LMTD correction factor $F = 0.92$ and $U = 1200$ W/(m²·K). State the **required heat-transfer area** in m² if $\Delta T_{LM} = 30$ K. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

### Part (a) Solution: Distillate and Bottoms Rates

**Given:**
- $F = 10{,}000$ kg/h
- $x_F = 0.60$ (methanol mass fraction in feed)
- $x_D = 0.95$ (methanol mass fraction in distillate)
- $x_B = 0.02$ (methanol mass fraction in bottoms)

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance**

$$F = D + B$$

$$10000 = D + B$$

**Step 2 — Component Mass Balance on Methanol**

$$F \cdot x_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_B$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
10000 \times 0.60 &= D \times 0.95 + B \times 0.02 \\
6000 &= 0.95 D + 0.02 B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Two Equations**

Substituting $B = 10000 - D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
6000 &= 0.95 D + 0.02(10000 - D) \\
6000 &= 0.95 D + 200 - 0.02 D \\
6000 - 200 &= 0.93 D \\
5800 &= 0.93 D \\
D &= \dfrac{5800}{0.93} = 6236.56 \text{ kg/h} \approx 6237 \text{ kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
B = 10000 - 6236.56 = 3763.44 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — Operating Reflux Ratio**

$$R = 1.5 \times R_{min} = 1.5 \times 0.8 = 1.2$$

**Step 5 — Vapor Boil-up Rate**

Convert distillate to molar basis using $M_D = 28$ g/mol = 28 kg/kmol:

$$D_{molar} = \frac{6236.56}{28} = 222.73 \text{ kmol/h}$$

For a distillation column, the vapor boil-up $V$ relates to distillate and reflux:

$$V = D_{molar} \times (R + 1)$$

$$
V = 222.73 \times (1.2 + 1) = 222.73 \times 2.2 = 489.99 \text{ kmol/h} \approx 490 \text{ kmol/h}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for mass balance setup, 2 for D and B calculation, 1 for reflux ratio, 2 for vapor boil-up)*

### Part (b) Solution: Reboiler Heat Duty and Area

**Given:**
- $B = 4118$ kg/h (revised bottoms)
- $\lambda = 2200$ kJ/kg
- $F = 0.92$
- $U = 1200$ W/(m²·K)
- $\Delta T_{LM} = 30$ K

**Step 1 — Compute Heat Duty**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m} \times \lambda \\
Q &= 4118 \text{ kg/h} \times 2200 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
Q &= 9{,}059{,}600 \text{ kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to kW (1 kW = 3600 kJ/h = 1 kJ/s):

$$
Q = \frac{9{,}059{,}600}{3600} = 2516.56 \text{ kW} \approx 2.52 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Corrected LMTD**

$$
\Delta T_{corrected} = F \times \Delta T_{LM} = 0.92 \times 30 = 27.6 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Heat-Transfer Area**

From $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{corrected}$:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{corrected}}
$$

Convert $Q$ to Watts: $Q = 2{,}516{,}560$ W

$$
A = \frac{2{,}516{,}560}{1200 \times 27.6} = \frac{2{,}516{,}560}{33{,}120} = 75.98 \text{ m}^2 \approx 76 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Result:** The reboiler requires a heat-transfer area of approximately **76 m²** to vaporize 4118 kg/h of bottoms.

*(7 marks: 2 for Q calculation, 2 for LMTD correction, 2 for area calculation, 1 for correct unit conversion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing mass and mole fractions** in distillation problems (–2 marks typical deduction).
> 2. **Forgetting the LMTD correction factor $F$** for multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers. The bare LMTD without $F$ gives an over-rated area → unsafe / oversized design.
> 3. **Unit conversion mistakes**: 1 kW = 3600 kJ/h. Many students leave Q in kJ/h and divide by $U$ in W/(m²·K) — dimensional error.
> 4. **Omitting stream numbering** in PFD questions — every PFD must have **all streams labelled**.
> 5. **Incomplete DOF analysis**: forgetting to list *all* equations, or miscounting variables. Always state $N_{var}$ and $N_{eq}$ explicitly.
> 6. **Not justifying the choice of design specifications** in DOF — just stating "fix conversion" is not enough; the examiner expects the *implication* on the rest of the system.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4**

- ✅ A **PFD** shows major equipment + stream data; a **P&ID** shows every pipe, valve, and instrument.
- ✅ The flowsheet hierarchy: **BFD → PFD → P&ID → Plot Plan**. Each level adds detail.
- ✅ **Stream tag format:** `S###` where the number indicates sequence. **Equipment tag format:** `Letter (function) + dash + 3 digits`.
- ✅ Function codes to memorize: **R (Reactor), C (Column), E (Exchanger), P (Pump), V (Vessel), T (Tank), J (Compressor), H (Heater), F (Filter)**.
- ✅ **DOF = Variables − Equations**. DOF = 0 → solvable; DOF > 0 → underspecified; DOF < 0 → overspecified.
- ✅ For a CSTR with a single reaction in a binary mixture: **DOF = 2** (e.g., fix $X$ and $V_R$, or $X$ and $T$).
- ✅ **Mass balance on a recycle flowsheet:** Fresh feed = product + purge. Recycle stream provides unreacted reactant recovery.
- ✅ **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — with $F$ correction for multi-pass exchangers.
- ✅ **Heat exchanger sizing:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM} \cdot F$.
- ✅ **Pump head:** $H = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} + \Delta z + h_f$. Brake power: $P = \dfrac{\rho g \dot{V} H}{\eta}$.
- ✅ **Compressor work (isentropic):** $W_s = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$.
- ✅ **Distillate/bottoms from overall + component mass balance** — always write both equations and solve the 2×2 linear system.
- ✅ **Reflux ratio convention:** $R = L/D$. Vapor boil-up: $V = D(R+1)$.
- ✅ **Purge stream purpose:** to prevent accumulation of inerts or impurities in a recycle loop; size using $\dfrac{F_{purge}}{F_{recycle}} = \dfrac{y_{in,feed}}{y_{in,purge} - y_{in,feed}}$.
- ✅ Standard units in KTU: **kg/h, kmol/h, °C, bar(g), kW, m²** for area, **m** for head.
- ✅ A PFD must include: **stream numbers, flow rates, compositions, T, P, phase, equipment tags, major control loops** (qualitative).
- ✅ **Fouling factor** and **LMTD correction** together account for real-world departures from ideal heat transfer.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw the PFD first in any process design question — the examiner awards 1-2 marks just for a labelled, well-organized schematic. Then proceed with mass balance → DOF → equipment sizing in that order. This mirrors the way professional engineers solve problems and is exactly the **valuation order** KTU examiners follow.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Emerging Technologies in Chemical Conversion: Catalytic Depolymerization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Overview of Emerging Technologies in Chemical Conversion: Catalytic Depolymerization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Catalytic Depolymerization** is a chemical conversion process that selectively cleaves the covalent bonds of a polymeric substrate (plastics, biomass, rubbers, or condensation polymers) into its constituent monomers, oligomers, or value-added platform chemicals, using a heterogeneous or homogeneous catalyst under controlled thermodynamic conditions (temperature, pressure, and reaction atmosphere). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition Tag:** According to the Process Systems Engineering (PECHT867) syllabus, catalytic depolymerization is classified under **Emerging Chemical Conversion Technologies**, sitting at the intersection of **reactor design, catalysis, and circular-economy process integration**. Mathematically, the generalized depolymerization reaction is expressed as: $$\text{Polymer}_{(n)} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst, } T, P} n \cdot \text{Monomer} + \text{By-products}$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of depolymerization as "molecular un-zipping."** Just as you can unzip a long zipper (the polymer chain) back into two separate halves (monomers), a catalyst acts like a pair of scissors that snips the chain at specific points — but unlike regular scissors, the catalyst is **regenerated** and reused millions of times. **Real-World Analogy — The Recycled Plastic Bottle:** - A used PET plastic bottle is a long polymer chain (terephthalic acid + ethylene glycol monomers linked by ester bonds). - When this bottle is exposed to a **catalyst** (e.g., zinc acetate) in the presence of **methanol** at ~180–200 °C, the ester bonds "unzip." - The result is **dimethyl terephthalate (DMT)** and **ethylene glycol (EG)** — the exact same monomers used to make the original bottle. - This is **chemical recycling at its purest**: a closed-loop circular economy where the bottle becomes a bottle again, not a downcycled product. ### 1.3 Classification of Depolymerization Routes | Type | Bond Cleaved | Reagent | Typical Catalyst | Example Polymer | |------|--------------|---------|------------------|-----------------| | **Hydrolysis** | Ester / Amide | H₂O (steam) | H₂SO₄, NaOH, solid acids | PET, Polyamides | | **Methanolysis** | Ester | CH₃OH | Zn(OAc)₂, Ti(OBu)₄ | PET (→ DMT + EG) | | **Glycolysis** | Ester | Ethylene glycol | Mn(OAc)₂, Zn(OAc)₂ | PET (→ BHET) | | **Aminolysis** | Ester | Amines | Metal acetates | PET, PU | | **Hydrogenolysis** | C–C / C–O | H₂ | Ru, Pt, Ni on supports | Polyolefins | | **Enzymatic** | Ester / Amide | Water + enzyme | Lipase, cutinase | PET, PLA | | **Thermal (Pyrolysis)** | C–C | Heat (no catalyst or zeolites) | ZSM-5, FCC catalysts | Mixed plastics | ### 1.4 Key Process Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory KTU Metrics** (often asked in exams): > - **Conversion (X)** = fraction of polymer consumed. > - **Selectivity (S)** = fraction of desired monomer among all products. > - **Yield (Y) = X × S**. > - **Turnover Frequency (TOF)** = moles of product / (moles of catalyst × time) — measures catalyst efficiency. > - **Space–Time Yield (STY)** = mass of product / (reactor volume × time) — measures reactor productivity. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown **Step 1 — Polymer Identification & Bond Mapping:** The first systems-engineering step is to identify the **type of chemical bond** linking the repeating units. The depolymerization route is dictated entirely by the bond: - **C–O bonds** (esters, acetals, ethers) → susceptible to hydrolysis / alcoholysis. - **C–N bonds** (amides, urethanes) → susceptible to aminolysis. - **C–C bonds** (polyolefins, polystyrene) → require hydrogenolysis or pyrolysis (highest energy barrier). **Step 2 — Catalyst Selection:** Catalysts lower the activation energy (Ea) without being consumed. Two classes: - **Homogeneous** (e.g., Zn(OAc)₂ in methanolysis): high selectivity, but separation is hard. - **Heterogeneous** (e.g., Ru/C, ZSM-5): easier separation, can be used in fixed-bed reactors. **Step 3 — Thermodynamic Driving Force:** Depolymerization is **endothermic** for most condensation polymers (entropy increases as one big molecule → many small ones, but bond-breaking absorbs heat). For polyolefins, hydrogenolysis is **exothermic** because C–H bond formation releases more energy than C–C bond cleavage consumes. **Step 4 — Kinetic Control:** The reaction rate is governed by catalyst loading, temperature, and monomer concentration. Most catalytic depolymerizations follow **pseudo-first-order kinetics** when the catalyst is in excess or constant. **Step 5 — Mass-Transfer Considerations:** In heterogeneous systems, the rate-limiting step may shift from intrinsic kinetics to: 1. External film diffusion (polymer to catalyst surface). 2. Internal pore diffusion (into catalyst pellet). 3. Surface reaction (true chemical step). **Step 6 — Process Integration (Systems Engineering View):** The output monomers are fed back as feed to the upstream polymerization reactor — closing the **material loop**. Energy integration (heat exchangers between exothermic polymerization and endothermic depolymerization) is a classic Pinch-Analysis application. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam tested. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.** | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | |---|---------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $X = 1 - \dfrac{C_P}{C_{P0}}$ | Conversion of polymer | dimensionless | | 2 | $S = \dfrac{n_{\text{desired monomer}}}{n_{\text{total products}}}$ | Selectivity | dimensionless | | 3 | $Y = X \cdot S$ | Yield | dimensionless | | 4 | $-r_P = k \, C_P$ | First-order depolymerization rate | mol·L⁻¹·s⁻¹ | | 5 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius equation | s⁻¹ | | 6 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{P0}}{C_P}\right) = k \, t$ | Integrated first-order law (batch) | dimensionless | | 7 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life | s | | 8 | $\text{TOF} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{product}}}{n_{\text{cat}}}$ | Turnover frequency | s⁻¹ or h⁻¹ | | 9 | $\text{STY} = \dfrac{m_{\text{product}}}{V_{\text{reactor}} \, \tau}$ | Space-time yield | kg·m⁻³·h⁻¹ | | 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = \dfrac{C_{P0} \, X}{k \, C_{P0}} = \dfrac{X}{k}$ | Space time (PFR, 1st order) | s | | 11 | $D_a = k \, \tau$ | Damköhler number (CSTR) | dimensionless | | 12 | $X_{\text{CSTR}} = \dfrac{D_a}{1 + D_a}$ | CSTR conversion (1st order) | dimensionless | | 13 | $X_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - e^{-D_a}$ | PFR conversion (1st order) | dimensionless | | 14 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Actual rate}}{\text{Rate at bulk conditions}}$ | Effectiveness factor (porous catalyst) | dimensionless | | 15 | $\phi = R_p \sqrt{\dfrac{k \, \rho_p}{D_e}}$ | Thiele modulus | dimensionless | | 16 | $\eta = \dfrac{3}{\phi}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{\tanh \phi} - \dfrac{1}{\phi}\right]$ | Spherical pellet effectiveness factor | dimensionless | **Standard Constants to Remember:** - **Universal gas constant R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** - **Activation energies** for PET methanolysis: ~70–90 kJ/mol - **Typical TOF** for Zn(OAc)₂ in PET methanolysis: ~10–50 h⁻¹ at 200 °C - **Operating temperature window**: 160–250 °C (most catalytic systems) ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Circular Plastics Economy:** Unilever, BASF, and Indorama Ventures use methanolysis/hydrolysis to produce food-grade rPET. - **Biomass Conversion:** Lignin depolymerization yields aromatic platform chemicals (vanillin, phenols) replacing petrochemical feedstocks. - **Waste-Tire Recycling:** Catalytic depolymerization of styrene-butadiene rubber (SBR) yields limonene and other valuable aromatics. - **CO₂ Capture Polymers:** Polycarbonate depolymerization releases monomer + CO₂ — a sustainable CO₂ utilization loop. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Batch Depolymerization Rate Law (First-Order Model) **Starting Point — Rate Definition:** The rate of polymer consumption for a catalytic depolymerization is proportional to the polymer concentration when catalyst is constant: $$-r_P = -\dfrac{dC_P}{dt} = k \, C_P$$ **Step 1 — Separate Variables:** We rearrange so that all $C_P$ terms are on the left and all $t$ terms are on the right: $$\dfrac{dC_P}{C_P} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** Integrate from initial concentration $C_{P0}$ at $t = 0$ to concentration $C_P$ at time $t$: $$\int_{C_{P0}}^{C_P} \dfrac{dC_P}{C_P} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 3 — Perform the Integration:** The integral $\int \dfrac{dC_P}{C_P} = \ln C_P$ gives: $$\ln C_P - \ln C_{P0} = -k \, t$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $C_P(t)$:** Exponentiating both sides: $$C_P(t) = C_{P0} \, e^{-k t}$$ **Step 5 — Express Conversion as a Function of Time:** Substituting $C_P = C_{P0}(1 - X)$: $$C_{P0}(1 - X) = C_{P0} \, e^{-k t}$$ $$1 - X = e^{-k t}$$ $$\boxed{X(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}}$$ **Step 6 — Derive Half-Life:** Setting $X = 0.5$ (i.e., $C_P = C_{P0}/2$): $$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-k \, t_{1/2}}$$ $$\ln 2 = k \, t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}}$$ **Step 7 — Temperature Dependence via Arrhenius:** Substituting $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ into the rate law: $$X(t, T) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-A \, t \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)\right]$$ This is the **master design equation** for batch catalytic depolymerization. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Reactor Type on Conversion | Reactor Type | Design Equation | Conversion @ $D_a = 1$ | Conversion @ $D_a = 5$ | Best Use Case | |--------------|-----------------|------------------------|------------------------|----------------| | **Batch** | $X = 1 - e^{-k t}$ | 0.632 | 0.993 | Lab studies, small-scale | | **PFR (Plug Flow)** | $X = 1 - e^{-D_a}$ | 0.632 | 0.993 | Continuous, no back-mixing | | **CSTR (Mixed)** | $X = \dfrac{D_a}{1 + D_a}$ | 0.500 | 0.833 | Slurry catalysts, viscous melts | | **Semi-Batch** | Differential form, $\dfrac{dC_P}{dt} = -k C_P + F/V(C_{P,in} - C_P)$ | Variable | Variable | Evolving catalyst, monomer removal | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** For the same Damköhler number, a **PFR gives 13.2% higher conversion than a CSTR** at $D_a = 1$. For depolymerization of viscous polymer melts where back-mixing is hard to achieve, the **PFR (extruder-reactor)** is preferred. ### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Style **Problem:** A PET methanolysis reactor operates at 200 °C with a pseudo-first-order rate constant $k = 0.04 \, \text{min}^{-1}$. The initial PET concentration is $C_{P0} = 2.0 \, \text{mol/L}$. Find: 1. The conversion after 30 minutes. 2. The time required for 95% conversion. 3. The reactor volume needed for a feed rate of 50 L/min (PFR). **Solution:** **Part (a) — Conversion at t = 30 min:** $$X = 1 - e^{-k t} = 1 - e^{-(0.04)(30)} = 1 - e^{-1.2}$$ $$e^{-1.2} = 0.3012$$ $$\boxed{X = 1 - 0.3012 = 0.699 = 69.9\%}$$ **Part (b) — Time for 95% Conversion:** $$0.95 = 1 - e^{-k t}$$ $$e^{-k t} = 0.05$$ $$k t = -\ln(0.05) = 2.996$$ $$t = \dfrac{2.996}{0.04} = 74.9 \, \text{min}$$ $$\boxed{t \approx 75 \, \text{minutes}}$$ **Part (c) — PFR Volume:** Space time for a PFR is: $$\tau = \dfrac{V}{v_0} = -\dfrac{1}{k} \ln(1 - X) = -\dfrac{1}{0.04} \ln(0.05)$$ $$\tau = \dfrac{2.996}{0.04} = 74.9 \, \text{min}$$ $$V = v_0 \cdot \tau = 50 \times 74.9 = 3745 \, \text{L}$$ $$\boxed{V \approx 3.75 \, \text{m}^3}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Catalytic Depolymerization Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[WastePolymerFeed
PET bottles, biomass, tires] --> B[PreprocessingUnit
Shredding, washing, drying] B --> C[FeedHopper
with screw conveyor] C --> D[ReactorSystem
Batch or PFR or CSTR] E[CatalystRecycle
Loop heterogeneous catalyst] --> D F[HeatExchangerNetwork
Pinch integration] --> D D --> G[ProductSeparator
Distillation or extraction] G --> H[MonomerPurification
Crystallization] H --> I[PolymerizationReactor
Feed-back loop] I --> J[VirginPolymerProduct
Closed-loop output] G --> K[ByproductRecovery
Energy or chemicals] K --> F D --> L[EmissionControl
Scrubbers, condensers] ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow — PET Methanolysis (Detailed) ```mermaid flowchart LR PET[PET flakes] --> MIX[Mixer] MEOH[Methanol feed] --> MIX CAT[Zinc acetate
catalyst] --> MIX MIX --> RX[Reactor
180-200 C, 2-4 MPa] RX --> SEP[Distillation Column] SEP --> DMT[DMT product] SEP --> EG[Ethylene glycol] SEP --> MEOH2[Methanol recycle] MEOH2 --> MIX RX --> GAS[Vent gas treatment] RX --> CAT2[Catalyst recovery] CAT2 --> CAT ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing a Depolymerization Route ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Select Polymer] --> Q1{Polymer type?} Q1 -->|Condensation
PET, PU, PA| Q2{Bond type?} Q1 -->|Addition
PE, PP, PS| Q3{Target monomer?} Q2 -->|Ester| R1[Hydrolysis or
Methanolysis] Q2 -->|Amide| R2[Hydrolysis or
Aminolysis] R1 --> C1[Catalyst: H2SO4 or
Zn acetate] R2 --> C2[Catalyst: NaOH or
enzyme] Q3 -->|Olefin wax| R3[Hydrogenolysis
Ru/C catalyst] Q3 -->|Aromatic feed| R4[Pyrolysis
ZSM-5 catalyst] C1 --> OUT[Product: monomers] C2 --> OUT R3 --> OUT R4 --> OUT ``` ### 4.4 Material-Balance Recycle Loop (Systems View) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph UPSTREAM POLY[Polymerization Plant] end subgraph CONSUMER USE[Consumer Product Use] end subgraph DOWNSTREAM COLL[Collection and Sorting] DP[Depolymerization Plant] MONO[Monomer Recovery] end POLY --> USE USE --> COLL COLL --> DP DP --> MONO MONO --> POLY DP -->|energy| EN[Energy Recovery] EN --> POLY ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip for Exams:** When drawing such flow diagrams in the exam, **always label the streams with composition**, the **reactor with T & P**, and **show recycle loops as dashed lines** with the recycle ratio written beside them. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define catalytic depolymerization. List any three industrial applications.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Catalytic depolymerization is a chemical conversion process in which a long-chain polymer is broken down into its constituent monomers or smaller molecular fragments in the presence of a catalyst that lowers the activation energy without being consumed. The catalyst may be homogeneous (e.g., zinc acetate in PET methanolysis) or heterogeneous (e.g., Ru/C in polyolefin hydrogenolysis). **Three industrial applications:** 1. **PET recycling** via methanolysis to recover dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) and ethylene glycol. 2. **Polyurethane foam recycling** via glycolysis to recover polyols. 3. **Waste-tire depolymerization** via catalytic pyrolysis to recover limonene and aromatic oils. --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis in depolymerization with one example each.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Homogeneous Catalysis | Heterogeneous Catalysis | |---------|----------------------|------------------------| | Phase | Same phase as reactants (usually liquid) | Different phase (solid catalyst, liquid/gas reactants) | | Selectivity | Generally higher | Moderate | | Separation | Difficult (distillation/ extraction needed) | Easy (filtration or sedimentation) | | Reactor | Batch / CSTR | Fixed-bed / slurry / fluidized-bed | | Example | Zn(OAc)₂ for PET methanolysis | Ru/C for polyethylene hydrogenolysis | | Regeneration | Harder | Easier (calcination or reduction) | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** #### **Question A:** **(a)** Derive the integrated rate law for a batch catalytic depolymerization following first-order kinetics. Starting from $-r_P = k C_P$, obtain the expression for conversion $X(t)$ and the half-life $t_{1/2}$. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A PET depolymerization is carried out in a PFR at 220 °C. The feed concentration of PET (in terms of repeating units) is 1.5 mol/L and the pseudo-first-order rate constant is $k = 0.06 \, \text{min}^{-1}$. Calculate: 1. The space time required for 90% conversion. 2. The reactor volume if the volumetric flow rate is 25 L/min. 3. The STY (space-time yield) if the monomer yield per pass is 85% of the conversion. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** Starting with the rate law: $$-r_P = -\dfrac{dC_P}{dt} = k \, C_P$$ Separate and integrate: $$\int_{C_{P0}}^{C_P} \dfrac{dC_P}{C_P} = -k \int_0^t dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_P}{C_{P0}}\right) = -k t$$ Using $C_P = C_{P0}(1 - X)$: $$\boxed{X(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}}$$ Half-life (when $X = 0.5$): $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}}$$ **Model Solution for (b):** **Part 1 — Space Time for PFR at X = 0.90:** $$\tau = -\dfrac{1}{k} \ln(1 - X) = -\dfrac{1}{0.06} \ln(0.10)$$ $$\ln(0.10) = -2.303$$ $$\tau = \dfrac{2.303}{0.06} = 38.4 \, \text{min}$$ **Part 2 — Reactor Volume:** $$V = v_0 \cdot \tau = 25 \times 38.4 = 960 \, \text{L} \approx 1.0 \, \text{m}^3$$ **Part 3 — Space-Time Yield:** Converted PET per minute = $v_0 \cdot C_{P0} \cdot X \cdot Y = 25 \times 1.5 \times 0.90 \times 0.85$ $$= 28.69 \, \text{mol/min of monomer}$$ Molar mass of DMT = 194 g/mol. $$\text{Mass rate} = 28.69 \times 194 = 5565 \, \text{g/min} = 5.565 \, \text{kg/min}$$ $$\text{STY} = \dfrac{5.565 \, \text{kg/min}}{0.960 \, \text{m}^3} = 5.80 \, \text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{min)}$$ $$\boxed{\text{STY} = 348 \, \text{kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{h)}}$$ --- #### **Question B (Alternative Choice):** **(a)** Explain the different routes of catalytic depolymerization (hydrolysis, methanolysis, glycolysis, aminolysis, hydrogenolysis) with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the catalyst in each route. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** With a neat flow diagram, describe the process of PET methanolysis for monomer recovery. List the operating conditions, the catalysts used, and the main products. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution for (a):** - **Hydrolysis:** Uses water (often as steam) to cleave ester or amide bonds. Catalysts: H₂SO₄ (acidic), NaOH (alkaline), or solid acids like zeolites. Example: PET hydrolysis gives terephthalic acid (TPA) and ethylene glycol. - **Methanolysis:** Uses methanol in the presence of metal acetate catalysts (Zn, Mn, Co) at 180–200 °C and 2–4 MPa. Products: dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) + EG. - **Glycolysis:** Uses ethylene glycol with trans-esterification catalysts (Mn(OAc)₂, Zn(OAc)₂) at 190–250 °C. Product: bis(2-hydroxyethyl) terephthalate (BHET). - **Aminolysis:** Uses amines (e.g., ethanolamine) with metal acetate catalysts. Useful for introducing N-functional groups. - **Hydrogenolysis:** Uses H₂ gas with supported metal catalysts (Ru, Pt, Ni) at 200–300 °C and high pressure. Applicable to polyolefins (PE, PP) producing liquid alkanes. **Role of catalyst:** Lowers activation energy, increases selectivity toward the desired monomer, enables milder operating conditions, and can be regenerated for continuous operation. **Model Solution for (b):** The PET methanolysis flow sheet (see Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram above) consists of: 1. **Feed Section:** PET flakes (washed, dried) and fresh methanol are mixed with recycled methanol in a stirred mixer. 2. **Reactor:** Operating at **180–200 °C** and **2–4 MPa**, fitted with an agitator. Catalyst: **Zinc acetate (Zn(OAc)₂)** at 0.5–1.0 wt%. 3. **Distillation Column:** Separates the reactor effluent into DMT (bottom product), EG (side stream), and unreacted methanol (overhead, recycled). 4. **Catalyst Recovery:** Solid catalyst is filtered and recycled; homogeneous catalyst is precipitated and recovered. 5. **Monomer Purification:** DMT is purified by crystallization; EG by vacuum distillation. **Main Products:** - Dimethyl terephthalate (DMT) → repolymerized with EG to make virgin PET. - Ethylene glycol (EG) → reused as monomer. - Methanol (recycled). --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pitfalls):** > 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark per missing/wrong unit. > 2. **Confusing CSTR and PFR design equations** — memorize $X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ vs $X_{CSTR} = k\tau/(1 + k\tau)$. > 3. **Skipping the conversion of catalyst kinetics** — when catalyst concentration changes, you must use the actual rate (not pseudo-first-order) and integrate differently. > 4. **Not drawing recycle loops** in flow diagrams — always show the methanol/catalyst recycle stream as a dashed line. > 5. **Writing "monomer recovery" without specifying which monomer** — always name DMT, EG, TPA, BHET, etc. > 6. **Mixing up hydrolysis vs. methanolysis products** — hydrolysis gives TPA; methanolysis gives DMT. This is a 2-mark deduction trap. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Catalytic depolymerization = breaking polymers into monomers using a catalyst (homogeneous or heterogeneous). - **Five major routes:** Hydrolysis, Methanolysis, Glycolysis, Aminolysis, Hydrogenolysis (+ Enzymatic, Pyrolysis). - **First-order kinetics is the default model:** $X(t) = 1 - e^{-k t}$ for batch; $X_{PFR} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$. - **Damköhler number $D_a = k\tau$** is dimensionless and central to comparing reactors. - **Arrhenius dependence** on T is strong: doubling T can increase $k$ by an order of magnitude. - **Catalyst selection drives selectivity** — Zn(OAc)₂ for PET, Ru/C for PE, lipase for bio-degradable PLA. - **Process integration:** Heat integration between endothermic depolymerization and exothermic polymerization is a **Pinch analysis** application. - **Key metrics:** Conversion (X), Selectivity (S), Yield (Y), TOF, STY. - **Real-world impact:** Enables **circular plastics economy**, **biomass valorization**, and **CO₂-utilizing polymers**. - **Critical constants:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/mol·K}$, $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$, typical operating T = 160–250 °C. - **Board-exam trap:** Always state **which monomer** is produced and **which catalyst** is used — generic answers fetch partial credit only. ---

topics.title: Plasma Gasification and Wet Oxidation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT867 - PROCESS SYSTEMS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plasma Gasification and Wet Oxidation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔬 Module 4: Plasma Gasification and Wet Oxidation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Plasma Gasification — Formal Definition

**Plasma Gasification** is an advanced thermal waste-to-energy conversion process that uses a plasma torch (electrically generated ionized gas stream at temperatures of **3,000 °C to 10,000 °C**) to dissociate molecular bonds in organic and inorganic waste materials into their elemental constituents. The process converts the carbonaceous fraction into a **Synthesis Gas (Syngas)** consisting primarily of **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** and **Hydrogen (H₂)**, while the inorganic fraction is vitrified into an inert, glass-like slag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (2 marks):**
> "Plasma gasification is a process that uses extremely high temperatures generated by a plasma arc to decompose waste into syngas and a non-leachable vitrified slag, in a closed, oxygen-starved environment."

### 1.2 Wet Air Oxidation (WAO) — Formal Definition

**Wet Air Oxidation (WAO)** is a liquid-phase thermal oxidation process used to treat aqueous industrial waste streams that are too dilute for economic incineration but too toxic for biological treatment. It operates in the **subcritical regime** (typically **150 °C – 320 °C** and **5 bar – 200 bar**) using **oxygen (O₂) or air** as the oxidizing agent, converting dissolved organic matter primarily into **CO₂, H₂O, and short-chain organic acids** (e.g., acetic acid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Related Process — Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO):**
> When operating conditions exceed water's critical point (**Tₑ = 374.15 °C**, **Pₑ = 220.64 bar**), the process is termed **SCWO**, where organic matter and oxidant exist in a single homogeneous supercritical fluid phase, achieving >99.99% destruction efficiency in seconds.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogies & Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Plasma Gasification = "The Solar Furnace in a Pipe"**
> Imagine focusing the sun's heat (which is ~5,500 °C at the surface) onto a pile of garbage through an electrical "lightning rod" (the plasma torch). The garbage cannot withstand such temperatures — its molecules are ripped apart. The carbon and hydrogen escape as a combustible gas (syngas, like the gas that powered old city buses), while the metals and minerals melt and fuse into a glassy rock (slag), similar to how sand melts into glass. The energy you spent to create the plasma is recovered by burning the syngas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Wet Oxidation = "The Pressure Cooker That Burns Sludge"**
> Picture a giant pressure cooker filled with toxic industrial wastewater. We seal it, raise the pressure (so water stays liquid despite high heat), inject oxygen, and heat it to ~250 °C. Under these conditions, oxygen aggressively attacks the dissolved organics — the same chemistry that rusts iron or rots apples — but accelerated by ~1,000,000×. The organics "burn" inside the water without ever boiling it, producing only CO₂ and water as end products. The catch: this only works for aqueous wastes, not solid trash.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Plasma Gasification | Wet Air Oxidation (WAO) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Temperature | **3,000 – 10,000 °C** (in plasma zone); **1,000 – 1,500 °C** (in reactor) | **150 – 320 °C** |
| Operating Pressure | Near-atmospheric (~1 – 5 bar) | **5 – 200 bar** |
| Oxidant | None (pyrolytic) or limited O₂ | **O₂ or Air** |
| Phase State of Water | Steam | **Subcritical Liquid** |
| Typical Residence Time | **1 – 10 seconds** (in plasma) | **30 – 120 minutes** |
| Energy Source | **Electrical (Arc/Plasma Torch)** | Thermal + Chemical exotherm |
| Destruction Efficiency | >99.99% (DRE) | **40 – 75% (TOC removal)** |
| Solid Product | **Vitrified Slag** | Mineral solids (if any) |
| Gaseous Product | **Syngas (CO + H₂)** | **CO₂, N₂, off-gas** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Plasma Gasification — Process Architecture

#### 2.1.1 What is Plasma?

A **plasma** is the **fourth state of matter** — a quasi-neutral gas of ions, electrons, and neutral particles formed when sufficient energy is supplied to strip electrons from atoms. In process engineering, two types of plasmas are relevant:

- **Thermal (Equilibrium) Plasma:** High electron & gas temperatures (Tₑ ≈ T_gas > 5,000 K). Used in plasma gasification.
- **Non-Thermal (Non-Equilibrium) Plasma:** Tₑ >> T_gas; used in surface treatment, not bulk waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plasma Torch Mechanism:**
> A plasma torch passes a working gas (typically **Ar, N₂, air, or steam**) between two electrodes. An electric arc (DC or AC, **1 – 10 MW** power level) ionizes the gas into plasma. The plasma jet exits the torch at velocities of **Mach 1 to Mach 2** and temperatures up to **10,000 K**.

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Feed Preparation:** Shredded waste (≤ 10 cm particles) is fed via a lock-hopper system to prevent air ingress.
2. **Drying Zone (~150 °C):** Moisture is driven off as steam.
3. **Pyrolysis Zone (~500 – 800 °C):** Volatiles released; tars and char form.
4. **Gasification Zone (1,000 – 1,500 °C):** Char reacts with limited O₂/steam:
   $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$
   $$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$
5. **Plasma Vitrification Zone (>1,500 °C):** Plasma jet melts inorganic fraction into a homogeneous molten slag.
6. **Quench & Slag Tap:** Slag is quenched in water → vitrified granules for road-base or aggregate.
7. **Syngas Cleanup:** Particulate removal, scrubbers for HCl/H₂S, tar reforming.

#### 2.1.3 Governing Chemistry of Plasma Gasification

The overall endothermic/exothermic balance is critical. The principal reactions are:

| # | Reaction | Enthalpy (kJ/mol) | Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 | C + ½ O₂ → CO | **−111** (Exothermic) | Partial Oxidation |
| R2 | C + O₂ → CO₂ | **−394** (Exothermic) | Complete Oxidation |
| R3 | C + H₂O → CO + H₂ | **+131** (Endothermic) | Steam Gasification (Water-Gas) |
| R4 | C + CO₂ → 2CO | **+172** (Endothermic) | Boudouard Reaction |
| R5 | CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ | **−41** (Exothermic) | Water-Gas Shift |
| R6 | CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ | **+206** (Endothermic) | Methane Steam Reforming |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Plasma Provides the Heat?**
> The plasma torch supplies the energy for **R3, R4, and R6** (endothermic reactions), while **R1 and R2** provide some heat. The net process is energy-autonomous when organic content > 30% by mass.

#### 2.1.4 Cold Gas Efficiency (η_cg)

The efficiency of converting feedstock chemical energy to syngas is:

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{\dot{m}_{syngas} \times LHV_{syngas}}{\dot{m}_{feed} \times LHV_{feed} + W_{plasma}} \times 100\%$$

where $W_{plasma}$ is the electrical input to the plasma torch. Typical values: **η_cg = 60 – 85%**.

---

### 2.2 Wet Air Oxidation (WAO) — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 The Thermodynamic Imperative

For dilute aqueous waste, three questions determine technology choice:

1. **Is the COD > 250 g/L?** → Incineration is economic.
2. **Is COD < 10 g/L?** → Biological treatment is economic.
3. **Is 10 g/L < COD < 250 g/L?** → **Wet Oxidation is the optimal choice.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Hurdle of Wet Oxidation — Why High Pressure?**
> At 200 °C, the saturation pressure of water is ~15.4 bar. To keep water in the liquid phase (necessary for the subcritical WAO mechanism), the system must operate at **P > P_sat(T)**, otherwise the water would flash to steam, halting the dissolved-oxygen reaction pathway.

#### 2.2.2 WAO Reaction Network

The generic reaction for WAO of a generic organic compound **CₘHₙOₚXᵧ** (X = heteroatom) is:

$$\text{C}_m\text{H}_n\text{O}_p\text{X}_q + \left(m + \frac{n - 2p - \eta}{2}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow m\text{CO}_2 + \frac{n}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Intermediate Acids} + \text{X products}$$

A simplified global representation is:

$$\text{Organics} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{k, T, P} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Residual Short-Chain Acids (e.g., Acetic Acid)}$$

The **rate-limiting step** is typically the conversion of low-molecular-weight carboxylic acids (acetic, propionic), which are remarkably resistant to further oxidation.

#### 2.2.3 WAO Kinetic Models

The standard rate expression is **second-order overall — first-order in both substrate (COD) and dissolved oxygen**:

$$-\frac{d[\text{COD}]}{dt} = k \cdot [\text{COD}]^a \cdot [\text{O}_2]^b$$

For most conditions with O₂ in excess, **a = 1, b = 0** (pseudo-first-order):

$$-\frac{d[\text{COD}]}{dt} = k_{obs} \cdot [\text{COD}]$$

The Arrhenius temperature dependence gives:

$$k_{obs} = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Typical values for WAO:
- **Eₐ (Activation Energy): 50 – 100 kJ/mol**
- **A (Pre-exponential factor): 10⁴ – 10⁸ min⁻¹**

#### 2.2.4 Supercritical Water Oxidation (SCWO) — Beyond WAO

Above the critical point, water becomes a **non-polar solvent** with:
- **Density:** ~100 – 200 kg/m³ (vs. 1,000 kg/m³ ambient)
- **Dielectric constant:** drops from 80 → ~2 (similar to acetone)
- **Complete miscibility** with O₂ and organics → single-phase oxidation

The rate of SCWO is **10² – 10⁶ times faster** than subcritical WAO, with destruction efficiencies **> 99.9999%** in residence times of **< 60 seconds**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | P[Pa], V[m³], T[K] | Reactor gas volume |
| 2 | Steam Gasification (R3) | $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$, ΔH = +131 kJ/mol | kJ/mol | Plasma energy demand |
| 3 | Water-Gas Shift (R5) | $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ | — | Syngas H₂/CO ratio |
| 4 | Cold Gas Efficiency | $\eta_{cg} = \frac{\dot{m}_{syn} \cdot LHV_{syn}}{\dot{m}_{feed} \cdot LHV_{feed} + W}$ | % | Process efficiency |
| 5 | Pseudo-1st Order WAO | $-\frac{dC}{dt} = k_{obs} C$ | min⁻¹, mg/L·min | COD removal |
| 6 | Arrhenius Rate Constant | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | s⁻¹ or min⁻¹ | T-dependence |
| 7 | Critical Point of Water | T_c = 374.15 °C; P_c = 220.64 bar | °C, bar | WAO vs SCWO boundary |
| 8 | WAO Lumped Stoichiometry | $\text{C}_m\text{H}_n\text{O}_p + (m + n/4 - p/2)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow m\text{CO}_2 + (n/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | mol | O₂ demand calc |
| 9 | Syngas LHV (approx) | $LHV_{syn} = (25.7 \cdot x_{H_2} + 30.0 \cdot x_{CO})\text{ MJ/Nm}^3$ | MJ/Nm³ | Energy content |
| 10 | Energy Recovery Ratio | $ERR = \frac{E_{syn}}{E_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Self-sufficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of These Equations:**
> 1. The **Cold Gas Efficiency** equation is used in industry to compare competing gasification technologies (entrained-flow, fluidized-bed, plasma).
> 2. The **Arrhenius equation** in WAO design allows engineers to size reactors for a target COD removal — reducing pilot plant costs.
> 3. **SCWO** units are deployed by the U.S. military (Model SCWO-100, General Atomics) for destroying chemical warfare agents, demonstrating the practical value of the critical-point concept.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand for WAO

**Problem:** Compute the theoretical oxygen demand for complete wet oxidation of **phenol (C₆H₅OH)**, with concentration **C₀ = 5,000 mg/L** in a wastewater stream. Determine the minimum O₂ required for full mineralization.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric equation:**

Phenol oxidation: C₆H₅OH(l) + O₂(aq) → CO₂(aq) + H₂O(l)

Balancing carbons and hydrogens first, then oxygens:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 7\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 6\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Verify atom balance:**

- Carbon: Left 6 → Right 6 ✓
- Hydrogen: Left 6 → Right 3 × 2 = 6 ✓
- Oxygen: Left (1 + 14) = 15 → Right (12 + 3) = 15 ✓

**Step 3 — Compute the theoretical oxygen demand (ThOD) per gram of phenol:**

Molar masses: M(phenol) = 94 g/mol; M(O₂) = 32 g/mol

$$\text{ThOD} = \frac{7 \times 32 \text{ g O}_2}{1 \times 94 \text{ g phenol}} = 2.383 \text{ g O}_2 / \text{g phenol}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the oxygen demand for 5,000 mg/L phenol solution:**

$$[\text{O}_2]_{demand} = 5{,}000 \text{ mg/L} \times 2.383 = 11{,}915 \text{ mg/L} \approx 11.92 \text{ g O}_2 / \text{L}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to molar concentration at standard conditions (STP):**

$$n_{O_2} = \frac{11.92 \text{ g}}{32 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.3725 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 6 — Express in air-equivalent volume (air contains 21% O₂ v/v):**

$$V_{air, STP} = \frac{0.3725 \times 22.4 \text{ L/mol}}{0.21} = 39.72 \text{ L air per L wastewater at STP}$$

**Step 7 — Inlet air volume at actual reactor conditions (T = 250 °C = 523 K, P = 50 bar):**

Using the ideal gas law, $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$:

$$V_{air, actual} = 39.72 \times \frac{523}{273} \times \frac{1}{50} = 1.52 \text{ L air (at reactor P,T) per L feed}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The 26× reduction from STP to reactor conditions highlights why **high-pressure WAO is mass-transfer favorable** — the same O₂ demand occupies only a tiny volume, enabling compact reactor design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reactor Sizing Using WAO Kinetics

**Problem:** A WAO reactor is to treat a feed stream with **COD₀ = 25,000 mg/L** at **T = 250 °C**, achieving **90% COD reduction**. The reaction follows pseudo-first-order kinetics with $k_{obs}(250°C) = 0.05 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Determine the **residence time (τ)** and **reactor volume (V)** for a flow rate **Q = 10 m³/h**.

**Step 1 — Write the design equation for a CSTR:**

For a first-order reaction in a CSTR:
$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{C_0 - C_f}{k_{obs} \cdot C_f} = \frac{X}{k_{obs}(1 - X)}$$

where $X = 1 - \frac{C_f}{C_0}$ is the fractional conversion.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$X = 0.90, \quad k_{obs} = 0.05 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{0.90}{0.05 \times (1 - 0.90)} = \frac{0.90}{0.005} = 180 \text{ min} = 3.0 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — For a PFR (plug flow), the design equation is:**

$$\tau_{PFR} = -\frac{1}{k_{obs}} \ln\left(\frac{C_f}{C_0}\right) = \frac{-\ln(1 - X)}{k_{obs}}$$

$$\tau_{PFR} = \frac{-\ln(0.10)}{0.05} = \frac{2.303}{0.05} = 46.05 \text{ min} \approx 0.77 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the required reactor volumes:**

$$V_{CSTR} = Q \cdot \tau_{CSTR} = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 3.0 \text{ h} = 30 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{PFR} = Q \cdot \tau_{PFR} = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 0.77 \text{ h} = 7.7 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and selection:**

$$\frac{V_{CSTR}}{V_{PFR}} = \frac{30}{7.7} \approx 3.9$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The PFR is **~3.9× smaller** than the CSTR for this first-order process. However, in practice, tubular PFR designs for WAO are limited by **exothermic heat removal** (tube-side ΔT control) and **corrosion**, so a staged CSTR or a series of PFRs is often used.

**Step 6 — Volumetric Oxygen Feed Rate:**

If the stoichiometric O₂ demand is 2 g O₂/g COD (typical), then:

$$\dot{m}_{O_2} = Q \cdot C_0 \cdot X \cdot 2 = \frac{10 \text{ m}^3}{3600 \text{ s}} \times 25{,}000 \text{ g/m}^3 \times 0.90 \times 2 = 125 \text{ g/s} = 7.5 \text{ kg/min}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Plasma Energy Balance for Syngas Production

**Problem:** A plasma gasifier processes **1,000 kg/h of dried MSW (Municipal Solid Waste)** with the following ultimate analysis (mass basis): C = 50%, H = 6%, O = 35%, N = 1%, S = 1%, Ash = 7%, Moisture = 0%. Compute: (a) the cold gas efficiency if syngas LHV = 8 MJ/Nm³ and plasma power = 1.5 MW, and (b) the syngas flow rate.

**Step 1 — Syngas Composition from Stoichiometry (assume 100% carbon conversion to CO+H₂):**

For a feedstock of 1,000 kg/h, the mass of carbon is:
$$\dot{m}_C = 1{,}000 \times 0.50 = 500 \text{ kg/h}$$

The hydrogen balance for steam gasification gives H₂ moles from H₂O + C. Assume the H in the waste is fully liberated as H₂:
$$\dot{m}_{H_2, \text{waste}} = 1{,}000 \times 0.06 = 60 \text{ kg/h} \rightarrow n_{H_2} = \frac{60{,}000}{2} = 30{,}000 \text{ mol/h} = 30 \text{ kmol/h}$$

The CO moles from carbon (50% of carbon → CO via partial oxidation, 50% → CO₂; simplified to 80% CO, 20% CO₂ for plasma conditions):

$$n_{CO} = 0.80 \times \frac{500{,}000}{12} = 33{,}333 \text{ mol/h} = 33.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = 0.20 \times \frac{500{,}000}{12} = 8{,}333 \text{ mol/h} = 8.33 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Total syngas molar flow rate:**

$$\dot{n}_{syn} = n_{CO} + n_{H_2} + n_{CO_2} = 33.33 + 30 + 8.33 = 71.66 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Syngas volumetric flow at STP:**

$$\dot{V}_{syn, STP} = 71.66 \times 22.414 = 1{,}606 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Syngas energy flow rate:**

$$\dot{E}_{syn} = 1{,}606 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} \times 8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 12{,}848 \text{ MJ/h} = 3{,}569 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Feedstock chemical energy input (assume LHV of MSW ≈ 12 MJ/kg):**

$$\dot{E}_{feed} = 1{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \times 12 \text{ MJ/kg} = 12{,}000 \text{ MJ/h} = 3{,}333 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Cold Gas Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{\dot{E}_{syn}}{\dot{E}_{feed} + W_{plasma}} = \frac{3{,}569}{3{,}333 + 1{,}500} = \frac{3{,}569}{4{,}833} = 0.738 = 73.8\%$$

**Step 7 — Mass Balance Check on Slag:**

$$\dot{m}_{slag} = \dot{m}_{feed} \times (\text{Ash fraction} + \text{unconverted C}) \approx 1{,}000 \times 0.07 = 70 \text{ kg/h (inorganic vitrified slag)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Even with **1.5 MW of plasma input**, the cold gas efficiency is 73.8% — which is competitive with conventional gasification (60–80%) and far better than incineration's electrical efficiency (~25%). The plasma energy is "repaid" because the high temperature allows complete carbon conversion that would otherwise be lost to tars and char in low-temperature gasification.

---

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: WAO vs SCWO vs Plasma Gasification

| Parameter | **Plasma Gasification** | **Subcritical WAO** | **Supercritical WAO (SCWO)** | **Incineration** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed Phase | **Solid / Sludge** | **Aqueous Liquid** | **Aqueous Liquid** | Solid |
| Temperature | **1,000 – 10,000 °C** | 150 – 320 °C | **400 – 650 °C** | 850 – 1,200 °C |
| Pressure | 1 – 5 bar | 5 – 200 bar | **> 221 bar** | ~1 bar |
| Energy Source | Electrical (Plasma) | Thermal + O₂ | Thermal + O₂ | Combustion |
| Product | Syngas + Slag | CO₂ + H₂O + Acids | CO₂ + H₂O | CO₂ + Ash + Flue gas |
| Best For | Hazardous, MSW | Dilute organics | Toxic organics | Combustible solids |
| Main Issue | Electrode wear, capex | Corrosion, slow kinetics | Severe corrosion, salt plugging | Emissions, dioxins |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Define plasma. Distinguish between thermal and non-thermal plasma with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A plasma is the **fourth state of matter**, consisting of a quasi-neutral ionized gas containing free electrons, ions, and neutral species, generated when sufficient energy (thermal or electrical) is supplied to a gas to ionize its atoms.

| Feature | Thermal Plasma | Non-Thermal Plasma |
|---|---|---|
| Electron & Gas Temperature | Approximately equal (T_e ≈ T_gas) | T_e >> T_gas (10² to 10³ times higher) |
| Thermodynamic State | Near-equilibrium | Non-equilibrium |
| Typical Temperature | 5,000 – 30,000 K | T_gas near 300 K, T_e ~ 10⁵ K |
| Example Application | **Plasma gasification of MSW** | Ozone generation, surface sterilization |

**Example of Thermal Plasma:** Argon plasma torch used in plasma gasification (used to vitrify waste at >1,500 °C).

**Example of Non-Thermal Plasma:** Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD) reactor used for VOCs removal at ambient temperature.

---

#### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Why is wet air oxidation preferred over incineration for aqueous industrial effluents? List the conditions of temperature and pressure used.**

**Model Answer:**

Wet Air Oxidation (WAO) is preferred over incineration for aqueous effluents due to the following reasons:

1. **Energy efficiency for dilute streams:** Incineration requires vaporization of all the water content, which demands ~2,260 kJ/kg of water. WAO operates in the liquid phase, **eliminating this energy penalty**.
2. **No air pollution from water evaporation:** Avoids the large volumes of wet flue gas and associated latent heat losses.
3. **Safe operation:** Aqueous waste is non-flammable in WAO; incineration requires controlled excess air to avoid incomplete combustion and CO/soot formation.
4. **Selectivity:** WAO preserves inorganic salts; incineration would cause ash-related slagging.

**Standard Operating Conditions of WAO:**

- **Temperature:** **150 – 320 °C** (subcritical, below the critical point of water).
- **Pressure:** **5 – 200 bar** (above the saturation pressure of water at operating temperature to maintain the liquid phase).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistake in Part A:**
> Students often write "WAO is used for solid waste" — **this is wrong**. WAO is exclusively for **aqueous** streams. Plasma gasification is for **solid** waste. The examiner deducts 1 full mark for confusing these two domains.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Option 1).** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a plasma gasification system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the plasma torch and the chemistry of syngas formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A plasma gasifier treats 500 kg/h of shredded plastic waste (ultimate analysis: C = 80%, H = 12%, N = 2%, S = 1%, Ash = 5%). Compute the syngas flow rate (Nm³/h) and the cold gas efficiency if the syngas LHV is 9 MJ/Nm³, the feedstock LHV is 42 MJ/kg, and the plasma power input is 800 kW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Part A(a):**

**Working Principle of Plasma Gasification:**

Plasma gasification is a closed, oxygen-starved thermal process that converts carbonaceous waste into syngas (CO + H₂) and an inert vitrified slag using the extreme heat (3,000 – 10,000 °C) generated by an **electric plasma torch**.

**Block Diagram and Process Description:**

1. **Feed Preparation:** Shredded waste (<10 cm) is fed through a sealed lock-hopper to prevent ingress of atmospheric oxygen (which would combust the syngas).

2. **Drying Zone:** The waste enters the upper zone of the reactor where it is dried at ~150 °C using sensible heat from rising hot gases.

3. **Pyrolysis Zone (500 – 800 °C):** In the absence of oxygen, the organic fraction decomposes thermally into volatile gases, tars, and char (solid carbonaceous residue):
   $$\text{Waste} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{no O}_2} \text{Char} + \text{Tar} + \text{Volatiles} (\text{CH}_4, \text{C}_2\text{H}_4, \text{CO}, \text{CO}_2, \text{H}_2)$$

4. **Plasma Vitrification Zone (>1,500 °C):** A plasma torch (fed with argon or nitrogen gas) injects an arc-heated jet (3,000 – 10,000 °C) into the lower reactor zone. This energy drives the endothermic gasification reactions:
   $$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2, \quad \Delta\text{H} = +131 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
   $$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2, \quad \Delta\text{H} = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
   The inorganic fraction (ash, metals) melts and forms a vitrified slag.

5. **Slag Discharge:** Molten slag is quenched in a water bath, producing inert glassy granules suitable for use as construction aggregate.

6. **Syngas Cleaning:** The raw syngas is cooled, particulates removed by cyclone, HCl/H₂S scrubbed, and the clean syngas is combusted in a gas engine or upgraded to liquid fuels.

**Role of the Plasma Torch:**

The plasma torch is the heart of the system. It uses an electric arc (DC or AC) to ionize a working gas (Ar, N₂, or air) into a high-temperature plasma stream. The plasma supplies:
- **Energy for endothermic gasification** reactions (R3, R4, R6).
- **Heat to melt inorganic matter** into slag (>1,500 °C), preventing ash-handling problems.
- **Localized ultra-high temperature** that ensures >99.99% destruction of hazardous organics (dioxins, furans, PCBs) that would survive conventional incineration.

**Chemistry of Syngas Formation:**

The principal reactions are R1 – R6 listed in Section 2.1.3. The hydrogen-rich syngas (H₂:CO ≈ 1:1) is suitable for downstream Fischer-Tropsch synthesis to liquid fuels, methanol production, or direct combustion for electricity.

---

#### **Model Answer to Part A(b):**

**Step 1 — Mass flow of carbon in feed:**
$$\dot{m}_C = 500 \times 0.80 = 400 \text{ kg/h}$$
$$n_C = \frac{400{,}000}{12} = 33{,}333 \text{ mol/h} = 33.333 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow of hydrogen in feed:**
$$\dot{m}_H = 500 \times 0.12 = 60 \text{ kg/h}$$
$$n_H = \frac{60{,}000}{1} = 60{,}000 \text{ mol/h} = 60 \text{ kmol H atoms/h}$$

**Step 3 — Syngas composition (assuming complete gasification, 80% carbon → CO, 20% → CO₂):**
$$n_{CO} = 0.80 \times 33.333 = 26.667 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$n_{CO_2} = 0.20 \times 33.333 = 6.667 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$n_{H_2} = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ kmol/h (H atoms pair up to form H}_2)$$

**Step 4 — Total syngas molar flow:**
$$\dot{n}_{syn} = 26.667 + 6.667 + 30 = 63.333 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Syngas volumetric flow at STP (0 °C, 1 bar):**
$$\dot{V}_{syn} = 63.333 \times 22.414 = 1{,}419.5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 6 — Energy in syngas:**
$$\dot{E}_{syn} = 1{,}419.5 \times 9 = 12{,}775.5 \text{ MJ/h} = 12{,}775.5/3.6 = 3{,}549 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Energy in feedstock:**
$$\dot{E}_{feed} = 500 \text{ kg/h} \times 42 \text{ MJ/kg} = 21{,}000 \text{ MJ/h} = 5{,}833 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Cold Gas Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{cg} = \frac{3{,}549}{5{,}833 + 800} = \frac{3{,}549}{6{,}633} = 0.535 = \mathbf{53.5\%}$$

**Final Answer:** Syngas flow rate = **1,419.5 Nm³/h**; Cold gas efficiency = **53.5%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Marks Lost in 14-Mark Numerical:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MJ/h to kW** (divide by 3.6). Many students write η_cg as a ratio of MJ/h to MJ/h and get 0.61 instead of 0.535.
> 2. **Not adding the plasma power in the denominator** — The denominator must be (Feedstock energy + Plasma energy), not just feedstock energy.
> 3. **Wrong assumption about carbon partitioning** — A safe assumption is 70–90% to CO and 10–30% to CO₂. If the problem doesn't specify, state the assumption.

---

#### **Question B (Option 2).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Wet Air Oxidation (WAO) system. Discuss the role of high pressure and elevated temperature in maintaining the liquid phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WAO reactor treats a phenol-laden wastewater with COD₀ = 20,000 mg/L at 250 °C. The pseudo-first-order rate constant is k = 0.04 min⁻¹. Calculate (i) the residence time needed for 95% COD removal, (ii) the volume of a CSTR required for a flow rate of 5 m³/h, and (iii) the rate of COD removal (mg/L·min) when COD = 5,000 mg/L. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer to Part B(a):**

**Block Diagram and Working of WAO System:**

The Wet Air Oxidation system consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Feed Storage and High-Pressure Pump:** Wastewater is stored and pressurized to reactor pressure (typically 50 – 100 bar) using a positive-displacement or centrifugal pump.

2. **Feed-Effluent Heat Exchanger:** Incoming cold feed is preheated by the hot reactor effluent, recovering ~60 – 70% of the heat. This is critical for energy economy.

3. **Steam Preheater:** The preheated feed is brought to reaction temperature (180 – 320 °C) using external steam or a fired heater.

4. **Reactor Vessel:** A bubble-column or packed-bed reactor where the liquid and oxygen/air contact intimately. The reaction:
   $$\text{Organics} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Residual Acids}$$
   The reactor is typically a tall, thick-walled pressure vessel with internal baffles or trays to enhance gas-liquid mass transfer.

5. **Cooler and Pressure Letdown:** The hot effluent is cooled against the incoming feed and then depressurized through a pressure-reducing valve.

6. **Gas-Liquid Separator:** The depressurized stream is separated into a clean liquid effluent (sent to biological polishing or discharge) and a vent gas (CO₂, N₂, trace VOCs).

**Role of High Pressure and Temperature:**

- **Why High Temperature?** WAO reactions have **high activation energies (50 – 100 kJ/mol)**. According to the Arrhenius equation, $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$, raising the temperature from 25 °C to 250 °C increases the rate constant by a factor of **10³ – 10⁶**. Without high temperature, the reaction is impractically slow.

- **Why High Pressure?**
  1. **Maintain Liquid Phase:** At 250 °C, the saturation pressure of water is ~40 bar. The reactor must operate above this pressure to prevent water from flashing to steam, which would destroy the dissolved-O₂ reaction pathway.
  2. **Increase Dissolved O₂ Concentration:** By **Henry's Law**, the dissolved O₂ concentration is proportional to the partial pressure:
     $$C_{O_2,aq} = k_H \cdot P_{O_2}$$
     At 50 bar air pressure, the dissolved O₂ is ~50× higher than at 1 bar, ensuring the reaction is not O₂-limited.
  3. **Suppress Volatilization:** High pressure keeps volatile organics in the liquid phase, ensuring they remain available for oxidation.

---

#### **Model Answer to Part B(b):**

**Given:** COD₀ = 20,000 mg/L; T = 250 °C; k = 0.04 min⁻¹; X = 0.95; Q = 5 m³/h.

**Part (i) — Residence time for 95% COD removal (PFR basis):**

$$\tau_{PFR} = \frac{-\ln(1 - X)}{k} = \frac{-\ln(0.05)}{0.04} = \frac{2.996}{0.04} = 74.9 \text{ min}$$

**Part (ii) — CSTR Volume:**

$$\tau_{CSTR} = \frac{X}{k(1 - X)} = \frac{0.95}{0.04 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.95}{0.002} = 475 \text{ min}$$

Converting flow rate:
$$Q = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{5}{60} = 0.0833 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

$$V_{CSTR} = Q \cdot \tau_{CSTR} = 0.0833 \times 475 = 39.6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Part (iii) — Rate of COD removal at COD = 5,000 mg/L:**

$$-\frac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C = 0.04 \times 5{,}000 = 200 \text{ mg/(L·min)}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) τ_PFR = **74.9 min**
- (ii) V_CSTR = **39.6 m³**
- (iii) Rate = **200 mg/(L·min)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — CSTR vs PFR Confusion:**
> 1. **Mistake 1:** Many students apply the CSTR formula to PFR or vice versa, losing 3 marks. **Memory aid:** CSTR uses the equation $\tau = X/[k(1-X)]$ which always gives a LARGER τ than PFR. CSTR τ / PFR τ ratio for first-order = $(X/(-(1-X)\ln(1-X)))$. At X = 0.95, this is 6.34 — a significant volume penalty.
> 2. **Mistake 2:** Not converting Q from m³/h to m³/min when τ is in minutes. This gives a 60× error in V.
> 3. **Mistake 3:** Using the COD₀ value (20,000 mg/L) for part (iii) instead of the **given instantaneous COD (5,000 mg/L)**. The pseudo-first-order rate is **always evaluated at the local COD**, not the inlet.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Rapid-Revision Cheat Sheet:**

#### **Plasma Gasification (5 must-know points):**
- ✅ Plasma is the **4th state of matter** (ionized gas); **plasma gasification uses thermal plasma** at 3,000 – 10,000 °C.
- ✅ The **plasma torch** uses an electric arc + working gas (Ar/N₂) to generate plasma.
- ✅ The 3 main products are: **Syngas (CO + H₂)**, **Vitrified Slag (inert)**, and **Heat**.
- ✅ Key reactions: **R3 (steam gasification)**, **R4 (Boudouard)**, **R5 (water-gas shift)**.
- ✅ **Cold Gas Efficiency** = (Syngas Energy) / (Feedstock Energy + Plasma Power); typical 60 – 85%.

#### **Wet Air Oxidation (5 must-know points):**
- ✅ WAO = **Liquid-phase thermal oxidation** of **aqueous** dilute waste streams.
- ✅ Operating conditions: **T = 150 – 320 °C, P = 5 – 200 bar** (subcritical regime).
- ✅ Used for waste with **COD = 10 – 250 g/L** (too dilute for incineration, too toxic for biology).
- ✅ Kinetics: **Pseudo-first-order** in COD; rate constant follows **Arrhenius** with **Eₐ = 50 – 100 kJ/mol**.
- ✅ **SCWO (T > 374 °C, P > 221 bar)** is the supercritical extension; gives >99.9999% destruction in <60 s.

#### **Comparison Memorization Aid:**
- ✅ **Plasma → SOLID waste → SYNGAS**
- ✅ **WAO → AQUEOUS waste → CO₂ + H₂O**
- ✅ **Incineration → SOLID waste → HEAT/POWER** (combustion-based)
- ✅ **SCWO → AQUEOUS waste → CO₂ + H₂O** (fast, supercritical)

#### **High-Yield Numerical Formulas to Memorize:**
- ✅ ThOD of phenol = **2.383 g O₂ / g phenol**
- ✅ Critical point of water: **T_c = 374.15 °C, P_c = 220.64 bar**
- ✅ Syngas LHV ≈ **(25.7 × x_H₂ + 30.0 × x_CO) MJ/Nm³**
- ✅ Henry's Law: $C_{O_2,aq} = k_H \cdot P_{O_2}$ (linear with pressure)
- ✅ Arrhenius: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ (R = 8.314 J/mol·K)

#### **Examiner's Top 3 Pet Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ❌ Do NOT confuse plasma gasification (solid) with WAO (aqueous) — examiner deducts 1 mark.
- ❌ Do NOT forget the **plasma power input** in the denominator of cold gas efficiency.
- ❌ Do NOT apply the CSTR design equation to a PFR or vice versa.
